blurb: Mount Rainier is an active andesite stratovolcano in Washington's Cascade Range, about 59 miles southeast of Seattle within its own national park; it carries 26 major glaciers (~36 sq mi of ice), making it the most heavily glaciated peak in the contiguous US and, at 13,210 ft of prominence, the most topographically prominent peak in the Lower 48. It was first climbed August 17, 1870 by Hazard Stevens and P.B. Van Trump via the Gibraltar Ledges route.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity, coordinates (46°51'06"N 121°45'37"W), and elevation are unambiguous and well-sourced. One caution for hierarchy placement: Mount Rainier is its own volcanic massif with its own National Park — it is not part of the North Cascades subrange proper (which lies well to the north). If this record is filed under a "North Cascades" parent bucket rather than a general Cascade Range / South-Central Cascades / Mount Rainier NP grouping, that placement is geographically loose and worth revisiting. Separately, the given elevationFt (14,406) matches the 2024 resurvey identifying the Southwest Rim as the current true summit; several NPS route briefs (2017-18) still cite the older Columbia Crest figure of 14,410-14,411 ft as the climbing target because the ice-dome high point has melted down since — both figures are legitimate but refer to different points in the crater, noted per-route below.

routes.fa: Early route history is unclear; the Ingraham Glacier itself was likely first ascended c.1885 by A.L. Brown and a Yakama party (did not summit). The Disappointment Cleaver only became the standard route after the traditional Gibraltar Ledges route collapsed in 1936; the 87th Mountain Infantry Regiment established the first known high camp atop the Cleaver (12,300') on May 8, 1942.
routes.corrections: The peak-level FA (Stevens & Van Trump, 1870) climbed via Gibraltar Ledges, not the Disappointment Cleaver — that is a peak FA, not a route FA, and should not be attributed to this route.
routes.season: Mid-May–late September (guide-service season); route is wanded and maintained through this window
routes.face: Muir Snowfield to the Ingraham Glacier and Disappointment Cleaver rock rib, south side
routes.overview: Rainier's most popular route (~75% of summit attempts): ascend the Muir Snowfield to Camp Muir, cross the Cowlitz and Ingraham glaciers to Ingraham Flats, then climb the loose rock rib of the Cleaver (3rd–4th class) before rejoining glacier travel to the crater rim and Columbia Crest.
routes.beta: A well-marked, guide-maintained route with the widest weather/skill margin of Rainier's standard lines, but heavy weekend traffic and two serious objective-hazard crossings (the Icebox and the Bowling Alley) below the Cleaver.
routes.approach: From Paradise Ranger Station (5,420 ft) follow the Skyline Trail 2.5 mi to Pebble Creek (7,200 ft) at the base of the Muir Snowfield, then ascend the snowfield in a leftward arc roughly 2.7 mi to Camp Muir (10,080 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: crater rim to the top of the Cleaver in one push, a break, then Ingraham Flats and Camp Muir back to Paradise; descent typically takes about two-thirds of the ascent time.
routes.descentText: From Columbia Crest, descend to the top of the DC in a single push, then reconfigure the rope for non-glaciated terrain before descending the Cleaver. Do not stop in the Bowling Alley — rockfall increases with daytime warming. Past Ingraham Flats most objective hazard is behind you for the walk back to Camp Muir.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Paradise (5,420') to Camp Muir (10,080'), 4.5 mi, 5-6 hrs. Day 2 (summit day): alpine start ~midnight-2am from Muir, across the Cowlitz Glacier, Cathedral Gap, Ingraham Flats, up the Cleaver and upper glacier to the crater rim and Columbia Crest, roughly 10-11 hrs round trip from Muir. Day 3 (optional): hike out.
routes.bail: The top of the Cleaver (12,300') is the standard decision point — from there it typically takes as long to reach the summit as it took from Muir; below that, retreat to Ingraham Flats or Camp Muir on the maintained boot path is straightforward.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting and hold to it regardless of proximity to the summit; NPS climbing rangers note most accidents happen on descent due to climber fatigue, and conditions above Camp Muir can deteriorate faster than parties expect.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-May–September; conditions are most stable July-August, though summit weather can turn violently even in summer (sub-zero wind chill, ~20 mph average summit wind).
routes.comms: Cell reception is available at most locations above roughly 6,000 ft per NPS climbing rangers; carry a phone in airplane mode plus a spare battery.
routes.detailedRack: Personal: ice axe w/ leash, crampons, helmet, harness, belay/rappel device, 2 pulleys, 2 prussiks, several carabiners, cordelette, 1-2 pickets, ice screw. Group: shovel, stove/fuel, tent, med kit.
routes.proNeeds: Each climber should carry at least two small pulleys, two prussiks (or mechanical ascenders), several carabiners, and at least two picket anchors for crevasse rescue — especially in parties of three or fewer.
whatToBring: Hooded base layer, soft-shell mid layer, waterproof shell, insulated jacket
GPS w/ topo loaded, headlamp, sleeping bag/pad, sunscreen
proTips: Start 15-20 minutes before or after guided parties' typical midnight-2am departure to avoid bottlenecks
Use pressure-breathing (forceful exhale through pursed lips) to blunt AMS symptoms on the ascent
Short-rope through the Cleaver's loose rock so a dragging rope doesn't trigger rockfall
Keep total exposure time through the Icebox and Bowling Alley under 10 minutes — never stop in the middle
watchOut: The Icebox serac zone (~12,000' on the Ingraham Glacier) is the site of the worst mountaineering accident in North American history (11 killed, Father's Day 1981)
The Bowling Alley below the Cleaver sees frequent human-triggered and spontaneous rockfall
Cathedral Gap and the rocks below Cathedral Rocks are exposed to rockfall
The Muir Snowfield sees the majority of SAR call-outs from whiteout navigation errors
knownHazards: Icefall from seracs on the Ingraham Glacier
Rockfall from cliffs above the Cleaver and from climbers above
Crevasse falls, especially above Ingraham Flats
Altitude illness (AMS/HAPE) and cold injury

pitchDetail.notes: Skyline Trail to Pebble Creek, then Muir Snowfield; 4.5 mi, ~4,660 ft gain
Cross the Cowlitz Glacier, over Cathedral Gap (10,800'), onto the Ingraham Glacier to Ingraham Flats camp (11,200')

pitchDetail.pitch: Ingraham Flats to top of DC
pitchDetail.notes: Through the Icebox and Bowling Alley hazard zones, then up the Cleaver proper (~1,200 vertical ft) to 12,300'

pitchDetail.pitch: Top of DC to crater rim
pitchDetail.notes: Uninterrupted glacier accumulation zone to ~14,150' near Guide Rock, then across the crater to Columbia Crest

road.name: WA-706 (Paradise Road) via Nisqually Entrance
road.status: Paved, generally open year-round to Paradise
road.driveNote: Paved road from the Nisqually Entrance to Paradise (5,420 ft); can close temporarily for winter storms.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast / NWAC Mount Rainier zone forecast
climate.typical: Often below freezing at the summit even in summer; average summit wind ~20 mph
climate.spring: Winter-like storms common May-June
climate.summer: Peak guide season July-August, still subject to sudden whiteouts and lightning
climate.fall: Route deteriorates and crevasses open further by late September

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $82/climber annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2024) plus park entrance fee ($30/vehicle, 7-day); $20 reservation convenience fee if reserved in advance
access.permit: Free climbing permit required (walk-up or reserved up to 24 hrs ahead); must check out at the trailhead afterward ($250 fine for not checking out); solo climbing requires separate written authorization
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful pass or $30/vehicle 7-day entrance pass

timing.recommendedStart: Midnight–2am alpine start from Camp Muir

emergency.rangerStation: Paradise Climbing/Wilderness Information Center — (360) 569-6641
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (Level I trauma; reached by air ambulance for serious injuries)
emergency.notes: NPS maintains a staffed exclusive-use SAR helicopter May 15–Sept 15 (1-3 hr response) and partners with JBLM's US Army Reserve CH-47 Chinook unit for high-altitude extractions. Dial 911; cell coverage is available above roughly 6,000 ft.

routes.fa: First climbed August 1884 (Warner, Forbes, Jones, Wells); first known ski descent 1947 (Roberts, Bengtson, Welsh, Schmidtke).
routes.corrections: Given fa text ('standard non-DC route; high camp at Camp Schurman') was a description, not an actual first-ascent record — replaced with the NPS-documented 1884 first ascent party.
routes.season: Late May (once White River Road opens, ~May 20) through August, before surface crevasses become unnavigable
routes.face: Emmons and Winthrop Glaciers, northeast side, split by Steamboat Prow
routes.overview: Rainier's longest glacier route and second most-climbed line: ascend the Inter Glacier to Camp Curtis, drop to Camp Schurman, then climb the Emmons Glacier's 'Corridor' and 'alpine meadow' to a bergschrund crossing and the crater rim.
routes.beta: Considered a step up from the DC — no guide-maintained trail, wands, or ladders, so route-finding, cramponing on unbroken snow, and self-sufficient crevasse rescue are mandatory skills; the upper mountain can exceed 35-40°.
routes.approach: From White River Campground (4,200 ft) follow the Glacier Basin Trail 3.5 mi to Glacier Basin Campground, then up the Inter Glacier between two nunataks to Camp Curtis (8,700 ft) and down to Camp Schurman (9,500 ft), ~0.5 mi further.
routes.descent: Reverse the route from Columbia Crest to Camp Schurman, typically about two-thirds of the ascent time; keep a GPS track log — the route changes weekly as crevasses and snow bridges shift.
routes.descentText: Descend from Columbia Crest to Camp Schurman via the same line ascended, using a current GPS track log since wands are not maintained on this route; the Corridor and alpine-meadow ramp positions shift throughout the season, so an outdated track cannot be relied upon.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: White River Campground to Camp Schurman (9,500'), ~5 mi. Day 2 (summit day): climb via Emmons Flats (9,800'), the Corridor (10,200'-11,600'), and the alpine meadow to a bergschrund ramp and the crater rim, then to Columbia Crest; Munter Method estimates 5.5 hrs up at a fast pace, 6-7 hrs average.
routes.bail: Below Emmons Flats retreat to Camp Schurman is straightforward; above the Corridor, navigation without a current track log becomes the main risk in poor visibility — shelter in place rather than attempt an unguided descent in a whiteout.
routes.turnaround: Aim for roughly 1,000 ft/hr; climbing slower than 500 ft/hr makes it hard to stay warm and hydrated for the full round trip — build in a hard turnaround time and honor it.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through July for best snow coverage and fewer open crevasses; by August the surface becomes an increasingly unnavigable crevasse labyrinth.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent on this side of the mountain; carry a PLB/satellite device — help may be much farther away than on the DC.
routes.detailedRack: Same core kit as the DC (ice axe, crampons, harness, pulleys, prussiks, pickets, ice screw) plus emergency-specific extras due to the route's remoteness.
routes.proNeeds: Crevasse-rescue competence is mandatory for all party members — there may be no other parties nearby able to assist.
whatToBring: Sleeping bag and pad per party (emergency/patient use)
Down jacket and warm pants per climber
Extra food and water per climber
proTips: The route is a moderately difficult ski descent (Rainier's most popular ski line) due to leeward snow and lack of a rocky cleaver to navigate
Reset your rope for glacier travel promptly once past the technical sections
Camp Schurman has a 48-person nightly limit, about 30 spaces reservable
watchOut: No guide-maintained trail, wands, ladders, or rope lines — tracks left by others are not necessarily the safest line
Crevasses on the Emmons open and close through the season, forcing route changes week to week
The upper Corridor/alpine-meadow section can exceed 35-40°
knownHazards: Crevasse falls on unmaintained, unmarked glacier terrain
Navigation errors in whiteout above Camp Schurman
Steep icy slopes near the summit

pitchDetail.pitch: White River TH to Camp Schurman
pitchDetail.notes: Glacier Basin Trail, Inter Glacier, Camp Curtis (8,700'), down to Camp Schurman (9,500')

pitchDetail.pitch: Camp Schurman to top of the Corridor
pitchDetail.notes: Via Emmons Flats (9,800') to the Corridor ridge feature, 10,200'-11,600'
Traverse right into the 'alpine meadow,' end-running crevasses in switchbacks to a bergschrund ramp

road.name: Sunrise Rd / White River Rd (SR410)
road.status: Seasonal
road.seasonalGate: SR410 closed at the north park boundary from mid-November until the road crew opens it, typically ~May 20
road.driveNote: Guiding is permitted weekdays only on this route; weekends are reserved for non-guided use.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast / NWAC Mount Rainier zone forecast
climate.typical: Sub-zero wind chill common near the summit even in summer
climate.spring: Route not accessible by road until ~May 20
climate.summer: Prime season through July; crevasses open badly by August

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $82/climber annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2024) plus entrance fee
access.permit: Free climbing permit at White River Wilderness Information Center
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful pass or $30/vehicle entrance pass
access.closures: SR410 gated in winter, reopening ~late May

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start from Camp Schurman

emergency.rangerStation: White River Wilderness Information Center — (360) 569-6641
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (Level I trauma; via air ambulance)
emergency.notes: More remote than the DC — fewer climbers on route, so rescue may take longer; NPS/NWAC advise a current GPS track log is essentially required to descend safely in a storm.

routes.fa: An 1857 attempt by Wapowety of the Nisqually people leading August V. Kautz's Army party reached ~12,000' but did not summit. Joe Hazard of The Mountaineers first proved the route practicable to the summit in 1921 and again in 1924.
routes.corrections: Route iceGrade corrected from given 'WI2' to 'AI3' — the NPS Kautz Glacier route brief explicitly states the ice chute crux is AI3 alpine ice ('the technical grade of AI3 does not take into account the effects of altitude and fatigue'). Given fa text was accurate as to naming/history but did not distinguish the 1857 non-summit attempt from the 1921/1924 successful route-proving ascents.
routes.season: Mid-May–early August (use drops sharply after August 1 as the approach and upper glaciers deteriorate)
routes.face: Wapowety Cleaver and the Kautz Ice Chute, south side
routes.overview: Rainier's third most popular route and a real step up from the DC/Emmons: cross the Nisqually Glacier to Wapowety Cleaver, then climb the two-step Kautz Ice Chute (AI3) before rejoining glacier travel to the crater rim.
routes.beta: No guide-maintained trail or fixed protection; requires proficiency in ice climbing, short-pitching, and crevasse rescue. The technical ice pitches occur near 12,000 ft, adding fatigue to the difficulty.
routes.approach: Begins at Paradise (5,420') via the Skyline Trail and Nisqually Moraine Trail, or at the Comet Falls Trailhead (3,650') via Van Trump Park; both converge at the Upper Castle camp (9,500') after crossing the Nisqually and Wilson Glaciers.
routes.descent: Most parties reverse the route (steep down-climbing/rappelling through the ice chute); some continue 'up and over' to descend the easier Disappointment Cleaver instead.
routes.descentText: From the Wapowety Cleaver bivy sites, descend the upper Kautz Glacier's crevasses, then rappel the ice chute's upper step (1-3 rappels depending on rope length, Zero-Thread anchors preferred over V-threads) and lower step (1-2 rappels), traverse back to the Rock Step (awkward 5th-class down-climb/rappel), then descend the Turtle Snowfield to camp and out across the Nisqually Glacier to Paradise.
routes.itinerary: 3-4 day standard strategy: Day 1 approach to ~8,200'+; Day 2 to Camp Hazard (10,800') or the Rock Step; Day 3 summit push and move camp down; Day 4 hike out. Strong teams sometimes complete it in 2 days from the Upper Castle.
routes.bail: The Rock Step (11,000') and the top of the ice chute are natural retreat points below the technical pitches; above the ice chute, crevasse navigation on the upper Kautz becomes the main obstacle for retreat.
routes.turnaround: Overconfidence leading to excessively long climbing times is the leading cause of SAR call-outs on this route — assess pace honestly at the Rock Step before committing to the chute.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-May through July; the ice chute holds good climbing conditions through summer, but the approach becomes progressively harder to travel as the season goes on.
routes.comms: Cell reception available at most locations above ~6,000 ft.
routes.detailedRack: 2 ice tools per person (or 1 axe + 1 tool), steel crampons, helmet, pickets, 2-4 ice screws per person, harness, belay/rappel device, pulleys, prussiks, cordelette.
routes.proNeeds: Modern ice-climbing technique is mandatory for the AI3 chute; teams weak on ice can take hours longer than budgeted at 12,000 ft.
proTips: V-thread/Zero-thread ice anchors placed on the way up can melt out before the return — build fresh rappel anchors on descent
Aim for the ridge crest at 13,000' and stay on glacier surface below that elevation to avoid large moats where the glacier meets Wapowety Cleaver rock
Camp Hazard (10,800') is the best position most parties use for the summit push, though it's exposed to high wind
watchOut: The traverse into the ice chute is exposed to icefall from the ice cliff above — never pause there
Alpine ice breaks in large 'dinner-plate' chunks — keep belays out of the leader's fall line
Some of the largest, cathedral-shaped crevasses on the mountain are found in the 1,000 ft above the ice chute
knownHazards: Icefall from the Kautz Ice Cliff during the chute traverse
Rockfall and small sluffs that can be impossible to self-arrest on steep icy terrain
Crevasse falls on the upper Kautz Glacier
Getting stranded overnight from underestimating the ice-chute time

pitchDetail.notes: Fixed rope on rock anchors at ~11,000'; belay or rappel to the Kautz Glacier
Less steep of the two chute steps; occasional narrow crevasses
Simul-climb or short-rope; exposed to ice/rockfall from above
Technical crux of the route; easier climbing sometimes found in penitentes climber's right

road.name: Paradise Rd (WA-706) or Longmire–Comet Falls Rd
road.status: Paved
road.driveNote: Two approach trailheads: Paradise Ranger Station (5,420') or the Comet Falls Trailhead (3,650') via Van Trump Park.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast / NWAC Mount Rainier zone forecast
climate.typical: Sub-zero wind chill and ~20 mph average summit wind
climate.summer: Best climbing conditions in the ice chute through summer; approach quality drops after early August

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $82/climber annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2024) plus entrance fee
access.permit: Free climbing permit at Paradise WIC (Paradise approach) or Longmire WIC (Comet Falls approach)
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful pass or $30/vehicle entrance pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from high camp for the ice chute

emergency.rangerStation: Paradise or Longmire Wilderness Information Center — (360) 569-6641
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (Level I trauma; via air ambulance)
emergency.notes: JBLM's US Army Reserve Chinook unit has supported multiple Kautz rescues, including a July 2008 crevasse-fall extraction.

routes.fa: Ome Daiber, Arnold Campbell, and Will Borrow, September 1935 (52-hour car-to-car push starting at the Carbon River, finishing at Paradise).
routes.corrections: Given FA name 'Jim Borrow' corrected to 'Will Borrow' per the NPS Liberty Ridge route brief, the most authoritative source located.
routes.season: Typically climbed May–June, before rock is exposed by melt-out; road closures before ~April/May add up to 24 mi round trip
routes.face: Liberty Ridge, between Liberty Wall/Willis Wall and Ptarmigan Ridge, above the Carbon Glacier on the north side
routes.overview: Widely regarded as the hardest and most dangerous regularly-climbed route on Rainier: an 'up-and-over' line up a steep cleaver from the Carbon Glacier over Thumb Rock, the Black Pyramid, and a summit bergschrund to Liberty Cap.
routes.beta: Named one of the '50 Classic Climbs of North America.' Sustained 40-60°+ snow/ice with a technical bergschrund crux near the top; committing because descent becomes harder than continuing up past roughly 11,000 ft.
routes.approach: From the Glacier Basin Trailhead (White River Campground), follow the Glacier Basin Trail 3.5 mi, then the Inter Glacier to St. Elmo Pass (7,500'), descend and traverse the Winthrop Glacier and Curtis Ridge to the Carbon Glacier, reaching the base of the ridge around 8,600'.
routes.descent: Standard descent is the Emmons-Winthrop route from Liberty Cap through Liberty Saddle to Camp Schurman (~3 hrs by Munter Method), then out to White River Campground.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap (14,112'), descend ~600 ft into Liberty Saddle, join the Emmons-Winthrop route, and descend to Camp Schurman; crevasses can be thinly covered between Liberty Cap and Columbia Crest, and large cornices form on the east side of Liberty Saddle. A current GPS track log is essentially required in poor visibility since wands aren't maintained on this route.
routes.itinerary: 3-day standard: Day 1 trailhead to Curtis Ridge camp; Day 2 short acclimatization day to Thumb Rock (10,500'); Day 3 summit day to Liberty Cap and down to Camp Schurman; a 4th night at Schurman is recommended as a weather buffer. Single-push option: leave the trailhead after dinner, climb through the night to be at Thumb Rock by a normal alpine-start time.
routes.bail: Below Thumb Rock, retreat to the Carbon Glacier and back over Curtis Ridge/St. Elmo Pass is straightforward; above roughly 11,000 ft there is no safe bivy option besides Thumb Rock, and ascending to the summit is generally safer than attempting to reverse the ridge.
routes.turnaround: Above 11,000 ft, 'up was down' — ascending typically becomes the safer option than downclimbing; make honest fitness/pace assessments before committing above Thumb Rock, where retreat options disappear.
routes.bestSeason: May and June for the best snow coverage over the ridge's loose rock; climbers must also wait for a stable high-confidence weather window with freezing levels between 6,000-14,000 ft and winds under 30 mph.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent; a PLB/satellite device is strongly advised given the route's remoteness and lack of other parties.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axes w/ leash, crampons with steel front points, helmet, pickets, ice screws, harness, belay/rappel device, pulleys (prussik-minding), carabiners, cordelette, prussiks/sewn runners; roughly 4-6 screws and a few pickets per Mountain Project.
routes.proNeeds: A broad technique toolbox is required: glacier travel, simul-climbing steep snow, short-pitching, and pitched ice climbing at the bergschrund; anchor the belayer and pitch out the schrund crossing if it's dicey.
whatToBring: Contingency food/fuel for unplanned bivouacs — parties commonly underestimate trip length
proTips: Use the Go/No-Go weather matrix (wind, precip, visibility, summit temp) before committing — precipitation and poor visibility alone can be showstoppers
Leave early on the lower ridge to beat near-constant rockfall as the day warms
Keep any music off and stay alert to falling rock, which can arrive with alarming speed and size on the lower ridge
The 3-day itinerary with a 4th contingency night is the most common and forgiving strategy
watchOut: Common incident factors: unexpected time on route, a smaller/worse weather window than forecast, unstable ice/small snow sloughs, and rockfall in warm sunny weather
The bergschrund near the top is typically the toughest technical crux and highly variable year to year
Large, unpredictable avalanches (15-ft crown observed in 2010) have swept nearly half the route
knownHazards: Avalanche — a 2014 avalanche killed six climbers (2 guides, 4 clients) near Thumb Rock, the deadliest known incident on the route
Rockfall on the lower ridge, worst in warm/sunny conditions
Crevasse falls crossing the Winthrop and Carbon Glaciers, including large pendulum-type falls on the Winthrop traverse
Storm exposure with no safe bivy above Thumb Rock

pitchDetail.notes: Glacier Basin Trail, Inter Glacier, St. Elmo Pass (7,500'), across Winthrop Glacier/Curtis Ridge to the Carbon Glacier
Carbon Glacier (8,600') to Thumb Rock (10,500'), the standard high camp
Rock band above Thumb Rock, then low-angle ice or steep snow along Black Pyramid's east side to 12,800'
Ridge crest to the bergschrund below Liberty Cap (~13,800')
Liberty Cap summit at 14,112'

road.name: Glacier Basin Trailhead / White River Rd (SR410)
road.status: Seasonal
road.seasonalGate: SR410 closed at the north park boundary mid-November to April/May
road.driveNote: Attempting the route before the road fully opens to White River Campground adds up to 24 mi round trip.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier Recreational Forecast / NWAC Mount Rainier zone forecast
climate.typical: Big storms generally track in from the southwest off the Pacific
climate.spring: Notoriously long-lasting storms bring winter-like conditions in May-June, the main climbing window

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $82/climber annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2024) plus entrance fee; $20 reservation convenience fee
access.permit: Free climbing permit at White River Ranger Station
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful pass or $30/vehicle entrance pass
access.closures: SR410 gated in winter; only one guide concession is permitted on the route per week, with a max guided group size of 6 including guides

timing.recommendedStart: Evening departure for single-push ascents; early morning from Thumb Rock for the 3-day strategy

emergency.rangerStation: White River Wilderness Information Center — (360) 569-6641
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (Level I trauma; via air ambulance)
emergency.notes: ~40 SAR missions/year park-wide; JBLM Chinook unit is a key cooperator on this route given its remoteness. The 2014 avalanche fatality accident is the route's defining case study — NPS notes 'a capable team making good decisions still ended up in a dire situation.'

routes.fa: Hans Fuhrer, Heine Fuhrer, Joseph Hazard, and Thomas Hermans, July 2, 1920.
routes.season: Late spring–early summer (stable but not overly wet snow)
routes.face: Fuhrer Finger couloir, south face between the Kautz and Nisqually Glaciers
routes.overview: A direct, steep snow couloir splitting the south face — the most direct line from Paradise to the summit, offering sustained 40-45° climbing in the couloir itself.
routes.approach: From Paradise (5,400') follow the Skyline Trail to Glacier Vista (6,300', the rope-up point), drop onto the Nisqually Glacier, cross it, and climb the snow gully to the base of the couloir (~700 ft gain to reach it).
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the Kautz Glacier route rather than downclimbing the Finger, trading rockfall exposure in the couloir for icefall exposure beneath the Kautz Ice Cliff.
proTips: An early start minimizes rockfall exposure in the lower couloir
Move quickly through the chute rather than stopping to rest
watchOut: Rockfall and avalanche hazard in the lower couloir — this is the route's defining hazard
Icefall exposure beneath the Kautz Ice Cliff if descending via the Kautz
knownHazards: Rockfall in the Finger couloir
Avalanche potential on the approach and lower couloir
Crevasse navigation above 13,000'
routes.proNeeds: A few pickets and runners; helmets are strongly recommended given rockfall on both the approach and the route.

road.name: WA-706 (Paradise Road)
road.status: Paved
road.driveNote: Approach from Paradise Ranger Station (5,400 ft).

routes.fa: Jim Wickwire, Charlie Raymond, and Tom Stewart, May 27, 1972.
routes.season: Late spring–early summer, similar window to the neighboring Fuhrer Finger
routes.face: Fuhrer Thumb couloir, immediately west of the Fuhrer Finger, south face
routes.overview: A narrower, steeper variation of the Fuhrer Finger, climbing the couloir immediately to its west before rejoining the Finger route on the west side of the Nisqually Glacier at about 11,500'.
routes.approach: Same as the Fuhrer Finger to a camp around 9,200', then traverse the Wilson Glacier below the Wilson Headwall to reach the Thumb couloir.
routes.descent: Not typically down-climbed; descend via the Kautz Glacier, Ingraham Glacier, or Disappointment Cleaver through Camp Muir instead.

road.name: WA-706 (Paradise Road)
road.status: Paved
road.driveNote: Same Paradise-based approach as the Fuhrer Finger.

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (NPS)
access.fees: $82/climber annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2024) plus entrance fee
access.permit: Free climbing permit at Paradise WIC

routes.fa: Hazard Stevens and P.B. Van Trump, August 17, 1870 — this is also the first ascent of the peak.
routes.season: Winter/spring, when the ledges are firmly frozen — extreme rockfall/icefall hazard once the route melts out
routes.face: Gibraltar Rock ledges and upper Cowlitz Glacier / Cowlitz Cleaver, south side
routes.overview: Historic route of the peak's 1870 first ascent: traverse ledges along the south flank of Gibraltar Rock to the Gibraltar Chute (~50° snow) and the 'Camp Comfort' saddle, rejoining the standard DC boot path near 13,500'.
routes.approach: Same as the DC: Paradise via the Skyline Trail to Pebble Creek and the Muir Snowfield to Camp Muir, then ascend the upper Cowlitz Glacier toward the Cowlitz Cleaver.
routes.descent: Typically via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct; the ledges themselves are only descended when temperatures are cold enough to keep rock and ice in place.
watchOut: Significant rockfall/icefall hazard on the ledges and in Gibraltar Chute once melted out — recommended only in winter/spring with firm snow/ice cover
Short sections of loose 4th-class rock may be exposed depending on conditions
knownHazards: Rockfall along Gibraltar Rock
Icefall in Gibraltar Chute
Exposure on the ledge traverse

routes.season: Early season, before the glacier breaks up
routes.face: Tahoma Glacier, west side, feeding from the Sunset Amphitheater icefall
routes.overview: Ascends the long, heavily crevassed Tahoma Glacier via Emerald Ridge, requiring a series of diagonal zigzag traverses to thread the icefall below the Sunset Amphitheater; route-finding shifts from year to year.
routes.approach: From the Westside Road, hike the Tahoma Creek Trail, turn north on the Wonderland Trail to ~5,600 ft, then northeast on moraine to a camp on the crest of Emerald Ridge (~6,100 ft) between the north and south lobes of the Tahoma Glacier.
knownHazards: Heavily crevassed glacier requiring careful, year-to-year route-finding
Icefall in the Sunset Amphitheater above

routes.fa: Wolf Bauer and Jack Hossack, September 8, 1935; a variation was added in 1966 by Bloomer, Kelsey, Pinsch, and Williams.
routes.corrections: Given FA 'Wiessner-House 1935' does not match sourced records — Mountain Project credits Wolf Bauer and Jack Hossack with the September 8, 1935 first ascent. Fritz Wiessner and Bill House were active elsewhere in the Cascades/Coast Mountains in this era but are not documented as the Ptarmigan Ridge first-ascent party.
routes.season: Often in good condition by May, though the Mowich Lake access road frequently doesn't open until early July
routes.face: Ptarmigan Ridge, a terraced buttress/ledge system between the Mowich Face and Liberty Ridge on the north side
routes.overview: A terraced buttress rather than a true ridge: a rising traverse threading rock bands toward the ice cliffs, then sustained 40-55° snow/mixed climbing to the summit plateau, roughly 1,000 ft more gain on summit day than Liberty Ridge.
routes.approach: Via Mowich Lake and Spray Park from the west, or White River Campground from the east; from Spray Park a climber's trail ascends the lower part of the ridge.
routes.proNeeds: Early-season ascents may need only 3-4 pickets; later in the season, verglassed rock and water-ice sections can require ice screws and a light rock/pin rack.

routes.face: Sunset Ridge, between the upper Mowich Face and Sunset Amphitheater on the west side
routes.overview: Climbs the ridge separating the upper Mowich Face from the Sunset Amphitheater via a series of steep gullies and open faces (50-55°), bypassing the bergschrund on 45° slopes before the ridge forces climbers onto the upper Mowich Face near 12,800'.
routes.approach: From the Westside Road gate (~2,900 ft), walk the road roughly 3.5 mi to Round Pass; leave the cleaver at 8,500 ft and traverse north across the Puyallup and South Mowich Glaciers to the base of the ridge.
routes.descent: Via Liberty Cap and the standard Emmons-Winthrop route, or a carryover descent on the Disappointment Cleaver.

routes.fa: Dee Molenaar, Gene Prater, Jim Wickwire, and Dick Pargeter (Central Mowich Face; exact year not specified in available sources).
routes.face: Central Mowich Face, northwest side above Mowich Lake
routes.overview: Two documented lines up the Central Mowich Face: a Grade III variation cutting left at 12,500' through an hourglass of snow/ice/rock to a 40-50° snow ramp, and a harder Grade IV variation further right with AI2-3 ice steps and 4-6 pitches of 50-60° snow finishing on the Liberty Cap Glacier.
routes.approach: Via the Wonderland Trail from Mowich Lake through Spray Park, then a climber's trail up the lower part of Ptarmigan Ridge; at 8,300 ft leave the ridge and descend ~1,000 ft to the North Mowich Glacier.
routes.descent: Carry over via the Emmons or Disappointment Cleaver route.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rack: a couple of ice screws for the steep steps plus 2-4 pickets.
knownHazards: Remote, self-sufficient objective with a long approach
Rockfall exposure on the face — best climbed in the morning before the sun hits the wall

routes.fa: Gene Prater and Marcel Schuster (exact year not specified in available sources).
routes.face: Curtis Ridge, between Liberty Ridge and Willis Wall on the north side
routes.overview: A committing, infrequently-climbed ridge considered technically harder, more confusing, and more committing than Liberty Ridge or Ptarmigan Ridge; the technical crux begins at 10,300' with a rappel to snow slopes that serves as the route's point of no return.
routes.descent: Down the Emmons Glacier to Camp Schurman.
routes.proNeeds: A light alpine rack is sufficient — a couple of pitons, nuts, ~4 cams, and cord for the rappel station.
watchOut: Some consider it a 'rockfall death route'; recommended only in good, cold conditions for fast, competent parties
The rappel at 10,300 ft is a firm point of no return

routes.fa: Charles Bell, solo, June 1961 (West Rib to the ice cliff); credit was contested until Jim Wickwire — who made his own 1963 East Rib ascent — later corroborated Bell's account and photographs.
routes.face: Willis Wall, the massive north face between Liberty Ridge and Curtis Ridge, capped by a 200-300 ft summit ice cliff
routes.overview: Described by climbers as 'the most fearsome alpine face in the lower 48 states' — a huge, continually-shedding north face beneath the summit ice cap, rarely and only briefly in condition.
routes.descentText: No established rappel line; parties typically top out on the summit plateau and descend via the Emmons-Winthrop or Disappointment Cleaver.
knownHazards: Very active seracs from the summit ice cap continually rake the wall
Considered one of the most dangerous objective-hazard faces in the contiguous US
watchOut: Icefall danger is severe and essentially constant — not a route for casual attempts
No standard descent line off the wall itself

routes.face: Edmunds Headwall, dropping from Liberty Cap on the northwest side
routes.season: June–July for ski descent, when the slope corns up in the afternoon sun
routes.overview: A sustained, direct line dropping off Liberty Cap on Rainier's northwest side, from roughly 13,400' to 9,900'; best documented as a ski-mountaineering descent rather than an ascent route.
routes.beta: First skied in 1997 by Armond DuBuque, Doug Ingersoll, Andrew McLean, and Carl Skoog; typically accessed via the Mowich Lake road.

routes.face: Kautz Ice Chute headwall (technical ice section of the Kautz Glacier route), south side
routes.overview: The technical ice-climbing crux of the Kautz Glacier route: roughly 300 ft of glacier ice (up to ~50°) climbable in two pitches with a 60m rope, following a lower ~200 ft of low-angle ice.
routes.corrections: This appears to describe the technical ice headwall/crux pitches within the standard Kautz Glacier route rather than a fully distinct named line — NPS and guide-service sources treat it as part of the Kautz Glacier climb, not a separate route. Kept as its own catalog entry per the given route list, but overlaps substantially with wa_mount_rainier_kautz_glacier.
knownHazards: The traverse into the chute is exposed to icefall from the ice cliff above — no stopping in this zone
Alpine ice breaks in large 'dinner-plate' chunks, hazardous to belayers below

routes.overview: The most steeply cascading glacier on Rainier's southwest flank; surveyed after its 1948 first ascent and subsequently discouraged as a standard climbing route to the summit because of persistent avalanche hazard.
knownHazards: Frequent avalanches in the chute between the icefall's eastern edge and the Nisqually Cleaver rocks
Numerous hidden crevasses/holes

routes.fa: No documented climbing first-ascent party specific to this line; the Ingraham Glacier was likely first ascended c.1885 by A.L. Brown and a Yakama party (did not summit), and first SKIED June 18, 1961 by John Ahern, Bill Briggs, Roger Brown, Gordie Butterfield, Joe Marillac, Roger Paris, and Jim & Lou Whittaker.
routes.corrections: Given fa (peak FA, Stevens/Van Trump 1870 via Gibraltar Ledges) does not apply to this route — replaced with the best-documented Ingraham Glacier history located.
routes.season: Mid-May–early June; has not remained climbable into June since 2005 per NPS climbing rangers, but is a viable winter/early-spring option
routes.face: Ingraham Glacier direct headwall, bypassing the Disappointment Cleaver's rock rib to its climber's left
routes.overview: Climbs the Ingraham Glacier directly to the top of the Disappointment Cleaver rather than traversing onto the Cleaver's rock rib; generally preferred over the DC when in condition, but the glacier has broken up too early in the season to remain viable past May in most recent years.
routes.approach: Same as the DC to Ingraham Flats (11,200'), then continue straight up-glacier via the Lower Ingraham Headwall instead of turning right onto the Cleaver.
routes.descentText: Reverse the route via the Lower Ingraham Headwall switchbacks back to Ingraham Flats and Camp Muir; this line avoids the DC's 'Icebox' icefall zone entirely.
watchOut: Avalanche hazard on the Lower Ingraham Headwall itself and from slopes above
Icefall hazard from seracs in the upper Ingraham Headwall — no stopping on this stretch
Route-finding through the heavily crevassed area above the headwall can force parties close to active rockfall zones under Gibraltar Rock
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on the Lower Ingraham Headwall
Icefall from upper Ingraham Headwall seracs
Heavy crevassing requiring careful route-finding, worse after storms bury open cracks
proTips: A fairly popular ski descent when in good condition
Guide companies often open the season on the ID for 1-2 trips in mid-May before switching to the DC for the rest of summer

blurb: Mount Adams (12,281 ft) is a broad, glacier-draped andesite/dacite stratovolcano in the southern Washington Cascades, the state's second-highest peak, straddling the Mount Adams Wilderness of Gifford Pinchot National Forest and the Yakama Indian Reservation. Its huge, gently domed summit ice cap feeds a dozen named glaciers (Adams, Lava, Lyman, Wilson, Rusk, Mazama, Klickitat and others) radiating down all sides, giving it a mix of easy snow walk-ups on the south and west and serious, rarely repeated glacier headwalls on the north and east. First climbed in September 1854 by A.G. Aiken, Edward J. Allen, and Andrew J. Burge, members of a military road-survey crew; the technical east face wasn't climbed until 1921 (Rusk and the Cascadians) and the west face until 1924 (Mazamas).
hierarchyNote: Peak identity checks out: name, coordinates (46.2026°N, 121.4906°W per Wikipedia/USGS), and elevation (12,276-12,281 ft depending on datum) are internally consistent and well documented. One geographic flag: Mount Adams sits in the SOUTHERN Washington Cascades (Mount Adams Wilderness/Gifford Pinchot NF, roughly midway between Mt. St. Helens and Mt. Hood), not in the North Cascades range proper (which lies ~100+ miles north, e.g. Mt. Baker/Glacier Peak/Cascade Pass area). If this peak's parent hierarchy bucket is labeled "North Cascades," it should instead be filed under a South/Southern Washington Cascades region.

routes.overview: Mount Adams's standard, non-technical route: a long snow slog up the broad southern flank via Crescent/Suksdorf Ridge, the Lunch Counter camp, and the false summit ('Piker's Peak') to the true summit beyond a shallow crater traverse.
routes.beta: The most popular and easiest way up the mountain. From the South Climb/Cold Springs trailhead the route follows an old roadbed through the 2012 Cascade Creek burn scar, climbs into subalpine meadows, crosses the Round-the-Mountain Trail and the PCT, then ascends Crescent Ridge to broad snowfields leading past Lunch Counter to Piker's Peak and the true summit. No rope or technical protection is used on the standard line in normal conditions — ice axe and crampons suffice.
routes.approach: From Trout Lake, drive north on Mt Adams Rd to the Forest Road 23 junction; bear right about a mile to the signed FR 80 junction for South Climb, turn left and follow FR 80 for 3.7 miles to FR 8040, then follow FR 8040 (steep, narrow, sharp switchbacks — no RVs/trailers) for 8.1 miles to its end at the South Climb/Cold Springs trailhead (5,600 ft). The trail follows an old roadbed through the burn scar, enters subalpine forest and meadows, crosses Round-the-Mountain Trail #9 and the PCT, then climbs Crescent Ridge to the base of the summit snowfields.
routes.descent: Descend the same snow slopes back to Lunch Counter and Crescent Ridge.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line: cross back over the shallow summit crater, down over Piker's Peak, and descend the broad snow slopes of Suksdorf/Crescent Ridge to Lunch Counter, then follow the old roadbed back to the trailhead. Glissading is common on the lower snowfields in firm-but-soft conditions; watch for exposed rock and runout hazards in late season as snow recedes.
routes.itinerary: Most parties do it in 2 days: hike in and camp near Lunch Counter (~9,000-9,400 ft) day one, alpine-start for the summit push, then descend to the car day two. Strong parties climb it car-to-car in a single long day (roughly 10-14 hours total).
routes.bail: Retreat is simple in any conditions — descend the same broad snow slopes; there are no technical sections or rappels required. In whiteout, navigate by compass/GPS bearing since the upper mountain is broad and featureless.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time based on weather and party pace rather than a fixed clock — afternoon thunderstorms and whiteout are the main hazards on this otherwise non-technical, featureless volcanic slope.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the standard window, once the access road has melted out; a Mt. Adams Climbing Pass is required above 7,000 ft from May 1 through September 30. Early/late season brings firmer snow and fewer crowds but colder temps and icier upper slopes.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is spotty at the trailhead and largely absent on-route; do not rely on cell phones for emergency communication.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is used on the standard line; ice axe and crampons only — rope and glacier gear are not needed.
whatToBring: Sun protection (glacier glasses, sunscreen, lip balm)
Extra water/filter (scarce above Lunch Counter early season)
Layered clothing for wind and whiteout
proTips: Start well before sunrise so you're off the upper snowfields before the afternoon sun softens them and increases rockfall/postholing
Water can be scarce above Lunch Counter, especially early season — fill up at Morrison Creek or seasonal melt pools
A high-clearance vehicle is strongly recommended for the rough FR 8040 approach road
watchOut: Sudden whiteout — the upper mountain is broad and featureless with no landmarks, and has caused a number of lost-climber incidents despite the route's low technical difficulty
Loose rock exposed around Piker's Peak/the false summit late season
No shade on the snow slopes — sunburn and heat exhaustion are common
knownHazards: Whiteout navigation on the featureless upper snow slopes
Exposed loose rock late season near Piker's Peak
Afternoon thunderstorms/rapid weather changes
routes.face: South Spur / Suksdorf Ridge

road.name: Forest Road 8040 (via FR 80 off FR 23)
road.status: Gravel, steep and narrow with sharp switchbacks; no RVs or trailers
road.seasonalGate: Typically melted out and accessible by June, snowpack-dependent
road.driveNote: From Trout Lake via Mt Adams Rd to FR 23, then FR 80 (3.7 mi) and FR 8040 (8.1 mi) to the trailhead

climate.typical: Typical Cascade-volcano weather — clear mornings can build to afternoon convective weather and thunderstorms; conditions change quickly and whiteout is common on the broad summit plateau.
climate.spring: Full snow coverage, cold, avalanche potential on steeper aspects
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; snow retreats to expose rock and terrain hazards below ~7,200 ft by late summer
climate.fall: Access road and snow conditions increasingly variable; early snow can close the road
climate.winter: Deep snow, avalanche hazard, access road typically snowed in/gated

emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Adams Ranger District, 2455 WA-141, Trout Lake, WA 98650 — (509) 395-3402

routes.overview: A non-technical alternative to the South Climb up the broad North Cleaver between the Adams and Lava glaciers, mixing steep snow with loose volcanic scree.
routes.beta: From high camp near the Adams Glacier moraine, gain the North Ridge (North Cleaver) and follow it upward, bypassing most obstacles on either side, to the summit ice cap; traverse roughly a mile across the plateau to reach the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Killen Creek Trailhead (Forest Road 2329), hike the Killen Creek Trail #113 about 3.5 miles, cross the Pacific Crest Trail, then navigate southeast/uphill toward the North Ridge. Snow can obscure the trail early season — carry a compass or GPS track.
routes.descent: Descend the same route (North Ridge).
routes.descentText: Retrace the ridge back down, staying alert for loosening volcanic scree as the snow recedes through the season; many parties glissade the upper snowfields where conditions allow.
routes.bail: Descend the ridge the way you came; no technical terrain or rappels are involved.
routes.turnaround: Use a weather- and pace-based turnaround; whiteout during the crossing of the broad, featureless summit ice cap is the main navigational hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through July is best, while the ridge is still largely snow-covered; by late summer the route can be thousands of feet of loose, rotten volcanic scree.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is limited to absent on this side of the mountain.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection needed; standard snow-climbing kit (ice axe, crampons) only.
whatToBring: Navigation aid for the summit plateau traverse
proTips: Climb it early season while snow still covers the scree for a much more pleasant ascent
Descend the same line rather than trying to shortcut through the Adams or Lava glacier basins
watchOut: Loose volcanic scree once the snow melts out — can be thousands of feet of it by late summer
Whiteout navigation across the broad, featureless summit ice cap
knownHazards: Loose rock/scree
Whiteout on the summit plateau
Steep snow to roughly 50°
routes.face: North Ridge / North Cleaver, between Adams and Lava Glaciers

routes.corrections: Grade corrected from 'Grade III, steep snow' to 'Grade II+, steep snow/scree to ~50°' — Mountain Project lists this route as Grade II with 3,500 ft gain, and Northwest Mountain School's guide describes it as Grade II+ with snow/ice to 50°; no source supports a Grade III rating. Also corrected 'fa' from the given 'A.G. Aiken party, 1854' to null: Mountain Project's North Ridge page appears to attribute the mountain's overall 1854 first ascent to this specific route, but the USGS Cascades Volcano Observatory historical account states the exact route/side of the mountain the 1854 party climbed was never recorded, so a route-specific FA claim for North Ridge is not supportable.

routes.overview: Mount Adams's classic north-face glacier climb: a moderately steep icefall route ascending the largest glacier on the mountain, with sections of alpine ice through crevassed terrain to the summit ice cap.
routes.beta: More an icefall tumbling between two rock ridges than a true face, the Adams Glacier route generally ascends the right third of the glacier before working left to avoid crevasses, with moderate-angle snow interspersed with steeper alpine-ice sections (between roughly 11,800 and 9,200 ft the glacier flows through a steep icefall with a maze of seracs). The upper icefall can be passed on either side before a long traverse of the summit plateau to the true summit.
routes.approach: Access via Forest Roads 23, 21 and 2329 to the Killen Creek Trailhead (#113), continue past the Pacific Crest Trail onto the High Camp Trail #10, and camp in the meadows around 6,900-7,600 ft with a full view of the route for planning.
routes.descent: Descend via the North Ridge (or the snowfield just west of it) back to high camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the North Ridge/North Cleaver, watching for loose rock, back down to the Adams Glacier moraine and high camp; some parties use a snowfield just west of the ridge crest instead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip: hike in and camp at high camp (6,900-7,600 ft) day one, alpine-start for the glacier climb and summit day two, descending via the North Ridge.
routes.bail: Retreating back down the glacier is possible but extends crevasse and icefall exposure; most parties instead continue to the summit and descend via the easier North Ridge.
routes.turnaround: Get off the glacier before the heat of the day increases icefall and snow-bridge collapse risk — use a conservative, conditions-based turnaround rather than a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Best in a fairly narrow early-to-midsummer window before crevasses fully open; by mid-to-late season, route-finding through the icefall's crevasses can become difficult to impossible.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this side of the mountain.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and ice screws for the steeper icefall sections; most of the route protects with a running belay off pickets, reserving screws for the steepest ice.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier rack: several pickets, 2-3 ice screws, one rope for the rope team; crevasse-rescue kit. Most terrain is simul-climbed.
whatToBring: Extra layers for a very early start
proTips: Get an alpine-early start to be off the glacier before the sun weakens snow bridges and triggers icefall
Camp at high camp (6,900-7,600 ft) for a full view of the route to plan your line the night before
Expect route-finding through crevasses to get progressively harder as the season goes on
watchOut: Crevasses, especially by mid-to-late summer
Icefall as the day warms
Steeper sections may require a belay with screws/pickets
knownHazards: Crevasse fall
Icefall/serac hazard in the icefall zone (~9,200-11,800 ft)
Rockfall on the North Ridge descent
routes.face: North Face (Adams Glacier icefall)

routes.corrections: Grade corrected from the given 'Grade IV, steep glacier ice/snow' to 'Grade III, steep glacier ice/snow' — Mountain Project rates it AI2-3/Grade III overall, and multiple trip reports title it 'Grade III, Steep Snow, AI2'; no source found supports Grade IV. iceGrade broadened from AI2 to AI2-3 to match Mountain Project's rating. Added fa: Fred Beckey, Dave Lind, and Robert Mulhall made the first ascent of the Adams Glacier route in July 1945 (per the glacier's Wikipedia history entry) — the given data had this route's fa as null.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Dave Lind, Robert Mulhall — July 1945

routes.overview: A snow-and-talus ridge climb between the Adams and White Salmon/Avalanche glaciers — a variation on the Adams Glacier approach that offers a less crevassed alternative while still delivering sustained 30-40° climbing near the top.
routes.beta: From high camp, traverse the lower Adams Glacier (staying below 8,000 ft to avoid its crevasses), gain the main ridgeline south of the glacier, and follow it — with detours off either side to bypass the steepest sections — to the Pinnacle at just over 12,000 ft; from there descend into the shallow summit crater and back up to the true summit. The lower ridge is mellow ~30° snow and loose volcanic talus/boulder field; around 10,000 ft it steepens to about 40°, and a large rock outcropping near 10,500 ft forces a detour, commonly a ~50-foot traverse onto 40° snow on the North Face of the Northwest Ridge.
routes.approach: Same as the Adams Glacier route: access via Forest Roads 23/21/2329 to the Killen Creek Trailhead (#113), continue past the PCT to High Camp Trail #10, and camp in the meadows around 6,900-7,600 ft.
routes.descent: Descend the same ridge, or via the adjacent North Ridge.
routes.descentText: From the summit/Pinnacle, most parties retrace the ridge; some instead descend the North Ridge to avoid re-crossing the steepest upper sections.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip mirroring the Adams Glacier route: hike to high camp day one, summit push and descend day two.
routes.bail: Descend the ridge back the way you came; no technical rappels are required in normal conditions.
routes.turnaround: Weather- and conditions-based turnaround; the main risk is straying too high on the lower Adams Glacier traverse into crevassed terrain.
routes.bestSeason: Similar window to the Adams Glacier and North Ridge routes — best in early-to-mid summer before the lower talus fully melts out.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is limited to absent on this side of the mountain.
routes.proNeeds: Strong parties typically climb it unroped (crampons, axe, helmet); rope, harness, pickets and a belay device are recommended for less experienced climbers on the steeper upper sections. No dedicated glacier-travel gear is needed.
whatToBring: Navigation aid for the ridge/crater traverse
proTips: Stay below 8,000 ft on the lower Adams Glacier traverse to avoid its crevasses before gaining the ridge
Expect to detour off the ridge crest around the 10,500 ft rock outcropping onto the North Face snow
watchOut: Do not go above 8,000 ft on the lower Adams Glacier traverse — crevasses open up above that
A large rock outcropping near 10,500 ft forces a detour onto exposed 40° snow
knownHazards: Loose volcanic talus/boulder field low on the ridge
Steepens to about 40° near 10,000 ft
Crevasse hazard if the Adams Glacier traverse strays too high
routes.face: Northwest Ridge, between Adams Glacier and White Salmon/Avalanche Glaciers, to the Pinnacle

routes.overview: A steep, triangular glacier headwall on Mount Adams's remote north/northeast side, wedged between the North Ridge and Lava Ridge — best done early season to avoid icy conditions and rockfall.
routes.beta: The headwall forms a triangle between the North Ridge and the Lava Ridge and is climbed as moderate-to-steep glacier ice/snow up through the triangle to gain the upper mountain. It qualifies for the Mazamas' '16 Major NW Peaks' and 'Guardian Peaks' award routes and is typically done car-to-car in two or more days.
routes.approach: From the Muddy Meadows Trailhead (#13, ~4,400 ft) on Mount Adams's east/northeast side, hike in toward the glacier basin between the North Ridge and Lava Ridge; this is a big, remote approach best treated as a multi-day car-to-car trip.
routes.descent: Descend via the North Ridge (or South Climb, depending on logistics) back toward a trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a 2+ day car-to-car trip: approach and camp near the glacier basin, then an early summit push up the headwall.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier/headwall the way you came; this is a remote route without established fixed rappel stations.
routes.turnaround: Start early and turn around if the headwall shows active rockfall or icy, unconsolidated conditions — this route is explicitly best climbed early season for exactly this reason.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (roughly June) is best, to avoid icy conditions and rockfall that increase later in summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this remote area of the mountain.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier-route rack: pickets and a few ice screws for the steeper headwall sections; rope for glacier travel.
whatToBring: Extra food/fuel for a multi-day approach
proTips: Climb it early season (June) — later-season conditions bring icy climbing and active rockfall
Budget 2+ days car-to-car given the remote approach
watchOut: Icy conditions and rockfall increase significantly later in the season
knownHazards: Rockfall off the headwall bands as the season progresses
Icy conditions early/late season
Remote terrain with limited rescue access
routes.face: Northeast headwall, between North Ridge and Lava Ridge

routes.overview: A moderate-to-steep glacier climb on Mount Adams's northeast side, ascending the Lyman Glacier's right side, with a narrow rock outcrop splitting the glacier into two branches.
routes.beta: Climbed in late spring, the route is primarily moderate and steep snow and sometimes alpine ice, ascending the glacier's right side under normal conditions; a narrow rock outcrop separates the glacier into halves, with the north portion being the original/standard line. Most of the route can be simul-climbed and is protectable with pickets and a running belay.
routes.approach: Approach via the Pacific Crest Trail from a lava field near Devil's Garden on Mount Adams's northeast side.
routes.descent: Descend via the North Cleaver (North Ridge) or the adjacent snowfield.
routes.descentText: Down-climb or glissade the North Cleaver or the snowfield beside it; this terrain includes loose, rotten volcanic rock in spots — take care not to release rockfall onto parties below.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier the way you came, staying on its more consolidated right/north side away from the crevassed south branch.
routes.turnaround: Conditions-based turnaround; the twin icefalls on the upper glacier (south portion heavily broken) are the main hazard driving route timing.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring (roughly May-June) is the typical window, while snow still covers the glacier and surrounding rock.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this side of the mountain.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and a running belay for most of the route; occasional alpine ice may call for a screw or two.
proTips: Stick to the north portion of the glacier (the original line) — the south portion is heavily broken and hazardous
Descend the North Cleaver carefully to avoid triggering rockslides on parties below
watchOut: Rockfall risk on the North Cleaver descent
Twin icefalls on the upper glacier — the south portion is heavily broken and hazardous
knownHazards: Crevasses/icefall on the upper glacier
Loose volcanic rock on the North Cleaver descent
routes.face: Northeast, Lyman Glacier

routes.overview: One of Mount Adams's most serious and rarely repeated technical lines, climbing through Roosevelt Cliff on the mountain's remote east side via the Wilson Glacier.
routes.beta: Grouped with Victory Ridge and the Rusk Glacier Headwall as among Mount Adams's most challenging and infrequently climbed routes, this line ascends Roosevelt Cliff — a rock rib that splits the Wilson Glacier, named (along with the glacier itself) by Claude Ewing Rusk in 1919. The Wilson Glacier is fed by avalanching snow and ice off the summit ice cap starting around 10,800 ft and descends to a terminus near 7,400 ft on the reservation side of the mountain.
routes.approach: Access is from Mount Adams's east side, either via the Yakama Indian Reservation (Bird Creek Meadows/Mazama Glacier area, tribal permit required) or a long traverse from the north-side Muddy Meadows/Killen Creek approaches; detailed, up-to-date approach beta is scarce given how rarely this route is climbed.
routes.turnaround: Climb only in early season under stable, cold conditions, and start early enough to be off any sun-exposed sections of the headwall before rock/icefall begins — consistent with the general guidance for Mount Adams's headwall routes.
routes.bestSeason: Early season, under stable and cold conditions — like Mount Adams's other steep headwall routes, later-season warmth brings significant rockfall/icefall risk.
routes.comms: No cell coverage expected on this remote side of the mountain.
whatToBring: Full glacier/mixed rack for a serious, rarely-repeated line
Extra time/food buffer — this is not a well-beaten path
proTips: This route has very little modern trip-report beta — study Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide and consult local clubs (Mazamas/Mountaineers) before attempting it
A Yakama Nation tribal-use permit is needed for reservation-side approaches
watchOut: This is one of Mount Adams's most serious and infrequently climbed lines — go with a party experienced on Cascades volcanic mixed/ice terrain and be prepared for minimal fixed beta
Rockfall/icefall typical of Mount Adams's exposed headwall routes
knownHazards: Remote, rarely repeated route with limited current beta
Rockfall/icefall off Roosevelt Cliff as conditions warm
Partly on Yakama Nation land — permit compliance required
routes.face: East face — Roosevelt Cliff, Wilson Glacier

climate.typical: Remote east-side terrain on the Yakama Reservation flank; limited weather-station coverage specific to this face.

access.permit: Yakama Nation tribal-use permit (reservation side) and/or Mt. Adams Climbing Pass (Forest Service side, above 7,000 ft)

routes.corrections: Important source-quality flag: Mountain Project's 'Wilson Headwall' route page (mountainproject.com/route/114097472) and an AAC Publications article titled 'Washington, Wilson Glacier Headwall' both actually document a climb on Mount Rainier (Pete Schoening and Dee Molenaar, July 20-21, 1958, via Paradise and the Nisqually Glacier) — not this Mount Adams route, despite the matching name. Those sources were excluded from this entry to avoid cross-contaminating the two mountains' route data; anyone auditing this route in the future should watch for the same name collision.

blurb: Mount Baker (10,781 ft) is a heavily glaciated stratovolcano in the North Cascades of Whatcom County, Washington, about 31 miles east of Bellingham and the northernmost major volcano in the Cascade Range. Built mostly of andesite and less than 100,000 years old, it carries eleven named glaciers and is one of the snowiest places on Earth (Mt. Baker Ski Area set the world single-season snowfall record, 1,140 in, in 1998-99). Edmund Coleman, John Tennant, Thomas Stratton, and David Ogilvy made the first ascent on August 17, 1868, via the glacier that now bears Coleman's name.

routes.fa: Edmund Coleman, John Tennant, Thomas Stratton, David Ogilvy — Aug 17, 1868 (first ascent of Mount Baker; this route follows essentially the same Coleman Glacier line used by that party, and remains today's standard route)
routes.overview: Mount Baker's most popular route: a moderate but crevassed glacier climb up the Coleman Glacier, across to the upper Deming Glacier, and up the steep Roman Wall to the broad summit plateau.
routes.beta: From Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead, hike through forest and meadow to the Hogsback moraine, then rope up and cross the lower Coleman Glacier, navigating crevasses and seracs, before climbing the Roman Wall (the route's steepest section) and crossing the flat summit plateau to the true summit.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542), turn south on Forest Road 39 (Glacier Creek Road) toward the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (3,650 ft). FR 39 has been washed out at roughly mile 3.8 since November 2021 flooding and remains closed to vehicles beyond that point, adding about 4.5 extra miles / 2,000 ft of walking or biking. From the trailhead, follow Heliotrope Ridge Trail through forest, crossing Kulshan Creek near 2 miles/4,500 ft, up the Hogsback moraine crest to campsites scattered between 6,000-6,200 ft on the edge of the Coleman Glacier.
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route: back down the Roman Wall and Coleman Glacier to Hogsback camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, retrace the ascent line down the Roman Wall (steep, exposed snow just below the plateau), then continue down through the crevassed lower Coleman Glacier to the Hogsback camp/trailhead. No rappels are required in normal conditions; downclimbing and careful crevasse-field navigation are the main considerations, especially in the afternoon when snow bridges have softened.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (walking/biking the FR39 washout if needed), hike in, establish high camp near Hogsback (6,000-6,200 ft). Day 2: Alpine start (typically 11pm-2am), rope up on the Coleman Glacier, ascend to the upper Deming Glacier, climb the Roman Wall, cross the summit plateau to the true summit, then descend the same line to camp and hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line; the Roman Wall and the lower Coleman Glacier crevasse fields are the only technical sections to downclimb/re-navigate.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push; afternoon whiteout, sun-softened snow bridges, and increasing crevasse exposure on the Coleman Glacier make a late-day summit attempt progressively more hazardous.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through September; the most stable glacier travel and weather are typically found June-August. Early season carries higher avalanche risk on approach slopes; late season opens more crevasses.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service should be expected on the trail, at camp, or on the glacier; carry a satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) for emergencies.
whatToBring: A bike for the FR39 washout approach (optional but time-saving)
Extra insulating layers for the exposed summit plateau
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit shared per rope team of 2-3: one rope, harness and 2-3 locking carabiners per person, prusik cords or a mechanical crevasse-rescue pulley, and 1-2 snow pickets per rope team. No rock or ice-specific protection is needed on this route.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel and crevasse-rescue gear only; no fixed protection is placed.
proTips: Start well before dawn so the Coleman Glacier crossing and Roman Wall are climbed on firm, refrozen snow
Carry a bike for the FR39 washout to save significant approach time
watchOut: Crevasses on the Coleman and Deming Glaciers, more open and harder to bridge late in the season
The Roman Wall steepens with real exposure just below the summit plateau
Icefall/rockfall risk crossing below seracs low on the route
knownHazards: Glacier crevasses
Roman Wall steep snow section
Sulfur/steam vents and volcanic gas near the summit crater

routes.overview: Mount Baker's other standard route, climbing the moderately sloped Easton Glacier on the mountain's south side to the Roman Wall and summit plateau; generally considered the gentlest and most scenic of the two standard glacier routes.
routes.beta: From the Park Butte/Schriebers Meadow trailhead, approach through forest and meadow, then follow the Railroad Grade lateral moraine up to the toe of the Easton Glacier. The glacier itself is moderately angled with one steeper section on the Roman Wall just below the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From Sedro-Woolley, drive Baker Lake Highway (FR 11) about 12 miles, turn left on FR 12 for 3.6 miles to the FR 13 junction, then follow FR 13 for 5.3 miles to the Park Butte/Schriebers Meadow trailhead (~3,360-3,400 ft). Hike through Schriebers Meadow, cross Sulphur Creek, then climb through timber and meadow to Morovitz Meadows (~2 mi), continuing up onto the Railroad Grade moraine and to high camp near the toe of the Easton Glacier (~6,000 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route: back down the Roman Wall and Easton Glacier to high camp and out via the Railroad Grade/Park Butte trail.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line from the summit plateau down the Roman Wall, then down the moderately angled Easton Glacier to high camp near the Railroad Grade, and hike out through Morovitz Meadows and Schriebers Meadow to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Park Butte trailhead, hike in via Schriebers Meadow and the Railroad Grade to high camp near the Easton Glacier toe (~6,000 ft), roughly 3-4 hrs. Day 2: Alpine start, ascend the Easton Glacier and Roman Wall to the summit plateau and true summit (5-8 hrs), then descend to camp and hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal down the Easton Glacier and Roman Wall; no technical rappels are needed on this route.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before the summit push; softening snow bridges and afternoon weather changes on the glacier make a late attempt increasingly risky.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July-August generally offering the most stable weather and easiest glacier travel; a Washington Sno-Park permit is additionally required November 1-April 30 at the trailhead.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent on the approach and glacier; a satellite communicator is recommended.
whatToBring: Extra insulation for the summit plateau
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier kit shared per rope team: rope, harness, 2-3 locking carabiners, prusiks or mechanical rescue pulley, and 1-2 snow pickets per team; no rock/ice-specific rack required.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel and crevasse-rescue gear only.
proTips: Get an early start; the south-facing Easton Glacier gets sun all day and softens quickly
Camp near the Railroad Grade toe of the glacier to shorten summit day
watchOut: Crevasses on the Easton Glacier, more pronounced by mid-late summer
Roman Wall steepens with real exposure below the summit plateau
knownHazards: Glacier crevasses
Sulfur/volcanic gas near the summit crater
Rockfall on the Railroad Grade moraine in early season

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's route page classifies this climb as 'Grade III,' while most other sources (The Mountaineers, guide services) describe it as the moderate standard route consistent with the existing 'Grade II / PD' rating; kept the existing grade/alpineGrade pending clearer consensus, and left commitment null rather than assert a specific NCCS grade.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Dick Widrig, Ralph Widrig — August 1948
routes.overview: An iconic, aesthetic ridge line rising nearly 4,000 ft directly to Mount Baker's summit, widely regarded as one of the best moderate big-mountain snow-and-ice routes in the Lower 48; the crux is a steep serac/ice wall around 9,600 ft.
routes.beta: From a Coleman Glacier camp, ascend the west slope of the North Ridge, then continue up the ridge as it steepens below an ice wall at about 9,600 ft — the route's crux, offering left, center, or right-side options through the serac band (the right side involves 70-80° ice with semi-hanging belays). Above the ice wall, moderate snow leads to the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA on SR 542, drive Glacier Creek Road (FR 39) toward the Mount Baker Trailhead (road washed out at ~mile 3.8 since Nov 2021, adding ~4.5 mi/2,000 ft on foot/bike). Follow the Heliotrope Ridge Trail, crossing Kulshan Creek near 2 mi/4,700 ft, then climb the Hogsback moraine to bivy sites around 6,000-6,800 ft on the edge of the Coleman Glacier; cross the glacier laterally to reach the base of the ridge.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard Coleman-Deming route: down the Roman Wall and Coleman Glacier to Hogsback camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Coleman-Deming standard route down the Roman Wall and lower Coleman Glacier back to the Hogsback camp and trailhead — this is a different line from the ascent ridge, so route-finding back onto the standard descent glacier is required.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a Coleman Glacier camp (6,000-6,800 ft). Day 2: Alpine start, cross the glacier to the base of the North Ridge, climb the ridge and ice-wall crux to the summit plateau, then descend the Coleman-Deming route to camp. Typical average-party time is 12-18 hours round trip from the Heliotrope Ridge camp.
routes.bail: Below the ice-wall crux, retreat back down the ridge/glacier the way you came; above the crux, the safest retreat is often to continue over the top and descend via the standard Coleman-Deming route rather than reverse the steep ice.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before committing to the ice-wall crux — descending steep alpine ice in deteriorating weather or softened snow is significantly more hazardous than climbing it.
routes.bestSeason: May through August; early season allows a more direct ridge approach on the right, while late season opens a 'secret passage' on the left around the steepest sections.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the glacier or ridge; carry a satellite communicator.
whatToBring: Extra screws late-season / one fewer picket
Bivy gear for a Coleman Glacier camp
routes.detailedRack: 2 snow pickets, 6 ice screws, 1 mountaineering axe and 1 technical axe (or 2 technical axes) per climber, long slings for serac/ice protection, and a 60m rope (doubled for parties of 3).
routes.proNeeds: Mixed snow and ice protection: pickets low, screws through the crux ice wall; semi-hanging belays possible on the steep right-hand variant.
proTips: Cross the Coleman Glacier and climb the crux ice wall on firm, refrozen conditions early in the day
Scope the serac band's left/center/right options before committing — the right side is steepest (70-80°) with hanging belays
watchOut: Serac collapse hazard around the ice-wall crux
Crevasse crossings on the Coleman Glacier approach
Large exposure over the west face
Rockfall in early season conditions
Variable snow/ice quality year to year
knownHazards: Serac collapse
Crevasses
Steep exposed ice (up to 70-80° on variants)
Rockfall

routes.corrections: Sources describe the ice-wall crux as typically WI/AI3 with some parties reporting AI4 sections (70-80° on the right-hand variant); updated iceGrade from the given 'AI3' to 'AI3-4' to reflect this documented range. alpineGrade (AD+) and overall grade text were consistent across sources and left unchanged.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Phil Bartow, Donald Grimlund, David Nicholson — August 1957
routes.overview: Arguably the most complex regularly-climbed route on Mount Baker: a sustained two-tool ice climb up the Coleman Glacier's northwest headwall, widely regarded as a Cascades steep-ice classic.
routes.beta: From a Coleman Glacier camp, climb a lower section of firm steep snow (up to 50°) onto an upper 800-foot band of ~60° ice, crossing two bergschrunds along the way; conditions and exact line vary by year and can involve up to a dozen or more short ice pitches.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Glacier Creek Road (FR 39) toward the Heliotrope Ridge/Mount Baker Trailhead (washed out at ~mile 3.8 since Nov 2021, adding ~4.5 mi/2,000 ft on foot/bike). Hike the Heliotrope Ridge Trail and Hogsback moraine to a Coleman Glacier camp (~6,000-6,800 ft), then approach the base of the headwall across the glacier.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard Coleman-Deming route down the Roman Wall and Coleman Glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, descend the standard Coleman-Deming line down the Roman Wall and lower Coleman Glacier back to camp; this is a different line from the ascent headwall.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a Coleman Glacier camp. Day 2: Alpine start, climb the headwall's lower snow and upper ice band (8-12 hours to the top depending on conditions and party pace), then descend via the Coleman-Deming route.
routes.bail: Retreat off the headwall is difficult once committed to the upper ice band; parties generally plan to continue over the top rather than reverse the route, so a clear weather window and early start are essential.
routes.turnaround: Commit only with a clear, stable weather window; the sustained ice band and limited protection make retreat difficult once high on the face.
routes.bestSeason: May through August overall, but the safer right-hand option is generally out of condition (open crevasses/bergschrunds) by July 1; crevasses become more of a problem as the season progresses.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the Coleman Glacier or headwall; carry a satellite communicator.
whatToBring: Extra screws for the upper ice band
Bivy gear for the Coleman Glacier camp
routes.detailedRack: 4 snow pickets, 5 ice screws, long runners for thin ice/rock protection, and a pair of technical ice tools per climber.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed snow and ice protection with limited placements available in the upper 60° ice band; two bergschrund crossings require careful route-finding.
proTips: Climb early season (before ~July 1) for the safer right-hand option before crevasses/bergschrunds open up
Move efficiently through the lower snow to conserve time and protection for the sustained upper ice
watchOut: Two bergschrund crossings
Thin ice plates over wet snow underneath, especially later in the day
Avalanche activity on the face
