routes.corrections: Added fa (Ed Cooper, Phil Bartow, Donald Grimlund, David Nicholson, Aug 1957) from Mountain Project; this could not be cross-confirmed on SummitPost, which blocked automated fetches, so treat this first-ascent attribution as single-sourced. iceGrade AI4 and routeFt 2000 added from Mountain Project's route data.

routes.overview: A long, remote ice route on Mount Baker's northeast flank, climbing the Park Glacier headwall via a steep schrund crossing and several pitches of alpine ice to the ridge crest and summit; one of the mountain's least-traveled technical lines.
routes.beta: Approach via the Mount Baker Ski Area, Artist Point, Table Mountain, and Ptarmigan Ridge to The Portals, then drop onto the Rainbow Glacier and across to the Park Glacier, heading for the headwall below the Cockscomb. Access across the bergschrund is typically gained via a steep ramp of snow/ice (~60° at the top); above it, roughly three pitches of ~50m ice at up to ~60° lead to the ridge crest, which is followed to the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From the Mount Baker Ski Area (Artist Point), hike past Table Mountain and along Ptarmigan Ridge to The Portals, then descend onto the Rainbow Glacier and traverse onto the Park Glacier beneath the headwall — a long approach cited at roughly 22 miles round trip.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard Coleman-Deming route down the Roman Wall and Coleman Glacier, or reverse toward the Cockscomb Ridge/Park Glacier depending on conditions.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties descend the standard Coleman-Deming route (Roman Wall, Coleman Glacier) rather than reversing the remote Park Glacier approach.
routes.itinerary: Given the length of the approach (~22 mi round trip), this route is typically done as a multi-day trip with a high camp near the Rainbow or Park Glacier below the headwall, followed by an early climb of the headwall and summit, then descent via the Coleman-Deming route.
routes.bail: Retreat below the bergschrund/ramp is possible by downclimbing; above it, on the sustained ~60° ice pitches, retreat is difficult and parties should commit only with a stable weather window.
routes.turnaround: This route's remoteness and difficult retreat above the schrund make a conservative turnaround time and a stable forecast essential before committing to the headwall.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through early July generally offers the best combination of stable snow and manageable crevasses/bergschrunds; by early August, large crevasses can force a longer, more circuitous route, and the northeast aspect gets sun early, softening snow quickly.
routes.comms: No cell coverage expected on this remote northeast side of the mountain; a satellite communicator is essential given the route's remoteness.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day approach
Extra food/fuel for the long remote approach
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine ice rack of screws and pickets for the schrund ramp and the roughly three ~50m ice pitches above it; exact numbers vary by year and conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws through the sustained upper pitches; limited/thin protection reported crossing the bergschrund lip.
proTips: Get an early start — the northeast aspect gets full sun and softens quickly
Climb in the late-May-to-early-July window before crevasses open up significantly
watchOut: Bergschrund crossing at the base of the headwall
Numerous large crevasses by early August
Cornice near the top of the headwall
Long, remote approach with route-finding challenges
knownHazards: Bergschrund
Crevasses
Cornice
Remoteness / long retreat

pitchDetail.notes: Access ramp across the bergschrund; narrow ledge traverse and step-chopping may be needed at the lip.
Sustained ice pitches to the ridge crest.
An overhanging cornice lip near the ridge crest/summit plateau has been noted in historical accounts (~8 ft).

routes.corrections: The distinct Mountain Project 'Park Glacier' page (Grade II, 22-mile round trip) describes a long, low-angle traverse route via Ptarmigan Ridge/Rainbow Glacier that bypasses the technical headwall — not the same climb as this technical 'Park Glacier Headwall' ice route, so its Grade II rating was not applied here; the given Grade IV/D rating was kept. A 1971 AAC Publications account (Bodine, Friar, Keliher, July 4-5, 1971) describes climbing 'a new variation' of this headwall directly, which may or may not represent the true first ascent of the line, so fa was left null rather than asserted from that single, ambiguous account.

routes.fa: Joe Morovits and party — July 2, 1894 (Morovits Route, via the ridge/cleaver between the Boulder and Park Glaciers)
routes.overview: A quieter, more strenuous alternative to the standard routes, climbing the Boulder-Park Cleaver and lower Boulder Glacier on Mount Baker's southeast side to either a moderate snow traverse or a harder rock-buttress finish near the summit.
routes.beta: From a high camp below the Boulder Glacier (~5,500 ft), follow the Boulder-Park Cleaver northwest onto the lower Boulder Glacier. Above about 9,300 ft, choose between a moderate 25-35° snow traverse toward Sherman Crater, or a harder 4th-class rock buttress direct to the summit area.
routes.approach: From Sedro-Woolley, drive SR 20 east 16 miles to milepost 82, turn north on Baker Lake Highway (FR 11) for about 18 miles, then (after Boulder Creek Campground) turn onto FR 1130 for about 1 mile to FR 1131, and follow it to the Boulder Ridge Trailhead. Hike the Boulder Ridge Trail (maintained about 2 miles to the wilderness boundary), then a steep, unmaintained boot path with a 3rd-class rock band around 3.4 mi/4,700 ft, continuing along a lateral moraine to the Boulder/Park Glacier near 6,300 ft and a high camp around 5,500 ft.
routes.descent: Descend the climbing route: back down the upper snow/rock terrain, Boulder-Park Cleaver, and Boulder Ridge Trail; a ski/snowboard descent is also possible in season.
routes.descentText: From the upper mountain, reverse the ascent line down through the chosen snow-traverse or rock-buttress terrain, back onto the Boulder-Park Cleaver and lower Boulder Glacier, then down the moraine, the 3rd-class rock band near 4,700 ft, and out the Boulder Ridge Trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Boulder Ridge Trailhead, hike the maintained trail and steep unmaintained boot path to a high camp near 5,500 ft below the Boulder Glacier. Day 2: Alpine start, climb the Boulder-Park Cleaver and upper mountain to the summit, then descend to camp and hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Boulder-Park Cleaver and lower glacier is straightforward; the 3rd-class rock band near 4,700 ft on the approach trail should be downclimbed with care.
routes.turnaround: Because this is an east-facing, sun-exposed route with a long, difficult approach, start early and set a firm turnaround time to avoid softened snow and a late, tiring descent.
routes.bestSeason: June through August generally offers the best conditions; the route's east aspect argues for an early start any time of year.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on this remote southeast side of the mountain.
whatToBring: Sturdy boots for the 3rd/4th-class rock sections
routes.detailedRack: Glacier rope, one snow picket per person, and standard crevasse-rescue gear; helmets are strongly recommended for the rockfall-prone traverse below the 9,300 ft buttress.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly glacier travel protection; the 4th-class rock-buttress option is typically climbed unroped or with minimal protection by capable parties.
proTips: This route sees far fewer parties than the standard routes — solid independent routefinding and glacier skills are essential
Helmets are highly recommended for the rockfall-prone traverse below the 9,300 ft buttress
watchOut: Rockfall while traversing below the 9,300 ft buttress
Crevasses on the upper Boulder Glacier, especially by mid-summer

routes.fa: Chuck Murley, John Musser, Klindt Vielbig — July 4, 1961 (first complete ascent of the ridge; a 1906 Mazama party had earlier been turned back by the 300 ft Cockscomb Tower)
routes.overview: The true north ridge of Mount Baker (distinct from the popular 'North Ridge,' which is actually the NW spur), climbing broken rock towers and icefall terrain up the mountain's north side to the Cockscomb pinnacle and summit.
routes.beta: From Heliotrope Ridge, cross the Coleman and Roosevelt Glaciers on low-to-moderate snow to gain the ridge around 7,500 ft (2-3 hours of glacier travel). Ascend moderate snow, bypassing a pinnacle on the right, then navigate junky rock towers and bergschrunds by bypassing them left/east on low-to-moderate ramps within the icefall. Pass the Cockscomb itself on the left via steep snow; later in the season this may require dropping to the Park Glacier and climbing its headwall (AI3, several screws) before regaining the ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Glacier Creek Road (FR 39) toward the Heliotrope Ridge/Mount Baker Trailhead (washed out at ~mile 3.8 since Nov 2021). Hike the Heliotrope Ridge Trail and Hogsback moraine to a Coleman Glacier camp, then cross the Coleman and Roosevelt Glaciers to gain the ridge near 7,500 ft.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard Coleman-Deming route: easy-to-moderate glacier travel down the Roman Wall and Coleman Glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Coleman-Deming standard route down the Roman Wall and lower Coleman Glacier to the Hogsback camp and trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a Coleman Glacier camp. Day 2: Alpine start, cross to the ridge (2-3 hrs), climb the 5-pitch ridge past towers and the Cockscomb to the summit, then descend via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Below the Cockscomb, retreat back across the glacier the way you came; above it, dropping to the Park Glacier and climbing its headwall is a documented alternate way through late-season, but is itself a technical (AI3) commitment.
routes.turnaround: Given the loose rock and icefall terrain, favor an early season/early-day ascent and a firm turnaround time before committing above the Cockscomb.
routes.bestSeason: Spring or early summer is best, since loose rock hazard increases as snow cover melts out later in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the glacier or ridge.
whatToBring: Sturdy boots/gear for mixed rock and ice terrain
Extra screws if a late-season Park Glacier headwall bypass is likely
routes.detailedRack: A few ice screws and snow pickets, glacier rope, and a light rock rack for the 4th-class tower sections; a late-season Park Glacier headwall bypass adds an AI3 ice pitch requiring several screws.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed glacier, rock, and ice protection; route-finding around loose towers and bergschrunds is as important as gear.
proTips: Climb early season/early summer to minimize loose rock hazard
Scope the Park Glacier headwall bypass option in advance for late-season attempts
watchOut: Loose, sketchy rock on the towers
Crevasses and bergschrunds in the icefall terrain
Late-season Cockscomb bypass adds a technical AI3 pitch

blurb: Glacier Peak (Dakobed/Tda-ko-buh-ba, "Great Parent" in Sauk-Suiattle Lushootseed) is a remote, glacier-clad stratovolcano in the North Cascades of Snohomish County, Washington, entirely encircled by the Glacier Peak Wilderness within the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests. Built of dacite lava domes and pyroclastic deposits, it is the most isolated of Washington's five major volcanoes, ringed by roughly a dozen named glaciers and reachable only by approaches of 15+ miles on foot. It was first climbed in 1897 by a USGS topographic survey party led by Thomas Gerdine (for whom Gerdine Ridge is named), and every standard route today remains a multi-day glaciated snow/ice climb.

routes.face: North side, above Boulder Basin
routes.fa: Standard route; first ascent of Glacier Peak, June 1897 (as given) — see corrections
routes.overview: Glacier Peak's easiest and most-traveled line, climbing moderate, low-angle snow up the Sitkum Glacier on the mountain's north side from a base camp in Boulder Basin.
routes.beta: From Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), traverse SE onto the Sitkum Glacier and ascend a corridor on its left (climber's) side heading NE toward a saddle near 9,500 ft by Sitkum Spire. From the saddle, follow the pumice ridge toward the summit, staying right of and below the peak, then finish up a snow chute to the top. Crevasse danger is generally minimal and the route rarely steepens significantly.
routes.approach: From the White Chuck River Trailhead near Darrington (FS-23), follow trail #643 about 5.5 miles to the former Kennedy Hot Springs site, then continue roughly 1.5 miles to Sitkum Creek and the PCT junction (~3,850 ft). Head north on the PCT about 0.5 mile to a forested campsite where a climbers' path splits off and climbs steeply to Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), the standard base camp. Approach is about 9 miles one-way to camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to Boulder Basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the summit snow chute and pumice ridge back to the ~9,500 ft saddle, then reverse the Sitkum Glacier corridor back to Boulder Basin. In flat light or whiteout, a GPS track of the ascent is strongly advised since the upper glacier and pumice ridge have few distinguishing features.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: White Chuck Trailhead to Boulder Basin camp (~9 mi, ~3,500-4,000 ft gain). Day 2: Alpine start, climb Sitkum Glacier to the summit and return to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: The route stays at moderate angle throughout, so retreat by reversing the ascent line is straightforward at any point; the main risk is losing the return line on the featureless upper glacier/pumice ridge in poor visibility.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before leaving camp and honor it regardless of proximity to the summit — afternoon cloud and thunderstorm buildup is common on Cascade volcanoes in summer, and softened snow bridges become more dangerous as the day warms.
routes.bestSeason: July is generally considered the sweet spot: most seasonal snow has consolidated enough to bridge crevasses well but the route isn't yet baked out. June can bring cold, wet, unsettled weather; by August glaciers on Glacier Peak are more melted-out and crevassed, increasing route-finding difficulty.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Glacier Peak Wilderness; the approach is 9+ trail miles from the nearest paved road. A satellite messenger (inReach/SPOT) is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: ice axe, crampons, harness, glacier rope, 2-3 pickets, and a full crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, locking carabiners). Some parties add 1-2 ice screws for the steeper ground near the 9,500 ft saddle.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is almost entirely for crevasse-rescue anchoring (pickets/screws); there is no sustained technical climbing.
whatToBring: 4-season tent for exposed Boulder Basin camp
stove/fuel for melting snow (limited water high up)
glacier glasses and strong sun protection
GPS/GPX track of the route for whiteout navigation
proTips: Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft) is the accepted base camp — established, sheltered sites with good water lower down.
This is one of the least technical standard lines on any Washington volcano, making it a common training/beginner glacier objective.
watchOut: Snow bridges soften and weaken through the day — get across early and rope up even where crevasse hazard seems minor.
The upper pumice ridge and glacier lack strong navigational features; whiteout can make the correct line hard to hold.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier, generally minor but present.
Loose pumice/scree on the summit ridge.

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse SE from camp onto the glacier, ascend the left-side corridor heading NE.
Aim for the saddle near Sitkum Spire.
Stay right of and below the peak, finish up a snow chute.

road.name: White Chuck River Road (FS-23)
road.status: Closed
road.driveNote: FS-23 has been closed from milepost 3.7 to its terminus since December 2025 flood damage, per a Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest closure order in effect through at least Dec 31, 2025 (subject to extension). This blocks the standard White Chuck Trailhead approach to Boulder Basin — verify current status with the Darrington Ranger District before planning this route.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA/NWS Zone WAZ567 - West Slopes North Cascades and Vicinity
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade climate: wet, snowy winters and springs; a shorter drier window in summer. Afternoon cloud buildup and occasional thunderstorms are common in July-August.
climate.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack; roads/trails often still snow-covered into June; not a typical climbing window.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jun-Sep); July is most reliable, June can be cold and wet, August-Sept sees more open crevasses as the glacier melts out.
climate.fall: Snow returns by October; glaciers heavily crevassed and days short — route becomes unsuitable for standard parties.
climate.winter: Not a standard climbing season; heavy snowfall, avalanche hazard, and a much longer approach on snow-covered roads/trail.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevationFt (10,550) differs slightly from the figure most widely cross-corroborated across sources (Peakbagger, Mountain Project, and multiple trip reports all give 10,541 ft; Wikipedia's newer NAVD88 remeasurement gives 10,525+ ft) — used 10,541 ft here as highPointFt since it's the most cross-confirmed figure, but treat exact summit elevation as having some real survey uncertainty. Separately, Mountain Project attributes the 1897 Gerdine-party first ascent specifically to the south-side 'Disappointment Peak Cleaver/Gerdine Ridge Direct' route (GPS-tagged at the summit), not to this north-side Sitkum line — the exact route the 1897 party climbed is not conclusively documented in the sources checked, so the given FA note is retained but should be treated as uncertain as to which specific line was climbed.

routes.face: South side, the rocky nose/cleaver leading to Disappointment Peak (9,755 ft)
routes.overview: A rock/scramble variant of the standard south-side route that climbs the loose cleaver directly through Disappointment Peak's summit to avoid crossing the Suiattle and lower Cool Glaciers, rejoining the standard line near a 9,100 ft col above the Cool Glacier.
routes.beta: Where the standard Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route would traverse onto the Suiattle Glacier around 8,000-8,500 ft, this variant instead continues directly up the nose of the cleaver on a climber's trail (when melted out), steepening into mostly 3rd/4th class terrain (with a few 5th-class moves) between about 8,500 ft and Disappointment Peak's 9,755 ft summit. From there, descend slightly to the saddle above the Cool Glacier to rejoin the standard route to Glacier Peak's summit. The main appeal is avoiding glacier travel and crevasse hazard entirely, traded for sustained loose, rockfall-prone scrambling.
routes.approach: Same as the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route: North Fork Sauk Trail #649 from the Sloan Creek/North Fork Sauk Trailhead to the PCT at White Pass, then cross-country via Foam Creek and the White Chuck Basin to Glacier Gap (7,300 ft) as high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route (most parties descend via the glacier line rather than reversing the loose cleaver).
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the Cool Glacier/Gerdine Ridge line back toward the 9,100 ft col, then down through the Suiattle Glacier basin and Gerdine Ridge to Glacier Gap camp — generally preferred over downclimbing the loose cleaver a second time.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to White Pass camp (~10 mi). Day 2: White Pass to Glacier Gap high camp (~4 mi). Day 3: Summit via the cleaver, descend via Cool Glacier to camp. Day 4: Hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is more constrained than the glacier line once committed above ~8,600 ft — downclimbing loose 3rd/4th class rock is slower and riskier than ascending; turn back early if weather or rockfall conditions (parties above, warming) are unfavorable.
routes.turnaround: Because downclimbing this loose terrain is harder and slower than climbing it, set a conservative turnaround time before starting the cleaver and commit to it — do not rely on being able to retreat quickly once above the initial slopes.
routes.bestSeason: Best when the rock is still partly snow/ice-bound in early-mid summer, which holds loose blocks in place; by later summer the cleaver sheds more rock as it fully melts out.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite messenger.
routes.detailedRack: Minimal fixed protection is used on this line — MP notes protection guidance is effectively 'don't fall.' A light rock rack (a few slings/nuts) is optional for confidence on the 5th-class moves; full glacier gear (axe, crampons, rope) is still needed for the approach across White Chuck Basin and the shared upper mountain.
routes.proNeeds: Little to no fixed protection is placed on the cleaver itself; this is essentially an unroped scramble above the approach snowfields.
whatToBring: sturdy approach shoes/boots for scree and talus
extra care/spacing between climbers to avoid knocking rock onto teammates
proTips: This variant trades crevasse hazard for rockfall/loose-rock hazard — a reasonable swap for parties without full glacier-travel comfort or climbing very late season.
Climb early in the day before the sun loosens rock and softens the connecting snow.
watchOut: The ridge continuously sheds rock — stay near the crest and keep tight spacing between climbers.
Rock is described as quite loose and unstable throughout the 3rd/4th class section.
knownHazards: Sustained rockfall exposure, described by Mountain Project as an ongoing hazard requiring active care not to knock rock onto climbers below.

pitchDetail.notes: Climber's trail when melted out, steepens with elevation.
Loose rock; stay on the crest.
Descend slightly to rejoin the Cool Glacier route.

routes.rockGrade: low 5th (mostly 3rd-4th class, incl. Rabbit Ears notch downclimb)
objHaz: whiteout/high wind on the summit ice pitches
routes.season: Jun-Sep; increasingly icy (not just snow) even early season in recent years per 2025 trip reports
routes.face: North ridge between Kennedy Glacier and the crater, via the Rabbit Ears pinnacles
routes.overview: A classic moderate alpine ridge route on Glacier Peak's north side, climbing steep snow/ice up Frostbite Ridge past the Rabbit Ears pinnacles to two short pitches of alpine ice at the crater rim.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Glacier Ridge/Kennedy Glacier, climb the ridge on snow or loose rock with occasional protection to the top, where the 'Rabbit Ears' notch requires descending class 3 rock for about 150 feet to a saddle. From the saddle, climb an alpine ice face (roughly two pitches, up to ~60° in recent conditions, AI1-2 to AI2) to reach the crater rim and summit. Conditions vary significantly by year and season — early-season snow climbing has increasingly given way to exposed glacier ice even in June/July per recent trip reports.
routes.approach: Primary current approach is via North Fork Sauk Trail #649 to the PCT, north on the PCT roughly 19-20 miles from the trailhead to Glacier Ridge (a climbers' path leaves the PCT about 0.7 mile past Glacier Creek), then off-trail up Glacier Creek to the Kennedy Glacier area, meeting Kennedy Peak's saddle around 8,200 ft. (An older alternate approach via the White Chuck Trailhead and Kennedy Ridge Trail also reaches this area but is currently blocked by the FS-23 closure.)
routes.descent: Standard descent is via the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route on the south side (a traverse of the mountain, not a reversal of Frostbite Ridge), requiring either a shuttle between trailheads or a long road walk/hitch back to the start.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend south via the Cool Glacier and Gerdine Ridge to Glacier Gap, then out via White Pass and the North Fork Sauk Trail if using that trailhead system, or continue the traverse to exit a different trailhead than the approach — plan logistics (shuttle/second car) in advance since this is a point-to-point traverse for most parties, not an out-and-back.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: North Fork Sauk Trailhead toward Glacier Ridge (~19-20+ mi, big day). Day 2: Approach to high camp (~8,200-8,800 ft) near Kennedy Glacier/Frostbite Ridge base. Day 3: Summit via Frostbite Ridge, descend via Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier. Day 4: Exit hike.
routes.bail: Below the Rabbit Ears notch, retreat by reversing the ridge is reasonable; once committed to the ice pitches above the notch, downclimbing steep alpine ice is harder than climbing it — turn around before the ice section if weather, visibility, or conditions (recent reports describe high wind and whiteout on this section) are marginal.
routes.turnaround: This route has increasingly been encountered as alpine ice rather than snow even early season — set a firm turnaround time before the ice pitches and be willing to retreat rather than push into deteriorating wind/visibility on exposed, technical ground.
routes.bestSeason: Historically a straightforward early-season snow climb, but recent (2025) trip reports note it's now common to encounter alpine ice on the crux regardless of how early in the season it's climbed — come prepared for AI2 ice technique even in June/July.
routes.comms: No cell coverage; this is one of the more remote/committing standard routes on the peak (40+ mile round trip) — a satellite communicator is strongly recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Steel crampons and 3/4-shank mountaineering boots (aluminum crampons/light boots are unreliable on the ice pitches); two ice tools per person for the crux; a 60m rope preferred (30m workable with simul-climbing experience); 3+ ice screws for belayed pitches; at least one picket per rope team; small-to-medium nuts optional for the rock sections.
routes.proNeeds: Rope, pickets, and ice screws for the glacier and ice-pitch sections; small nuts optional on the ridge's rock steps.
whatToBring: breathable approach shoes for the long trail miles, mountaineering boots for the technical upper mountain
contractor bags for melting silty high-camp snow
extra water capacity — carry-and-fill strategy along the PCT before Red Pass
wind-resistant shelter for an exposed high camp
proTips: Consider camping on protected lee-side snow rather than the exposed rock ridge at high camp — high wind at exposed camps has caught out multiple parties.
A light pack matters enormously given the 19-20+ mile one-way approach before any technical climbing begins.
Many parties link this with Kennedy Glacier or Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier as a north-to-south (or vice versa) traverse rather than an out-and-back.
watchOut: Kennedy Peak regularly sheds rock into the approach gully — stay alert and move efficiently through it.
The Rabbit Ears notch downclimb (~150 ft of class 3) is exposed; the summit ice pitches have been encountered in high wind and whiteout.
Crevasses on Kennedy Glacier are described as 'choppy' with hidden slots, though generally minor on the glacier's left half.
knownHazards: Rockfall from Kennedy Peak and glacial till near the ridge base.
Crevasse hazard crossing Kennedy Glacier.
Corniced sections along the upper ridge.
Alpine ice (not just snow) increasingly present on the summit pitches even early season.

pitchDetail.notes: Protectable with pickets/occasional rock gear.
~150 ft down to a saddle below the ice face.
1-2 pitches of alpine ice; screw belays; conditions vary widely by year.

routes.face: North side, above Boulder Basin
routes.fa: 1897 (as given) — see corrections
routes.overview: This listing's name does not correspond to an established, distinct route name found on Glacier Peak — see corrections. Filled here using the well-documented Sitkum Glacier line, which best matches the given grade description ('Class 2, Glacier').
routes.beta: See the Sitkum Glacier entry (wa_glacier_peak_sitkum_glacier) for the full route description — this appears to be the same line.
routes.approach: Same as Sitkum Glacier: White Chuck River Trailhead (FS-23) to Kennedy Hot Springs to the PCT, then a steep climbers' path to Boulder Basin (~9 mi one-way).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back to Boulder Basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the summit chute and pumice ridge, then reverse the Sitkum Glacier corridor back to Boulder Basin.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: White Chuck Trailhead to Boulder Basin. Day 2: Summit push and return to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Same as Sitkum Glacier — retreat by reversing the ascent is straightforward at any point on this moderate-angle line.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time and honor it; afternoon cloud buildup is common in summer.
routes.bestSeason: July is generally the sweet spot; June is colder/wetter, August-Sept sees more open crevasses.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite messenger.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: ice axe, crampons, harness, glacier rope, pickets, crevasse-rescue hardware.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is for crevasse-rescue anchoring; no sustained technical climbing.
proTips: Verify with a current source which specific line this listing is meant to represent before publishing it as distinct from Sitkum Glacier — see corrections.
watchOut: Snow bridges soften through the day — rope up and cross early.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier, generally minor but present.

routes.corrections: 'Disappointment Cleaver' is not an established route name on Glacier Peak in any source checked (Mountain Project, SummitPost search results, Cascade Alpine Guide-derived web sources, trip reports) — that name is normally associated with Mount Rainier's DC route, or loosely with the separate south-side 'Disappointment Peak Cleaver' variant (a rocky ridge on Glacier Peak's south side climbing through Disappointment Peak, already captured as wa_glacier_peak_disappointment_peak_cleaver in this same route set). This entry's own grade description ('Class 2, Glacier') and the paired name 'Sitkum Glacier' both point to it being a duplicate of the standard Sitkum Glacier route rather than a distinct line. Recommend deduplicating against wa_glacier_peak_sitkum_glacier or renaming/removing this entry — filled with Sitkum Glacier's sourced facts in the meantime rather than leaving it entirely blank, but this is a data-quality flag, not a confirmed distinct route.

routes.face: South side, from Glacier Gap via Gerdine Ridge and the Cool Glacier
routes.overview: The standard, most-climbed route on Glacier Peak today: a moderate snow climb up Gerdine Ridge and the Cool Glacier on the south side from a high camp at Glacier Gap.
routes.beta: From Glacier Gap (7,300 ft), ascend northward, initially contouring the left slope below Glacier Gap Peak (7,739 ft), then regain the ridge and continue to about 8,400 ft. Traverse NE onto the upper Suiattle Glacier, then ascend the Cool Glacier westward until regaining the ridge at its top, finishing to the summit. An alternate avoids all glacier travel by climbing directly up the South Ridge to Disappointment Peak (9,755 ft) before descending slightly to the saddle above the Cool Glacier — useful for parties without full glacier gear or climbing very late season.
routes.approach: From the North Fork Sauk/Sloan Creek Trailhead, follow trail #649 (via Mackinaw Shelter) to White Pass on the PCT, then continue cross-country above the White River drainage past Foam Creek, across White Pass and through the White Chuck Basin's volcanic talus to Glacier Gap (7,300 ft), the standard high camp. Roughly 18 miles one-way, 36 miles round trip.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back to Glacier Gap.
routes.descentText: Descend the Cool Glacier eastward back to the ridge crest, then down Gerdine Ridge to Glacier Gap camp, retracing the approach back through White Chuck Basin, White Pass, and the North Fork Sauk Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to White Pass/Mackinaw Shelter area (~7-8 hrs, ~3,600 ft gain). Day 2: To Glacier Gap high camp (~4-5 hrs, ~1,400 ft gain). Day 3: Summit day via Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier (~6-8 hrs round trip from camp, ~3,200 ft gain) and return to camp or descend further. Day 4: Exit to trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line is straightforward at any point on the glacier sections; the South Ridge/Disappointment Peak variant, once above ~8,600 ft, involves the same loose downclimbing considerations as the dedicated cleaver variant.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before the summit push and hold to it — rockfall below Disappointment Peak and softening snow bridges both worsen as the day warms.
routes.bestSeason: July is the recommended window — most snow has consolidated for good travel and route-finding without yet being fully crevassed out. In low-snow years, climbers have described the Cool Glacier as 'a maze of crevasses' by August.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in this part of the wilderness; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons, and full glacier travel gear (harness, rope, pickets, crevasse-rescue kit); a helmet is strongly advised for the rockfall-exposed traverse below Disappointment Peak.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier protection (pickets/screws for crevasse-rescue anchors); no sustained technical rock/ice protection needed on the standard glacier line.
whatToBring: 4-season tent for the exposed, windy Glacier Gap camp
stove/fuel (limited natural water at high camp)
sun protection for extended glacier travel
extra rope-team communication plan for the rockfall traverse
proTips: This is the current standard/easiest way up Glacier Peak with the shortest overall distance among the main routes.
Move efficiently through the traverse below Disappointment Peak on the Cool Glacier — climbers have reported car-sized rockfall in this section.
Glacier Gap sits between the Gerdine and Suiattle Glaciers and has multiple established, wind-sheltered campsites.
watchOut: Active, obvious rockfall crossing below Disappointment Peak — move through quickly and don't linger.
Snowbridges on the Cool Glacier deteriorate through the day; rope up for all glacier travel.
knownHazards: Rockfall below Disappointment Peak on the Cool Glacier traverse (car-sized rock reported).
Crevasses on the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers, worse in low-snow/late-season years.

pitchDetail.notes: Contour below Glacier Gap Peak, then regain the ridge.
NE traverse around 8,400 ft; rockfall-exposed below Disappointment Peak.
Ascend westward, regain the ridge at the glacier's top.

routes.face: North side, via Kennedy Ridge and the Kennedy Glacier
routes.overview: A more direct and, per multiple sources, more aesthetic north-side line than Frostbite Ridge, climbing the Kennedy Glacier to a steep headwall below the crater rim; commonly used as the up-route in a Frostbite Ridge loop (with Frostbite Ridge as the descent).
routes.beta: From a camp near Glacier Creek, follow a climbers' trail up a gully to the top of Kennedy Ridge, then descend (right) to the foot of the Kennedy Glacier. Ascend the lower glacier keeping right, then the center of the upper glacier. Approach the bergschrund at the head of the glacier by ascending left to the top of a snow dome, find a snow bridge to cross it, then climb the center of a roughly 40° headwall, staying left of but close to prominent rock outcrops, to the crater rim.
routes.approach: Standard historical approach: from the White Chuck River Trailhead (FS-23, currently closed), follow trail #643 about 4 miles, then Kennedy Ridge Trail #639 for 1.7 miles to the PCT; turn left (north) and follow the PCT about 2 miles to the Glacier Creek crossing and campsites (pack-in time 4-5 hours). An alternate approach via the North Fork Sauk Trail and PCT over Red Pass to Glacier Creek is also used and is currently the more reliably open option given the FS-23 closure.
routes.descent: Many parties descend via Frostbite Ridge to the south side of the same basin rather than reversing the Kennedy Glacier, forming a loop; otherwise reverse the ascent line.
routes.descentText: From the crater rim, either reverse the 40° headwall and glacier back to Kennedy Ridge, or traverse to descend via Frostbite Ridge (downclimbing the class-3 Rabbit Ears notch and ridge) back to the same Glacier Creek basin if that was not the ascent line.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to Glacier Creek camp (~4-5 hrs via White Chuck side, or a longer day via North Fork Sauk/PCT). Day 2: Ascend Kennedy Ridge and Kennedy Glacier to the summit; descend via Frostbite Ridge or reverse the route. Day 3: Exit hike.
routes.bail: Below the bergschrund/headwall, retreat by reversing the glacier is straightforward; once on the 40° headwall above the bergschrund, downclimbing is harder — commit only with good weather and a clear line back to the snow bridge.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting the headwall, since finding the snow bridge over the bergschrund again in deteriorating visibility is a real navigational hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep; conditions on the steep headwall section vary from icy to slushy through the season, and many parties want protection here especially when icy.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Rope, crampons, ice axe, pickets, and about 4 ice screws are typically carried for the bergschrund crossing and headwall.
routes.proNeeds: Screws and pickets for the headwall/bergschrund section; standard glacier crevasse-rescue protection below that.
whatToBring: sturdy boots for variable icy/slushy headwall conditions
proTips: Considered more aesthetic than Frostbite Ridge by climbers who've done both, and is often paired with it as a loop (Kennedy Glacier up, Frostbite Ridge down, or vice versa).
Expect the headwall to range from icy to slushy depending on time of day and season — plan your timing to hit it while still firm but not rock-hard.
watchOut: The bergschrund at the head of the glacier requires finding a viable snow bridge — this can change year to year and even week to week.
The ~40° headwall can be icy; many parties want screw/picket protection here.
knownHazards: Bergschrund crossing hazard at the glacier's head.
Steep, occasionally icy headwall below the crater rim.

pitchDetail.pitch: Kennedy Ridge to foot of glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Descend right off the ridge to the toe of the Kennedy Glacier.
Keep right on the lower glacier, center on the upper glacier.
Find a snow bridge over the bergschrund, climb center-left of prominent rocks to the rim.

blurb: Mount Stuart (9,415 ft) is the highest peak in the Stuart Range within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades, and the second-highest non-volcanic summit in the state. Built of heavily jointed Late Cretaceous granite, it is a complex massif of ridges and glaciers (Stuart, Ice Cliff, and Sherpa Glaciers on its north side) whose 3,000-ft north face and long ridgelines host some of the finest alpine rock climbing in the Cascades, headlined by the Complete North Ridge — one of the "Fifty Classic Climbs of North America." A stick inscribed "Angus McPherson—1873" found at the summit suggests an early first ascent, though surveyors Frank Tweedy and Richard U. Goode are also documented reaching the top in 1883.
hierarchyNote: Mount Stuart (47.475°N, 120.903°W, 9,415 ft) and the Stuart Range sit in the central Washington Cascades — Alpine Lakes Wilderness, on the Chelan/Kittitas county line, south of Stevens Pass — which is normally classed as "Central Cascades," not "North Cascades." Coordinates and elevation check out for the correct peak; if this entry is filed under a North Cascades parent bucket in the app's area hierarchy, it should be reviewed/moved to a Central Cascades / Alpine Lakes Wilderness parent.

routes.overview: The standard scramble and descent route on Stuart's south side: a long, loose gully climbing from Ingalls Creek to the false-summit notch (~9,000 ft), then a rising traverse to the true summit.
routes.beta: Class 3 rock when melted out in summer; a popular snow climb/ski descent in winter and spring. Bypasses several small cliff bands low in the gully.
routes.approach: From the Ingalls Creek Trail (reached via Longs Pass or Esmeralda/Ingalls Way trailheads), leave the trail on one of several boot-paths about 0.1 mile downstream of the Longs Pass junction (~4,800-5,000 ft), then climb through trees and brush as the gully steepens into loose rock and scree, crossing a minor ridge near 8,000 ft.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir (standard descent for the whole peak)
routes.descentText: From the false-summit notch (~9,000 ft) stay high enough to pass through the notch before dropping down — descending too early puts you in the wrong couloir. Descend the loose 3rd-class gully (or steep snow early season) back to Ingalls Creek.
routes.bail: The couloir itself is the bail-out for nearly every other route on the peak; retreat down it at almost any point.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before committing to the upper couloir — afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Cascades and the loose rock/scree becomes far more dangerous to descend in the wet.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable nearly year-round: an axe-and-crampons snow climb in winter/spring, transitioning to a loose, sandy 3rd-class scramble by mid-to-late summer.
routes.face: South side, above Ingalls Creek
routes.fa: Samuel Gannett, 1895 (route first ascent per Mountain Project); peak FA 1873 by A. McPherson party per summit register
proTips: Stay high enough to pass through the false-summit notch (~9,000 ft) before dropping down — descending too early drops you into the wrong couloir.
By late summer this is a loose, sandy scramble rather than a snow climb — expect rockfall from parties above.
watchOut: Loose rock and sand throughout the gully
Cornices can form near the summit ridge in winter/spring
Easy to descend into the wrong couloir below the false summit
knownHazards: Rockfall from parties above in the couloir
Cornice hazard near the false-summit notch in winter/spring
Steep snow requiring self-arrest skill in early season

road.name: Teanaway River Rd / FS-9737 to Esmeralda/Longs Pass TH
road.status: Paved then gravel, generally passable to passenger cars May–Nov
road.seasonalGate: FS-9737 beyond the pavement is closed to wheeled travel roughly mid-Nov to early Apr
road.driveNote: From I-90 exit 85, take WA-970 north ~6.5 mi, then North Fork Teanaway Rd ~13 mi to end of pavement, then FS-9737 to the trailhead.

climate.summer: Melts to loose scree/talus by Jul-Aug

emergency.notes: SAR on Stuart is coordinated by Chelan or Kittitas County Sheriff depending on the side of the mountain, sometimes with helicopter hoist support from Naval Air Station Whidbey Island for high-angle rescues (documented June 2018 fatality near the false summit/Cascadian Couloir).

routes.overview: A long, sustained alpine rock climb up Stuart's prominent north ridge: roughly 11 pitches of low/mid-5th-class climbing from the North Ridge Notch to the base of the Great Gendarme, then two 5.9 crux pitches (a fingers/hands dihedral and an offwidth) through the Gendarme, finishing with easier pitches to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Considered one of the finest alpine rock climbs in the Cascades and one of the '50 Classic Climbs of North America.' The lower ~11 pitches are exposed but moderate (low/mid 5th, one 5.7 move); the Gendarme supplies the technical crux.
routes.approach: From Longs Pass TH: hike to Ingalls Lake, traverse ledges on the lake's east side to Stuart Pass, descend talus northwest to Goat Pass (between Pt. 7893 on Jack Ridge and Stuart's West Ridge), then cross the Stuart Glacier to a 4th-class gully leading to the North Ridge Notch (bivy sites here). Alternatively approach from the north via Mountaineer Creek.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir (standard)
routes.descentText: From the false summit, descend blocky 3rd/4th-class terrain, then follow cairns and a ledge system east to the top of the Cascadian Couloir snowfield; descend the loose 3rd-class couloir to Ingalls Creek.
routes.bail: Rappel back down the ridge/gully system to the North Ridge Notch and retreat the approach; escape is otherwise difficult once committed above the notch.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing route with limited retreat above the notch — set a firm turnaround time at the base of the Gendarme and be prepared to bivy if behind schedule rather than rushing the crux pitches.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is mid-summer once the route and approach glacier crossing are free of new snow; a full-day or overnight objective for most parties.
routes.face: North Ridge / Great Gendarme
routes.fa: John Rupley & Don Gordon, 1956, reached only the North Ridge Notch (bypassing/traversing around the Great Gendarme); the first complete ascent through the Gendarme was made by James Wickwire & Fred Stanley in 1964
routes.corrections: The existing FA note ('Complete North Ridge: Rupley & Gordon, 1956; Gendarme free climbed later') conflates two separate ascents documented on Mountain Project: Rupley & Gordon's 1956 climb reached only the North Ridge Notch, bypassing the Gendarme; Wickwire & Stanley made the first complete ascent including the Gendarme in 1964. The harder Direct North Ridge variant (2,800 ft, 20 pitches, 5.9+, an extra ~1,200 ft of climbing before joining this line) is a separate route credited to Mead Hargis & Jay Ossiander — sources conflict on whether that was 1963 (Mountain Project) or 1970 (secondary trip-report accounts).
routes.rappels: None on ascent — the route is descended via the Cascadian Couloir scramble, not by rappelling the climbing line.

pitchDetail.notes: From the North Ridge Notch to the base of the Great Gendarme; exposed but moderate climbing on solid granite.
Steep fingers-and-hands dihedral with good rests.
Traverse into and climb an offwidth crack; a fixed #1 and #4 cam aid the crux moves.
One more hard pitch after the Gendarme, then three easier pitches to the ridge crest.

routes.detailedRack: A standard long alpine rock rack (single set of cams to ~3in, set of nuts) plus one 3.5in piece for the Gendarme's offwidth pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Bring whatever you'd normally carry on a long alpine rock route, plus one 3-3.5in piece specifically for the offwidth on the Gendarme.
whatToBring: bivy gear if not climbing it car-to-car
proTips: Bring a single wide piece (3-3.5in) just for the Gendarme offwidth — you won't need it anywhere else on route.
The Gendarme sits in near-permanent shade and can be cold even on a hot day.
watchOut: Loose, lichen-covered rock off the main crest in places
Long and committing once past the North Ridge Notch
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Cascades in summer
knownHazards: Rockfall potential from parties on shared gully approach sections
Extended commitment with limited retreat above the notch
routes.comms: Cell coverage is spotty to absent on the north side of the peak; occasional signal reported from the ridge crest and summit.

routes.overview: A long, moderate ridge climb — mostly 3rd/4th-class scrambling on excellent granite — with a short technical step (5.4-5.6) near the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: One of the most popular technical routes on the peak; long and route-finding-intensive, with many opportunities to wander onto harder terrain. Key waypoints: Long John Tower, the West Ridge Notch, then fifth-class rock on the north side to the summit.
routes.approach: Recommended southern approach: Esmeralda/Longs Pass Trailhead to Ingalls Lake, then follow the ridge toward Stuart Pass and cut right across talus to the base near Long John Tower (a few hours trailhead-to-base). Northern approach via Stuart Lake Trailhead to Stuart Pass is longer (~4.5 hrs to the base).
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: Descend the Cascadian Couloir — a steep, loose gully losing over 4,000 ft — then continue via the Longs Pass Trail back to the Esmeralda Trailhead (or Ingalls Creek Trail back to Stuart Lake Trailhead for the northern approach).
routes.bail: Downclimb the ridge itself back toward Long John Tower/Stuart Pass at most points below the summit pyramid.
routes.turnaround: Given the length and route-finding demands, turn around with enough daylight margin to downclimb the ridge rather than descend the Cascadian Couloir in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep, once the ridge is free of significant snow.
routes.face: West Ridge
routes.fa: Lex Maxwell, Fred Llewellyn & John Vertrees, 1935
routes.corrections: Mountain Project and several guide sources (The Mountaineers, StephAbegg) commonly grade this route 5.6 overall (2,200-2,300 ft, 10 pitches) rather than the 5.4 given, because although the line-of-least-resistance goes at 5.4 via a short dihedral with a fixed piton near the summit pyramid, most parties end up on a 5.6 lieback variation nearby; both grades are documented for the same crux step. Route length is cited as both 2,200 ft (Mountain Project) and 2,300 ft (StephAbegg/Mountaineers) — used 2,300 ft as the more commonly cited figure.

pitchDetail.notes: Long scramble past Long John Tower on solid granite.

pitchDetail.pitch: West Ridge Notch to summit block
pitchDetail.notes: A short dihedral with a fixed piton (5.4) or a lieback further along the ledge (5.6); old pitons visible on the final step.

routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: set of nuts, small-to-medium cams (~#0.3-#2), long runners, helmet; rock shoes optional for the summit step.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear given the mostly 3rd/4th-class nature — a few small-to-medium cams and nuts protect the short technical step near the top.
proTips: The route is long and complex with many chances to wander onto harder terrain — route-finding matters as much as climbing ability.
Solo or move together through most of the 3rd/4th-class terrain to save time; save the rope for the summit step.
watchOut: Easy to get off-route onto more difficult terrain
Extended exposure on 3rd/4th-class scrambling
Long day — budget 10-12+ hours car to car
knownHazards: Exposure on 3rd/4th-class terrain with rockfall potential
Afternoon thunderstorms

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start (aim to leave the trailhead by 3-4am)

routes.overview: A committing, early-season glacier/ice climb up the icefall and cirque on Stuart's northeast side, weaving through crevasses to a ~50-degree upper couloir, then a mixed/rock finish near the false summit.
routes.beta: Starts from the moraine below the North Ridge at ~6,600 ft; traverse under the cliff and ascend the icefall by either flank — early season favors the left side (AI2 ice), late season may expose a 5.8 slab alternative on the right. Continue through the cirque, pass the bergschrund on the right, ascend the ~50° upper couloir taking the right fork near the summit, and pass a cornice to the south-facing ridge below the false summit (~8,600 ft).
routes.approach: From Stuart Lake Trailhead (Icicle Rd), follow the main trail staying right at the first fork; a climbers' trail branches off near 47.49742°N, 120.86218°W. Ascend talus or snow staying east of Mountaineers Ridge to campsites below the Sherpa Glacier at ~5,400 ft.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier
routes.descentText: From the false-summit area retrace toward the summit ridge, then descend the Sherpa Glacier starting around 8,200 ft back toward the Stuart Lake approach.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier/icefall is difficult once committed above the bergschrund; this route has genuinely limited bail-out options once underway.
routes.turnaround: Given the crevasse/icefall exposure and limited retreat, commit to a firm early turnaround if conditions deteriorate — this is not a route to push through worsening weather on.
routes.bestSeason: Apr-Jul, condition-dependent: early season gives the most straightforward ice; by late summer the icefall becomes heavily crevassed.
routes.face: Northeast Face (Ice Cliff Glacier cirque)
routes.fa: Bill Prater, Gene Prater, and Dave Mahre, August 5, 1957
routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists the overall time-commitment as Grade III, differing from the Grade IV given here (both are valid grading conventions but the primary-source route page specifically states Grade III). Ice grade is confirmed as AI2-3 (not a flat AI3) per Mountain Project.

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse under the cliff and ascend the icefall by either flank; left is easier ice early season.
Pass the bergschrund on the right, ascend the couloir taking the right fork near the summit.
Cornice leads to the south-facing ridge below the false summit; traverse to the true summit.

routes.detailedRack: 1-4 ice screws, 2-4 pickets, a small rock rack if taking the late-season rock-slab variation.
routes.proNeeds: Screws and pickets for the glacier/couloir; a few small cams/nuts if using the late-season rock option.
proTips: Early season (before the bergschrund opens up) offers the most straightforward ice; by late summer the icefall can be heavily crevassed and the right-side rock slab (5.8) becomes the easier option.
This is a genuinely glaciated route — carry full crevasse-rescue gear even though it 'reads' like an ice climb.
watchOut: Crevasses and an open bergschrund
Icefall/serac hazard on the lower glacier
Committing — limited retreat once above the bergschrund
knownHazards: Crevasse-fall potential on the Ice Cliff Glacier
Icefall/serac collapse
Cornice hazard near the top of the route

climate.spring: Best ice conditions

routes.overview: A serious early-season ice/mixed line up the glacier on Stuart's northwest side: a narrowing snow/ice couloir to the West Ridge notch, then 6 pitches of mixed rock/ice along the ridge to the summit area.
routes.beta: Entry starts at ~7,800 ft on the Stuart Glacier; the couloir climbs 40-50° snow with two steeper steps, widening to a snowfield leading to the West Ridge notch. From there, 6 pitches combine fifth-class rock with ice/snow — early season features dry-tooling M5 climbing, later season may reduce difficulty to M3-M4.
routes.approach: From Icicle Rd/Stuart Lake Trailhead, follow the Stuart Lake trail ~3 miles to the Colchuck Lake turnoff, continue 1 mile, veer left into the main valley, then proceed ~1.5 miles to a steep slope and ascend rightward to a small flat valley (~5,400 ft) below the Sherpa Glacier — good camping here.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier (standard early-season descent)
routes.descentText: Descend the east ridge toward the false summit, then traverse the southeast snowfield past the Ice Cliff Glacier to the Sherpa Couloir/Glacier.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ascent snow/ice couloir before committing to the mixed ridge pitches; above that point options are limited.
routes.turnaround: Conditions swing hard with temperature — turn around early if the couloir is softening or the ridge pitches are running wetter/icier than expected.
routes.bestSeason: May-Jul, early season for the best mixed conditions; the route degrades and eases by mid-summer.
routes.fa: Fred (Helmy) Beckey and Larry Strathdee, June 1944
routes.corrections: Mountain Project grades this WI2-3, M5- (mixed dry-tooling), notably harder than the plain AI2 given in the existing data — corrected accordingly. Route length/pitch count (1,700 ft / 6 pitches) sourced from Mountain Project and added since not previously filled.
routes.face: Northwest side (Stuart Glacier)

pitchDetail.notes: Enter at ~7,800 ft on the Stuart Glacier and climb as the couloir narrows, with two steeper steps.
Wider snowfield climbing to the West Ridge notch.
Mixed fifth-class rock with ice/snow sections; early season features dry-tooling.

routes.detailedRack: Ice screws, pickets (early season), 5 cams to BD #3, 8 nuts, 4 pitons, plus extra small cams.
routes.proNeeds: A genuine mixed rack — screws/pickets for the couloir, plus a fuller rock rack (nuts, cams to #3, a few pins) for the ridge pitches.
whatToBring: glacier travel gear for the approach
proTips: Climb it early season (before it 'goes out') for the best ice; late season the couloir degrades and the ridge pitches ease to M3-M4.
Camping at the ~5,400 ft flat below Sherpa Glacier sets up a reasonable single push.
watchOut: Highly condition-dependent — difficulty swings a lot with snowpack/temperature
Difficult early-season route-finding
Two steep steps low in the couloir
knownHazards: Avalanche/wet-slide potential in the couloir in spring
Rockfall/icefall on the ridge in warm conditions
Mixed dry-tooling terrain requiring solid ice-tool skills

climate.spring: Prime mixed conditions
climate.summer: Route degrades, easier but wetter/looser

routes.overview: A serious rock pillar route above the Ice Cliff Glacier on Stuart's northeast face: four easy pitches gain the pillar, followed by three sustained crack pitches (5.10-5.11-) up the pillar itself, then easier climbing to the false summit.
routes.beta: The named pillar sits high on the northeast face above the Ice Cliff Glacier icefall. After the glacier approach and four easy pitches, three engaging crack pitches form the crux; large loose blocks are noted near the top of the pillar.
routes.approach: Same glaciated approach as Ice Cliff Glacier — from Stuart Lake Trailhead, via the climbers' trail branch near 47.49742°N, 120.86218°W, up talus/snow east of Mountaineers Ridge to the Sherpa Glacier basin, then onto the Ice Cliff Glacier icefall to reach the base of the pillar.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier
routes.descentText: From near the false summit, descend the Sherpa Glacier back toward the Stuart Lake approach, as with Ice Cliff Glacier.
routes.bail: Rappel the pillar's crack system on gear; retreat down the glacier approach is otherwise the main option below the pillar.
routes.turnaround: This route mixes glacier travel and sustained free climbing — budget a full day and set a turnaround that leaves time to re-cross the glacier in daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Aug, once the glacier approach is passable and the rock is dry.
routes.face: Northeast Face, above Ice Cliff Glacier
routes.fa: Kit Lewis & Jim Nelson, 1983
routes.corrections: Given pitch count (6) appears to reflect only the pillar's named crux pitches; Mountain Project documents the full route as 11 pitches (4 easy pitches to gain the pillar + the pillar's 3 crux pitches + additional pitches to the false summit) totaling ~2,000 ft — corrected/filled accordingly.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep hand crack; belay at a small, poor ledge.
Thin finger crack with a fixed stopper protecting the crux, finishing on a hand crack.
Steep hands on somewhat dirty rock; beware very large loose blocks near the top.

routes.detailedRack: Ice gear for the glacier approach plus a rock rack to 3 inches for the pillar's crack pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Full ice kit for the approach glacier, switching to a rock rack to 3in for the pillar.
proTips: Bring dedicated ice gear just for the approach glacier, then switch fully to rock gear for the pillar — this route mixes disciplines more than most Stuart lines.
The P1 belay ledge is poor — bring slings to equalize an anchor.
watchOut: Very large loose blocks near the top of the pillar (P3)
Glaciated, committing approach
Sustained, sandbagged crack climbing
knownHazards: Loose rock on the upper pillar
Crevasse hazard on the Ice Cliff Glacier approach

routes.overview: Mount Stuart's north side rises roughly 3,000 ft in a broad, glaciated face split by the Stuart, Ice Cliff, and Sherpa Glaciers — the setting for the mountain's hardest technical lines (Stuart Glacier Couloir, Ice Cliff Glacier, Girth Pillar) rather than a single independent 'North Face' route.
routes.corrections: No Mountain Project, SummitPost, or Peakbagger page titled exactly 'North Face' exists for Mount Stuart (checked directly against all three required primary sources plus AAC Publications). The north side is instead subdivided into several distinctly named, well-documented lines — Ice Cliff Glacier, Stuart Glacier Couloir, and Girth Pillar (already separate entries on this peak) — plus a historical 'East Face' (Beckey/Niccoli, 1961, ~1,500 ft of piton-protected 5th class on the east/northeast headwall). This entry is treated here as the general north-face aspect of the mountain rather than one specific documented route; pitch-, grade-, and length-specific numeric fields are left null because no single source supports them for a route by this exact name. If this ID was meant to represent a specific historical line, it could not be confirmed and should be reviewed against the guidebook (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) directly.
routes.approach: Via Stuart Lake Trailhead (Icicle Rd) to the Mountaineer Creek/Sherpa Glacier basin — the same approach shared by Ice Cliff Glacier, Stuart Glacier Couloir, and Girth Pillar.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier (shared standard descent for north-side routes)
routes.bestSeason: Best snow/ice conditions Apr-Jun; the north side's rock lines dry out by Jul-Aug.
routes.face: North Face (general aspect encompassing the Stuart/Ice Cliff/Sherpa glacier cirques)
knownHazards: Crevasses and icefall on the north-side glaciers
Loose rock typical of Cascades granite at altitude
Rapid weather changes

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Slopes Central / Stevens Pass zone
climate.typical: Shaded north aspect holds snow/ice much later in the season than the south side

routes.overview: The technical crux of Stuart's North Ridge: a striking 5.8 lieback pitch followed by a 5.9+ offwidth pitch through the 'Great Gendarme,' a prominent rock tower about two-thirds of the way up the ridge; it sits in near-permanent shade and can be cold.
routes.corrections: 'The Gendarme' is not a standalone car-to-summit route on Mountain Project, SummitPost, or Peakbagger — it is the 2-pitch crux buttress ('Great Gendarme') that both the North Ridge and Direct North Ridge climb through, and which can also be bypassed via a ~75 ft rappel and gully traverse. It has no independent approach/descent — parties reach and leave it via the North Ridge line. First-ascent credit for climbing the Gendarme itself is ambiguous in the sources checked: Mountain Project credits James Wickwire & Fred Stanley (1964) with the first complete ascent of the North Ridge through the Gendarme, while the Direct North Ridge's direct finish is credited to Mead Hargis & Jay Ossiander, with secondary sources disagreeing on 1963 vs. 1970 for that date — no single unambiguous FA party/year for the Gendarme specifically could be confirmed, so fa is left null rather than guessed.
routes.beta: P1 is a lieback (5.8, climbable more directly at 5.9): a few steps of liebacking lead to one final vertical lieback section to the top of the column. P2 is the 'feared' offwidth, easier than its reputation — easy traversing moves on good holds and hand jams (aided by a fixed #1) lead into an easy fist-crack entrance (protectable with a #3), a few fist jams and an armbar to a fixed #4, then easy terrain traversing right.
routes.bail: Most parties who don't want to free the Gendarme rappel ~75 ft off the right side of the ridge below the buttress, then traverse a gully (snow, wet, or icy rock until mid-summer, low 5th class) to bypass it entirely.
routes.turnaround: As the final technical obstacle before the summit ridge, don't commit to freeing the Gendarme late in the day — the bypass rappel/gully option exists specifically for parties running behind schedule.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, as part of a North Ridge or Direct North Ridge ascent.
routes.face: Great Gendarme, upper North Ridge

pitchDetail.notes: A few steps of liebacking, then one final vertical lieback section to the top of the column; can be pushed to 5.9 with a more direct line.
Easy traverse on good holds/hand jams (fixed #1) into an easy fist-crack entrance (protect with #3), fist jams and an armbar to a fixed #4, then easy terrain traversing right.

routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rack; a single wide piece (3-3.5in) is the one Gendarme-specific addition for the offwidth pitch — fixed gear (a #1 and #4 cam) is reportedly left in place at the crux moves.
routes.proNeeds: Fixed gear (#1 and #4 cams) is reported in place at the crux moves of the offwidth pitch, but bring your own #3-3.5in cam as backup and for the fist-crack section.
proTips: The offwidth pitch (P2) is easier than its reputation — most of it is protectable hand/fist jamming rather than a true wide-crack grovel.
The Gendarme sits in near-permanent shade; bring a layer even on a hot day.
watchOut: Cold, shaded rock even in summer
Committing position high on the ridge with limited retreat other than the bypass rappel
Fixed gear left in place should be inspected, not solely relied upon
knownHazards: Exposure high on the ridge
Cold/wet rock in the shade reducing friction
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage this high on the north side of the peak.

blurb: Dragontail Peak (8,840 ft) is the second-highest summit in the Stuart Range, part of the Central Cascades' Alpine Lakes Wilderness, anchoring the west end of the Enchantments basin above Colchuck Lake near Leavenworth, WA. Its unusually sound granite and 2,000+ foot north face form one of the finest alpine walls in the Cascades, hosting classic rock lines (Serpentine Arête, Backbone Ridge) alongside the celebrated Triple Couloirs mixed route. The peak was named in 1955 by climbers Lex Maxwell, Bob McCall, and Bill Prater for the jagged "dragon tail" ridge of rock needles southwest of the true summit.
hierarchyNote: Geography checks out: Dragontail Peak (47.4789, -120.83318, 8,840 ft) is a real, well-documented summit in the Stuart Range, Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Chelan County WA, confirmed consistently by Mountain Project, Peakbagger, Wikipedia, WTA, and USFS. One nuance worth flagging for the app's area hierarchy: the Stuart Range/Enchantments is generally classified as the Central Cascades (Icicle Creek/Leavenworth corridor), not the North Cascades proper (which usually refers to North Cascades National Park and the terrain north of Highway 2 — Mt. Baker, the Pickets, Cascade Pass, etc.). If this peak is filed under a "North Cascades" region bucket rather than "Central Cascades/Stuart Range," that's a mild mis-grouping worth checking against the broader WA hierarchy audit, though it's commonly lumped into "Washington Cascades" content colloquially so may be intentional.

routes.overview: A long, high-quality granite line up the right (west) side of Dragontail's north face, roughly 200 ft right of Backbone Ridge, following corners and crack systems to two sustained 5.8 crux pitches before ridge scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: Climbs an obvious dihedral system past two trees with webbing to a large terrace, then crack systems and a small dihedral to the crux — two consecutive 5.8 pitches (a finger crack into a splitter hand crack, then a dihedral with bomber finger locks) that can be linked with a 70m rope. Above the crux the route stays on the ridge crest with mostly 3rd-4th class/low-5th scrambling, a short downclimb, and a sandy gully to the summit area.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (FR 7601), hike the Colchuck Lake Trail ~4 mi / 2,280 ft gain to Colchuck Lake (5,574 ft). Round the lake's east/south shore, boulder-hop onto the Colchuck Glacier's lateral moraine, and traverse it toward the orange-colored rock marking the route start; 4th-class ledges lead up to a snow finger and the base of the technical climbing. Roughly 3 hours car-to-base at a brisk pace.
routes.descent: Descend the sandy summit gully to the Backbone Ridge/Serpentine junction, then down via the Snow Creek Glacier side or over to Aasgard Pass, back to Colchuck Lake and out the trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties head down the loose sandy gully shared with the top of Backbone Ridge, boot-ski or downclimb the upper Snow Creek Glacier snowfield where present, then traverse to Aasgard Pass (7,841 ft) and descend its steep, loose talus/scree back to Colchuck Lake. Crampons and an ice axe are important for this descent whenever snow/ice remain on the upper mountain or in Aasgard Pass. Some parties instead reverse via Colchuck Glacier/Col — route-find carefully, as the descent gully is loose and cliffs out if the wrong line is taken.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Stuart Lake TH (or backpack in with an Enchantments overnight permit), hike to Colchuck Lake, camp on the south shore. Day 2: Alpine start (2-4am), approach to route base (~3 hrs), climb the route (7-10 hrs), descend to Colchuck Lake and hike out (~13-14 hrs car-to-car total is typical for a fit party going car-to-car in a single push).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on the lower pitches (walk-off ledges/terrace); once committed to the crux pitches, rappel the route on gear anchors back to the large terrace below. Higher on the ridge, continuing to easier terrain and the Backbone Ridge descent gully is usually faster than reversing the crux.
routes.turnaround: Budget the full ~3 hr approach, 7-10 hr climb, and a multi-hour descent; set a turnaround time that keeps you off the loose summit gully and Aasgard Pass descent after dark, since benightment and afternoon thunderstorms are recurring causes of Enchantments rescues.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, once the moat/moraine approach and any snow finger low on the route have mostly melted out; a lingering snow patch can require crampons/axe into early July. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer — start early.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin and on Dragontail's faces is unreliable to absent; do not rely on a phone signal — carry a satellite messenger.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rack to 3 inches with doubles through the smaller-to-mid sizes for the crack systems; the crux pitches take good, solid gear. All of the technical climbing is on solid rock even though the low-5th-class sections are loose.
routes.proNeeds: Gear to 3 inches is sufficient; no wide gear required. Long runners help manage rope drag on the wandering ridge pitches.
proTips: Link the two 5.8 crux pitches with a 70m rope to save time
Most pitches above the crux can be simul-climbed rather than pitched out — climberkyle notes only ~4 pitches really need pitching
Watch route-finding on pitch 1; it's easy to wander onto harder terrain than necessary
watchOut: Loose rock through much of the low-5th-class climbing
Lichen-covered slabs are slick, especially early season or after rain
knownHazards: Rockfall from parties above or from the loose upper gully
The moraine/glacier crossing to the route base can require an ice axe and has exposure to a fall onto the Colchuck Glacier

pitchDetail.notes: Up an obvious dihedral past two trees with webbing to a large terrace; some parties end up on harder 5th class here by mistake
Crack systems and a small dihedral toward the crux; a lichen-covered slab section feels sketchy
The two consecutive crux pitches: a finger crack into a splitter hand crack, then a dihedral with bomber finger locks; link with a 70m rope
Ridge-crest scrambling, a short downclimb, sandy gully to the summit — mostly simul-climbable

routes.corrections: Pitch count corrected to 13 (Mountain Project's stated count for the route) from the given 11; a detailed trip report (climberkyle.com) counted 15 pitches for their specific line/pitching choices, so 11-15 all appear across sources depending on how sections are combined. routes.overview: Three snow/ice couloirs splitting the middle of Dragontail's north face, separated by rock/ice bands (the Runnels), delivering ~2,500 ft of classic Cascades mixed climbing to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Enter the first ('Hidden') couloir from the base of the north face above Colchuck Lake's south end and climb steep, exposed snow (~45-50°). The crux Runnels — 2-3 ropelengths of steep (70-80°) ice/thin ice over slabby rock — connect the first and second couloirs and are highly conditions-dependent, sometimes requiring a mixed variation around unclimbable thin ice. The second couloir is often loose sugar snow with dramatic views straight down to Colchuck Lake; the third and easiest couloir finishes with a short ice step that can often be soloed, exiting to the summit ridge.
routes.approach: Stuart Lake Trailhead → Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) → camp or continue around/across the frozen lake to the base of the north face below the Hidden Couloir entrance at ~6,400 ft. Winter/spring approach typically requires snowshoes or skis on the trail and lake crossing.
routes.descent: Descend down and right to a notch near the top of the couloirs, then take a direct line toward Aasgard Pass and down to Colchuck Lake.
routes.descentText: From the top of the third couloir, traverse/descend down and right to a notch on the summit ridge, then descend toward Aasgard Pass, following it down steep talus/scree (or snow in early season, with avalanche awareness) back to Colchuck Lake and out the trail. Rope up for any exposed downclimbing near the notch if icy.
routes.itinerary: Camp at Colchuck Lake the night before; alpine start well before dawn to climb the couloirs in firm, cold conditions and be off the upper mountain before afternoon warming destabilizes the snow/ice; climbing the route itself typically takes 5-9 hours from Colchuck Lake per Alpine Institute route notes.
routes.bail: The Hidden Couloir and lower Runnels can be downclimbed/rappelled from gear or rock anchors if conditions deteriorate before committing to the second and third couloirs; retreat gets harder and more exposed the higher you go.
routes.turnaround: Given avalanche and rockfall exposure that increases sharply with daytime warming, turn around well before the couloirs go isothermal or start shedding rock/ice — plan to be off the technical terrain by early-to-mid afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed in spring (best conditions per Alpine Institute), and sometimes in winter if avalanche conditions are stable; trip reports document ascents from November through April. Highly conditions-dependent year to year — check recent trip reports before committing.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin and on the north face is unreliable to absent; carry a satellite communicator.
gear: 4-5 ice screws (short screws valuable in thin ice)
whatToBring: snowshoes or skis for winter/spring approach
routes.detailedRack: Medium rack including small nuts, 4-5 pitons, cams 0.5-2, 4-5 ice screws, and pickets; run-out sections and simul-soloing are typical, so this is not a beginner-friendly rack situation.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is often sparse in the Runnels' thin ice — screws in marginal ice plus occasional rock gear/pitons; comfort soloing or running it out on moderate terrain is expected.
proTips: Assess the Runnels' ice coverage from Colchuck Lake before starting — look for complete ice coverage on the rock band below the second couloir
Short (10cm) ice screws are often the only useful size in thin conditions
Climb early/cold to keep the couloirs firm and reduce rockfall
watchOut: The Runnels can be too thin/fragile to climb directly in some years, forcing a mixed detour
Loose rock and rotten snow in the second couloir
Avalanche hazard on approach and in the couloirs themselves
knownHazards: Avalanche
rockfall, especially as the day warms
thin/variable ice in the Runnels crux
cornices near the couloir exits

pitchDetail.notes: Steep, exposed snow with possible ice on the right side; starts ~6,400 ft directly above the south end of Colchuck Lake
Crux — steep, often thin ice over slabby rock connecting couloirs 1 and 2; conditions vary significantly year to year
Often knee-deep, loose sugar snow; dramatic exposure above Colchuck Lake

pitchDetail.grade: Moderate, with a WI2 ice step
pitchDetail.notes: Least steep of the three; the ice step can often be soloed; exits to the summit ridge/notch

routes.corrections: Pitch count corrected to 20 (Mountain Project) from the given 12; grade/length (Grade IV, AI3, 5.8, 2,500 ft) is well corroborated across Mountain Project and American Alpine Institute and matches the given data closely.
