routes.overview: A long, left-trending granite ridge line up the lower-right side of Dragontail's north face, mixing clean crack-and-corner pitches with looser scrambling, capped by the striking 'Fin' feature near the summit.
routes.beta: After 4th-class scrambling to the first technical pitches, the route climbs an easy 5.8 pitch into the crux 5.9 offwidth (needs a #6 Camalot), then two more 5.8 pitches and a simul-climbing section to the base of the Fin. The Fin itself offers three spectacular pitches — an enjoyable 5.8 off the main ledge, a longer/harder pitch to a semi-hanging stance, then a single, almost-horizontal crack requiring 20+ meters of exposed, holds-poor traversing. Two mellow but loose pitches and more simul-climbing lead to the summit.
routes.approach: Stuart Lake Trailhead → Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) → around the lake and up the moraine to the route base (roughly 200 ft left of Serpentine Arête); initial bushwhacking on brushy ledges until the party cliffs out and traverses up/right toward the ridge crest.
routes.descent: Standard descent via the east ledges/summit gully shared with Serpentine Arête; in icy late-season conditions, an alternate 2-rappel line (70m rope, 60m possible) drops to Aasgard Pass.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the east ledges and sandy summit gully toward Aasgard Pass and down to Colchuck Lake. When the upper descent gully is icy (common late season), rappel instead — two rappels on a 70m rope (60m works) using rock anchors to avoid the ice slope, with a documented rappel station near 47.479°N, 120.832°W. Watch for loose rock and verify anchor integrity on any rappel here.
routes.itinerary: Camp at Colchuck Lake or day-push from the trailhead; alpine start to be climbing by first light. Trip reports describe an ~18-hour car-to-car day as 'civilized' for the grade — move efficiently to locate the descent before dark.
routes.bail: Retreat below the Fin is possible by rappelling the route on gear anchors back toward the moraine approach; once on the Fin itself, continuing over the top to the established rappel line is generally faster and safer than reversing the exposed traverse pitch.
routes.turnaround: This is a long day (commonly reported around 18 hours car-to-car); set a firm turnaround time on the route to ensure the Fin and descent rappels aren't tackled in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: July through September for the driest rock; in early season, aluminum crampons and a light ice axe are useful for a snow slope on the approach and for the standard descent gully.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin and on Dragontail is unreliable to absent; carry a satellite communicator.
gear: aluminum crampons and light ice axe (early season)
whatToBring: layers for an all-day exposed ridge
routes.detailedRack: A #6 Camalot is required for the crux offwidth pitch. Beyond that, a medium rack to #3.5 covers most of the route, but a #5 becomes handy in multiple spots, including on 'the Fin'.
routes.proNeeds: The offwidth pitch is protection-dependent on having the #6; elsewhere gear is generally good in the clean granite cracks, though the Fin's traverse pitch is notably run-out/exposed with minimal holds.
proTips: Simul-climb the lower moderate sections and the approaches to/from the Fin to keep the long day moving
Bring the #6 specifically for the offwidth — don't substitute
The Fin's three pitches (Blake Herrington's far-left variation in Cascades Rock) are the highlight of the route
watchOut: Rockfall on the lower scrambling sections
Loose 4th-class rock near the summit
Late-season descent gully can be pure ice, requiring the rappel alternative
knownHazards: Rockfall
loose rock on the descent, including at rappel anchors — verify integrity
the Fin's traverse pitch is highly exposed with minimal protection

pitchDetail.notes: 60m — white corner to the ridge crest
30m offwidth requiring a #6 Camalot
60m — blocky corner with a small roof
50m — finger crack and hands layback
~100m ridge scramble with 5th-class steps
60m — ramp approach to the base of the Fin
30m — twin cracks to a large ledge (Fin pitch 1)
40m — right-facing corner and hand crack to a semi-hanging stance (Fin pitch 2)
60m+ — blocky crack, horizontal traverse across the Fin's face (Fin pitch 3)
~100m — Fin crossing to the summit, simul-climbable

routes.rappels: 2 raps on a 70m rope (60m possible), rock anchors, near 47.479°N 120.832°W

routes.overview: The lower, initially hidden first couloir of the Triple Couloirs system on Dragontail's north face — a shorter, moderate snow/ice objective when climbed as its own outing rather than continuing through the Runnels into the full Triple Couloirs.
routes.beta: Named for being out of view from Colchuck Lake, this couloir starts at roughly 6,400 ft directly above the lake's south end and trends south for about 800 vertical feet at roughly 45°, typically holding good neve snow into mid-summer. Climbed alone, most parties top out at the steep rock band (the top of the Runnels crux) rather than continuing into the second and third couloirs.
routes.approach: Stuart Lake Trailhead → Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) → around/across the lake to the base of the north face below the couloir entrance (~6,400 ft).
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel the couloir itself back to its base, then reverse the approach to Colchuck Lake and out.
routes.descentText: Because this is the lower portion of a longer face, parties stopping here typically downclimb the couloir (facing in on steeper sections) or make one or two rappels from available gear anchors/rock horns back to its base, then walk off to Colchuck Lake and the trail.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before daytime warming loosens the snowpack or triggers rockfall from the band above; this is a short enough objective that there is little reason to push into deteriorating conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Dec-May, conditions-dependent; best when a solid neve base has formed and before spring warming turns the couloir to loose, wet snow.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin is unreliable to absent; carry a satellite communicator.
proTips: Assess snow coverage from Colchuck Lake before committing — the couloir is visible once you're on the lake's south shore despite the name
watchOut: Rockfall from the band above where it meets the Runnels
Variable snow conditions (neve to sugar snow) through the season
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard on approach and within the couloir
rockfall

pitchDetail.notes: Steep, exposed snow with possible ice on the right side of the couloir

routes.face: North Face — lowest of the three Triple Couloirs couloirs, directly above the south end of Colchuck Lake
objHaz: rockfall from the rock band above
routes.corrections: This is not an independently first-ascended, separately named route — it is the documented first (lowest) couloir of the Triple Couloirs system on Dragontail's north face (see Mountain Project / climberkyle.com Triple Couloirs beta). It can be and is sometimes climbed alone as a shorter outing, which the given entry appears to treat it as; no standalone FA exists in any source found. highPointFt/routeFt reflect the couloir's own top (~7,200 ft after ~800 vertical ft), not the true summit, since it doesn't continue there on its own.

routes.overview: A sustained line up the lowest point of Dragontail's north face (the 'nordwand'), between Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge — a moderate 4th/low-5th class rock route in summer, and an increasingly popular steep ice-and-mixed testpiece (WI3+ M4) in winter/spring conditions.
routes.beta: Also called the North Face route. First climbed in summer as mostly 4th/low-5th class rock with a couple of 5.7 cruxes; in recent years it has become a sought-after winter/spring alpine ice and mixed objective, following snow ramps just right of the low point of the face and then the runnels splitting the dark rock band two-thirds up. Winter conditions are highly variable with multiple line options; in good nick it's described as offering more sustained exposure than the neighboring Triple Couloirs.
routes.approach: Stuart Lake Trailhead → Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) → around the lake to the base of the north face at its lowest point, between the Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge starts.
routes.descent: Descend via Aasgard Pass to Colchuck Lake, as with the other north-face routes on this peak.
routes.descentText: From the upper mountain, traverse to the summit ridge/Aasgard Pass area and descend the pass's steep talus/scree (or snow, with avalanche awareness in winter/spring) back down to Colchuck Lake and out the trail.
routes.bail: The lower snow ramps and initial pitches can be downclimbed or rappelled from natural/gear anchors; retreat becomes more involved once into the sustained runnels two-thirds up the face.
routes.turnaround: In winter/spring conditions, turn around before daytime warming destabilizes the snow ramps and ice; this is a conditions-dependent route where 'good enough to climb' can change day to day.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable in two very different modes: summer (Jul-Sep) for the original 4th/low-5th class rock line, or winter/spring (roughly Feb-Apr) for the WI3+/M4 ice-and-mixed version, which is conditions-dependent and best assessed via recent trip reports.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin and on Dragontail is unreliable to absent; carry a satellite communicator.
gear: 4+ ice screws including a stubby, single rack, pitons (winter)
whatToBring: extra insulation for a cold north-face route
routes.detailedRack: Summer: medium rack to 3 inches. Winter: 4+ ice screws (a stubby can be useful), a single rack, with pitons recommended depending on conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Winter protection is conditions-dependent between ice screws and rock gear/pitons in the runnels; summer protection is straightforward rock gear on the 4th/low-5th terrain.
proTips: Check recent trip reports before a winter attempt — conditions vary hugely year to year
In good nick this offers more sustained climbing than the adjacent Triple Couloirs
watchOut: Highly conditions-dependent winter ice/mixed climbing — can range from thin/unclimbable to excellent
Loose rock on the summer 4th-class terrain
knownHazards: Avalanche and rockfall exposure on the face
variable ice quality in winter

routes.face: North Face — lowest point of Dragontail's nordwand, between Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge
routes.fa: Gerber and Sink (route named for the first-ascent party; exact given names/year not documented in sources found)
routes.corrections: rockGrade corrected to 5.7 (Mountain Project's stated YDS grade) from the given 5.9. iceGrade (WI3+ M4, winter conditions) and pitches (10) were null in the given data and filled from Mountain Project. alpineGrade adjusted from 'III-IV' to 'IV' to match Mountain Project's stated overall grade — treat as a minor refinement, not a hard conflict.

routes.overview: A deep, striking couloir on Dragontail's northeast-facing side offering sustained snow/snice climbing with a technical mixed crux (roughly M4-M5) at about a third height, finishing with loose mixed terrain to an exit notch.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Lake, approach staying right of the standard moraine trail; low-angle water ice with a few short WI2- steps and snow-covered slabs lead to the couloir entrance. The couloir itself is an incredibly deep, striking feature roughly 10 ft wide with walls hundreds of feet tall, climbing sustained 50-60° snow/snice/mixed terrain. At about a third of the way up, a constriction holds the technical crux — an overhung chockstone requiring mixed moves (M4-M5). The upper couloir continues through broken steps and slabs to a runout, loose mixed finish (a mantle onto questionable ice/rock) below an exit notch.
routes.approach: Stuart Lake Trailhead → Colchuck Lake Trail (~4 mi, 2,280 ft gain) → Colchuck Lake, staying right of the moraine (rather than the standard climber's trail) to reach the base of the northeast-facing couloir.
routes.descent: A single rappel from an established anchor at the exit notch, then an easy scramble toward the Snow Creek Glacier and down.
routes.descentText: From the top of the couloir's difficulties, rappel once from a fixed/established anchor at the exit notch, then scramble down toward the Snow Creek Glacier drainage and continue to Colchuck Lake / the trail. Confirm the anchor's condition before weighting it, as reports note deteriorating, loose rock in this upper section.
routes.bail: The lower two-thirds (below the mixed crux constriction) can be downclimbed on the moderate snow/snice; above the crux, retreat is harder and the exit-notch rappel anchor may be the most practical way off if conditions turn.
routes.turnaround: Given the loose, runout mixed finish, set a conservative turnaround before the crux if conditions (ice quality, snow stability, visible rockfall) look marginal; this route has minimal protection in its hardest section.
routes.bestSeason: Nov-May, conditions-dependent; documented ascents range from a November mixed climb (thin, technical) to an easier spring snow-couloir ascent once more snow has filled in.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Colchuck Lake basin and on Dragontail's northeast side is unreliable to absent; carry a satellite communicator.
whatToBring: extra insulation — exposed to wind at upper elevations
routes.detailedRack: A light rack works: two short ice screws, a set of nuts, and cams from 0.2 to 1.0 for the mixed crux and upper steps.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is minimal through the loose, runout mixed finish above the crux constriction; the crux itself takes gear in the rock flanking the chockstone.
proTips: Solo the easier lower snow/ice to save time and rope drag, roping up at the crux constriction
Check recent conditions reports — this route swings from a straightforward snow couloir to a technical mixed pitch depending on the season
watchOut: Loose, deteriorating rock, especially in the upper mixed section
Minimal protection through the runout finish
Wind exposure at the upper couloir/exit notch
knownHazards: Rockfall
loose rock
thin/variable ice depending on season

pitchDetail.notes: Solo-able moderate snow/ice below the couloir proper, past snow-covered entrance slabs
Crux at a constriction — an overhung chockstone requiring mixed moves
Broken steps and slabs, finishing with a runout mantle onto questionable ice/rock below the exit notch

routes.face: Northeast-facing couloir on Dragontail's north side
routes.rappels: 1 rap at the exit notch (established anchor), then a scramble
routes.corrections: IMPORTANT NAMING CONFLICT: sources describe two distinct, separately-documented Dragontail features that this entry's name conflates. (1) A technical 'NE Couloir' (WI2, M4-M5 R) on Dragontail's northeast-facing side — deep, narrow, with a mixed crux — is what this entry's route data (ice/mixed grade, Grade III-IV) most closely matches, and is what I've populated the fields against. (2) 'Pandora's Box' (aka the 'W Couloir') is a separate, much easier snow couloir (Grade I/II, ~3,200 ft) on Dragontail's southwest/west side near Colchuck Col, climbed with just crampons/axe as a scramble-grade snow route — not an ice/mixed climb, and not northeast-facing. These are two different, well-documented lines with different names, aspects, and difficulty; they should not be combined under one route ID. I could not confirm which the original data intended, so I've left the name as given but recommend the app data be split into two separate routes (or the name/ID corrected to whichever single feature is actually intended) rather than trusting this entry's current fields as a coherent whole. gainFt/highPointFt left null because the NE Couloir's documented descent (rappel at an exit notch, scramble toward Snow Creek Glacier) does not clearly reach the true 8,840 ft summit.

blurb: Forbidden Peak is an 8,815-foot glacial horn in the Boston Basin area of North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, its three narrow ridges (west, north, east) of Skagit Gneiss converging on a needle-point summit between the Forbidden and Boston Glaciers. First climbed June 1, 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Jim Crooks, and Dave Lind via the West Ridge, it is one of the most popular technical peaks in the Cascades yet is famous for having "no easy way off" — nearly every route requires a committing rappel/downclimb descent.

routes.fa: Peak first ascended June 1, 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Jim Crooks, and Dave Lind (this was the West Ridge line, confirmed by Mountain Project and Wikipedia)
routes.face: West Ridge
routes.overview: A 1,500-ft ridge climb of scrambling and low 5th-class rock leading directly to the summit up the peak's classic western skyline; included in Steve Roper and Allen Steck's Fifty Classic Climbs of North America.
routes.beta: Mostly 3rd/4th class scrambling with sustained exposure; the crux is a short steep step ("the Tower") about two-thirds up the ridge, climbed at 5.6 on its left side past a fixed piton. The first two or three pitches are often simul-climbed or soloed by experienced parties.
routes.approach: From Seattle take I-5 north to WA-20 east to Marblemount, turn onto Cascade River Road and park at the pullout opposite Johannesburg Mountain near mile marker 22 (elev. ~3,200 ft). The Boston Basin trail climbs steeply and is badly eroded in places, reaching Boston Basin (~5,700 ft) with designated low/high camps. From high camp, ascend scree slopes and gullies to the obvious snowfield below the West Ridge couloir, then climb the couloir (~40-45° snow early/mid season) to the base of the ridge notch; late season, a rocky gully left of the couloir (the "Cat Scratch Gully") is used instead once snow has melted out.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the West Ridge (or the Cat Scratch Gullies late-season) back toward Boston Basin using a single rope; do not descend the East Ridge/East Ledges unless very familiar with that terrain.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb everything climbable and rappel the steep sections (including the Tower crux) with a single 60m rope, retracing the ascent line back to the couloir/gully and down to Boston Basin. An alternate descent via the East Ridge and NE-face ledges (3rd-4th class) exists but is longer and more complex; most parties simply reverse the West Ridge.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: drive to trailhead, hike in ~2.5-3 mi to Boston Basin high camp (~5,700 ft). Day 2: alpine start, climb West Ridge to summit (5-7 hrs up, 3-5 hrs down from upper basin), return to camp. Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on the lower ridge via downclimbing 4th-class terrain; above the Tower crux, rappel slings/fixed gear allow retreat down the route itself rather than continuing.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time based on your slowest pitch of the day and current/forecast weather — Cascades afternoon thunderstorms and the peak's notoriously difficult, multi-option descent make it important to be off the ridge and back to the notch well before dark or deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the standard window; early-to-mid season parties get firm snow in the couloir (ice axe/crampons needed), while late-season (Aug-Oct) climbers use the drier but looser Cat Scratch Gully. The route is popular and can be congested with parties (and rockfall) on summer weekends.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the ridge; a satellite communicator/inReach is recommended for trip planning and emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack to ~2-3 inches, ice axe and crampons for the couloir, and a single 60m rope suffice for most parties.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack sufficient for 4th/low-5th class climbing with one steeper 5.6 step; natural gear and slings supplement a small cam/nut set at the Tower crux.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light boots (rock shoes not needed)
proTips: Rock shoes aren't necessary — the ridge is blocky with positive holds
Start pre-dawn to beat other parties to the Tower crux and avoid rockfall
Feel free to rappel the Tower crux on descent rather than downclimbing it
watchOut: The Tower crux is the technical and route-finding crux — trend left when the ridge goes vertical to find the fixed piton
Rockfall from parties above on this popular route
Cat Scratch Gully is loose and best belayed, not simul-climbed
knownHazards: Loose rock in the couloir/gully sections
Crevasse hazard on approach glaciers if the Torment-Forbidden traverse is combined
No easy descent off the peak in poor weather
Popular route can have multiple parties and attendant rockfall

pitchDetail.notes: Scree/gully to the snowfield below the couloir
Leads to the ridge notch; Cat Scratch Gully is the late-season alternative
Exposed ridge-crest scrambling
Climb the left side past a fixed piton
Continue along the crest toward the summit block
Final short scramble to the true summit

road.status: Open (paved to ~mile 10, then gravel to road's end); verify current conditions before driving
road.seasonalGate: Subject to seasonal/weather closures (snow, flooding, slides); dates vary year to year — check the NPS road-conditions page
road.driveNote: No RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft; low-clearance sedans not recommended. Boston Basin trailhead pullout is near mile marker 22 opposite Johannesburg Mountain (~3,200 ft), with room for 6-8 cars.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North / North Cascades mountain weather
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate — wet, cloudy winters with heavy snowfall; a drier, more stable window from roughly June through September for climbing
climate.spring: Deep, avalanche-prone snowpack often persists into June; couloir climbing conditions but higher avalanche/rockfall risk
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; afternoon thunderstorms possible, route generally dries out by mid-to-late July
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows; Cat Scratch Gully typically dry and preferred by October before first snow
climate.winter: Heavy snow and road closures; route not normally climbed

access.permit: Overnight camping in Boston Basin requires a wilderness permit reserved via Recreation.gov and picked up in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.passRequired: No park entrance fee; wilderness permit fee applies for overnight stays
access.closures: Cascade River Road can be seasonally gated or closed due to snow, flooding, or slides — check the NPS road-conditions page before driving

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Joe Hieb, May 25, 1958, established the direct East Ridge line (commonly called "East Ridge Direct"); photographed at the time by Ed Cooper. Sources are inconsistent on whether an easier, non-direct original East Ridge line is a truly separate, independently first-ascended route — treat this FA as applying to the commonly climbed direct line.
routes.rappels: 5 rappels via East Ledges (varies by party)
routes.face: East Ridge
routes.overview: The most sustained rock climbing on Forbidden Peak: 6-8 pitches up to 5.8 along a spectacular arete of gendarmes and gaps, dividing the smooth south-face slabs from the crevassed Boston Glacier to the north.
routes.beta: From a notch on the East Ridge, climb the crest and both flanks of the ridge for 6-8 pitches, bypassing several gendarmes on ledges or short rappels into notches; the crux is a well-protected ~40 ft 5.8 section on the last gendarme, followed by easier simul-climbing to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead, hike to upper Boston Basin, then continue up moraines and gullies below the glacier on the east side of the peak to reach the base of the East Ridge notch; the approach gully is loose and becomes progressively looser toward the ridge.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the West Ridge and Cat Scratch Gullies (a "traverse" descent); an alternative is a series of rappels down the East Face/NE ledges back toward the approach gully.
routes.descentText: Two common options: (1) traverse over and down the West Ridge, rappelling the Cat Scratch Gullies back to Boston Basin — more straightforward due to higher traffic and cairned route; or (2) from the summit, make a series of rappels (roughly 3 rappels to a grassy ledge, then additional rappels/scrambling) down East Ledges terrain back to the approach gully — used historically but with looser, less-traveled terrain.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed car-to-car in one long day (~12 hrs) from Boston Basin camp, or as a 2-3 day trip with a basin camp; an alpine start well before dawn is standard to finish rappels in daylight.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ridge is difficult once committed past the first gendarmes; carrying a few extra slings/cord for tat-based rappel anchors at notches between gendarmes is prudent for a bail.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent (whether West Ridge traverse or East Ledges rappels) is long and exposed, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight — parties have been caught finishing rappels after dark.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; snow on the ridge in early season significantly raises the difficulty of the gendarme bypasses, so most parties prefer mid-to-late summer once the ridge is dry.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the ridge; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Cams doubled 0.3-1in with a single 0.2, 2, and 3in; six single and four double-length slings; a GriGri or similar is used by some parties for efficient self-belaying on easier terrain.
routes.proNeeds: A moderate rack emphasizing mid-size cams and extra slings for gendarme-notch anchors; offset nuts reported as unnecessary by at least one party.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the loose gully approach
Bivy gear if camping in Boston Basin
proTips: Build cairns on the loose approach gully in low light to aid navigation on return
Time the climb to finish rappels before dark
The West Ridge/Cat Scratch descent is more straightforward than East Ledges due to higher traffic
watchOut: The crux gendarme pitch often feels harder than its stated grade
Route-finding around gendarmes is non-obvious with minimal traffic/cairns
Loose rock throughout the approach gully, worsening toward the ridge
knownHazards: Crevassed Boston Glacier visible/adjacent to the north side of the ridge
Loose rock on approach and in bypass ledges
Rockfall from parties on the adjacent, more popular West Ridge
Wet slush/snow on descent in early season

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed ridge crest between the first two gendarmes
Belayed climb up the first major gendarme
Second gendarme bypassed via a ~50m traverse on the right (south) side
Crux — roughly 40 ft of sustained climbing on the last gendarme
Simul-climbing to the summit

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.6; Mountain Project ("East Ridge Direct"), SummitPost, The Mountaineers, and multiple independent trip reports consistently rate the crux at 5.7-5.8, so rockGrade was corrected to 5.7-5.8. This route is widely known as "East Ridge Direct" (Fred Beckey & Joe Hieb, May 25, 1958); sources disagree on whether a separate, easier, non-direct "East Ridge" also exists as an independently first-ascended line, so treat the FA/grade here as describing the commonly climbed direct line.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jack Schwabland, and Don Wilde, 1952
routes.face: North Ridge
routes.overview: A long, scenic, and relatively uncrowded alternative to the West and East Ridges, crossing two major glaciers on the approach before a short, steep rock crux leads to a sustained final ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Boston-Forbidden col, scramble to a short steep left-facing corner (the 5.6 crux), then continue along the ridge crest past a wildly exposed bivy platform; above ~8,400 ft the ridge steepens with noticeably better rock quality to the summit block.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead (~3,200 ft), hike to Boston Basin (~5,700 ft), then continue up onto and across the Quien Sabe Glacier; a gully leads to a rappel station accessing the Boston Glacier, which is crossed to reach the Boston-Forbidden col (bivy sites at ~7,600 ft). Total elevation gain on the approach and route is nearly 5,000 ft.
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge (downclimb/rappel) or via East Ledges (roughly 400-500 ft of exposed terrain requiring rappels).
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the West Ridge, downclimbing and rappelling with a single rope back toward Boston Basin. The East Ledges option involves ~400-500 ft of exposed 3rd/4th-class terrain and rappels down the east side, used less often.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: day 1 approach to Boston Basin or a col bivy; day 2 cross the glaciers, climb the ridge, and descend; day 3 hike out if needed.
routes.bail: Retreat from the lower ridge back across the glaciers to Boston Basin is possible but involves reversing the rappel into/traverse of the Boston Glacier — a roped, crevasse-aware retreat, not a quick bail.
routes.turnaround: Given the long glacier approach and crevasse exposure, turn around early enough to recross the Quien Sabe/Boston Glaciers and reach camp before dark or before glacier bridges soften further in the afternoon sun.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, when the Quien Sabe and Boston Glacier crossings are most consolidated and crevasse bridges most predictable; the route sees far less traffic than the West or East Ridges.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the glaciers or ridge; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Crampons, ice axe, and full crevasse-rescue gear for the glacier crossings, plus a light alpine rock rack and a 60m rope for the ridge; ice screws are useful late-season if descending or climbing the NW-Face-of-North-Ridge variation.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel gear (crevasse rescue kit) is as important as the rock rack given two significant glacier crossings.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a col camp
Bear canister for Boston Basin camps
proTips: Expect solitude compared to the West Ridge or East Ridge Direct, which draw most parties
The rock quality noticeably improves above 8,400 ft
watchOut: Rappel into the Boston Glacier from the approach gully is committing
Crevasse hazard crossing both the Quien Sabe and Boston Glaciers
Exposed bivy platform on the ridge crest
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on both approach glaciers
Loose rock near the Boston-Forbidden col
Long, remote approach with limited traffic for a rescue

pitchDetail.notes: Cross Quien Sabe Glacier, rappel into and cross the Boston Glacier to the Boston-Forbidden col
Short, steep left-facing corner from the col
Ridge crest past a wildly exposed bivy platform
Above ~8,400 ft, steeper terrain on notably better rock to the summit block

routes.corrections: Given data listed rockGrade 5.8; both Mountain Project's dedicated North Ridge route page and an independent trip-report title (spokalpine.com, "Forbidden Peak – North Ridge (III 5.6)") agree on 5.6, so rockGrade was corrected to 5.6. gainFt of ~5,000 ft is a directly sourced approximate figure from Mountain Project's route description ("nearly 5000ft of elevation"), not the simple trailhead-to-summit arithmetic used for the other routes.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Ed Cooper, July 15, 1959
routes.face: Northwest Face
routes.overview: Called "an exceptional climb of purity" by Fred Beckey and often cited as Forbidden's best route: 1,200 ft over 7 pitches of steep, sustained rock and mixed climbing straight up the peak's northwest aspect.
routes.beta: Solo or simul-climb steep, somewhat loose 4th-to-low-5th-class rock for about 500 ft to a knife-edge section, traverse it at 5.6, then tackle the 5.8 crux (stemming and crack climbing) while staying on the ridge line; the upper face features a wide chimney where the rock steepens before easing to low-5th/5.6 climbing to the summit.
routes.approach: Two options: via Sharkfin Col and the Boston Glacier/North Ridge approach (6-7 hours, less avalanche/rockfall-hazardous, one rope needed), or via the West Ridge notch (~4 hours, shorter but shares terrain with the crowded West Ridge).
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge or via East Ledges.
routes.descentText: Most parties downclimb/rappel the West Ridge with a single 60m rope back to Boston Basin; the East Ledges option (exposed 3rd/4th class with rappels) is a secondary choice.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip with a Boston Basin camp: day 1 approach, day 2 climb via Sharkfin Col or West Ridge notch and descend the West Ridge, day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat from the lower two-thirds of the face (below the crux) is feasible by downclimbing/rappelling the same line; above the crux, continuing to the summit and descending the West Ridge is generally more practical than reversing the face.
routes.turnaround: Given the sustained, technical nature of the face and the need to then descend the separate West Ridge, plan a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight for both the remaining pitches and the full descent.
routes.bestSeason: June through August is favored, when the face's mixed snow/ice patches are still climbable but before the route melts out into looser late-season conditions; some parties climb into September in good years.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: A double rack from 0.3 to 2 inches, a single set of nuts, and a light axe and crampons for the mixed snow/ice sections.
routes.proNeeds: Emphasis on mid-size cams (doubled) for the sustained 5.6-5.8 climbing, plus light ice gear for any residual snow/ice patches on the face.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the Sharkfin Col or West Ridge notch approach
Bivy gear for Boston Basin camp
proTips: Fred Beckey called it "an exceptional climb of purity" and, per some guides, Forbidden's best route
The Sharkfin Col approach is longer but less hazardous than the West Ridge notch approach
watchOut: Somewhat loose rock on the initial 500 ft below the knife-edge
Sustained 5.6-5.8 climbing with limited bail options mid-face
Two different approach options each with their own routefinding/glacier hazards
knownHazards: Loose rock on lower face
Crevasse hazard if approaching via Sharkfin Col/Boston Glacier
Mixed snow/ice conditions vary significantly by season

pitchDetail.notes: ~500 ft solo/simul-climbing, somewhat loose, to a knife-edge
Knife-edge ridge traverse
Crux — stemming and crack climbing while maintaining the ridge line
Wide chimney where the rock steepens
Continue to the summit

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's route page lists the Northwest Face as "Overall Grade III," whereas the given data (and some published guide references) list it as Grade IV; the discrepancy could not be resolved with certainty from the sources checked, so the given Grade IV / alpineGrade D was retained rather than overwritten on a single conflicting source.

routes.fa: Edward (Ed) Cooper and Stuart Ferguson, September 15, 1961 (per American Alpine Club Publications)
routes.face: Northeast Face
routes.overview: A rarely repeated mixed rock-and-ice line straight up the northeast face from the Boston Glacier: roughly 300 ft of Grade III rock, a 300-ft, 50° ice patch, and a final 600 ft of steep but easier upslabbing rock to the top of the face.
routes.beta: From the Boston Glacier, the original party climbed the rock just below and to the left of a large ice patch on the lower face (about 300 ft, Grade III, minimal pitons), crossed the 300-ft, 50°-angle ice patch, then continued up 600 ft of steep terrain made easier by the upslab character of the rock.
routes.approach: Start via the Boston Mine trail to the Boston Glacier, cross Sharkfin Col, rappel into the bergschrund and ascend a short vertical ice wall to reach the main glacier, then traverse west across the Boston Glacier to the base of the face.
routes.descent: Descend via the peak's normal route (West Ridge).
routes.descentText: The first-ascent party descended via Forbidden's established normal route (the West Ridge) after topping out the face.
routes.bail: Below the ice patch, retreat down the rock section is possible; once committed to the ice and upper rock, continuing to the summit and descending the West Ridge is the more practical option given the remote, glacier-guarded base of the route.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late summer (roughly July-September), when the Boston Glacier approach and bergschrund crossing are most passable and the face's snow/ice patch is climbable without being excessively unstable.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the Boston Glacier or face; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.proNeeds: A mixed rack combining rock protection for the lower Grade III rock and upper upslab section with ice screws for the 300-ft ice patch.
whatToBring: Full glacier travel/crevasse rescue kit for the Boston Glacier approach
watchOut: Rappelling into and climbing out of the bergschrund at the start of the glacier crossing is a committing move
This is described by some sources as one of the least-traveled named lines on the peak — expect minimal beta/traffic
knownHazards: Crevassed Boston Glacier approach
Bergschrund crossing
Objective rockfall/icefall potential on a rarely repeated face route
Remote location complicates any rescue

pitchDetail.notes: ~300 ft, minimal pitons required, just left of a large ice patch
~600 ft to the top of the face

routes.corrections: Note for future review: at least one source (AAC Publications) states that the term "northeast face route" is sometimes used colloquially for a traverse of the upper East Ridge face used to bypass gendarmes, rather than for this standalone 1961 Cooper-Ferguson technical face route — there may be two different things sharing the "northeast face" name. routeFt (1200) is a computed sum of the three segment lengths quoted in the AAC first-ascent account (300+300+600 ft), not a single directly stated total.

Could not independently verify this as a distinct, named IV/5.9 route in any of the required primary sources. Mountain Project's Forbidden Peak area page explicitly lists only 4 established routes (West Ridge, North Ridge, East Ridge Direct, Northwest Face) — no separate "East Face" or "Catscratch" route appears there, on SummitPost, or in AAC Publications. Every source found (SummitPost, Climber Kyle, Peakbagger ascent logs, Cascade Climbers) describes "Cat Scratch Gully/Gullies" as a late-season scrambling variation used to access the West Ridge (not a standalone technical line, and not rated IV/5.9). The given grade fields were left as-is per the no-overwrite-without-better-source rule, but this route entry should be manually reviewed — it may be a data-entry conflation of two unrelated things (an unconfirmed "East Face" line and the West Ridge's Cat Scratch Gully approach variation), or may correspond to an obscure/unpublished line not indexed by the sources checked.

blurb: Eldorado Peak (8,872 ft / 8,868 ft on older maps) is a remote Cretaceous granodiorite peak in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, WA, rising above the Cascade River roughly 27 miles east of Concrete at the edge of the Inspiration-McAllister-Klawatti icefield — one of the largest contiguous non-volcanic ice masses in the Lower 48. Fred Beckey called it the "Queen of the Cascade River," and it is famous for its knife-edge, once-glaciated summit ridge, now largely melted to bare rock as its historic summit ice cap has receded. The peak was first ascended August 27, 1933 via the East Ridge by Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Wilson, and Arthur Winder.

routes.fa: Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Wilson, Arthur Winder — August 27, 1933 (also the peak's first ascent)
objHaz: Rockfall on the approach gully/talus in early season
High/fast Cascade River crossing, especially in the afternoon
routes.season: Late April–September (best June–August); a well-known spring ski descent line into June
routes.face: East Ridge / Inspiration Glacier
routes.overview: The standard route: a moderate glacier climb up Eldorado's East Ridge via the Inspiration Glacier, finishing on an airy, often-corniced knife-edge summit ridge.
routes.beta: From the Eldorado Creek trailhead, a steep boot trail gains a talus basin near 4,000 ft; open slopes lead to a high camp between roughly 5,400 and 7,800 ft near the Eldorado Glacier margin. From camp, rope up and cross the Eldorado and Inspiration Glaciers, gain 'the Gap' on the lower East Ridge, then follow the exposed snow crest to the small summit.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road (FR 15) about 20 miles from Marblemount to the Eldorado Creek trailhead (2,100 ft; privy, no fee). Cross the Cascade River on a log crossing roughly 90 ft downstream of the parking area, pick up the climbers' trail heading upstream/east, climb steeply through timber to a boulder field, cross Eldorado Creek, and continue up talus into the open alpine basin toward a high camp near the Eldorado Glacier (5,600–7,800 ft depending on snowline/season).
routes.descent: Descend the East Ridge and Inspiration/Eldorado Glaciers back to high camp, then retrace the approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the knife-edge ridge to 'the Gap,' then descend the Inspiration and Eldorado Glaciers on the ascent track, watching for softening snow bridges and crevasses in the afternoon. From camp, follow the boot trail down through the boulder field and timber; the Cascade River crossing is more difficult in the afternoon when meltwater is highest. The route is also a popular spring ski descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Eldorado Creek TH (2,100 ft), hike ~4 mi / ~5,300 ft gain to high camp (5,600–7,800 ft). Day 2: Alpine start, rope up, cross the Eldorado/Inspiration Glaciers to the East Ridge, climb the knife-edge to the 8,872 ft summit (3–5 hrs up), return to camp. Day 3: Hike out. Strong parties complete it car-to-car in one long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the glacier and ridge to high camp; the knife-edge crux can be downclimbed or short-pitched in poor conditions. In whiteout or high avalanche hazard, retreat to camp rather than push the corniced summit ridge.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before leaving high camp based on weather, glacier snow-bridge condition, and party pace — the glacier crossing and exposed, sometimes-corniced summit ridge become more hazardous as the day warms and if visibility drops.
routes.bestSeason: Late May–September is the standard climbing window, with best conditions June–August. The East Ridge/Inspiration Glacier is also one of the 'Fifty Classic Ski Descents of North America,' typically skied April–June before the ridge melts out and becomes crevassed and exposed.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Eldorado Creek basin or on the glacier; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: harness, crampons, mountaineering axe, 2–3 snow pickets per rope team, crevasse-rescue gear (prusiks or mechanical ascenders, pulley, cordelette), and a rope per team.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is almost entirely glacier-travel/crevasse-rescue gear plus pickets on the steeper ridge sections; no rock rack is typically needed on the standard line.
whatToBring: Overnight/bivy gear for a 2–3 day itinerary
Water treatment (no reliable water above the boulder field)
proTips: Cross the Cascade River early before afternoon meltwater raises the level
The knife-edge is most solid and least corniced by mid-to-late summer
A strong party can go car-to-summit-to-car in under 24 hours, though most take 2–3 days
As a ski line, go before it melts to bare ice/rock in high summer
watchOut: Crevasses opening on the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier as the season warms
Cornices along the north side of the summit ridge
Afternoon whiteout/thunderstorm buildup common in the North Cascades
High, fast Cascade River crossing, especially after rain or on warm afternoons
knownHazards: Roped glacier travel with real crevasse-fall potential
Rockfall in the approach gully/talus during early-season melt-out
Exposed, sometimes corniced knife-edge summit ridge
Wet-snow avalanche potential during warm, high-freezing-level periods

pitchDetail.notes: Steep timber then talus to ~4,000 ft
Open basin to Eldorado Glacier margin, 5,400–7,800 ft
Watch crevasses; gain 'the Gap' on the lower East Ridge
Exposed, often-corniced knife-edge finish

routes.fa: Dan Cauthorn and Bill Pilling (year not confirmed by available sources)
routes.rappels: 1-2 rappels of ~33m from a fixed piton anchor at the Eldorado–Dean's Tower notch, using a 70m rope
objHaz: Extreme icefall/rockfall funnel in the couloir
Crevasses on the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier approach
Complex glacier route-finding to reach the notch
routes.season: Best mid-October–early November when neve/ice forms; conditions vary widely (AI2 to WI4/M4) by year
routes.face: Northwest Ice Couloir, Marble Creek Cirque side of the peak
routes.overview: A sustained mixed/ice line up the steep northwest couloir, reached via a long glacier approach through the Marble Creek Cirque; harder and more serious than the East Ridge, joining the upper East Ridge near the summit.
routes.beta: Approach via the East Ridge glacier track to a rappel notch between Eldorado Peak and Dean's Tower (~8,100 ft), rappel into the west-facing cirque, then climb the couloir (~1,000 ft, 7,800–8,800 ft) on snow, neve, or ice depending on conditions — steps to 70–80° — to join the upper East Ridge.
routes.approach: Same lower approach as the East Ridge (Eldorado Creek trailhead, boulder field, Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier) to about 8,100 ft on the East Ridge, then traverse north to the notch/rappel station between Eldorado and Dean's Tower, rappel (~33m) into the cirque, and traverse to the base of the couloir.
routes.descent: Descend the standard East Ridge route
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the East Ridge/Inspiration Glacier line back to camp and out via the standard approach trail; the couloir itself is not used for descent.
routes.bail: Retreat down the couloir is difficult once committed on steep ice; the most viable bail is to continue to the notch/East Ridge and descend the standard route, or rappel the couloir on ice-screw/picket anchors if gear allows.
routes.turnaround: Given the couloir's icefall funnel and sustained 70–80° climbing, set a conservative turnaround and avoid being in the couloir once sun hits the face or with a party climbing above.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-October through early November is most cited for good neve/ice before winter storms bury the route; considered a rare, condition-dependent 'testpiece' out of shape most of the year.
routes.comms: No cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: 6–8 ice screws, 2–3 snow pickets, a light rock rack (nuts, small cams/tricams) for mixed sections, 70m rope for the notch rappel, technical ice tools and crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws are primary protection in the couloir; supplement with pickets on lower-angle snow and occasional rock pieces where ice thins over rock.
whatToBring: Extra ice screws beyond the minimum
proTips: Climb in October when ice is forming and before deep winter snow buries the line
Start pre-dawn to be through the couloir before warming loosens ice from above
Bring more screws than expected — parties report placing 7+
watchOut: Extreme icefall funnel — falling ice can shower parties below
Complex glacier route-finding to reach the notch
Sustained 70–80° ice/mixed climbing is serious for the grade
knownHazards: Icefall/rockfall funneled into the couloir from above
Crevasse hazard on the glacier approach
Rappel from a fixed piton anchor of variable condition at the notch

pitchDetail.notes: Via East Ridge route to ~8,100 ft, traverse to notch
Piton anchor at Eldorado–Dean's Tower col
~1,000 ft; screws placed throughout
Joins upper East Ridge near summit

routes.corrections: Could not verify a distinct 'East Face' route on Eldorado Peak separate from the East Ridge or the Northeast Face after checking mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, peakbagger.com, cascadeclimbers.com (First Ascents Wiki + trip reports), and general web search. Mountain Project's Eldorado Peak area page lists only East Ridge, Northwest Ice Couloir, and West Arete as named routes on the main peak; SummitPost and Mountaineers.org document a 'Northeast Face' (Grade II+/III, 50-60° snow/ice) but no separate 'East Face.' This entry's given grades (III, 5.7 rock, AI2 ice, D) may be a duplicate/mislabeling of the Northeast Face, or an obscure/unrecorded line — recommend verifying against a primary guidebook (e.g., Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) before publishing route-specific stats. Left technical/route-specific fields null rather than guess; filled only peak-wide access/road/climate/emergency facts that apply regardless of exact line.

objHaz: Icefall/rockfall potential on the steep face
Highly condition-dependent: can be firm neve, alpine ice, or thin/mixed
routes.season: Best late September-mid October, before major snowstorms
routes.face: Northeast Face, climbers' right of the East Ridge
routes.overview: A steeper snow/ice alternative to the East Ridge, climbing the face directly right of the standard route with running-belay protection, finishing at the same corniced summit ridge.
routes.beta: From the East Ridge glacier approach, at roughly 7,500-8,200 ft leave the standard track and angle up and right beneath a rock band on 50-60° snow/ice, protected with a running belay of pickets and ice screws, then join the summit knife-edge near the top of the East Ridge.
routes.approach: Same as the East Ridge: Eldorado Creek trailhead (2,100 ft) via Cascade River Road, boulder field, Eldorado Creek basin, and the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier to around 7,500-8,200 ft, where the Northeast Face angles off climbers' right of the standard glacier track.
routes.descent: Descend the standard East Ridge route
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the East Ridge/Inspiration Glacier line (not the face) back to camp and out the approach trail.
routes.bail: Retreat by downclimbing the running-belayed snow/ice back to the East Ridge glacier track; this can be slow and exposed in poor conditions, so assess early.
routes.turnaround: Because the route depends on firm neve/ice and involves sustained 50-60° terrain above a rock band with only intermittent protection, set a conservative turnaround and avoid the face if it has gone soft, thin, or wet.
routes.bestSeason: Late September through mid-October, when the face has consolidated into firm neve/ice before winter storms bury or destabilize it — described as best 'before the first snow storms arrive.'
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A handful of ice screws and 2-4 snow pickets for running-belay protection on the face, plus a standard glacier/mountaineering rack for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is placed as a running belay (not full pitches) using pickets on snow and screws where the face has firmed to ice; not consistently protectable in soft or variable conditions.
whatToBring: Extra pickets/screws for the running belay
proTips: Climb in early fall (late Sept-Oct) for the firmest, most protectable conditions
Scope the rock band from the glacier beforehand to pick the best line up and right
watchOut: Face condition is highly variable — soft/unconsolidated outside the early-fall window
Rock band above requires careful route-finding to avoid loose rock
Significant exposure given sparse, running-belay-only protection
knownHazards: Sustained 50-60° snow/ice climbing with only intermittent protection
Rockfall potential near the rock band
Same glacier/crevasse and corniced-ridge hazards as the East Ridge approach

pitchDetail.notes: Running belay with pickets and screws under a rock band

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15 / Skagit County Road 17)
road.status: Paved to gravel; gravel section prone to washouts
road.seasonalGate: Gated at Eldorado Creek (MP 20) in late fall through spring
road.driveNote: Last ~10 miles are gravel and wash out most years; check road status before driving

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park (nearby NWAC Cascade Pass zone)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short summer dry window typical of the western Cascade crest
climate.spring: Face often still buried/unstable
climate.summer: Often too soft/rotten to protect well
climate.fall: Prime season — face firms to neve/ice, especially late Sept-Oct
climate.winter: Buried; avalanche hazard high

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: No trailhead/parking fee
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness permit required from the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Approved food canister required Jun 1-Nov 15 for camping; blue bags mandatory for human waste

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start (parties reported waking and roping up before sunrise)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County 911 (emergency: 911; non-emergency: (360) 428-3211)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, (360) 854-7245

routes.corrections: Given data implies Grade IV (via routes.grade context of 'Grade IV'), but multiple reliable, cross-checked sources (Mountaineers.org: 'Grade III snow or ice climb'; Northwest Mountain School: 'Grade II+, 50 snow and ice'; SummitPost route page and trip-report cross-checks: 'several pitches of snow and ice to 60 degrees') consistently describe this as Grade III (or II+), not IV — corrected commitment to 'III'. Trip reports (Mountaineers.org Oct 2020 climb) describe the technical crux as a running-belay of 2 pickets + 2 screws on ~50-60 snow/ice rather than sustained roped ice pitches, which reads more moderate than the given 'D' alpine grade / AI3 ice rating; flagging this discrepancy for review since no source gives an explicit French-scale or formal AI-grade rating to justify overriding those two fields outright.

blurb: Johannesburg Mountain (8,200 ft) rises directly above the Cascade Pass trailhead in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County — its dramatic granite/gneiss Northeast Face drops roughly 5,000 feet in under a mile, one of the steepest big walls in the range. First climbed July 26, 1938 by Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray Clough and Tom Myers via the Cascade-Johannesburg couloir and East Ridge during the famed Ptarmigan Traverse, it is listed among Dallas Kloke's "Washington Difficult Ten" and is notorious even on its easiest line for loose rock, hanging glaciers, and long, complex, committing descents.

routes.overview: A long, sustained line up the buttress/rib that splits the lower Northeast Face, mixing thousands of feet of steep brush, heather and 3rd/4th-class rock with a short band of true 5.8 rock, a knife-edged snow arête, and a final ice pitch below the summit.
routes.beta: Historically climbed as the 'direct route' up the Northeast Rib of the North Face — first done by David Harrah and Tom Miller in 1951. Modern parties climbing the fuller/harder version of the same buttress rate it Grade V, 5.8, AI2: a short approach leads to loose lower slabs, then thousands of feet of near-vertical vegetation (alder, turf, heather), a 5.5-5.8 rock band to a ~7,100 ft bivy, an exposed snow arête, and a short ice pitch to the summit ridge.
routes.fa: David Harrah & Tom Miller, July 1951 — first ascent of the 'direct route' up the Northeast Rib of the North Face (published by Harrah in the 1952 American Alpine Journal).
routes.rappels: Up to ~7 rappels down the East Ridge ledge/gully/fin system to the Cascade-Johannesburg col (or descend via Doug's Direct over Mixup Peak's North Ridge).
objHaz: Rockfall from the Sill Glacier and hanging ice above the face
Extremely loose rock on the lower slabs and rib
Unreliable vegetation (alder, turf, heather) used as holds/anchors
Crevasses and bergschrunds if descending via the CJ couloir
routes.face: Northeast Face / Northeast Buttress-Rib (the 1951 'direct route' line)
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass trailhead (3,600 ft, end of Cascade River Road, 23 miles from Marblemount), walk back down the road about a quarter mile, then drop off the roadbed down a dirt embankment to the Cascade River, ford or rock-hop it, push through brush, and cross the terminal moraine/snowfield to reach the base of the buttress at roughly 3,500 ft — directly across from the parking lot. A short approach (30-45 minutes) with no maintained trail.
routes.descent: East Ridge (up to 7 rappels) down to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, then the CJ couloir; or the longer 'Doug's Direct' via Mixup Peak's North Ridge.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the East Ridge: downclimb and rappel (up to ~7 rappels) through a maze of ledges, fins and gullies to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, then descend the roughly 3,500-ft CJ couloir on snow/ice, negotiating multiple crevasse and bergschrund crossings back to the Cascade River and trailhead. An alternative used by many parties is 'Doug's Direct': traverse under Cascade Peak and the Triplets, then descend Mixup Peak's North Ridge (multiple rappels) to the Cache Glacier and the Cascade Pass trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike/approach to base (30-45 min), climb ~8-12 hours of brush/rock to the ~7,100 ft bivy ledge below the snow arête. Day 2: 1-3 hours to the summit, then 3-6 hours descending via the East Ridge/CJ couloir or Doug's Direct back to the trailhead. Strong parties occasionally push it in a single long day.
routes.bail: Below the bivy ledge, retreat is difficult — the lower buttress has little natural protection or an established rappel line, so most parties who commit past the initial slabs continue upward rather than reverse. If turned back very low on route, downclimb the ascent gully back to the river crossing. Above the bivy, the only realistic option is to continue to the summit and descend via the East Ridge/CJ couloir.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, technical, and non-trivial descent (multiple rappels down loose terrain followed by a crevassed couloir), climbers should plan to be off the summit with ample daylight remaining rather than push for the top late in the day; NPS rangers note Johannesburg's complexity is severe enough that they allow climbers extra overdue time before launching search efforts, which is not a substitute for a conservative turnaround decision.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once the lower brush/heather has dried out and the snow arête/ice pitch have consolidated; July-August is the usual window. Very early season leaves excessive snow on the lower slabs, while late season can strip protective snow from the arête.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the face or at the trailhead; North Cascades NP explicitly advises climbers here to be self-sufficient and not rely on personal locator/cell devices for rescue (nps.gov climb-safely guidance).
routes.detailedRack: A single light rock rack (small cams/nuts to about a #2 Camelot) plus a few knifeblade pitons covers the moderate 5.5-5.8 rock band; ice protection (3 screws, 1-2 snow pickets) covers the snow arête and the short ice pitch near the top.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed rock/snow/ice protection in modest quantities — this is a route where speed and simul-climbing on marginal gear matter more than a heavy rack.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light boots for the brush/heather section
Bivy gear (most parties bivy near 7,100 ft)
Headlamp for a pre-dawn start or late finish
proTips: Start pre-dawn so the lower brush/heather is climbed before the day heats up and so you're off the exposed snow arête before afternoon softening.
Carry only a small rack to about a #2 Camelot plus a few knifeblades — big gear is dead weight on this route.
Much of the heather section is simul-climbed with protection placed only every ~200 feet; be comfortable with that style before committing.
watchOut: Rock on the lower slabs and throughout the bushwhack is notoriously loose and unreliable — don't trust vegetation as protection.
The final ~200-ft ice pitch varies year to year from a walk-up to genuinely steep water ice; bring screws regardless of forecast conditions.
knownHazards: Rockfall and icefall off the hanging Sill Glacier onto the Northeast Face
Crevasses and bergschrunds on the CJ couloir descent option
Extremely loose rock on the lower buttress/rib
Steep, exposed heather and turf pitches that are treacherous when wet

pitchDetail.notes: Steep snow to the base of slabs, then work across/up past waterfalls; minimal gear placements.
Trend right toward the ridge through heavy brush/alder; hand-over-hand on vegetation.
Simul-climbed by many parties, marginal pro roughly every 200 ft; ice axe/crampons help.
Traverse left into a chimney system leading to the ~7,100 ft bivy site.
Drops off steeply on both sides; a highlight and crux of exposure.
Conditions vary year to year from easy snow to sustained water ice.

climate.typical: Wet, snowy North Cascades climate with a short reliable climbing window (roughly July-August)
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack persisting on shaded terrain into June
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Aug) once brush and heather are snow-free
climate.fall: Early snow can return by late September; shortening daylight
climate.winter: Road gated, trail unmaintained, avalanche terrain throughout; ascents are rare (e.g., the Feb 1985 winter ascent of the neighboring Northeast Rib)

routes.corrections: Sources use 'Northeast Buttress,' 'Northeast Rib,' and '1951 Route' interchangeably for this Northeast Face line. The documented first ascent (Harrah & Miller, 1951) is for the original moderate rib; the V/5.8/AI2 grade cited here (matching the given Grade IV-V, 5.8) reflects the fuller, harder version of the same buttress climbed by later parties. No separate first-ascent record specific to a distinctly-named 'Northeast Buttress' (as opposed to the Northeast Rib) was found — treat the 1951 FA as for the same broad line.

routes.overview: The mountain's original and most-traveled line: a steep 3,400-ft snow/ice couloir climbed to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, followed by a broken East Ridge of rock pitches, gullies and ribs to the summit.
routes.beta: First climbed July 26, 1938 by Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray Clough and Tom Myers during the Ptarmigan Traverse — still the standard/easiest way up Johannesburg, though it is a serious glacier/couloir climb, not a walk-up. The couloir ascends 30-50° with a tight, crevassed bottleneck and an occasional water-ice step (AI2); above the col, the East Ridge follows a mossy, broken gneiss rib/gully system with a knife-edge traverse to the summit.
routes.fa: R. W. Clough, W. Cox, C. T. Bressler & T. Myers — first ascent of Johannesburg Mountain, July 26, 1938, via the Cascade-Johannesburg couloir and the south side of the East Ridge (Ptarmigan Traverse party).
routes.rappels: ~3 rappels (60m rope) down the East Ridge gully/rib system back to the Cascade-Johannesburg col.
objHaz: Wall-to-wall crevasses in the upper couloir
Rock/icefall from the Sill Glacier threatening the lower couloir
Water-ice sections that appear as the couloir dries out
Loose gneiss on the East Ridge rock pitches
routes.face: Cascade-Johannesburg col / East Ridge
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass trailhead, descend the steep slope from the road's hairpin turn down to and across the Cascade River, then work up through brushy talus for roughly 45-50 minutes to the base of the avalanche fan/terminal moraine at about 3,300-3,400 ft, directly below the Cascade-Johannesburg couloir.
routes.descent: Reverse the East Ridge (~3 rappels) to the col, then downclimb/descend the couloir back to the approach talus and trailhead.
routes.descentText: Reverse the East Ridge with roughly three rappels (60m rope) down the gully/rib system back to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, then descend the couloir itself with ice axe and crampons, negotiating crevasse crossings and any exposed water-ice sections, exiting onto the avalanche fan and retracing the approach back to the Cascade River and trailhead. A faint climber's trail on the south side of the creek avoids the worst brush on the way out.
routes.itinerary: Strong parties: alpine start, ~50 min approach, ~2.5 hrs up the couloir to the col, ~2.5 hrs col to summit via the East Ridge, then ~2.5 hrs summit to col and ~4 hrs col to car on descent — roughly a 12-hour car-to-car day. Many parties instead bivy at the col or on the ridge and split the climb over two days.
routes.bail: The couloir itself is the easiest bail option: downclimb or rappel back down the same snow/ice line used on the ascent. On the East Ridge above the col, retreat to the col and descend the couloir; do not attempt to descend the ridge's north side.
routes.turnaround: Because the couloir becomes progressively more rockfall- and icefall-prone as the day warms, plan to be through the couloir (both directions) during the cooler morning/night hours rather than climbing or descending it in the afternoon heat.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; cool overnight temperatures help stabilize the snow and crevasse bridges. As the season progresses and the Sill Glacier breaks up, the couloir becomes discontinuous, icier, and more rockfall-prone, so early-to-mid season with a consolidated snowpack is generally preferred.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on-route or at the trailhead; North Cascades NP advises climbers to be self-sufficient rather than rely on personal locator/cell devices for rescue.
routes.detailedRack: Snow and ice protection dominates the rack — ice screws and pickets for the couloir; a rock rack is essentially unnecessary since the East Ridge's gneiss is loose and rarely takes gear, and many parties climb the ridge unroped or simul-climbed.
routes.proNeeds: Primarily snow/ice protection (screws + pickets) for the couloir's bottleneck and any water-ice sections; minimal rock gear needed above the col.
gear: Ice axe (one tool per person for technical sections)
whatToBring: Full Gore-Tex shell for weather contingency
Rock shoes/rack are generally unnecessary — protection on the ridge is poor and rarely used
proTips: Climb the couloir at night or in the early morning while the snow is still frozen, and aim to be through it (both up and down) before afternoon warming loosens rock and ice.
The crux bottleneck (roughly 300 vertical feet, 20-50 ft wide, 40°+) is the spot most likely to need a belayed pitch — carry screws even if the forecast looks mild.
watchOut: As the season progresses and the Sill Glacier breaks up, the couloir becomes discontinuous, icier, and considerably more rockfall-prone.
East Ridge rock quality varies gully to gully — the first gully above the col has the best rock; others are loose, mossy gneiss.
knownHazards: Direct rock/icefall exposure from the Sill Glacier for nearly the entire couloir
Wall-to-wall crevasses in the upper couloir requiring careful snow-bridge assessment
Loose gneiss on the East Ridge rock pitches
Sustained, nearly continuous exposure with few safe rest stops

pitchDetail.notes: Ascends from the avalanche-fan base; crevasses begin to appear.
~300 vertical ft, only 20-50 ft wide; water ice near the top may require a belay.
Multiple crevasse crossings; snow bridges vary by season and time of day.
Broken gneiss rock pitches, gullies and ribs; first gully has the best rock quality.
Significant exposure; a two-person bivy site sits about 30 minutes from the summit.
