blurb: Primus Peak (8,508 ft per Mountain Project/Wikipedia/CascadeClimbers, though some sources list 8,501 ft) is a remote, glaciated summit in North Cascades National Park, sitting at the north end of the Eldorado Icecap between the Borealis Glacier (northeast) and North Klawatti Glacier (south), immediately west of Tricouni Peak. Composed of Skagit Gneiss, it is part of the Inspiration/Klawatti/McAllister ice-cap massif alongside Eldorado, Klawatti, Austera, and Tricouni peaks, and is reached only via long glacier approaches — from Thunder Creek to the north or the Eldorado Icecap to the south — making the approach, not the climbing, the peak's defining challenge.

routes.face: East ridge/slopes above the Borealis Glacier (Lucky Pass side)
routes.overview: The standard route to Primus Peak's summit climbs its east side: a long approach (via Thunder Creek and Lucky Ridge from the north, or the Eldorado Icecap from the south) gains the Borealis Glacier, crosses to the Primus-Tricouni col ('Lucky Pass'), and finishes up class 2-3 terrain on the east ridge/upper slopes to the summit.
routes.beta: Primus Peak's difficulty lies almost entirely in its remoteness and glacier approach rather than technical climbing. Mountain Project rates the standard line (there called the 'East Slope') 3rd class; multiple trip reports independently describe class 2-3 scrambling and moderate snow once above Lucky Pass. Most parties treat it as a 3-day trip with a high camp above Borealis Lake. A harder 'North Ridge' variant (5.6 trad, loose and exposed) exists for parties wanting technical climbing.
routes.approach: From Colonial Creek Campground (SR-20, milepost 130, on Diablo Lake), follow the Thunder Creek Trail south roughly 6 miles (~600 ft gain) to the McAllister Camp area (~2,000 ft), fording Thunder Creek near a large logjam — commonly the crux of the whole trip, safest crossed early morning before snowmelt raises the flow. Leave the trail and climb the forested ridge (Lucky Ridge) dropping northwest off Tricouni Peak, gaining roughly 4,000 ft over about 4 miles through dense brush and devil's club, passing two short exposed cliff-band scrambles (~2,300 ft and ~4,250 ft), to a high camp on cliffs above Borealis Lake (~6,000-6,700 ft). From high camp, rope up around 7,000 ft and cross the Borealis Glacier, staying right/east of a central icefall, to the Primus-Tricouni col ('Lucky Pass,' ~7,200 ft). An alternate southern approach climbs Eldorado Creek to Roush Creek Basin and onto the Eldorado Icecap, then follows 7-8 miles of glacier travel (via the Inspiration Glacier and Klawatti Col, ~7,800 ft) north to Lucky Pass; it avoids the Thunder Creek ford but adds significant glacier mileage and is best done early season.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: descend from Lucky Pass back across the Borealis Glacier to high camp, then down Lucky Ridge and back across Thunder Creek to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the east ridge/upper slopes (traversing around the ~8,270 ft false summit) back to Lucky Pass, re-rope for the Borealis Glacier crossing back to high camp, then descend Lucky Ridge — watching for loose rock and rolling logs on the steep off-trail sections and cliff bands — before re-fording Thunder Creek, which runs highest later in the day. There are no fixed rappels on the standard line; parties should be comfortable downclimbing loose, exposed terrain if conditions deteriorate.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead to high camp above Borealis Lake (~10 mi one-way, ~6,000 ft gain). Day 2: High camp - Borealis Glacier - Lucky Pass - east ridge/slopes - summit - back to high camp. Day 3: Hike out to trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the route at any point; from the glacier or upper ridge, downclimb back to high camp above Borealis Lake. No fixed rappel stations exist on the standard line, so parties should be prepared to downclimb loose rock on Lucky Ridge or the upper mountain if weather or conditions force a retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach is roughly 9-10 miles one-way with substantial elevation gain and ends in a creek ford that grows more hazardous later in the day as snowmelt increases flow, parties should set a firm summit turnaround time that leaves enough daylight and margin to re-cross Thunder Creek and descend Lucky Ridge before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. Earlier-season attempts (June) benefit from more continuous snow cover that bridges glacier crevasses and eases the moat crossing onto the Borealis Glacier, but Thunder Creek runs highest and most dangerous during peak snowmelt. Late-season attempts (August-September) have a safer creek ford but more open crevasses and a more broken, sometimes icy, Lucky Pass.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Thunder Creek valley or on the Borealis Glacier/Primus massif; sporadic signal is occasionally reported on higher ridgelines. A satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is strongly advised given the remoteness and multi-day approach.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier rack rather than a rock rack: harness, helmet, ice axe, and crampons (worth carrying even in June — several trip reports found icy sections despite otherwise soft snow); one 30m rope per rope team of 2-3 for the Borealis Glacier crossing, plus a standard crevasse-rescue kit (prusik cords, a picket or two, pulley/ascender). No fixed rock protection is needed on the standard class 2-3 line; parties attempting the harder North Ridge (5.6) should bring a standard trad rack instead.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are glacier-focused rather than rock-focused on the standard route: crevasse-rescue gear (pickets, prusiks) matters far more than a rock rack.
whatToBring: Bear-resistant food storage (Ursack/canister, required in NP backcountry)
Wind-resistant shelter with solid anchoring for high camp
proTips: Cross Thunder Creek early in the morning, before the day's snowmelt raises the flow.
Carry crampons even in early summer — several parties found icy sections on the glacier moat and upper mountain despite otherwise soft snow.

Break the trip into 3 days with a high camp above Borealis Lake; the approach alone (roughly 10 miles and ~6,000 ft one-way to high camp) is too long to combine comfortably with the summit push in a single day.
The southern Eldorado Icecap approach avoids the Thunder Creek ford entirely but adds 7-8 miles of glacier travel and is best done early season while crevasses remain well bridged.
watchOut: Thunder Creek's crossing near the McAllister Camp logjam is powerful and has been flagged by multiple trip reports as the crux of the whole trip.
Two short cliff-band scrambles on Lucky Ridge (~2,300 ft and ~4,250 ft) involve loose, exposed terrain with rockfall risk onto parties below.
An icefall sits in the middle of the Borealis Glacier — stay right/east when crossing toward Lucky Pass.
Cornices can form along Lucky Pass in early season.
This is an active bear and cougar corridor; proper food storage (Ursack/canister) is effectively required.
knownHazards: Glacier crevasse fall on the Borealis Glacier (and Eldorado Icecap on the southern approach).
Rockfall and loose rock on the Lucky Ridge cliff bands and upper mountain.
Swift-current creek-crossing hazard at Thunder Creek, worst during peak snowmelt.
Cornice hazard near Lucky Pass, especially in early season.

pitchDetail.notes: ~6 mi, ~600 ft gain on the Thunder Creek Trail from Colonial Creek Campground; ford Thunder Creek near a logjam.

~4 mi, ~4,000 ft gain up forested Lucky Ridge (below Tricouni Peak) through brush and devil's club, with two short cliff-band scrambles (~2,300 ft and ~4,250 ft), to a bivy above Borealis Lake (~6,000-6,700 ft).
From ~6,700-7,000 ft, rope up and cross the glacier, staying right/east of a central icefall, to the Primus-Tricouni col ('Lucky Pass,' ~7,200 ft).
Scramble/steep snow up the east ridge/slopes, traversing climber's-left around a false summit (~8,270 ft), to the 8,508 ft true summit.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Colonial Creek Campground, milepost 130
road.status: Paved highway to the trailhead; normally open through the Jun-Sep climbing season.
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally between roughly Ross Dam Trailhead (MP134) and Silver Star Gate (MP171), typically mid-November to April/May, due to avalanche danger; Colonial Creek Campground (MP130) is west of that gate. (Note: a March 2026 rockslide near MP130-131 caused an additional temporary closure resolved by late June 2026.)
road.driveNote: Drive SR-20 to milepost 130 at Diablo Lake; park in the lot above the boat ramp on the south side of Colonial Creek Campground and walk through the campground to the Thunder Creek trailhead near the amphitheater.

climate.forecastZone: No dedicated NWAC zone for this peak; nearest avalanche/weather guidance comes from the West Slopes North Cascades / North Cascades Highway NWAC zones and point forecasts from mountain-forecast.com.
climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate: wet, snowy winters and drier, more stable summers, as Pacific weather fronts undergo orographic lift over the range.
climate.spring: Heavy, persistent snowpack; approach and glacier are largely snow-covered, easing some crevasse/moat hazards but keeping Thunder Creek running high.
climate.summer: The Jun-Sep climbing window generally sees the clearest, most stable weather as Pacific high pressure builds, though afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving fronts remain possible.
climate.fall: Shorter days and building fall storms narrow the window; by September glaciers are at their most broken and crevassed.
climate.winter: Effectively inaccessible for climbing due to heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard on the approach ridge; the North Cascades Highway also closes seasonally well east of the trailhead.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness).
access.fees: Backcountry/wilderness permit fee of roughly $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for advance reservations (NPS fee schedule); no fee for day use of the trail itself.
access.permit: An overnight wilderness permit is required, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or reserved in advance via recreation.gov for the relevant cross-country zone (Klawatti/Thunder Creek zones).
access.passRequired: No park entrance pass is required (North Cascades NP has no entrance fee); only the backcountry camping permit is required for overnight trips.
access.closures: SR-20 closes seasonally roughly mid-November through April/May east of Ross Dam Trailhead; Colonial Creek/Thunder Creek Trailhead (MP130) is normally accessible whenever the western section of SR-20 is open.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start (pre-dawn) from high camp on the glacier/summit day, to cross the glacier on firmer snow and return before afternoon warming increases rockfall and creek-flow hazard.

waypoints.name: Thunder Creek Trailhead / Colonial Creek Campground

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Skagit 911 non-emergency (360) 428-3211; Skagit County Sheriff's Office Search and Rescue coordinates backcountry rescues.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III trauma center), Mount Vernon, WA.
emergency.notes: Remote, glaciated national-park backcountry with no road access and no reliable cell coverage; rescue typically requires NPS climbing rangers/SAR and may involve helicopter evacuation. Carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: No primary source found (Mountain Project, SummitPost, CascadeClimbers, Mountaineers.org, WTA, or any trip report checked) documents a Primus Peak route named 'South Ridge' or a 'McAllister Glacier' approach. McAllister Glacier is a real, verified glacier, but it sits in a separate cirque northeast of Dorado Needle used to approach Klawatti Peak — it does not adjoin Primus's summit or its documented routes. The two named routes actually documented for Primus are the standard 'East Slope'/'East Ridge' line (Mountain Project: 3rd class; CascadeClimbers/Mountaineers.org/independent trip reports: class 2-3 scrambling above the Borealis Glacier via Lucky Pass, reachable from Thunder Creek to the north or the Eldorado Icecap to the south) and a harder 'North Ridge' (5.6 trad, loose terrain, per a documented 2016 attempt). The database's listed grade of 'Grade III, Class 4' does not match any sourced grade for a Primus Peak route — the standard line is closer to Class 2-3/Grade I-II. This entry has been treated as the standard climbing route to the summit (the only Primus route with a well-documented approach and line), and the fields above describe that route; the routeName field is left as originally given since routeId/routeName correspondence to a specific undocumented line could not be independently confirmed. Separately, peak elevation is given as 8,501 ft, but Mountain Project, Wikipedia, WTA, and a CascadeClimbers trip-report title all independently give 8,508 ft — a minor (7 ft) likely survey/LIDAR discrepancy worth reconciling at the peak level.

hierarchyNote: Anderson's Thumb is NOT in the North Cascades/Cascades. Multiple independent sources (Mountain Project's "Mt. Anderson/Anderson Massif" area page, GPS 47.72075/-123.33175; two Jim Brisbine trip reports on trailcatjim.com; the Olympic Mountains climbing guide) place it in the OLYMPIC MOUNTAINS, Olympic National Park, Jefferson County — specifically as a subsidiary rock pinnacle near the Anderson Glacier moraine on the flank of Mount Anderson (the Dosewallips/Anderson Pass/Quinault drainage area), a completely separate range from the Cascades. If this peak is currently parented under any North Cascades/Cascades region node in the hierarchy, that parent assignment is geographically wrong and should be moved to an Olympic Mountains / Olympic National Park branch. Separately, I could not independently confirm the given elevationFt (6785) through any of the three required primary sources: it has no Mountain Project or SummitPost page of its own, and its Peakbagger page (peak.aspx?pid=36175, confirmed to exist via search-result titles "Andersons Thumb, Washington") returned HTTP 403 on every fetch attempt (direct, translate-proxy, jina.ai, corsproxy, allorigins — all blocked). The 6785 ft figure is plausible for a subsidiary feature near the Anderson Glacier moraine (main Anderson summits are ~7,157–7,321 ft per Mountain Project/Wikipedia) but remains unverified by me; I have not overwritten it since I have no better-sourced alternative.
blurb: Anderson's Thumb is a rock pinnacle in the Anderson Massif of the Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park, near the Anderson Glacier moraine on the flank of Mount Anderson at the head of the Dosewallips/Quinault drainages. Unlike the crumbly, sharp, steeply-dipping meta-sedimentary rock typical of the surrounding massif, the Thumb is composed of solid sandstone, giving it a reputation among the few parties who've noted it as "a fun rock climb on good sandstone." It has no dedicated guidebook entry and is climbed only opportunistically as a side objective by mountaineers already in the area for Mount Anderson.

routes.overview: An informal scramble/low-5th-class rock line up the sandstone pinnacle known as Anderson's Thumb, climbed as a short side-trip from a camp near the Anderson Glacier moraine rather than as a stand-alone objective.
routes.beta: Anderson's Thumb has no dedicated guidebook route description or topo. The only documented accounts are trip-report asides from parties climbing Mount Anderson: one pair was tempted to scramble it on their way out from the Anderson Glacier moraine and described it as 'a fun rock climb on good sandstone' — notably solid rock compared with the loose, meta-sedimentary terrain elsewhere on the massif — but turned back when heavy rain began. Photos from another Mount Anderson trip report show it as a recognizable landmark near the moraine/glacier basin below Echo Rock and Flypaper Pass. Treat it as an optional, unrated scramble added onto a Mount Anderson trip, not a route to travel for on its own.
routes.approach: Drive Hwy 101 to Brinnon, then Dosewallips Road (FR 2610) about 8.5 miles to the vehicle road-end; the road has been washed out at roughly mile 5.45 since a 2002 slide and will not be rebuilt, so most of that mileage is now a walk on the old roadbed. From the former Dosewallips Trailhead, follow the West Fork Dosewallips River Trail approximately 16 miles one-way (about 4,460 ft of gain) to Anderson Pass, then continue roughly another mile up toward the Anderson Glacier moraine below Mount Anderson's summits, where Anderson's Thumb comes into view as a rock tower above camp.
routes.descent: Downclimb/reverse the scramble line back to the moraine; no rappel stations or fixed descent route are documented.
routes.descentText: No rappel anchors, fixed lines, or established descent gully are documented for Anderson's Thumb. Parties who have climbed it appear to have downclimbed the same line back to the talus/moraine below rather than rappelling.
routes.bestSeason: The broader Anderson Massif is generally climbable June through August/September, but the classic glacier approaches are best in spring/early summer before the seasonal snow finger to Flypaper Pass melts out; by late summer the Eel Glacier's bergschrund can be difficult to cross. Since Anderson's Thumb is a rock feature reached from dry moraine rather than glacier travel, it is best attempted once the approach trail and moraine are snow-free, roughly July through September.
watchOut: Weather on the Anderson Massif can turn quickly — one party abandoned an attempt on the Thumb when heavy rain moved in during their descent
No maintained trail, cairns, or topo lead to the base of the pinnacle; expect routefinding on talus and moraine

The rock surrounding the Thumb (elsewhere on the Anderson Massif) is loose, sharp, steeply-dipping meta-sedimentary rock — stay on the solid sandstone of the Thumb itself and use caution on approach ledges
knownHazards: Loose, crumbly meta-sedimentary rock on the approach terrain immediately around the solid-sandstone pinnacle
Rapid, unpredictable weather changes typical of the Olympic Mountains' marine climate
A long, remote approach (roughly 17 miles one-way from the highway) with no maintained trail for the final stretch to the pinnacle and no cell coverage
proTips: Treat it as an add-on scramble from an Anderson Pass / Anderson Glacier camp rather than a dedicated trip — there is no separate trailhead or approach
Go only in settled weather; the only documented attempt was turned back by incoming rain

road.name: Dosewallips Road (FR 2610)
road.status: Closed to vehicles roughly 5.45 miles from US 101; a 2002 washout took out the roadbed and NPS/USFS plans call for it to remain a trail rather than be rebuilt for vehicles
road.driveNote: From US 101 at Brinnon, drive Dosewallips Road to the gate at the washout and park; walk the abandoned roadbed to reach the former Dosewallips Trailhead before starting the West Fork Dosewallips Trail toward Anderson Pass.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS) for the upper trail/Anderson Pass area; Olympic National Forest (USFS) for the lower Dosewallips Road corridor
access.fees: Olympic NP wilderness camping fee: $8 per adult per night plus a non-refundable $6 reservation fee, or a $45 per-person Annual Olympic Wilderness Pass
access.permit: Wilderness backpacking permit required for any overnight stay; reserve via recreation.gov or through the Wilderness Information Center
access.passRequired: NPS entrance pass or Northwest Forest Pass may apply depending on trailhead used; overnight wilderness permit mandatory
access.closures: Dosewallips Road washout ~5.45 mi from US 101 adds roughly 11 miles round-trip to any approach to Anderson Pass

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jeffcom 911 (Jefferson County's public-safety dispatch/communications center, co-located with the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office) — dial 911 in an emergency
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center — (360) 565-3100
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare, Port Townsend (24-hour emergency department)
emergency.notes: Jefferson County Search and Rescue (JSAR), founded 1976, provides mutual-aid backcountry search-and-rescue support to NPS in this part of Olympic National Park. No reliable cell coverage should be expected in the Dosewallips/Anderson Pass backcountry; carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevationFt (6785) could not be independently verified: Anderson's Thumb has no Mountain Project or SummitPost page of its own, and every attempt to fetch its Peakbagger page (pid=36175, confirmed to exist via search results) was blocked (HTTP 403/401 across direct fetch and multiple proxy methods). No route-level grade (Class 4/low 5th, alpine grade AD) could be confirmed or contradicted by any source found — no FA record, dedicated topo, or guidebook entry exists for this feature; it appears only as an unrated side-scramble in trip-report asides. Left those fields as given rather than overwrite with an unsourced value.

sources: https://www.peakbagger.com/peak.aspx?pid=36175 (existence confirmed via search; content fetch blocked HTTP 403)

blurb: Chair Peak (6,238 ft) is a striking, steep-sided summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Central Cascades, rising directly above Source Lake and Snow Lake about three miles northwest of Snoqualmie Pass on land managed by the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Its short approach from the Alpental ski area and consistently steep faces make it one of the most-climbed technical objectives near Seattle, offering Class 3-4 rock scrambling in summer and popular moderate ice/mixed lines - most notably the Northeast Buttress and North Face - once conditions fill in for winter. The peak was first climbed in 1913 by Hec V. Abel and L.F. Curtis.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity and location check out: Chair Peak at 6,238 ft (47.4597°N, 121.4669°W) is a real, well-documented summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Snoqualmie Pass, King County, WA (matches Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and Mountain Project). One flag for the hierarchy placement: geographically this peak sits in the Central Cascades / Snoqualmie Pass (I-90) corridor, not the North Cascades proper (which is generally considered to begin further north, around Stevens Pass/Highway 20). Worth confirming this area's parent range/region bucket in the DB is not mislabeled "North Cascades," consistent with the mis-bucketing issues found in prior WA hierarchy audits.

routes.overview: A moderate line up Chair Peak's East Face that follows the buttress crest to the summit; climbed as an easy Class 3-4 rock scramble in summer and as one of Washington's most popular moderate ice/mixed alpine routes (WI3, 5.4-5.6) once the S-shaped gully and an ice bulge fill in for winter.
routes.beta: From the basin below Chair Peak's East Face, the route climbs a curving gully system on climber's right before trending up and left along the buttress crest to the summit. The technical crux in winter conditions is a short, steep ice bulge encountered partway up; in summer the route is straightforward exposed scrambling with occasional low-5th-class moves. Mountain Project describes it in 4 pitches with a 60m rope, though many parties simul-climb sections or break it into 5-6 shorter pitches, especially in early-season snow. Short approach and moderate climbing make it very popular and prone to queues on clear winter weekends.
routes.approach: Park at the upper Alpental parking lot (3,100 ft, Exit 52 off I-90). Follow the Snow Lake Trail (#1013) toward Source Lake, then leave the trail to ascend into the basin below Chair Peak's East Face (~4,200 ft), passing a landmark rock formation known as 'The Thumbtack.' From the basin, climb a short snow gully (~300 ft) to gain the ridge that forms the base of the Northeast Buttress; look for flagging or cairns marking the start of the buttress on the East Face.
routes.descent: Downclimb from the summit to the saddle, then descend the steep SW-facing gully with 2 rappels (~30m each).
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb to the notch/saddle and continue down the steep, southwest-facing descent gully. Most parties make two ~30m rappels from natural or fixed anchors near the base of the gully; some instead descend via the Southeast Chimney with 2-3 rappels totaling roughly 400 ft. The gully is exposed to rockfall from parties above and to cornice collapse in winter/spring - tie stopper knots and watch overhead terrain throughout.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed car-to-car in a single day: pre-dawn start from Alpental (~5:00 AM in trip reports), roughly 2 hours to reach the base at Thumbtack Rock, then 3+ hours of climbing to the summit, followed by the descent. No camping required given the short approach.
routes.bail: The lower buttress has natural retreat options (trees, boulders for anchors) - parties can rappel or downclimb back the way they came before committing to the upper ice bulge/steeper pitches. Leave slings/rap rings if bailing mid-route.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting based on daylight, crowding, and forecast warming - the descent gully is exposed to rockfall and cornice hazard that worsens as the day warms, so parties should plan to be off the upper mountain and through the gully before afternoon sun softens the snow and ice.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed nearly year-round in different styles: as a rock scramble roughly June through October once the buttress is snow-free, and as a moderate ice/mixed alpine climb roughly December through March when the gullies and the crux ice step fill in. Late winter into early spring (Jan-Mar) is the most popular window for the ice version, before spring warming increases rockfall and wet-slough hazard.
routes.commitment: II-III (sources vary; Mountain Project lists Grade III, other guides list Grade II)
routes.rappels: 2 x 30m rappels from the base of the descent gully (some parties do 3-5 shorter raps via the SE Chimney variation)
objHaz: Rockfall on the approach traverse and descent gully
Cornice collapse near the summit and descent notch in winter/spring
Overhead hazard from other parties given the route's popularity
routes.face: East Face (Northeast Buttress)
routes.detailedRack: A single 60m rope is standard. Rock rack: small-to-mid cams (~0.3-1), assorted small-to-medium nuts, a few slings. For ice/mixed conditions add 3-4 ice screws (mix of 10cm and 13cm) and 1-2 pickets. Many parties simul-climb or scramble the lower sections unroped and pitch out only the ice step and upper snow to the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Placements are sparse on the lower scrambling terrain (roughly 2-3 solid pieces per rope-length); focus small cams and nuts on the steeper pitches, plus ice screws for the crux ice bulge in winter conditions.
whatToBring: Crampons and ice axe/tools for winter ascents
Approach shoes or mountaineering boots depending on season
Extra layers - route is exposed to wind and sun
Nylon webbing/rap rings for descent anchors
proTips: Start early (pre-dawn) in winter to beat crowds - this is one of the most popular ice routes in Washington and can back up at the crux ice step
A single 60m rope is sufficient; two ropes aren't needed for the standard descent
Watch for flagging/cairns marking the start of the buttress on the East Face after traversing from the Source Lake basin
watchOut: Cornices form above the descent gully in winter - assess stability before committing
Loose rock on the approach traverse and lower scrambling sections
Afternoon warming rapidly destabilizes snow/ice on the southwest-facing descent terrain - plan an early start and finish
knownHazards: Rockfall on the approach traverse and descent gully, especially with parties above
Cornice collapse hazard near the summit and descent notch in winter/spring
Overhead hazard during the rappels off the descent gully

pitchDetail.notes: S-shaped gully on climber's right; conditions vary from easy snow to mixed climbing; a left slab variation exists. Tree anchor.
Long pitch (70-80m) to the base of the ice bulge.
The route's crux - an obvious, steep ice bulge (or rock step when dry); awkward anchor placement above.
Leads to the summit saddle; many parties unrope for the final scrambling.

road.name: Alpental Road
road.status: Paved, open year-round
road.driveNote: From I-90 eastbound take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass), turn left/north onto Alpental Road and follow it about 1.5 miles to the upper Alpental parking lot (3,100 ft).

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade climate with heavy winter snowfall, wet springs, and drier, milder summers
climate.spring: Wet and increasingly unstable snowpack; rising rockfall and wet-slough hazard as the season progresses
climate.summer: Generally dry and mild; afternoon thunderstorms possible
climate.fall: Cooling with increasing precipitation; early-season snow possible by Oct/Nov
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and significant avalanche hazard typical of the Snoqualmie Pass corridor

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Alpental lot requires a paid Summit at Snoqualmie parking permit ($25/vehicle) on winter weekends & select holidays (late Nov-late Mar); a Northwest Forest Pass is generally required for National Forest trailhead parking outside ski season
access.permit: No special wilderness climbing permit required
access.passRequired: Summit at Snoqualmie winter parking pass or Northwest Forest Pass depending on season

timing.recommendedStart: ~5:00 AM (typical in trip reports, to beat crowds and afternoon warming)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 206-296-3311 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend office, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF: (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, Snoqualmie, WA (~25 mi via I-90); Swedish Issaquah and Valley Medical Center (Renton) are larger regional facilities further west
emergency.notes: King County Sheriff's Office Search & Rescue Unit coordinates backcountry rescues in this area. Cell coverage in the Source Lake basin and on the peak is inconsistent - do not rely on it.

routes.overview: A classic, moderate winter alpine face route up Chair Peak, climbing snow and ice at roughly 50-70° (with one short steeper step) directly up the north-facing wall above Snow Lake to a col just below the summit.
routes.beta: Reached via a short traverse from the Source Lake-Snow Lake divide, the North Face offers about four pitches of steep snow and ice climbing on generally moderate-angle terrain (50-65°) with one short crux step that can approach 80° (sometimes rock, 5.4-5.6, if thin on ice). Abundant natural/fixed protection and a short approach make it a popular introduction to Cascade ice climbing, though it backs up with parties on clear winter weekends. Note: a distinct, harder rock variation called 'North Face Direct' (5.9, 5 pitches) starts about 200m from the standard line and should not be confused with this route.
routes.approach: Park at the upper Alpental parking lot (3,100 ft, Exit 52 off I-90). Follow the Snow Lake Trail toward Source Lake and into the basin below Chair Peak's East Face (~4,200 ft), then continue up the short snow gully (~300 ft) to the Source Lake-Snow Lake divide/ridge. As there are few good staging areas beneath the face itself, rope up and rack at the divide, then make a roughly 100m traverse (climber's right/north) around the face to reach the base of the North Face route.
routes.descent: Traverse over the false summit and descend the East Face gully (2 rappels) back to the basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, traverse over the false summit and head down the normal descent gully to a notch in the southeast ridge, where rappel gullies drop into the east-face basin. Most parties make about two rappels down a gully on the south side of the East Face, downclimbing where conditions allow; bring extra webbing and rap rings, as anchors are often natural (trees/boulders) or aging fixed gear. Cornice formation near the summit and the descent notch requires careful navigation in winter/spring.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed car-to-car in a single day with a pre-dawn/early start: approach to the Source Lake-Snow Lake divide, traverse to the base, climb the four-pitch face, then descend the East Face gully. No camping required given the short approach; the route can be linked with the Northeast Buttress by strong parties in one long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible by downclimbing/rappelling the route itself on lower pitches before committing above the crux step; anchors are mostly natural (trees), so bring extra webbing/rap rings if bailing.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting based on daylight and crowding - this is a popular, largely single-file route that can back up at the crux step, and cornice/overhead hazard near the top means parties should avoid being caught high on the face after dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: A winter-specific line, typically in good condition December through March. Early season the route is often steep, unconsolidated snow over rock; ice builds up through the season and is generally best by February-March. Favorable conditions are cold and clear.
routes.rappels: ~2 rappels down the East Face gully to the basin (natural anchors/trees; bring extra webbing and rap rings)
objHaz: Bergschrund/moat at the base of the route, especially early/late season
Cornice near the summit and descent notch
Rockfall/icefall from parties above on a popular route
Weekend crowding can extend exposure time on the face
routes.face: North Face (above Snow Lake)
routes.detailedRack: A light rack is standard: a handful of ice screws (mix of stubbies and 13-16cm), 1-2 pickets, a few small-to-medium nuts and small cams for the mixed step, and two ice tools with crampons. A single 60m rope is sufficient for the route and the descent rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly ice screws and occasional pickets on the snow sections, supplemented by small rock gear (nuts/small cams) on the short mixed/rock crux step.
whatToBring: Extra webbing/rap rings for descent anchors
Extra layers - can be cold and windy on a north-facing wall
proTips: Rope up and rack at the Source Lake-Snow Lake divide before making the ~100m traverse to the base - there's little room to gear up under the face itself
Bring full screw/picket kit even though much of the route is 'steep snow' - conditions swing from unconsolidated snow early season to hard ice by March
Go early to avoid the line-up at the crux step - the route is popular and largely single-file on busy winter weekends
watchOut: A bergschrund/moat can guard the base of the route and create a committing, exposed start - assess before committing
The face gets crowded on clear winter weekends; expect potential queues at the crux ice/rock step
Cornices can form near the summit and the descent notch
knownHazards: Bergschrund/moat at the base of the face
Overhead rockfall and icefall hazard from other parties given the route's popularity
Cornice hazard near the summit and descent notch

pitchDetail.notes: 50-60m to a belay; guarded by a bergschrund at the very base in some conditions.
~60m to a tree belay.
~50m, using tree anchors.
Leads to a col about 60 ft below the true summit; short crux step (~80°, occasionally 5.4-5.6 rock if thin) encountered on the upper face.

routes.fa: Kit Lewis, Charlie Hampson, Rob Harris, and Greg Jacobson - January 1975
routes.corrections: iceGrade corrected from the given AI3 to AI2-3: Mountain Project (the primary route database for this line) grades the standard North Face AI2 ('steep snow' terrain, 50-65° with one short ~80° step); general trip-report descriptions cite WI2-3. A distinct, harder companion line ('North Face Direct,' 5.9 rock, 5 pitches, starting ~200m away) exists and may be the source of confusion with a harder ice/rock grade - it is a separate route from this standard North Face line.

blurb: Sloan Peak (7,835 ft) rises in the North Cascades of Snohomish County, Washington, between the North and South Forks of the Sauk River within the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, near the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness. Its steep, isolated summit block — underlain by the Cretaceous-age tonalite of the Sloan Creek pluton — and more than a vertical mile of local relief on its east and southwest sides have earned it the nickname "The Matterhorn of the Cascades." It was first climbed on July 30, 1921 by Harry Bedal and Nels Skaar via what is now the standard Corkscrew Route.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity and parent region (North Cascades / Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, Snohomish County) check out and are unambiguous. One data-quality flag: the given elevationFt (7,839) is off by 4 ft — every cross-checked source (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Mountain Project, WTA) independently gives 7,835 ft; recommend correcting elevationFt to 7835. Also note the two given routes ("Corkscrew Route" and "West Face (Corkscrew route)") appear in sources to describe the same standard mountaineering line (Wikipedia/Peakbagger describe the standard route as ascending the "South Face and Upper West Face," i.e. the Corkscrew Route's upper section) — this is distinct from a separate, much harder roped 5.7-5.8 rock "West Face" route documented by SummitPost/AAC/Mountaineers/Lemke Climbs, which does not match the given "Grade II, glacier/snow + Class 3 scramble" description. See per-route corrections.

routes.overview: Sloan Peak's easiest and standard line: a glacier crossing beneath the East Face gains the Southeast ridge, then a winding ('corkscrewing') climber's trail circles the South ridge and Upper West Face on exposed 3rd-class (with occasional 4th-class) terrain to the narrow summit block.
routes.beta: From either the Bedal Creek or Cougar Creek (North Fork Sauk) trailhead, a climber's trail leaves the maintained tread and climbs through forest and talus to a saddle near Bedal Peak, then crosses the Sloan Glacier. Parties step off the glacier at the Southeast ridge and follow a boot path that corkscrews south then north around the South ridge, finishing with a short, exposed 3rd/4th-class scramble to the summit.
routes.approach: Two standard approaches. Bedal Creek (preferred, shorter): from Darrington drive ~18.25 mi south on Mountain Loop Highway (or ~7 mi north of Barlow Pass), turn onto Forest Road 4096 and drive ~2-3 rough miles (high-clearance 4WD/AWD recommended) to a small trailhead (2,790-2,800 ft, 7-9 car lot). Hike ~1.2 mi of trail to ~3,560 ft, then leave the trail (pink flagging marks the junction near 3,500 ft) and go cross-country/talus to the ~5,300 ft saddle between Bedal and Sloan Peak, then onto the Sloan Glacier. Cougar Creek/North Fork Sauk alternative: from Darrington drive ~17 mi south on Mountain Loop Highway, turn onto North Fork Sauk River Road (FR 49) and drive ~4.5 mi to a marked pullout (~1,900 ft); this route involves a brushy, flagged climber's trail with a difficult ford of the North Fork Sauk River (dangerous except at low water, typically late summer/fall) and an icy crossing of Cougar Creek, then steep switchbacks past a waterfall to ~5,000-5,500 ft to rejoin the standard line.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: downclimb the corkscrew scramble back to the glacier, re-cross the glacier, and descend the approach ridge/trail to the trailhead. No rappels are typically required in normal season conditions.
routes.descentText: Descend by reversing the exact ascent line. From the summit, downclimb the exposed 3rd/4th-class corkscrew scramble back to where snow/glacier gear was cached (~7,300 ft), re-rope for the glacier crossing back to the Bedal/Sloan saddle, then follow the approach trail back to the trailhead. In early/late season with soft or icy snow this descent can be slower and more hazardous than the ascent; some parties glissade suitable snow slopes below the glacier where safe run-out exists.
routes.itinerary: Typical 1-2 day plan: Day 1 - drive to trailhead, hike/climb to the ~5,300 ft Bedal/Sloan saddle and camp (stagnant pond nearby, water available above ~5,500 ft). Day 2 - alpine start, cross the Sloan Glacier, complete the corkscrew scramble to the summit, then reverse the route to the car. Strong parties do it car-to-car in one long day (~13 hours from Bedal Creek) with a pre-dawn or car-bivy start.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward in good visibility: reverse the corkscrew scramble down to the glacier and descend the glacier and approach ridge/trail back to the saddle and trailhead. The technical crux is confined to the upper 3rd/4th-class scramble band close to the summit, so parties can bail before committing to that section with minimal downclimbing.
routes.turnaround: Because the route ends with glacier travel and an exposed scramble that both become more hazardous as the day warms (softening snow bridges, increased rockfall/wet-slide risk on the upper snowfields), parties should set a turnaround time that leaves enough margin to finish the glacier crossing and scramble descent before afternoon warming — several parties have turned back within a few hundred feet of the summit for exactly this reason.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August is the most reliable window: the high alpine heather ledges need to be snow-free (typically late June onward) and July-August offers the most stable glacier and scramble conditions. Attempts as early as May are possible but involve full snow/glacier travel and higher avalanche/wet-slide risk; by September the Sloan Glacier crossing becomes increasingly crevassed and technical.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: harness, crevasse-rescue gear (pickets, prusiks/ascenders), ice axe and crampons, and a rope for the glacier crossing (many parties use a shorter/lighter rope and short-rope sections of the scramble). A helmet is recommended for the loose 3rd/4th-class terrain near the summit.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed rock protection is needed on the standard line; a picket or two covers the glacier crossing, and a light rack/short rope is optional for parties who want to protect brief 4th-class moves near the summit.
whatToBring: Bivy/camp gear if splitting the climb into two days
proTips: Camp at the ~5,300 ft Bedal/Sloan saddle to split the long day in two.
Start well before dawn (car-bivy start common) so the glacier crossing and scramble are finished before afternoon sun softens snow and raises rockfall/wet-slide hazard.
Refill water above ~5,500 ft near the saddle rather than carrying a full day's supply from the trailhead.
watchOut: The North Fork Sauk River and Cougar Creek crossings (Cougar Creek approach) can be swift and dangerous, especially in early season/high water.
Afternoon warming on the upper snowfields has produced sluffing/wet-slide activity close to the summit, turning back parties within a few hundred feet of the top.
The 'class 3' scramble includes exposed heather ledges with real fall consequence (~500 ft exposure in places), not casual walking terrain.
knownHazards: Glacier travel with genuine crevasse hazard crossing the Sloan Glacier beneath the East Face.
Rockfall/icefall off the summit block, worst during afternoon warming.
A basin below Bedal Peak on the approach is a noted avalanche/wet-slide terrain trap in spring conditions.

pitchDetail.pitch: Trail junction to Bedal/Sloan saddle (3,500-5,300 ft)

Cross a rock ridge for first views of/access to the Sloan Glacier.

road.name: Forest Road 4096 (Bedal Creek) / North Fork Sauk River Road, FR 49 (Cougar Creek)
road.status: FR 4096 is rough, rutted, and pothole-scarred in its final stretch; high-clearance 4WD/AWD strongly recommended, maintain momentum on steep/potholed sections.
road.driveNote: Bedal Creek TH: Mountain Loop Highway to FR 4096, then ~2-3 mi to a small (7-9 car) trailhead lot at 2,790-2,800 ft. Cougar Creek/North Fork Sauk alternative uses FR 49 to a pullout near 1,900 ft; the N Fork Sauk Road has been reported closed short of the highway in some years, adding road-walking distance.

climate.typical: Maritime North Cascades climate: wet, cool winters/springs and drier, warmer summers; the peak's glacier and steep terrain hold snow late into summer.
climate.spring: Heavy, unstable snowpack; creek crossings run high and dangerous; upper snowfields prone to wet-slide/sluffing activity by midday.
climate.summer: July-August is the prime window once heather ledges and the glacier approach are snow-free.
climate.fall: The Sloan Glacier crossing becomes increasingly crevassed/technical by September as snow bridges deteriorate.
climate.winter: Full winter mountaineering/ice conditions; the West Face has seen documented winter ice/mixed ascents (WI3-4).

routes.face: South Face and Upper West Face
routes.corrections: Peak elevationFt given as 7,839 ft; Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Mountain Project, and WTA all independently confirm 7,835 ft — highPointFt corrected accordingly. gainFt/distKm reflect the Bedal Creek approach (9.5 mi round trip / ~5,300 ft gain per a 2024 trip report); the alternate Cougar Creek/North Fork Sauk approach is longer (~18.7 mi round trip in one account, partly due to a road closure requiring extra road-walking) with reported gain up to ~5,882-5,935 ft — approach distance/gain vary meaningfully by trailhead choice.

routes.overview: The upper Corkscrew Route as it finishes on Sloan Peak's Upper West Face: after crossing the Sloan Glacier and circling the South ridge, the standard boot path climbs the final exposed 3rd/4th-class scramble on the west side of the summit block.
routes.beta: This entry corresponds to the same standard mountaineering line as the Corkscrew Route (routeId wa_sloan_peak_corkscrew) — Wikipedia and Peakbagger both describe the peak's standard route as ascending the 'South Face and Upper West Face.' See that route's approach/beta for the full line; this entry's grade (Grade II, glacier/snow + Class 3 scramble) matches the Corkscrew Route, not the separate roped 5.7-5.8 technical 'West Face' rock route documented independently by SummitPost, the American Alpine Club, The Mountaineers, and Lemke Climbs (first ascent Merrill-Minton), which is a distinct, much harder climb not reflected in the given grade/discipline data.
routes.approach: Identical to the Corkscrew Route: via Bedal Creek trailhead (FR 4096) or the Cougar Creek/North Fork Sauk trailhead (FR 49), joining the Sloan Glacier crossing before the final west-face scramble to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the west-face scramble to the glacier, then descend the glacier and approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the exposed scramble on the Upper West Face back to the glacier-access point (~7,300 ft), re-cross the Sloan Glacier roped, and follow the approach ridge/trail back to the trailhead, reversing the Corkscrew Route's approach.
routes.bail: Retreat down the west-face scramble to the glacier is straightforward in good visibility; the technical difficulty is confined to a short band just below the summit.
routes.turnaround: As with the Corkscrew Route, plan to be off the upper snowfields and glacier before afternoon warming increases rockfall and wet-slide hazard on this west-facing terrain.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August, once the upper ledges are snow-free and before the September glacier becomes more crevassed; matches the Corkscrew Route's season.
routes.detailedRack: Same as the Corkscrew Route: glacier-travel kit (harness, pickets, prusiks, ice axe, crampons, rope) plus a helmet for the scramble.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection needed on this class 3/easy class 4 scramble terrain; light rack optional.
proTips: Treat this as the finishing pitch of the Corkscrew Route rather than a separate line — see that route's tips for the full-day plan.
watchOut: West-facing upper slopes catch afternoon sun and soften/sluff readily — climb this section early.
knownHazards: Loose rock and exposure on the final scramble to the summit block.

pitchDetail.pitch: Glacier exit to summit via Upper West Face (7,300-7,835 ft)
pitchDetail.notes: Final section of the standard route; exposed scrambling on the west side of the summit block.

climate.summer: July-August is the prime window.
climate.fall: Glacier becomes more crevassed/technical by September.
climate.winter: Full winter conditions; not the standard season for this line.

routes.corrections: This DB entry ('West Face (Corkscrew route)', Grade II / glacier-snow / Class 3) matches sourced descriptions of the standard Corkscrew Route's finish on the Upper West Face (per Wikipedia/Peakbagger: 'South Face and Upper West Face' route), and appears to be the same physical climb as routeId wa_sloan_peak_corkscrew rather than a distinct route. It should not be confused with the separate, harder roped 5.7-5.8 technical 'West Face' rock route (FA Merrill-Minton, documented by SummitPost/AAC/Mountaineers/Lemke Climbs), which has a materially different grade/discipline than what's given here. Elevation correction: highPointFt set to 7,835 ft (see wa_sloan_peak_corkscrew corrections) rather than the DB's 7,839 ft peak elevation.

blurb: Sherman Peak is a small, loose volcanic pinnacle (roughly 10,160 ft) on the south rim of Sherman Crater, the historically active vent on the south flank of Mount Baker in the North Cascades near the Whatcom–Skagit county line. It sits on the same andesitic stratovolcano as Baker's true summit (Grant Peak, 10,781 ft) but is a subsidiary high point, not the mountain's summit. Active sulfur fumaroles vent from Sherman Crater near the peak — a legacy of Baker's 19th-century eruptive history (recorded activity 1843–1880, with a notable 1975 thermal unrest episode) — and the peak's rock is described by climbers as "chossy" and unpleasant when snow-free, so it is almost always climbed as a snow-covered side-trip from the standard Easton or Squak Glacier routes rather than a destination in its own right.
hierarchyNote: Sherman Peak and its Mount Baker parent hierarchy check out geographically (coordinates ~48.767, -121.814 place it directly on Sherman Crater's south rim, consistent with the given peak). However, the listed elevation of 10,139 ft does not match any source found: Peakbagger.com and SummitPost both give ~10,160 ft, the USFS Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie recreation page also states ~10,160 ft, PeakVisor gives 10,128 ft, and a first-hand trip report (LEMKE Climbs) gives 10,165 ft. 10,160 ft (Peakbagger/SummitPost/USFS, the two required baseline sources plus a federal source) is the best-supported figure; consider updating the peak's elevationFt from 10,139 to ~10,160 ft.

routes.overview: A short, non-technical side-trip off Mount Baker's standard Easton Glacier route: from the crater rim just below Baker's summit plateau, climbers break off the main track, circle around the south rim of Sherman Crater (Baker's historically active vent), and climb a final steep snow/scree ridge to the small summit of Sherman Peak.
routes.beta: Sherman Peak is reached by climbing the Easton Glacier (or, alternatively, the Squak Glacier) exactly as for a normal Mount Baker ascent, then peeling off near the crater rim instead of continuing left/north to Baker's true summit (Grant Peak). Where the climbers' track meets the crater rim, go right (south/southeast) toward Sherman instead of left toward Baker's summit. The route makes a rising traverse around the south side of Sherman Crater, past active fumaroles venting sulfurous gas, to a saddle on Sherman's west/south ridge, then ascends roughly 400 ft of increasingly steep snow (or loose rock/scree if snow-free) to the small summit. Multiple trip reports emphasize the peak should only be attempted with good snow cover — bare, it is described as a dangerous, chossy mudpile.
routes.approach: From Sedro-Woolley, WA follow WA-20 east ~16 miles to milepost 82, turn left (north) onto the Baker Lake Highway (Forest Road 11); at about mile 12 turn left onto FR-12, follow it 3.6 miles to the FR-13 junction, stay right on FR-13 for 5.3 miles to its end at the Park Butte Trailhead. From the trailhead, follow the Railroad Grade / Park Butte climbers' trail west through Schriebers Meadow; cross the creek near 3,600 ft and turn north toward Railroad Grade Ridge, following it above treeline. Around 8,000–9,000 ft, leave the ridge and rope up for glacier travel onto the Easton Glacier, ascending the moderately sloped glacier (avoiding crevasses) toward the crater rim below Baker's summit, then branch off toward Sherman Peak as described in beta.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb/plunge-step off Sherman's summit ridge to the saddle, traverse back around the south crater rim, and descend the Easton Glacier and Railroad Grade to camp and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ridge back down to the saddle below Sherman Peak, then re-trace the rising traverse around Sherman Crater's south rim back to the point where the route joins the main Easton Glacier climbers' track. Descend the glacier on the ascent track, watching for softened crevasse bridges and wet-loose avalanche activity on warm afternoons; where snow conditions allow, glissading sections of Railroad Grade and the upper glacier speeds the descent considerably. Continue down Railroad Grade Ridge and back through Schriebers Meadow to the Park Butte Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties climb Sherman Peak as a 2-day trip combined with (or as an alternative summit to) Mount Baker itself: Day 1 — drive to Park Butte Trailhead, hike ~3–4 hours to a high camp on Railroad Grade or the lower Easton Glacier; Day 2 — alpine start, climb the glacier to the crater rim, detour to Sherman Peak's summit, then descend to camp and out to the trailhead. Strong, ski-equipped parties have completed the full car-to-car trip in a single long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward in good weather: reverse the ascent line down the south crater-rim traverse and Easton Glacier to camp. In deteriorating weather or whiteout, the crater rim and glacier are the main routefinding hazards — a GPS track or visible wands/tracks are recommended since the crater's fumarole steam can reduce visibility and disorient climbers near the rim.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before departing camp based on current snow, weather, and party pace, and honor it — glacier travel and the summit ridge become more hazardous as the day warms (softening crevasse bridges, wet-loose avalanche risk, and post-holing), so descending in worsening conditions is safer than pushing for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring through mid-summer (roughly May–August) is favored, timed for maximum snow cover on Sherman's summit ridge — trip reports are explicit that the peak is not recommended once it melts out, since the exposed rock is loose and unpleasant. This overlaps with the broader Mount Baker climbing season (June–September) but skews earlier since Sherman's final ridge relies on snowpack that other Baker routes don't.
routes.comms: Cell coverage on Mount Baker's south side (Park Butte/Easton approach) is spotty and highly location- and carrier-dependent above treeline — don't count on a signal near camp or on the glacier. A satellite communicator (e.g., Garmin inReach) is commonly recommended for this area.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier mountaineering rack: one rope per rope team, a full crevasse-rescue kit (prusik cords or mechanical ascenders, pulleys, carabiners, an ice screw or picket for anchors), harness, helmet, ice axe, and crampons. No rock protection or pitched climbing gear is needed for the scramble sections; a single picket or ice screw for a crevasse-rescue anchor is the extent of placed protection most parties carry.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are limited to glacier travel: pickets or ice screws for crevasse-rescue anchors/belays rather than any rock or ice protection on the summit ridge itself.
whatToBring: Glacier glasses/sunglasses (high UV exposure and snow glare)
Buff or bandana (sulfurous fumarole gas near the crater rim)
Blue bags/WAG bags for human waste (required within the wilderness/NRA)
Recreation pass or Northwest Forest Pass for the trailhead
Extra layers for wind/exposure on the crater rim
proTips: Ski-equipped parties can climb and descend Railroad Grade and the glacier quickly, turning this into a feasible single long day car-to-car; most others plan two days with a high camp.
Wash clothing and gear after the trip — sulfur-rich gas near Sherman Crater deposits sulfuric acid residue that can degrade fabric over time.
Use the crater rim's fumarole steam as a landmark to find the Sherman turnoff, but don't linger downwind of vents.
watchOut: Sherman Peak is explicitly not recommended when snow-free — the rock is loose, chossy, and described by climbers as dangerous to walk on.
Active fumaroles on Sherman Crater vent hydrogen sulfide and other volcanic gases; avoid lingering in or downwind of steam plumes.
Crevasse bridges on the Easton Glacier soften through the day; an early alpine start and firm turnaround time reduce exposure.
A bergschrund has been reported on south-facing slopes near the route in some seasons.
knownHazards: Open and hidden crevasses on the Easton Glacier require roped travel and crevasse-rescue readiness.
Sherman Crater's active fumaroles emit hydrogen sulfide (H2S) and other volcanic gases that are a chemical and respiratory hazard at close range.
Wet-loose avalanches are reported on warm-weather ascents of the glacier and summit ridge.
The final summit ridge is loose scree/choss when not snow-covered, increasing rockfall and slip hazard.

pitchDetail.notes: Park Butte Trailhead through Schriebers Meadow to the creek crossing (~3,600 ft), then up onto Railroad Grade Ridge.
Ridge walk above treeline to roughly 8,000-9,000 ft, where the route leaves rock/moraine and gains the Easton Glacier.
Moderately sloped glacier travel avoiding crevasses, ascending to the crater rim below Baker's summit plateau.

pitchDetail.pitch: Crater rim traverse to Sherman saddle
pitchDetail.notes: Rising traverse around the south rim of Sherman Crater past active fumaroles to a saddle on Sherman Peak's ridge.
Final ~400 ft of steep snow (firm step-kicking when in condition) or loose scree/rock if snow-free, to the small summit.

road.name: Baker Lake Highway (FR-11) to FR-12 to FR-13
road.status: Paved on FR-11 (Baker Lake Highway); gravel Forest Service road on FR-12/FR-13 to the Park Butte Trailhead
road.seasonalGate: FR-12 is gated at Wanlick Pass from Nov 1 through Jul 1, roughly 5.4 miles before Pioneer Camp/the Park Butte access, blocking vehicle access in that window
road.driveNote: From the Mt. Baker Ranger District in Sedro-Woolley: SR-20 east 16 miles to milepost 82, left (north) on Baker Lake Highway (FR-11), ~12 miles to a left onto FR-12, 3.6 miles to a right onto FR-13, then 5.3 miles to the Park Butte Trailhead at road's end.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker area)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades snowpack with rapid weather changes; glacier travel conditions depend heavily on recent freeze-thaw cycles.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; best window for snow-covered conditions on Sherman's summit ridge but higher avalanche/crevasse-bridge risk.
climate.summer: Peak climbing season with the most stable glacier travel, but Sherman's exposed final ridge may be melted out and loose by mid-to-late summer.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency; route becomes less traveled and more serious as snow cover recedes.
climate.winter: Road access to Park Butte Trailhead is gated (FR-12 closed Nov 1-Jul 1); winter ascents require additional approach mileage and full winter mountaineering conditions.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness / Mount Baker National Recreation Area)
access.fees: Recreation Pass required at Park Butte Trailhead May 1-Oct 31 ($5/vehicle/day or annual Interagency/Northwest Forest Pass); Washington Sno-Park permit required Nov 1-Apr 30 ($25/day or $50-70/season)
access.permit: No climbing permit is required by the USFS for Mount Baker/Sherman Peak
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Recreation Pass, or Recreation.gov digital day-use pass at the trailhead
access.closures: FR-12 is gated and closed to vehicles at Wanlick Pass from Nov 1 through Jul 1 each year, adding significant approach distance in that window; camping below 6,000 ft is restricted to designated tent-pad sites within the National Recreation Area.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from high camp for firm snow and to cross glacier terrain before daytime warming

emergency.county: Skagit County (Park Butte/south-side approach); the summit area lies near the Whatcom-Skagit county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit 911 (dial 911 for emergency dispatch; Skagit County Sheriff coordinates Search and Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Sedro-Woolley, WA (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest)
emergency.nearestHospital: United General District Hospital, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA, (360) 856-6021; Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA for higher-level care
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search & Rescue also operates in the broader Mount Baker area for north-side approaches; confirm jurisdiction with 911 dispatch based on exact location, as the peak sits near the county boundary.

Loose, chossy rock on the summit ridge when snow-free
routes.season: May-Aug (best with full snow cover; not recommended once melted out)
routes.face: South rim of Sherman Crater
routes.corrections: The given peak elevation of 10,139 ft is not matched by any source found; Peakbagger.com and SummitPost.org (both required baseline sources) plus the USFS Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie recreation page all cite ~10,160 ft for Sherman Peak, which is used here for highPointFt. PeakVisor (10,128 ft) and a first-hand trip report (10,165 ft) bracket this figure closely, but 10,160 ft has the strongest, most consistent sourcing.

blurb: Snowfield Peak (8,351 ft) is a remote, glacier-draped summit in the Cascade Range within the south unit of North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, rising roughly 6,000 ft above Diablo Lake about 5 miles ESE of Newhalem. It is built of the metamorphic/plutonic bedrock typical of the North Cascades crystalline core, uplifted and heavily glacially sculpted terrain, and the Neve Glacier drapes its north slopes while the Colonial Glacier flanks its approach from Pyramid Peak. No verifiable first-ascent party/date could be found in available online sources.

routes.overview: A two-day glacier mountaineering outing that climbs from Pyramid Lake over the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak, across the Colonial Glacier and the Colonial-Neve col, then across the broad Neve Glacier to a class 3-4 rock finish on Snowfield Peak's summit — the classic and essentially only established line up the mountain (Mountain Project lists it as "Neve Glacier / West Ridge").
routes.beta: From the Pyramid Lake Trailhead, hike the well-worn trail to Pyramid Lake (~2 mi), then follow a steep, less-traveled climbers' trail up the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak into alpine terrain. Traverse south past the east flank of Paul Bunyan's Stump onto the Colonial Glacier, climb ~30-degree snow to the Colonial-Neve col (~6,800-6,840 ft), then descend roughly 150-250 ft onto the Neve Glacier. Rope up and cross the Neve — trip reports describe the cleanest line through the center of its crevasse field — to the ridge near The Horseman on Snowfield's west side (~7,700 ft), then finish with roughly 400-600 ft of class 3-4 (occasionally class 5 on variant lines) rock scrambling to the summit.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) east approximately 21 miles (about 6.4 miles past Newhalem, shortly after crossing the Skagit River/Gorge Lake area) to roadside parking for the Pyramid Lake Trailhead on the north side of the highway near milepost 127 (room for roughly 6 cars, no restroom). Hike the maintained Pyramid Lake Trail about 2.1 miles to Pyramid Lake, then pick up the unmaintained climbers' trail on the lake's south/east side that climbs steeply through brush and slabs onto the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak and into open alpine terrain, from which the Colonial Glacier and the route toward Snowfield Peak become visible to the south.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back across the Neve and Colonial Glaciers and down the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak to Pyramid Lake and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 3-4 gully/ridge back to the Neve Glacier, re-rope for glacier travel, re-cross to the Colonial-Neve col, descend the ~30-degree snow back onto the Colonial Glacier, and retrace the climbers' trail over the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak to Pyramid Lake and the trailhead. No distinct rappel line is documented in available trip reports — parties downclimb the summit block.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-day plan: Day 1 - hike the Pyramid Lake Trail (~2 mi) then the climbers' trail up the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak to a camp at the Colonial Glacier moraine (~5,500-5,700 ft) or push on to the Colonial-Neve col (~6,800 ft); Day 2 - alpine start, cross the Colonial Glacier and col, descend onto and rope up across the Neve Glacier, climb the class 3-4 summit gully/ridge, then reverse the entire route back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The glacier crossings are the main commitment point on this route; retreat is essentially by reversing the line — downclimb the summit block to the Neve Glacier, re-cross to the Colonial-Neve col, and descend to camp. There is no shorter escape once committed to the Neve Glacier crossing, so turn back before committing if weather or visibility deteriorates.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before leaving high camp and stick to it — deteriorating weather, whiteout on the glaciers, or softening snow bridges on the Neve are all reasons to retreat rather than push for the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Most ascents are done in July, once the approach trail is snow-free and the Neve/Colonial Glacier snow bridges are consolidated but before late-season melt-out opens up crevasses; the window generally runs from July into September.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent and satellite phone coverage is described by the National Park Service as spotty across this terrain; carry a PLB or two-way satellite communicator as a primary emergency contact rather than relying on a phone.
routes.face: North side via the Neve Glacier, finishing near the west side of the summit block
objHaz: Crevasses on the Colonial and Neve Glaciers
Rockfall on the summit gully/ridge, especially with parties above
Rapid weather changes typical of the North Cascades marine climate
Lingering avalanche hazard on approach slopes in spring/early season
Verglas, rime ice, or old snow on the summit rock into early summer
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: ice axe, crampons, climbing harness, and a 30-50 m rope for the roped Neve and Colonial Glacier crossings, plus 2-4 snow pickets for crevasse-rescue anchors and a personal crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, locking carabiners). Mountain Project's grade data notes short sections of easy ice (up to about I2) and easy mixed ground (M1b) near the col that may warrant an extra tool or a screw or two for less-experienced parties. No rock rack is typically needed for the class 3-4 summit scramble.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is almost entirely glacier/crevasse-rescue gear rather than rock pro — pickets and a crevasse-rescue kit for the Colonial and Neve Glacier crossings. The class 3-4 summit gully/ridge is normally climbed unprotected; parties uncomfortable soloing it may want to short-rope the airier steps.
gear: Glacier harness and crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks, pulley, locking biners)
whatToBring: Overnight/bivy gear for a camp at the Colonial Glacier moraine or Colonial-Neve col
Water filter or treatment (tarn/glacier meltwater sources)
Sturdy approach shoes plus mountaineering boots for glacier travel
Extra insulating layers for an exposed high camp
Map/compass or GPS for route-finding in whiteout
proTips: Time the Neve Glacier crossing for morning while snow bridges are firmest; several trip reports note crossing before mid-morning softening.
Camping at the Colonial Glacier moraine (~5,500-5,700 ft) or at the Colonial-Neve col (~6,800 ft) breaks the long approach into two manageable days.
Multiple trip reports describe the cleanest crevasse-free line as through the center of the Neve Glacier.
A reliable water source exists at the tarn near the northwest toe of the Colonial Glacier.
watchOut: Long, steep, brushy-to-alpine approach with major elevation gain before any glacier travel begins.
Active, crevassed glacier travel on both the Colonial and Neve Glaciers requires roped travel and crevasse-rescue readiness.
The final class 3-4 summit scramble has real exposure and can hold snow, rime ice, or verglas well into early summer.
Cell and satellite coverage are unreliable in this terrain — do not rely on a phone alone for emergencies.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Neve and Colonial Glaciers, some opening up by mid-to-late season.
Rockfall potential on the summit gully/ridge.
Fast-changing North Cascades marine weather, including afternoon thunderstorms in summer.
Avalanche hazard on approach slopes with lingering spring snowpack.

pitchDetail.notes: Pyramid Lake Trail (~2 mi) then a steep climbers' trail up the East Ridge of Pyramid Peak to a camp around 5,500-5,700 ft at the Colonial Glacier moraine.
Rope up and cross the Colonial Glacier past the east flank of Paul Bunyan's Stump, climbing ~30-degree snow to the Colonial-Neve col (~6,800-6,840 ft).

pitchDetail.grade: Glacier travel, short ice steps to ~I2
pitchDetail.notes: Descend ~150-250 ft from the col onto the Neve Glacier and cross roped through its central, less-crevassed section to the ridge near The Horseman on Snowfield's west side (~7,700 ft).
Final ~400-600 ft of class 3-4 rock scrambling, occasionally verglas/rime-covered early season, to the summit; can be taken as difficult as class 5 on alternate lines.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Paved 2-lane state highway; the trailhead is reached without leaving pavement.
road.seasonalGate: SR-20's winter closure gate for the high passes lies well east of this trailhead (near Ross Dam/Diablo); the Marblemount-to-Pyramid-Lake segment typically stays open through winter even when the pass itself is shut, though sources vary on exact closure dates so check current WSDOT status before driving.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive SR-20 east about 21 miles (roughly 6.4 miles past Newhalem) to roadside pullout parking on the north side of the highway near milepost 127; the signed trail starts on the south side of the road.

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ301 (Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate — wet, cloudy, and cool with heavy orographic precipitation off the Pacific; summers are the driest and clearest window for climbing.
climate.spring: Continued heavy, wet snow loading and avalanche hazard on approach slopes into June.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers are most negotiable, though afternoon thunderstorms and rapid weather changes are common.
climate.fall: Shortening days and an increasing chance of early snow by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and significant avalanche hazard make this a very serious, rarely-attempted winter objective.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (within the Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: No NOCA entrance fee; overnight backcountry camping requires a permit costing $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee.
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry camping permit is required for camps at the East Ridge of Pyramid and beyond, issued at the Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA.
access.closures: Fixed anchors are prohibited park-wide under NPS wilderness regulations — clean climbing techniques (temporary slings, cams, nuts) only.

timing.recommendedStart: Early departure from high camp (roughly 6-7am) to cross the Neve Glacier while snow bridges are firm, per multiple trip reports.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff: 360-336-9450 (or 911, which may route to a distant dispatch center from wilderness terrain)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — 360-854-7200; after-hours NPS Communications Center 360-873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 360-856-6021 (Skagit Valley Hospital in Mount Vernon, 360-424-4111, is the larger regional facility further away)
emergency.notes: Cell and satellite phone coverage are both described by the NPS as unreliable/spotty in this terrain; carry a PLB or two-way satellite communicator and expect rescue response to potentially take hours to days.

routes.corrections: Elevation for the summit varies slightly by source: Mountain Project and willhiteweb.com list 8,347 ft, while Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and PeakVisor list 8,351 ft (matching the given data) — the higher, more consistently cross-confirmed figure was used. Mountain Project catalogs this same line under the name "Neve Glacier / West Ridge" rather than "Standard Route"; the given routeName was retained as instructed. No first-ascent information for the peak or this route could be found despite checking Wikipedia, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountain Project, and multiple guidebook-referencing trip reports.

blurb: Snowking Mountain is a glaciated 7,433-foot granite summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, Skagit County, about 18 miles northeast of Darrington and managed by the Mt. Baker Ranger District (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest). Often called an "under-visited granite massif clad in ice," it carries an informally-named glacier (the Snowking Glacier) on its north face and is flanked by two subsidiary summits, West Peak (7,425') and Middle Peak (7,400'), with Found, Cyclone, and Snowking Lakes ringing its base. No first-ascent record for the peak could be confirmed from available sources.
hierarchyNote: No hierarchy mismatch found — name, location (Glacier Peak Wilderness/North Cascades, Skagit County, ~18 mi NE of Darrington), and character are consistent and well cross-checked across Wikipedia, SummitPost, WTA, and Peakbagger listings. Minor elevation discrepancy only: given peak elevation is 7,439 ft; Wikipedia/SummitPost/Willhiteweb/LemkeClimbs/Eric's Base Camp consistently cite 7,433 ft, while ListsOfJohn/Peakbagger search results show 7,440 ft. The 7,433 ft figure is the most cross-corroborated (4+ independent sources) — see route-level correction note.

objHaz: crevasses / blue ice on the glacier (especially late summer)
avalanche hazard on wet spring snow
route-finding / whiteout on the undulating ridge
routes.season: Apr-Jul (glacier/snow route; late-season travel exposes crevasses and blue ice requiring roped travel)
routes.face: North Face / Snowking Glacier
routes.overview: The standard line follows the Kindy Ridge climbers' trail and undulating ridge crest west past Found Peak to the Cyclone Lake basin, then climbs the broad, informally-named Snowking Glacier on the peak's north side to a short class 2-3 summit block.
routes.beta: This is the most-used of Snowking's four standard lines, chosen by roughly three-quarters of parties for its directness, though it starts low (about 2,300 ft) and doesn't tour the whole mountain. Most groups treat it as a two-day trip with a bivy near Cyclone Lake, though strong parties have done it car-to-car in a long day (9-14 hrs) on firm spring snow. It is glacier travel, not technical climbing — ice axe, crampons, and a rope with basic crevasse-rescue gear are standard, and parties are advised to stay roped through the upper snowfield/glacier, especially once blue ice and crevasses open up in high summer.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive about 14.2 miles up Cascade River Road and turn right (north) onto Forest Road 1570 (Kindy Creek Road). The road has historically required two small creek/river crossings and high-clearance vehicles; recent trip reports describe washouts around 1,200-1,300 ft that force roughly 3 miles of road-walking to reach the true trailhead near 2,300 ft (check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF road conditions before driving). From the road's end, a steep, unmaintained but well-flagged climbers' trail gains about 1,700 vertical feet in roughly a mile through old-growth forest, easing above ~4,000 ft for another 600 ft/0.5 mile to the top of the trail near 4,950-5,000 ft. From there follow the base of Kindy Ridge west, over or around Found Peak (~5,600 ft) and an undulating ridge crest, to the Cyclone Lake basin (common camp) at roughly 5,600-5,800 ft. Continuous snow is typically encountered by about 3,800-4,800 ft depending on the season and aspect.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down-climb/glissade the glacier and snowfield back to Cyclone Lake, then retrace the ridge and climbers' trail to the FR-1570 trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend the glacier the same way it was ascended, staying roped on any crevassed sections and watching for breakable crust or soft, wet afternoon snow that can make the descent slower and more strenuous than the climb. On the ridge, several trip reports flag the descent from the roughly 4,760-ft saddle as a place parties lose the route — track elevation/bearing carefully (altimeter or GPS recommended) to avoid dropping into the wrong drainage. Below the snowline the climbers' trail reappears; watch for the unmarked junction near 2,300-2,350 ft where the trail meets the old logging road, and budget for the multi-mile road-walk if FR-1570 remains washed out at the lower end.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the FR-1570 trailhead (or its washout-limited parking) and hike/ski the Kindy Ridge climbers' trail and ridge crest to a high camp near Found Peak or the Cyclone Lake basin (5,000-5,800 ft) — roughly 7-11 miles and 5,000-7,000 ft of gain depending on trailhead access. Day 2: Pre-dawn start on firm snow, ascend the Snowking Glacier/snowfield to the summit (7,433 ft), then descend the same route to camp and out, exiting car-to-car in a single long day if conditions, daylight, and fitness allow.
routes.bail: There are no fixed rappel anchors on this route; retreat means reversing the same terrain — descending the glacier/snowfield to the Cyclone Lake basin or the Found Peak saddle and walking off the ridge and climbers' trail the way you came. Turn back before committing to the upper glacier if visibility drops or snow conditions deteriorate, since route-finding on the ridge in a whiteout is a documented hazard.
