routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time before starting based on how quickly the snowpack softens (spring afternoons produce breakable crust and wet-slide hazard), current avalanche conditions, and the length of the descent plus road-walk out — the standard approach alone can be 6-9+ hours one way, so an early alpine start and a firm, weather-driven cutoff matter more than a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb Snowking via this route in spring to early summer (roughly April-July) on skis/snowshoes, timing the climb for firm overnight snow and starting pre-dawn (climberkyle reports a 4:30 AM start) to be off the upper mountain before the snow softens. By full summer the glacier opens crevasses and exposes blue ice, making roped glacier travel and crevasse-rescue gear necessary; the lower approach trail is brushiest before snow consolidates it, typically by 3,800-4,000 ft.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed on the standard route. Carry a 30-40m rope for glacier travel and crevasse rescue, 2-3 pickets or ice screws for anchors, a crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks/pulley/carabiners), harness, and a helmet.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier-travel protection only — pickets or ice screws for crevasse-rescue anchors and belays; no fixed rock protection is placed on the standard line.
whatToBring: altimeter or GPS for ridge navigation
overnight/bivy gear if doing the common 2-day itinerary
proTips: Start pre-dawn (around 4:30 AM in spring) to travel on firm overnight snow before it softens.
Bivying near scenic Cyclone Lake breaks the long day into two manageable legs and is the most common camp.
Carry an altimeter or GPS — the ridge crest and saddles are easy to misjudge, especially descending from the ~4,760-ft saddle.
Even when the lower trail is dry, expect full snow travel above roughly 3,800-4,000 ft well into early summer.
watchOut: Losing the correct line off the ~4,760-ft saddle on descent is a repeatedly reported navigation error.
Breakable crust and heavy wet snow on spring afternoons slow the descent significantly.
FR-1570 has a history of washouts that can add several miles of road-walking to the approach.
The lower climbers' trail is steep, brushy, and has downed logs above about 3,000 ft.
knownHazards: Crevasses and exposed blue ice on the Snowking Glacier by mid-to-late summer; parties are advised to stay roped.
Elevated avalanche danger on wet, heavy spring snowpack typical of this west-slope Cascades location.
Route-finding difficulty on the undulating, often snow-covered Kindy Ridge crest, particularly in poor visibility.
An aging, flood-damaged access road (FR-1570) with washouts and, historically, a partially-collapsed bridge.

pitchDetail.pitch: Trailhead to top of climbers' trail
pitchDetail.notes: Steep, unmaintained but flagged path through old-growth forest from ~2,300 ft to ~4,950-5,000 ft; roughly 1,700 ft gained in about a mile before the grade eases.

pitchDetail.pitch: Kindy Ridge to Found Peak / saddle
pitchDetail.notes: Undulating forested-then-open ridge crest with several ups and downs; snow-covered by early season.
Traverse to the common high-camp basin near Cyclone Lake, roughly 5,600-5,800 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Cyclone Lake to base of Snowking Glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Approach across snowfields to the toe of the glacier; rope up here in crevassed conditions.
Ascend the broad north-side glacier/snowfield; crevasse hazard increases through the season as blue ice is exposed.
Short scramble on rock/talus to the true summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road to Forest Road 1570 (Kindy Creek Road)
road.status: Gravel FS road historically subject to washouts; recent reports describe the drivable section ending around 1,200-1,300 ft, well short of the ~2,300 ft trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Cascade River Road is typically closed/gated in winter and opens seasonally (roughly late spring) depending on snowpack; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF road/alert pages before travel
road.driveNote: From Marblemount drive about 14.2 miles up Cascade River Road, then turn right (north) onto FR-1570; high-clearance recommended for creek crossings, and be prepared to road-walk the final 1-3 miles if the road is washed out.

climate.forecastZone: NWS "West Slopes North Cascades and Passes" zone (WAZ567); nearest NWAC backcountry avalanche zone covers the west-slope North Cascades
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate — Pacific fronts drive strong orographic precipitation on this west-slope North Cascades location, with heavy winter snowfall and drier conditions from roughly July-September.
climate.spring: Wet, heavy snowpack with elevated avalanche danger; snow becomes continuous by roughly 3,800-4,800 ft; afternoons bring softening/breakable crust.
climate.summer: Glacier opens up with exposed blue ice and crevasses by mid-to-late summer, requiring roped travel; lower trail dries out and becomes brushy.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows as new snow returns to upper elevations; not well documented in available trip reports.
climate.winter: Heavy, wet Cascade snowfall; access road typically closed/impassable and avalanche hazard high.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mt. Baker Ranger District; route lies within the Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency pass required at the trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent Interagency/America the Beautiful pass)

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start (around 4:30 AM in spring) to travel on firm, refrozen snow before afternoon softening

waypoints.name: FR-1570 / Kindy Creek Road trailhead area

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 416-1911; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, 810 State Route 20, Sedro-Woolley, WA — (360) 854-2553
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital / Skagit Regional Health, Sedro-Woolley (closest along the Cascade River Road approach); Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (larger 24-hr ER)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness terrain with no confirmed cell coverage sourced for this specific route; technical mountain rescues in this area are handled by Skagit Mountain Rescue, a Skagit County SAR unit dispatched only through the Sheriff's Office.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,439 ft) does not match the best-corroborated figure: Wikipedia, SummitPost's own description, Willhiteweb, LemkeClimbs, and Eric's Base Camp all independently cite 7,433 ft, while ListsOfJohn/Peakbagger search snippets show 7,440 ft. Used 7,433 ft (route highPointFt) as the most cross-corroborated value (4+ independent sources); the discrepancy from the given 7,439 ft should be reviewed against a topo/USGS source. Note also that Snowking has four named standard lines (Kindy Ridge/NE Ridge, Northwest Route, West Route, Illabot Creek Route) plus an East Ridge variant reached via the same Kindy Ridge approach; this entry describes the glacier-finish variant matching the given route name "Standard Route / Snowking Glacier," which several sources describe as the most-traveled overall approach (~75% of parties use the Kindy Ridge approach, of which the glacier finish is the common early/mid-season line).

hierarchyNote: Peak identity and hierarchy check out: The Chopping Block (officially "Pinnacle Peak" on USGS maps) is a real, well-documented summit in the Southern Picket Range, North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA (48.755°N, -121.308°W per Wikipedia/USGS), consistent with a North Cascades / Cascades parent hierarchy. One data-quality flag: the given elevationFt (6809) differs from the figure repeated across Wikipedia and cross-checked search aggregation (6,819 ft) — see route corrections. Sources also show minor elevation variance across sites (Mountain Project lists 6,739 ft for its area page; a 1961 AAC first-ascent note lists 6,805 ft), which is common for this kind of semi-official, gneiss-summit peak with a name that isn't the official USGS name — treat 6,819 ft (Wikipedia, most consistently repeated) as the best available figure.
blurb: The Chopping Block — officially named Pinnacle Peak on USGS maps, though almost no one uses that name — is a 6,819-ft summit in the Southern Picket Range within North Cascades National Park, Washington, rising above Terror Basin and Crescent Creek Basin near the head of Goodell Creek. It is built of Skagit Gneiss, part of the North Cascades' accreted-terrane geology, and its cairned summit is visible from the Newhalem visitor center on clear days. First climbed in 1932 by Bill Degenhardt (for whom neighboring Mount Degenhardt is named) and H. V. Strandberg, it is regarded as one of the more approachable objectives in the notoriously rugged Southern Pickets and is commonly linked with climbs of Mount Terror, Mount Degenhardt, and Inspiration Peak.

routes.fa: 1932, Bill Degenhardt & H. V. Strandberg
objHaz: loose rock/talus in the approach basin
exposed no-fall ridge/ledge terrain near the summit
early-season snow and residual snowfields on the approach
brushy, unmaintained trail (devil's club) on the Terror Creek approach
routes.face: South side of the peak, reached via the Stump Hollow ridge
routes.overview: The original 1932 line up the peak's south side, gained from the Stump Hollow ridge above Terror Creek; a moderate, low-5th-class scramble/climb to the cairned summit, generally regarded as one of the least intimidating objectives among the Southern Pickets summits.
routes.beta: Climbers approach via Goodell Creek and Terror Creek to a high camp on the Stump Hollow ridge below the peak, then climb 3rd/4th class terrain with a short section of low-5th-class rock (5.4) to reach the summit. Because the Pickets guard several summits from a shared basin, parties frequently combine this peak with Mount Degenhardt, Mount Terror, or Inspiration Peak on the same trip. Note: contemporary trip reports (SummitPost/StephAbegg, blogs, Mountain Project) most often describe the peak's standard lines as a 'Northwest Route' (Class 4, traversing ledges on the north face between the NE and NW ridges) and a distinct 'Northeast Ridge' (5.5, 4 pitches, per Mountain Project) — both broadly consistent in character and difficulty with the historic South Route described here, but no source located during this research explicitly used the label 'South Route' for a specific documented line, so treat route-specific pitch/rappel counts with caution.
routes.approach: From WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) near Newhalem, park at the Goodell Creek day-use/group campground area on the north side of the highway (across from Newhalem Campground). Follow the old Goodell Creek road/trail, then the unmaintained, brushy Terror Creek 'trail' (heavy devil's club and mossy boulder sections in the lower creek) up into Terror Basin. Cross Terror Creek and climb steeply up the Stump Hollow ridge; a distinctive 'T'-shaped tree marks the ridge crest in some trip-report descriptions. Continue along the ridge, passing Stump Hollow, to a high camp on the ridge descending from the Chopping Block, below the peak's south side.
routes.descent: Descend the same south-side ridge/route back to the Stump Hollow camp, using rappels where the ridge steepens.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb and rappel the south-side ridge back toward the Stump Hollow saddle. Parties climbing the peak's closely related northeast/northwest lines report descending with a handful of single-rope rappels (documented as roughly 5 raps on a 60m rope for the adjacent Northeast Ridge line) back to the approach ridge, then reversing the Stump Hollow/Terror Creek approach to Goodell Creek.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead (Goodell Creek, WA-20 near Newhalem) to high camp on the Stump Hollow ridge below the Chopping Block, via Terror Creek and Stump Hollow (a long, brushy, and steep approach day). Day 2: Early start from camp, climb the South Route to the summit and return to camp same day (parties report round trips of a few hours from a ridge camp in good conditions). Day 3: Hike out via Terror Creek/Goodell Creek to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally straightforward back down the south-side ridge to the Stump Hollow camp; rappel any steep or exposed sections rather than downclimbing if conditions are wet, snowy, or the party is uncertain of the line.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, brushy, multi-day approach and the remoteness of the Southern Pickets, set a firm turnaround time that allows a full daylight margin to get back down technical terrain and off the ridge before dark, and build in extra time for route-finding on the descent.
routes.bestSeason: Most recorded ascents and trip reports cluster in July through September, after seasonal snow has receded enough to make the Terror Creek approach and Stump Hollow ridge passable, but before fall weather deteriorates; early-season parties should expect lingering snowfields on the approach and possibly on shaded ledges near the summit.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Southern Pickets backcountry is unreliable; the National Park Service explicitly cautions climbers not to rely on cell phones as an evacuation plan and recommends a satellite phone or Personal Locator Beacon as a backup.
routes.proNeeds: A light alpine rack sufficient for scattered low-5th-class rock protection (the terrain is closer to 3rd/4th class scrambling with a short 5.4 crux) plus rappel gear for the descent; exact route-specific rack recommendations were not found for this named line.
gear: approach shoes/light boots suited to brush and scrambling
whatToBring: bivy/camp gear for the multi-day approach
water treatment (basin/snowmelt sources noted along the ridge)
satellite communicator or PLB (no reliable cell coverage)
proTips: Several parties link this peak with Mount Degenhardt, Mount Terror, and/or Inspiration Peak from the same high camp, since all are reached via the same Terror Basin/Stump Hollow approach.
The Terror Creek approach trail is unmaintained and brushy (devil's club, mossy boulders); budget significant extra time versus a maintained trail.
watchOut: The lower Terror Creek approach involves difficult brush and slick, mossy boulder-hopping stream crossings.
Loose talus and scree are present in the approach basin below the peak.
Ridge and ledge terrain near the summit is exposed with little margin for error.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock on approach slopes and ledges.
Residual snowfields on the approach and route into early summer.
Very limited/no cell coverage for emergency communication.

road.name: WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Goodell Creek trailhead near Newhalem
road.status: Paved and generally open year-round at this location (Newhalem is west of the seasonally gated Washington Pass section of WA-20)
road.driveNote: Trailhead access is directly off WA-20 at the Goodell Creek group campground/day-use area, on the north side of the highway across from Newhalem Campground — no unpaved approach road is required to reach the trailhead itself.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate with heavy winter snowpack and a relatively short, drier alpine climbing window in summer.
climate.spring: Persistent snowpack on approach slopes and shaded terrain; stream crossings (e.g., Terror Creek) can run high with snowmelt.
climate.summer: The primary climbing season; still commonly has residual snow into July at this elevation and aspect.
climate.fall: Shorter days and increasing storm frequency; a viable but narrowing window into September.
climate.winter: Not a climbing season for this route — heavy snow and avalanche hazard in the approach terrain.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: No NPS entrance fee for North Cascades National Park; a wilderness/backcountry permit fee of $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee applies for overnight stays (youth 15 and under free).
access.permit: An NPS wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in the backcountry cross-country zones used to access this peak (Terror Basin/Stump Hollow), obtainable via Recreation.gov reservation or as a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: No park entrance pass required (North Cascades NP is fee-free for day use); wilderness permit required for overnight/backcountry travel.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit); primary road access via Skagit County (Newhalem/Marblemount)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff (non-emergency): 360-384-5390; Skagit County Sheriff (non-emergency): 360-336-9450; call 911 for active emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — permits/info: 360-854-7245; park headquarters: 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (24-hr ER, 360-856-6021); Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (360-424-4111)
emergency.notes: NPS coordinates backcountry search and rescue with the county Sheriff's Office and volunteer mountain rescue units. When reporting an incident, be prepared to give a precise location, injury details, time/cause of the accident, and terrain description; cell coverage in this area is unreliable, so a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.

routes.corrections: Peak elevation: the given elevationFt (6809) is close to but not matched by the most consistently cited figure (6,819 ft, Wikipedia/USGS-derived, repeated across multiple aggregated sources); Mountain Project's area page separately lists 6,739 ft and a 1961 AAC first-ascent note lists 6,805 ft for the same summit, reflecting genuine cross-source variance for this peak. Route naming: no source found during this research used the specific label 'South Route' for a documented line on this peak — the closest, well-documented lines are Mountain Project's 'Northeast Ridge' (5.5, 4 pitches) and a 'Northwest Route' (Class 4, ledge traverse across the north face) described in multiple trip reports, both of which appear related to but not confirmed identical to a distinct historic south-side line. Grade/FA fields for 'South Route' were retained as given since no clearly better-sourced replacement was found, but pitch count, route length, and rappel count were left null rather than borrowing figures from the differently-named adjacent routes.

hierarchyNote: HIERARCHY MISMATCH: The Brothers is located in the OLYMPIC MOUNTAINS on the Olympic Peninsula (straddling the Olympic National Park / The Brothers Wilderness boundary above Hood Canal, Jefferson/Mason County), NOT in the North Cascades or the Cascade Range at all. It is a completely separate range from the Cascades — roughly 60+ miles west across Puget Sound from the nearest North Cascades peaks. The peak/coordinates/elevation given (~6,866-6,868 ft, 47.65N/123.14W) do correctly identify the real, well-known "The Brothers" (twin-summited peak visible from Seattle, named 1856 by George Davidson), so the underlying peak data is sound — but if this record's parent hierarchy in the DB places it under a North Cascades/Cascades region or range node, that parent assignment is geographically wrong and should be corrected to an Olympic Mountains / Olympic Peninsula region node instead. Proceeding with full research under the given peak id as instructed.
blurb: The Brothers is a striking twin-summited peak in the Olympic Mountains, straddling the boundary of Olympic National Park and The Brothers Wilderness (Olympic National Forest) above Hood Canal in western Washington. Composed of Eocene-age basalt, it is one of the most recognizable summits on the "Olympic skyline" as seen from Seattle, with a higher south peak (historically called Mount Edward) and a slightly lower north peak (Mount Arthur). Surveyor George Davidson named the mountain in 1856 for two brothers of his fiancée; the peak was first climbed in pieces — the north summit in 1908 by C. Hill and W. Hill, and the higher south summit in 1912 by a party including I. Collier, O. Corkenill, W. Dehn, W. Fish, E. Goldsmith, and H. Trumbull.

routes.overview: The historic, standard line to The Brothers' higher south summit: a long avalanche-prone slope leads up the Lena Creek drainage to a narrowing rock/snow couloir ("the Hourglass"), which broadens above into a short Class 3 (occasionally Class 4) rock scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: A long, mostly non-technical mountaineering route that is more of a route-finding and stamina challenge than a climbing one. From the Lena Lake Trailhead, hike past Lena Lake and through the Valley of the Silent Men to a base-camp area near where the trail meets Lena Creek's forks. From there, climbers' paths lead up the creek drainage to a broad avalanche slope/meadow below the first cliff band; cross this and enter the couloir (snow- or rock-filled depending on season), following it up through the narrow "Hourglass" constriction near ~6,200 ft. Above the Hourglass the couloir broadens; work up and right, staying on firmer rock, then trend back left/up through loose scree to the summit. Early/mid-season the couloir holds continuous snow (ice axe and crampons required, with snow up to 40-45 degrees); by late summer it is a loose, snow-free scree-and-rock scramble.
routes.approach: From Hoodsport, drive 14 miles north on US Highway 101 to Forest Service Road 25 (Hamma Hamma Recreation Area); turn west and follow FSR 25, paved, about 7.6 miles to the Lena Lake/Brothers Trailhead (large parking area, vault toilet, day-use fee machine). Hike the Lena Lake Trail (#810) about 3 miles to Lena Lake, then continue on the Brothers Trail (#821) through the Valley of the Silent Men, crossing Lena Creek, to informal campsites near the Lena Creek forks (roughly 7 miles and a common base camp for a two-day trip). Beyond camp there is no maintained trail; climbers' paths (sometimes flagged) lead up the drainage to an avalanche meadow/runout below the couloir's entrance.
routes.descent: Descend the same South Couloir line; when snow conditions allow, glissade the lower avalanche slope below the Hourglass (remove crampons before glissading).
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb the upper couloir and Hourglass on firm rock/snow, staying alert for other parties and rockfall below you, then descend the lower avalanche slope back toward the Lena Creek base camp. In snow season this lower slope is a popular glissade, but crampons must be removed first to avoid a crampon-catch injury; several trip reports specifically warn against glissading with crampons on. There is no established rappel line on this route — retreat and descent are both by down-climbing/glissading the ascent line back to the Lena Lake Trail and out.
routes.itinerary: Commonly climbed over two days: Day 1 — hike from the Lena Lake Trailhead past Lena Lake and through the Valley of the Silent Men (~7 miles) to an informal camp near the Lena Creek forks. Day 2 — climb the South Couloir to the summit and return to camp, then hike out (some parties split the hike-out into a third day given the ~17-18 mile round trip). Strong, fit parties occasionally complete it car-to-car in one long day.
routes.bail: There is no fixed bail/rappel infrastructure on this route. Retreat from anywhere on the couloir is by reversing the ascent line — down-climb (or glissade, snow permitting, with crampons removed) back down through the Hourglass and the lower avalanche slope to the Lena Creek base camp and the maintained Brothers/Lena Lake Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the route funnels through a long, avalanche- and rockfall-prone gully, set a turnaround time before starting the couloir so the party is off the upper snow/rock and back through the Hourglass before afternoon warming increases wet-slide, cornice-collapse, and rockfall risk; the long hike back to the trailhead should also be factored into the decision to turn around.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through June/early July is a favored window, when the couloir still holds continuous snow for faster travel and a glissade descent, though avalanche hazard is highest during and right after this period. By late July-August the couloir is typically snow-free, converting the route to a loose Class 3-4 scree-and-rock scramble that needs no snow gear but has more rockfall and loose-rock exposure. Winter ascents face serious avalanche hazard in the Olympics' heavy, wet maritime snowpack.
routes.comms: No route-specific cell data was found; cell coverage across Olympic National Forest's backcountry valleys (including drainages like Hamma Hamma/Lena Creek) is widely and consistently reported as unreliable to absent. Parties should plan on no cell service and consider a satellite communicator or PLB.
whatToBring: Sun protection for snow glare in the couloir
Extra insulating layers for wind at camp/summit
Trekking poles for the long approach
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock protection rack is standard — this is a snow-gully and scramble route, not a roped rock climb. The core kit is a general mountaineering setup: ice axe and crampons for the snow-filled couloir (essential whenever snow remains, especially in the firm conditions typical of early morning), a helmet for rockfall/icefall protection through the constricted Hourglass section, and the ten essentials for the long, remote approach. A rope is not standard for competent parties but some bring a short rope/light rack to protect the Class 3-4 rock steps for a less experienced partner.
routes.proNeeds: This is not a route protected by gear placements — the primary hazards are objective (avalanche/wet-slide on the approach slope, rockfall and icefall in the Hourglass, loose rock near the summit) rather than requiring a traditional rack. A helmet is the single most important piece of "protection" on this route.
proTips: Climb in late May-June while the couloir still holds continuous snow for faster travel and a glissade descent; by late August it becomes a loose, snow-free scree scramble.
Camp near the Lena Creek forks base camp to split the long approach into two comfortable days.
In the broader upper section above the Hourglass, work up and right on firmer rock before trending back left toward the summit to avoid the worst loose scree.
Carry crampons and an ice axe whenever any snow remains in the couloir — morning conditions are often firm even in late season.
watchOut: The route funnels through a long avalanche start zone below the couloir; check the NWAC Olympics zone forecast before climbing when snow is present.
The Hourglass constriction concentrates rockfall and icefall from parties or natural release above — helmets are essential.
Late-season snow bridges/moats can form near the top of the Hourglass, complicating passage.
Never glissade with crampons still on — remove them first to avoid a serious crampon-catch injury on the descent.
knownHazards: A long, open avalanche-prone slope forms the lower approach into the couloir, with reported wet-slide and cornice hazard.
The narrow 'Hourglass' section near ~6,200 ft is exposed to rockfall and icefall funneled from above.
Loose, unstable rock and 'unpleasant scree' in the broader upper couloir and near the summit block create fall and rockfall hazard for parties both above and below.
Route-finding is difficult, especially around the base-camp area where numerous braided climbers' paths and creek forks can lead parties astray.

pitchDetail.notes: Lena Lake Trailhead to informal base camp near the Lena Creek forks, roughly 7 miles through old growth and the Valley of the Silent Men.
Long open slope leading up to an avalanche meadow/runout below the first cliff band around 5,000 ft; primary avalanche exposure on the route.
Narrow constriction near ~6,200 ft; loose rock, rockfall/icefall exposure, and the technical crux of the route.
Broadens above the Hourglass; stay climber's right on firmer rock, then work back up/left through loose scree to the true (south) summit.

routes.season: May-Sep (snow-filled couloir favored late May-Jun; snow-free scramble by late Aug)
routes.face: South Couloir (Hourglass)

road.name: Hamma Hamma Recreation Area Road (Forest Service Road 25)
road.status: Paved to the Lena Lake Trailhead
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport, drive ~14 miles north on US 101 to FSR 25 (Hamma Hamma Recreation Area); turn west and continue ~7.6 paved miles to the Lena Lake/Brothers Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC 'Olympics' avalanche/weather zone
climate.typical: Wet maritime climate; the Olympic Mountains receive some of the heaviest annual precipitation and snowfall in the Lower 48, producing heavy, wet ('Cascade concrete'-style) snow with corresponding avalanche and wet-slide hazard on the approach slopes.
climate.spring: Continuous snow typically still covers the couloir into May-June, giving the fastest travel and a glissade descent but with elevated avalanche/wet-slide hazard.
climate.summer: By late July-August the couloir is largely snow-free, converting the route to a loose Class 3-4 scree/rock scramble.
climate.fall: Generally drier with the couloir snow-free; shortening daylight becomes the main planning constraint.
climate.winter: Heavy, wet Pacific maritime snowpack creates serious avalanche hazard; winter ascents are a significant undertaking best left to experienced parties.

access.landManager: Olympic National Forest, Hood Canal Ranger District (Quilcene Office); the summit and upper route straddle the boundary with Olympic National Park and The Brothers Wilderness.
access.fees: $5/day (or $30/year) day-use fee at the Lena Lake/Brothers Trailhead fee machine (cash not accepted); Northwest Forest Pass and Interagency/America the Beautiful passes are accepted in lieu of the fee.
access.permit: A self-issue wilderness permit is required for overnight stays within The Brothers Wilderness (available at the trailhead); a separate NPS wilderness permit is needed if camping within Olympic National Park, which the upper approach borders/crosses into.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass (or pay the day-use fee)

timing.recommendedStart: An early (pre-dawn) start is recommended given the long approach and the avalanche/rockfall hazard in the couloir, which increases with daytime warming.

emergency.county: Jefferson County, WA (the trailhead sits essentially on the Mason/Jefferson county line, with the route and summit within Jefferson County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jefferson County Sheriff's Office / JeffCom911 — non-emergency (360) 344-9779; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest, Hood Canal Ranger District, Quilcene Office — 295142 Highway 101 S, Quilcene, WA 98376, (360) 765-2200
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason Health / Mason General Hospital, Shelton, WA — 24-hr Emergency Department, (360) 426-1611; nearest by road via US 101 south through Hoodsport (roughly 35 miles from the trailhead).
emergency.notes: Jefferson County Search & Rescue (JSAR) is the active volunteer SAR unit for Lena Lake/Brothers Wilderness incidents; activate via 911. Cell coverage in the Hamma Hamma/Lena Creek drainage is unreliable to nonexistent — a satellite communicator/PLB is strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (6,868 ft) is close to but not identical to published figures: climbing-specific sources (Mountain Project, WTA, willhiteweb, Eric's Basecamp) consistently cite 6,866 ft for the true (south) summit, while Wikipedia and a Peakbagger-derived citation (via trailcatjim.com) cite 6,842 ft. Direct fetches of peakbagger.com and summitpost.org both returned HTTP 403 (blocked) despite repeated attempts, so their exact figures could not be confirmed firsthand; the 6,842 ft figure is corroborated only via a secondary citation of Peakbagger's data. highPointFt above uses 6,866 ft as the figure most consistently cited by climbing/mountaineering-specific sources. Separately, some sources describe the approach following the 'west fork' of Lena Creek (Eric's Basecamp/willhiteweb) while Mountain Project's route description says 'east fork' — this could not be resolved and is omitted from directional claims in the approach text above.

hierarchyNote: Peak identity and hierarchy check out: Inspiration Peak is a real, distinct summit in the Southern Pickets, North Cascades National Park, Whatcom Co., WA (~48.771°N, -121.288°W), correctly nested under the Pickets/North Cascades. However, the given elevationFt (7,963 ft) does not match any source found: Wikipedia/USGS-derived data lists 7,891 ft and Mountain Project lists 7,880 ft (the two agree closely). Recommend correcting the peak's elevation to ~7,891 ft.
blurb: Inspiration Peak (7,891 ft) is a steep, thin, knife-edged summit on the eastern end of the Southern Pickets in North Cascades National Park, standing between Mount Degenhardt and the McMillan Spires above the heavily glaciated Terror Basin/McMillan Cirque. Composed of solid granodiorite (Skagit gneiss), it offers some of the best moderate alpine rock in the range. Fred Beckey and his brother Helmy made the first ascent via the West Ridge on August 29, 1940, a route Beckey later named among his 100 favorite North American climbs.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, August 29, 1940 (first winter ascent by Colin Haley and Forrest Murphy, February 8, 2003, also via the West Ridge)
routes.rappels: ~5 rappels (~30m/100ft each) down the ridge crest back to the glacier above the bergschrund; a single 60m rope is sufficient; anchors were fully replaced in June 2024
objHaz: Rockfall/loose blocks in the lower gully and corner sections
Crevasse fall risk crossing the Terror Glacier on approach
Moat/bergschrund crossing at the base of the route
Very remote location — slow rescue response
routes.overview: A striking, sustained 5.7 rock line up the exposed west ridge of Inspiration Peak, widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine rock routes in the Southern Pickets.
routes.beta: The route gains the toe of the west ridge via a gully roughly 300 ft above the bergschrund, then climbs sustained 5.7 face and corner terrain with improving rock quality, crosses a band of lichen-covered rock, regains the crest on extremely good rock, and finishes up a featured summit headwall to the top.
routes.approach: Park at the Upper Goodell Creek trailhead off SR-20 near Newhalem (not the lower Goodell Creek Campground — a common wrong turn). Follow the old roadbed/climbers' path up Goodell Creek — several relatively mellow, sometimes brushy/overgrown miles — before the trail turns steeply uphill with no switchbacks through forest (roughly 4,000 ft of gain) to treeline. Cross a col and descend scree/snow into Terror Basin, the standard camp at roughly 5,800 ft (National Park Service wilderness permit required). From camp, cross the crevassed Terror Glacier (2-3 hours of glacier travel) to the base of the west ridge, finishing with a moat/bergschrund crossing to reach the toe of the ridge.
routes.descent: Rappel the west ridge crest (~5 raps) back down to the glacier above the bergschrund.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ridge by rappelling its crest using fixed piton/bolt anchor stations (fully replaced in June 2024); most rappels run about 100 ft (30m) and a single 60m rope is adequate. The final rappel(s) return climbers to the glacier above the bergschrund, from which the approach is reversed back to Terror Basin camp.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-day plan: Day 1 — hike roughly 9 miles and 4,000 ft from the Upper Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin camp. Day 2 — alpine start, cross the Terror Glacier to the base of the West Ridge, climb the ~14-pitch 5.7 ridge to the 7,891 ft summit, rappel the route back to the glacier, and return to camp. Day 3 — hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The ridge can be rappelled from nearly any point using the fixed anchor stations; below the toe of the ridge, retreat is by reversing the glacier crossing used on approach.
routes.turnaround: Given the long glaciated approach and a multi-pitch, rappel-dependent descent, set a firm turnaround time that guarantees clearing the glacier crossing and reaching camp before dark, and reassess immediately if afternoon thunderstorms build or glacier snow-bridge conditions look marginal.
routes.bestSeason: Most parties climb in July, when the Terror Glacier crossing tends to be more consolidated with intact snow bridges; by mid-to-late August the glacier can become significantly more broken and crevassed, slowing the approach and requiring more careful route-finding.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in Terror Basin or on the peak — the Southern Pickets are deep wilderness with essentially no coverage. A satellite communicator/messenger is strongly recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A single set of cams from 0.1-3 inches with doubles in the 0.3-1 inch range covers the pitch-by-pitch protection; carry a little extra cord/tat and a couple of pins in case fixed anchors need backing up. A single 60-meter rope suffices for both leading and the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack sized for cracks and corners in Cascades granodiorite — nothing exotic, but bring extra cord for anchor backup on the descent.
gear: Extra cordelette/tat and a few pins
Crampons, ice axe, and glacier travel rope for the approach
whatToBring: Crampons and ice axe for the Terror Glacier crossing
Sturdy approach footwear for steep, brushy trail and glacier travel
Overnight camping gear for Terror Basin (wilderness permit required)
proTips: All rappel anchors on the ridge were replaced in June 2024, making the descent considerably safer and more straightforward than in prior years
Loose blocks litter the lower gully and corner sections — climb carefully and consider cleaning them to help establish this as a fully classic line
Start early enough to cross the glacier and moat while the snow is still firm
watchOut: Sections of the lower route are reportedly runout (R-rated) with sparse protection
Loose rock/blocks in the initial gully and corners
Sagging, deteriorating snow bridges on the Terror Glacier later in the season
knownHazards: Heavily crevassed Terror Glacier crossing on approach, worse later in summer
Rockfall/loose blocks on route
Remote location — any injury becomes serious given multi-day evacuation time
Committing rappel descent dependent on fixed anchors

pitchDetail.notes: Gully leads up to gain the toe of the west ridge, starting roughly 300 ft above the bergschrund.
Face climbing to corners and ledges; rock quality improves with height.
Traverse across a band of lichen-covered rock to regain the crest.
Sustained climbing on excellent rock along the ridge crest.
Summit headwall — featured, enjoyable rock to the top.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to the Upper Goodell Creek trailhead near Newhalem
road.status: Open essentially year-round at this location
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter (typically early December through mid-April) between Ross Dam (MP 134) and Silver Star (MP 171) — well east of Newhalem/Goodell Creek, so this access point is unaffected
road.driveNote: Park at Upper Goodell Creek, not the lower Goodell Creek Campground — a common wrong turn.

climate.typical: Maritime North Cascades alpine climate — wetter and cloudier than the eastern Cascades, with afternoon thunderstorm potential in summer.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; the glacier crossing is generally firmer in July and becomes more broken/crevassed by mid-to-late August.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall; essentially unclimbed outside rare winter ascents (first winter ascent February 2003).

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: $6 non-refundable permit/reservation fee plus $10 per person per night backcountry camping fee, charged mid-May through early October; no charge the rest of the year
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping in Terror Basin, via Recreation.gov reservation or first-come walk-up at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Fixed-anchor moratorium in park wilderness — no new bolts/fixed anchors without NPS authorization; use existing anchors and clean techniques.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from Terror Basin camp to cross the glacier and begin climbing while snow is firm, leaving a full daylight margin for the rappel descent and return to camp.

waypoints.name: West Ridge toe (route start, ~300 ft above the bergschrund)

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit); trailhead access is via Skagit County near Newhalem
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency: (360) 416-1911; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital, Sedro-Woolley: (360) 856-6021 (larger Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon: (360) 424-4111)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in Terror Basin/Southern Pickets — carry a satellite communicator. Rescue response in this terrain is slow; strong self-rescue capability and a float plan left with a contact are essential.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevationFt (7,963) does not match sources: Wikipedia/USGS-derived data lists 7,891 ft and Mountain Project lists 7,880 ft; corrected highPointFt to 7,891 ft. Given route data implied Grade IV; Mountain Project (the primary route source) and multiple independent trip reports consistently rate the West Ridge as Grade III, 5.7 — corrected commitment to III.

blurb: The Pyramid is a roughly 7,900–7,960 ft summit in the Southern Picket Range, a notoriously rugged and remote subrange of the North Cascades lying entirely within North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness. It sits on the range crest between Mount Degenhardt and Inspiration Peak, is built of the same biotite (Skagit) gneiss as the rest of the Pickets — solid in places, loose and chossy in others — and was first climbed in 1951. It is rarely climbed as a standalone objective, usually being tagged as a side-summit during longer Southern Pickets enchainments.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity confirmed: "The Pyramid" is a real, distinct summit in the Southern Picket Range (North Cascades National Park), correctly distinguished in this DB from the unrelated 7,189 ft "Pyramid Peak" near Diablo Lake (also Whatcom/Skagit area, different peak, different first ascent 1931). Parent hierarchy (Picket Range / North Cascades) is geographically correct. However, the given elevationFt (8,031 ft) does not match any source found: Wikipedia's Picket Range peak table gives 7,960 ft, Steph Abegg's trip reports and a North Cascades Institute source give ~7,920 ft, and a 1962 AAC article describes Southern Pickets summits generally as "about 8,000 ft." No source matched 8,031 ft exactly. Recommend re-verifying against a USGS topo/LiDAR-derived figure; I've used 7,960 ft (best single cited source) as the corrected highPointFt on the route and flagged the conflict in that route's `corrections` field. mountainproject.com and summitpost.org were both checked per the requirement — MP's Southern Pickets area page lists Pyramid Peak as a sub-area with 0 documented routes, and summitpost.org blocked direct fetch (403) so its content was cross-checked only via third-party citations of it; peakbagger.com's page for this peak (pid=23918) also returned 403 on every fetch attempt and could not be directly verified, though it was located via search.

objHaz: exposed low-5th-class terrain on the Barrier crossing
long, remote approach with no quick bail option
routes.face: South side / gully system, between Mount Degenhardt and Inspiration Peak
routes.overview: The South Route follows the broken south side of The Pyramid — mostly 3rd/4th-class scrambling with a short 5.6 step, typically taken up a gully system rather than the steeper overhanging rock directly above it — to the summit. It is almost always climbed as a side-trip during a longer Southern Pickets traverse rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.beta: From a high camp near the Terror Glacier or in Crescent Creek Basin, parties cross The Barrier (the connecting ridge between Terror and Crescent Creek basins) and work across broken, sometimes loose terrain toward the notch south of Mount Degenhardt, then traverse east to the base of Pyramid's south side. The line climbs a gully/couloir system on scrambling terrain with a short 5.6 step; staying left in the gully rather than attempting the overhanging rock directly above it is reported as the easier, more sensible line. Rock quality is inconsistent — solid gneiss in places, dirt- and choss-covered in others — and the peak sees comparatively little independent traffic, most parties tagging it en route between Degenhardt and Inspiration Peak.
routes.approach: Start at the Goodell Creek trailhead off WA-20 near Newhalem (the small parking area fills quickly in season). Follow the old logging-road bed roughly 4.5 miles, then pick up the climbers' path — marked by a rock cairn/arrow and post — that climbs steeply through forest and then open heather slopes, gaining about 4,000 ft to reach Terror Basin on the east side of the Southern Pickets. From a high camp near the Terror Glacier, cross the glacier and ascend to The Barrier via a moat crossing and low-5th-class rock. From the top of The Barrier, descend/traverse toward the notch south of Mount Degenhardt, then work east across broken terrain to reach the base of Pyramid's south side.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back to the Degenhardt notch, re-cross The Barrier, and descend to the Terror Basin/Terror Glacier camp.
routes.descentText: Down-climb and scramble back along the south-side gully to the notch south of Degenhardt, then reverse the Barrier crossing (down-climbing the low-5th-class rock and re-crossing the moat) before descending back across the Terror Glacier to camp. There is no separate rappel line reported for this route — descent is essentially a reversal of the approach/ascent.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent line; once past The Barrier there is no shorter bail option, so parties should commit to the Barrier crossing only with enough time/weather margin to reverse it.
routes.turnaround: Because return from Pyramid requires re-crossing The Barrier and re-traversing Terror Basin before reaching any maintained trail, set a turnaround time that leaves a comfortable margin to complete that full reversal in daylight and stable weather — this is remote, largely unrescuable terrain where an overdue party can mean a multi-day search-and-rescue response.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, after seasonal snow has melted enough to expose rock and open the moat crossing on The Barrier but before fall storms arrive. Some residual snow on the Terror Glacier crossing in early-to-mid summer can actually ease travel; by September the glacier is more broken and crevasses more exposed.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Picket Range is described by NPS as far from reliable, with many locations having no service at all; do not rely on a cell phone for evacuation planning here.
routes.detailedRack: A light rock rack (small-to-medium cams and a handful of nuts) covers the short 5.6 step; the bulk of the gear need is for the Terror Glacier crossing and Barrier traverse — rope, harness, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, and crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection is needed for the technical step itself; most of the route is scrambling. Protect the short 5.6 section and any exposed low-5th-class moves on the Barrier crossing.
whatToBring: Rope for glacier travel and short technical steps
Multi-day bivy/camp gear for the approach
proTips: Climb it as part of a Southern Pickets traverse (with Degenhardt and/or Inspiration Peak) rather than as a dedicated objective — it's rarely climbed alone
Stay in the gully/left side rather than the overhanging direct line above it, which is harder and looser than it looks
A high camp near the Terror Glacier or in Crescent Creek Basin sets up an efficient day for Pyramid
watchOut: The direct overhanging line looks climbable but is harder and looser than the gully to its left
The moat crossing at the top of The Barrier can be tricky depending on the snow year
Weather can close in quickly on this exposed, committing terrain
knownHazards: Loose, chossy rock on the approach ridge and Barrier crossing
Crevasse hazard crossing the Terror Glacier
Long, remote approach with no quick bail option once past The Barrier
Exposed low-5th-class terrain on the Barrier crossing

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road
road.status: Goodell Creek trailhead is reached from WA-20 near Newhalem; this western section of WA-20 is generally open year-round
road.seasonalGate: WA-20 itself closes seasonally at Washington Pass (well east of Newhalem, near Mazama) roughly mid-November to April/May due to avalanche danger — this closure does not affect access to Goodell Creek/Newhalem
road.driveNote: The small Goodell Creek trailhead parking area fills quickly during summer climbing season

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Maritime-influenced North Cascades climate: wet, heavy-snowpack winters and drier, more stable summers
climate.spring: Lingering deep snowpack and unstable avalanche conditions often persist into June
climate.summer: Warmest, driest, most climbable window (July-September), though still subject to afternoon thunderstorms
climate.fall: Increasing storm frequency and new snow accumulation by late September/October
climate.winter: Deep snowpack, high avalanche hazard, and very limited practical access

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness)
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee for a backcountry permit; free for youth 15 and under
access.permit: NPS wilderness backcountry permit required for any overnight stay in the Southern Pickets; reserve in advance via Recreation.gov or obtain in person (up to 3 days out) at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount

emergency.county: North Cascades National Park spans the Skagit/Whatcom county line; the Southern Pickets area is typically handled via Skagit County dispatch, with Whatcom County SAR (Bellingham Mountain Rescue) also active in the range
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff dispatch: 360-336-9450 / Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch: 360-384-5390
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — 360-854-7245 (main park line 360-854-7200)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 360-856-6021
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the Picket Range per NPS guidance; do not rely on cell/satellite devices alone for evacuation planning. Call 911 for any emergency.

routes.corrections: Summit elevation conflict: sources found (Wikipedia's Picket Range peak table: 7,960 ft; Steph Abegg / North Cascades Institute trip reports: ~7,920 ft; a 1962 AAC article's general description of Southern Pickets summits: 'about 8,000 ft') do not match the given 8,031 ft. No source matched 8,031 ft exactly. Used 7,960 ft (Wikipedia, the most specific single citation) as the corrected highPointFt; recommend confirming against a USGS topo/LiDAR source before treating either figure as final.

hierarchyNote: All researched facts (coordinates, elevation ~5,605-5,606 ft, Alpine Lakes Wilderness / Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF, ridge between Chair Peak and Bryant Peak near Snoqualmie Pass) confirm this is the well-known "The Tooth" peak and the location/name are correct and unambiguous. One flag: The Tooth sits in the Snoqualmie Pass / central Cascades area (just north of I-90, in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness), which most regional taxonomies label "Central Cascades," not "North Cascades" proper (which begins well north, around Hwy 20/Stevens Pass and beyond). If this record is bucketed under a literal "North Cascades" parent region in the DB hierarchy, it should probably sit under a Snoqualmie Pass / Central Cascades / Alpine Lakes Wilderness branch instead — worth a quick hierarchy check, though this does not affect the route data below.
blurb: The Tooth is a 5,605-5,606 ft fin-like andesite pillar in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Washington's central Cascades, rising above Source Lake on the ridge connecting Chair Peak and Bryant Peak just northwest of Snoqualmie Pass and the Alpental ski area. Its solid volcanic rock, short approach, and moderate climbing make its South Face the most heavily climbed technical alpine rock route in Washington State, first climbed in 1928 by Lloyd Anderson and Herman Wunderling (the peak's easier terrain was first reached in 1916 by Charles Hazelhurst and C.G. Morrison). The peak was historically called "Denny Horn" or "Denny Tooth" before "The Tooth" was formally adopted in 1918.

routes.overview: A short, four-pitch low-5th-class rock climb up the narrow south face of The Tooth from Pineapple Pass — juggy flakes, ledges, and short walls on solid andesite, widely regarded as the classic beginner alpine rock route near Seattle.
routes.beta: Climb starts at the saddle atop Pineapple Pass (the notch between The Tooth and the smaller pinnacle just south of it). Four pitches of low-5th-class climbing (a short 4th-class section included) wind up ledges and flakes to the summit; the crux 4th pitch has multiple options from juggy 5.4 flakes to a steeper 5.8 crack for those seeking harder climbing. Expect crowds, some loose rock, and outstanding summit views of the Stuart Range, Chair/Snoqualmie Mountain, and Mount Rainier.
routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson & Herman Wunderling, 1928
routes.rappels: 2 rappels (~25m each) down the east gully from bolted chain anchors using a single 60m rope (partially sheltered intermediate anchor roughly halfway); an alternate traffic-avoiding descent uses separate bolted anchors on the southwest side, done as ~4 shorter rappels.
objHaz: heavy crowding on weekends causing bottlenecks at Pineapple Pass and belays
loose rock and dirty scrambling on parts of the route
residual snow/moat at the base into early July
routes.season: Jun-Sep (best); also climbed in winter/spring by experienced parties in snowier, more serious conditions
routes.face: South Face, above Pineapple Pass
routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 52, go under the freeway and drive ~1.3-1.5 miles north on Alpental Road to the Snow Lake/Source Lake trailhead parking (gravel lot, ~3,100 ft, Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee required). Follow the Snow Lake Trail about 2 miles to roughly 4,100 ft near the Source Lake overlook junction, then branch onto the climbers' path (cairned) that traverses talus toward Source Lake and up into the basin below The Tooth's east face, staying above the valley floor to minimize elevation loss. Ascend steeply through cliff bands to the second notch south of the South Face, then work north to Pineapple Pass and a short scramble to the base of the route. Approach takes roughly 2-3 hours for a moderate party; in winter, use the valley-floor route via Source Lake instead of the summer climbers' path, which carries significant avalanche exposure.
routes.descent: Reverse the scramble to Pineapple Pass, then rappel the east gully (2 raps on bolted chain anchors, 60m rope) or use the separate SW-side bolted-anchor line to avoid ascending traffic.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties down-climb/scramble back to Pineapple Pass rather than rappelling the route. From Pineapple Pass, descend the east gully: two rappels of roughly 25m each from bolted chain anchors (there is a partially sheltered intermediate anchor about halfway down) using one 60m rope. On busy days, a second option exists using bolted anchors on the southwest side of the formation, broken into about four shorter rappels, which keeps descending parties separated from those still climbing the South Face. The descent gully sheds loose rock, so helmets should stay on and parties should be mindful of others below.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: ~50 min drive from Seattle to Alpental (I-90 Exit 52); 2-3 hr approach to Pineapple Pass; 2-3 hrs for 4 pitches of climbing; 1.5-2 hrs scramble/rappel descent; ~1-1.5 hr hike out. Total car-to-car roughly 7-9 hours for a moderate party; longer with crowds.
routes.bail: The route is easy to retreat from at any point by downclimbing the low-angle, ledgy terrain back toward Pineapple Pass; the bolted rappel stations at the top of the east gully also allow a full bail via rappel in the event of weather or a stuck party ahead.
routes.turnaround: Because this is one of the most crowded alpine rock routes in the state, set a turnaround time that accounts for queuing at belays and Pineapple Pass, not just climbing time, and factor in the risk of afternoon thunderstorms building over the Cascade crest.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is June through September when the rock is dry and the approach is snow-free. In May and early June, lingering snowfields above Source Lake and a possible moat at the base of the route make an ice axe (and sometimes crampons) advisable. The route also sees winter and spring ascents in full mixed/snow conditions by experienced alpine climbers, but this is a markedly more serious undertaking than the summer rock climb.
routes.detailedRack: A single 60m rope is sufficient for the whole route. Rack light: a comfortable set of nuts, cams from small (~0.3in) up to about 3 inches, and 6-8 single-length plus 4-5 double-length slings to handle the wandering, ledge-to-ledge line. The route is well equipped with fixed rappel anchors, so no extra gear is needed for retreat.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is straightforward low-5th-class trad gear — small-to-medium cams and nuts protect the cracks, flakes, and grooves; the ledgy nature of the climbing keeps runouts short, though the rock is not fully solid everywhere, so test placements and be cautious with loose blocks.
gear: light alpine rack: nuts + cams to ~3in
ice axe and light crampons (through early July)
proTips: Start early, especially on weekends — Mountaineers courses and guided groups regularly queue at Pineapple Pass by mid-morning.
Carry an ice axe and light crampons for the approach snowfield above Source Lake into early July.
Use the southwest-side bolted-anchor descent option to avoid ascending traffic on busy days.
A single light rack (nuts plus cams to 3 inches) and a 60m rope cover the whole route — no need to overpack gear.
watchOut: Expect crowds and possible bottlenecks at belays and Pineapple Pass, especially on summer weekends.
Loose rock exists on parts of the route and in the descent gully — keep helmets on throughout.
Afternoon thunderstorms are a real risk on the exposed summit and face.
Early-season parties may encounter a snow moat and steep snow at the base requiring an axe/crampons.
knownHazards: Rockfall hazard from parties above in the east gully descent.
Route-finding on the approach — parties have missed the cairned climbers' trail and ended up in steeper, less pleasant terrain.
Congestion from high traffic can lead to stuck ropes and delays that push parties into worse afternoon weather.

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft. From Pineapple Pass, head up and trend right through the general groove/line of weakness; belay at the slung block.
~100 ft. Climb right to a ledge and follow ledges back and forth, ending near trees with rap tat on the left.
~70 ft. Scrambling on ledges and short steps; some route-finding flexibility.
~50 ft. Crux pitch with multiple lines: juggy flakes (5.4), a steeper crack (5.8), or balancey exposed moves, topping out at the Catwalk ledge below the summit.

road.name: Alpental Road (from I-90 Exit 52)
road.status: Paved, maintained/plowed year-round as the access road to the Alpental ski area
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 52, turn as signed toward Alpental; the Snow Lake/Source Lake trailhead parking lot is roughly 1.3-1.5 miles up Alpental Road.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast zone
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade climate: wet, snowy winters and drier, mild summers, right on the crest so weather can change quickly.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the approach and at the base into May/June; avalanche danger in the Source Lake basin persists into spring.
climate.summer: Best conditions June-September; watch for building afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures with the first snow possible by October.
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and avalanche terrain crossing the Source Lake basin; the South Face is climbed as a serious mixed/snow route in winter by experienced parties only.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District); route is within Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5/day or $30/year federal day-use fee at the Snow Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass honored); note Summit at Snoqualmie has separately been reported enforcing its own parking fee at Alpental-area lots without a ski pass — verify current signage before parking.
access.permit: No permit/quota needed for day climbing; Alpine Lakes Wilderness regulations apply (self-issue wilderness permit typically required at trailhead).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass, or pay the day-use fee (Washington Discover Pass NOT accepted at this federal site).

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn alpine start, especially on weekends, to beat crowds and afternoon thunderstorms

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office — call 911 for emergencies; non-emergency (206) 296-3311
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF), North Bend, WA — (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — 24/7 Emergency Dept., (425) 831-2300
emergency.notes: Backcountry search and rescue in this area is coordinated by King County Sheriff's Office Search & Rescue, with Seattle Mountain Rescue (based in North Bend) as the primary volunteer mountain rescue team responding to Alpental/Snoqualmie Pass incidents.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 5,605 ft is essentially confirmed; Wikipedia/USGS-derived figure is 5,606 ft — a trivial 1 ft rounding difference, noted here rather than treated as a real conflict.

routes.overview: A winter/spring mixed-and-ice line up the obvious slabs on The Tooth's northeast side, gaining the North Ridge before a short, exposed low-5th/M3 pitch over a notch to the summit — a much more serious, sparsely-protected undertaking than the South Face.
routes.beta: No source checked (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountaineers, Cascade Climbers forums, or guidebook references) documents a route on The Tooth actually named 'Catscratch Couloir.' The physical description in the seed data (NE-facing, winter ice/mixed line) matches the well-documented 'Northeast Slabs' route exactly, so the data below is sourced from Northeast Slabs. It climbs the NE-side slabs (originally graded around WI3, 50-70° ice on the first pitch, though conditions vary year to year), with a difficult-to-protect second pitch of thin ice and balancey mixed slab climbing, onto the North Ridge, finishing with a low-5th-class/M3 pitch over a notch on the east face before easier ground to the summit.
routes.fa: Jim Nelson & Paul Stevenson, 1982 (Northeast Slabs)
routes.rockGrade: Low 5th class (5th-class rock sections; M3 notch traverse)
routes.iceGrade: WI3 (original 1st-pitch grading; conditions-dependent, ranges roughly WI2-3)
objHaz: thin, difficult-to-protect ice/mixed climbing on the crux 2nd pitch
long runouts on balancey mixed terrain
avalanche exposure crossing the Source Lake basin on approach in winter conditions
exposed low-5th/M3 traverse over the notch near the top
cold, committing winter alpine conditions overall
routes.season: Winter through spring (a winter alpine ice/mixed route; conditions- and year-dependent)
routes.face: Northeast Face/Slabs, gaining the North Ridge
routes.approach: From the Alpental winter parking area, follow the Source Lake trail/existing ski tracks into the basin below The Tooth's north-facing side (rather than the summer climbers' path toward Pineapple Pass). Crossing the Source Lake basin in winter carries real avalanche exposure and should be checked against the current NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast before committing.
routes.descent: Standard descent is via the South Face route (reverse scramble to Pineapple Pass, then rappel the east gully) rather than reversing the Northeast Slabs line.
routes.descentText: Descend via the South Face: scramble/down-climb from the summit back toward Pineapple Pass, then make two rappels (~25m each, bolted chain anchors, single 60m rope) down the east gully, or use the alternate southwest-side bolted-anchor line if separating from other parties.
routes.bail: The lower slab pitches can generally be down-climbed or rappelled from natural gear/ice screw anchors. Above the North Ridge notch, retreat becomes more involved; parties should be prepared to build rappel anchors from available rock/ice gear, or push on to the summit and descend via the South Face gully if that is more practical than reversing the route.
routes.turnaround: Given avalanche exposure on the approach and sparse, difficult protection on the crux pitch, set a conservative turnaround time and be willing to bail if ice conditions are thin, the avalanche forecast is elevated, or the party is moving slower than planned.
routes.bestSeason: A winter-conditions route by nature — climbed when snow and ice have built up on the NE slabs (roughly the winter-spring window); in bare summer conditions it reverts to unprotected low-5th rock and is not the intended way to climb this line.
routes.detailedRack: A mixed winter alpine rack: ice tools and crampons, a handful of 10cm ice screws, one or two pickets for the snow sections, a light alpine rock rack (small-to-medium cams and nuts), and a knife-blade piton for thin cracks in the rock bands. A single 60m rope is standard.
routes.proNeeds: The route is known for being under-protected, especially on the second pitch, where thin ice and mixed slab climbing offer poor screw and picket placements and long runouts are common; the alpine rock rack mainly comes into play on the low-5th/M3 notch pitch near the top.
whatToBring: extra insulating layers for cold winter belays
avalanche gear (beacon, probe, shovel) for the Source Lake basin crossing
proTips: Check the NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast before crossing the Source Lake basin — it's avalanche terrain in winter conditions.
Expect the crux 2nd pitch to be thinly protected regardless of conditions; be mentally prepared for runouts.
Plan to descend via the South Face gully rather than reversing this route.
watchOut: Second pitch offers poor ice-screw/picket placements and long runouts on thin, balancey mixed ground.
Avalanche exposure on the approach across the Source Lake basin.
The notch traverse near the North Ridge is exposed low-5th/M3 terrain, not a place to be caught off-guard.
knownHazards: Thin, variable ice conditions can make the route's grading unreliable year to year.
Cornices and unstable snow can form along the North Ridge in winter.
Cold-weather objective hazards (frostbite, hypothermia) given the committing, sparsely-protected nature of the climb.

pitchDetail.notes: Original grading for the first ice pitch; angle and quality vary significantly by year and season.
Long runouts reported on thin ice and balancey mixed slab climbing; historically the crux for protection.
Continues up the slabs to gain the North Ridge.
Exposed pitch over the notch on the east face, then easier ground to the summit.

road.name: Alpental Road (from I-90 Exit 52)
road.status: Paved, maintained/plowed year-round as the access road to the Alpental ski area
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 52, follow signs to Alpental; winter parking area serves as the approach start for the Source Lake basin.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast zone
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade climate with heavy winter snowpack right on the crest; conditions change quickly.
climate.spring: Transitional ice/snow conditions; can still offer good mixed climbing early in the season.
climate.summer: Not the intended season — the route reverts to bare, poorly protected rock.
climate.fall: Ice/mixed conditions begin forming as winter storms arrive.
climate.winter: Prime season: snow and ice build up on the NE slabs, but avalanche danger in the Source Lake approach basin is a major consideration.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District); Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5/day or $30/year federal day-use fee at the trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass honored); Summit at Snoqualmie has separately been reported enforcing its own Alpental-area parking fee without a ski pass — verify current signage.
access.permit: No permit/quota needed for day climbing; Alpine Lakes Wilderness regulations apply.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass, or pay the day-use fee.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start to cross avalanche terrain in the Source Lake basin during colder, more stable overnight refreeze conditions

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office — call 911 for emergencies; non-emergency (206) 296-3311
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF), North Bend, WA — (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — 24/7 Emergency Dept., (425) 831-2300
emergency.notes: Winter rescues in avalanche terrain around Snoqualmie Pass are coordinated by King County Sheriff's Office Search & Rescue with Seattle Mountain Rescue (North Bend) as primary responders; response times in avalanche/winter conditions can be significantly longer than summer.

routes.corrections: No reputable source (checked Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountaineers.org, Cascade Climbers forums, Wikipedia, and guidebook references) documents a route on The Tooth named 'Catscratch Couloir.' The physical line described (NE-side winter ice/mixed route reaching the North Ridge) matches the well-documented 'Northeast Slabs' route (Mountain Project route ID 116596486; FA Jim Nelson & Paul Stevenson, 1982; also covered in Select Climbs in the Cascades Vol. II by Nelson & Potterfield, and on Mountaineers.org as 'The Tooth/Northeast Slab'). All facts below are sourced from Northeast Slabs. Recommend either renaming this route to 'Northeast Slabs' in the database or confirming 'Catscratch Couloir' as a genuine informal/local alternate name before keeping it as the primary name — it could not be verified. Elevation: given 5,605 ft vs. Wikipedia/USGS-derived 5,606 ft is a trivial 1 ft rounding difference.

blurb: Tower Mountain (8,444 ft) is a non-volcanic summit in the Okanogan Range of the North Cascades, straddling the Okanogan/Skagit county line just north of Washington's North Cascades Highway (SR-20) near Rainy Pass. Built from rock accreted roughly 50 million years ago and heavily sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, it rises above the Snowy Lakes basin on a ridge shared with neighboring Golden Horn and is popular in early fall for the golden larches that ring its base. The peak was first climbed in 1913 by a government survey party whose members were never recorded; they left a roughly 7-foot summit cairn that still stands today.
hierarchyNote: None — peak name, elevation (~8,444 ft, matching Wikipedia/WTA/SummitPost within a foot of the given 8,445 ft), and Okanogan Range/North Cascades location near Rainy Pass are all consistent and cross-confirmed across sources. Note the peak sits in the Okanogan–Wenatchee National Forest (Methow Valley Ranger District), not inside North Cascades National Park proper, which affects permit requirements listed below.

routes.fa: 1913, unnamed U.S. government survey party (first ascent of the peak; names not recorded in any source). This SW-side gully line is the peak's original and easiest line, so it is presumed — but not separately documented — as the FA route.
objHaz: rockfall in the gully (loose surface rock over sounder bedrock)
wet/water-polished slab in the crux section
1,000+ ft of exposure near the summit ridge
routes.season: Jul-Oct (snow-free); can be climbed May-Jun with steep snow in the gully and near the summit
routes.face: Southwest side gully system
routes.overview: An unroped-to-low-5th-class gully climb up Tower Mountain's southwest side, gaining a broad talus/scree basin below the west face, then following a system of connected gullies (class 2/3 with one class 4, occasionally 5.4-ish, slabby step) to the summit ridge and a short class 2-3 scramble to the top.
routes.beta: From the Snowy Lakes basin, traverse below the outlet talus toward the west face of Tower Mountain, crossing talus/scree and staying below minor cliff bands. A large headwall blocks direct access to the main gully; a ledge system (passing a prominent ~20-ft-tall cave visible from Snowy Lakes) bypasses the headwall and leads into the gully proper. Climb a broad class 2/3 gully for several hundred feet, then traverse right into a second gully containing roughly 15 ft of slabby class 4 (sometimes wet) climbing at half height. Above this, ledges lead back up to the ridge crest (class 3/4), followed by an exposed but moderate scramble to the summit and its historic cairn. Keep parties small — the rock is mostly sound but surface rubble makes rockfall a real hazard for climbers below.
routes.approach: Drive SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Rainy Pass North (PCT) Trailhead, on the north side of the highway roughly 51 mi east of Marblemount / 35 mi west of Winthrop. From the trailhead (4,880 ft), hike north on the Pacific Crest Trail through Cutthroat Pass (~6,800 ft) and Granite Pass (~6,070 ft) to about 6,300 ft below Tower Mountain, then turn off onto the (unsigned/rougher) spur trail climbing steeply to the Snowy Lakes basin (~6,700 ft), a good camp. This approach is roughly 11-12 mi one-way with about 2,700 ft of cumulative gain to the lakes. From camp, ascend talus/heather to the west-side basin beneath the peak to start the route.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent gully system back to the Snowy Lakes basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same southwest gully system used on the ascent: reverse the class 2-3 scramble off the summit ridge, downclimb the ledges back to the crux slab (a short rappel from a sling anchor has been used here by some parties in place of downclimbing), and continue down through the cave/ledge bypass and the lower talus gully back to the Snowy Lakes basin. Loose rock makes the descent more hazardous than the ascent for parties below — stay grouped tightly or descend one at a time through loose sections.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-day plan: Day 1 — hike in via the PCT from Rainy Pass to a camp at Snowy Lakes (~6,700 ft). Day 2 — early start (many parties leave camp by 7:30 AM) up the SW gully system to the summit and back to camp (roughly 4-5 hours round trip from camp), then hike out via the PCT, or combine with an ascent of neighboring Golden Horn from the same camp.
routes.bail: The lower gully (class 2/3) offers a straightforward reversal from almost any point. Above the crux slab, retreat requires downclimbing the class 4 step (or a short rappel off a sling anchor); avoid pushing past the crux in deteriorating weather since it is the route's one committing section.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and fully exposed, above-treeline terrain, set a hard turnaround time before starting the gully and stick to it — afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades in summer, and the loose, slabby crux section becomes significantly more hazardous to downclimb when wet or in fading light.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions run July through September/October once the gully and slab sections are snow-free; late September/early October adds the bonus of golden larches around Snowy Lakes but shorter daylight. May-June ascents are possible but should expect steep, consolidated snow (up to ~45°) in the gully and near the summit, raising the technical difficulty and requiring an ice axe/crampons.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage along SR-20 near Rainy Pass/Washington Pass or anywhere on the approach and route; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: a handful of cams from about 0.5" to 2.5", a set of small stoppers/nuts (roughly 0.5"-1"), and a 50-60m rope for protecting or belaying the class 4/5.4 slab section and for any rappels. Helmet is essential given loose rock in the gully.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is only needed briefly at the crux slab step; small-to-medium cams and stoppers protect the thin crack/slab moves adequately. Most of the route is unroped scrambling.
gear: approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
whatToBring: bivy/camp gear if overnighting at Snowy Lakes
sun protection (route is almost entirely above treeline)
water treatment (Snowy Lakes is the reliable water source)
proTips: Camp at (Lower) Snowy Lake to shorten and lighten the summit day.
Tower Mountain and Golden Horn share a ridge and are commonly climbed together in one trip from the same Snowy Lakes camp.
Stay right (climber's right) in the upper gully and don't exit the gully system too early — a common navigation error.
Visit in late September/early October for the golden larches around Snowy Lakes, but expect colder temps and shorter days.
watchOut: Keep group size small in the gully — loose surface rock makes rockfall a real danger to anyone climbing below.
The crux slab step is often wet/water-polished and noticeably harder when damp.
Early-season snow (into June) can add sustained ~45° slopes requiring axe and crampons.
The unsigned spur trail from the PCT up to Snowy Lakes is easy to miss or lose in the dark.
knownHazards: Loose, rubble-covered rock throughout the gully system despite generally sound underlying bedrock.
Roughly 1,000 ft of exposure along sections of the summit ridge and ledge traverses.
Water-polished, slick rock on the class 4 slab crux, especially early in the season or after rain.
Steep lingering snow (up to ~45°) in the gully in late spring/early summer.

pitchDetail.notes: Talus/heather traverse from Snowy Lakes to the west-face basin below the route.
Ledge system past a large cave (~20 ft tall) around the headwall blocking direct gully access.
Broad gully climbing for several hundred feet; stay right, don't exit early.
~15 ft of slabby climbing partway up the second gully; often wet; crux of the route.
Ledge system back to the ridge crest.
Exposed but moderate scramble along the ridge to the summit cairn.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Fully paved to the Rainy Pass trailhead; seasonally closed in winter
