road.seasonalGate: Gates typically near milepost 134 (Ross Dam) and milepost 171 (Silver Star gate), sometimes moved to milepost 120 (Newhalem) and 178 (Early Winters) later in the closure; historically closes ~Nov-Dec and reopens ~Apr-May depending on snowpack/avalanche conditions
road.driveNote: Trailhead parking lot is on the north side of SR-20 at Rainy Pass, reached via a short paved/unpaved access spur; no 4WD or unpaved-road driving required.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with strong orographic effects; Rainy Pass sits near the Cascade crest so weather can shift quickly between wet/west-side and drier/east-side patterns.
climate.spring: Cool, often snowy at elevation with lingering steep snow in the gully into June.
climate.summer: Typically the driest, clearest window (Jul-Sep); still subject to afternoon thunderstorm buildups.
climate.fall: Cool, stable weather common in September-October with the added draw of golden larches; shorter daylight and colder nights.
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and high avalanche danger; the approach road is closed for the season.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District (not inside North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year at the Rainy Pass North Trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass and America the Beautiful/Interagency passes accepted
access.permit: No wilderness/backcountry permit required for climbing here since the peak lies outside North Cascades National Park; a free self-issue day-use wilderness permit may be posted at some nearby trailheads for backcountry travel generally.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent Interagency pass) for trailhead parking
access.closures: SR-20 closes seasonally each winter at Rainy Pass/Washington Pass, cutting off road access to the trailhead entirely until spring reopening; no overnight camping within 1/4 mile of Cutthroat Lake along the approach.

timing.recommendedStart: Early start from Snowy Lakes camp (parties commonly leave by ~7:30 AM) to be off exposed terrain before afternoon thunderstorms build

emergency.county: Okanogan County (the peak lies on the Okanogan/Skagit county line)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff's Office, 509-422-7200 (non-emergency); dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Winthrop, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA is the nearest full-service hospital serving western Okanogan County; smaller clinics (e.g. Methow Valley Clinic, Winthrop) offer limited urgent care
emergency.notes: No cell coverage along SR-20 near Rainy Pass/Washington Pass or on the approach/route — carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Okanogan County Search and Rescue (OCSAR), a volunteer team, supports backcountry rescues under the Sheriff's Office.

blurb: Tomyhoi Peak (7,439 ft) is a broad summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, about one mile south of the Canada-US border in the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), Whatcom County. Built largely of greenstone and carrying a small, unofficially-named glacier on its north flank, it rises over 4,400 ft above Damfino Creek in about a mile and is overshadowed in visual prominence by neighboring giants Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, Mount Larrabee, and the Border Peaks. Sources disagree on the exact first-ascent party — likely USFS surveyor Lage Wernstedt in 1927, though Ben Thompson or Clarence A. Fisher in the early 1930s are also credited.
hierarchyNote: None — Tomyhoi Peak (7,439 ft, 48°58'30"N 121°42'35"W) is a real, uniquely-identifiable Skagit Range summit in the North Cascades, Whatcom County, WA, within the Mount Baker Wilderness / Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Name, elevation, and location are consistent across Wikipedia, Peakbagger, SummitPost, and USFS, so the parent-area placement is geographically plausible.

routes.overview: The standard and easiest route on Tomyhoi Peak: a long approach on the Yellow Aster Butte trail system followed by an off-trail scramble up the Tomyhoi Arm ridge, a short traverse along the top edge of the Tomyhoi Glacier bench, and a final exposed Class 3(-3.5) scramble up the summit block.
routes.beta: From the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake Trailhead, follow maintained trail up to the high meadows and tarns below Yellow Aster Butte (~5,580 ft), then leave the trail and head NNW over a hump to a col (~6,266 ft). Climb the broadening ridge (Tomyhoi Arm); where it narrows, bypass on broad benches on the north side, then traverse the top of the Tomyhoi Glacier to avoid rock pinnacles lower on the ridge. Cross a moat/notch below the final summit pyramid (hard snow, ice axe useful) and finish with a delightful but exposed Class 3 scramble on solid rock to the true summit; one account describes a V-shaped Class 3.5 slot near the top that some parties belay or lower packs through, especially on descent.
routes.fa: Uncertain / likely Lage Wernstedt (USFS surveyor), 1927 — some sources instead credit Ben Thompson or Clarence A. Fisher in the early 1930s; this is the peak's first-recorded-ascent history in general, not a confirmed route-specific first ascent, and the exact line climbed by the earliest parties is undocumented.
routes.face: Southeast Ridge / Tomyhoi Arm
routes.bestSeason: Best from June through September depending on snowpack. Early season (June) climbers should expect lingering snow on the approach benches and the moat crossing below the summit block, and should carry an ice axe (crampons optional). Mid-to-late summer (Jul-Aug) offers the driest rock and least snow but can be hot on the long, exposed ridge. Fall (Sept, into early Oct in low-snow years) brings stable rock and fewer crowds but shorter days and colder temperatures at the notch; the road and trail become impassable once snow returns.
routes.approach: From the Glacier Public Service Center on Mt. Baker Highway (SR-542), drive east roughly 12-13 miles to the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance facility and turn left (north) onto Twin Lakes Road (Forest Road 3065). Follow FR-3065 about 4.5-4.7 rough, narrow miles to the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead (~3,600-3,700 ft; roadside pullout parking only, arrive early). Hike the Yellow Aster Butte/Gold Run Pass Trail up switchbacks to about 5,900 ft, then bear left onto the Yellow Aster Meadows Trail and continue to the meadow and small tarns at roughly 5,580 ft (about 3-3.5 miles in). This is the end of maintained trail; from here the route continues off-trail.
routes.descent: Descend the same Southeast Ridge line in reverse back to the Yellow Aster Meadows Trail and trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed Class 3 (locally Class 3.5) summit block — some parties belay or lower packs through the V-shaped slot near the top rather than downclimb it free. Retrace the traverse around the rib and across the moat/notch, regain the top edge of the Tomyhoi Glacier bench, and drop back onto the broad benches on the north side of the ridge to rejoin the ridge crest. Continue back down to the col (~6,266 ft) and over the hump to the Yellow Aster tarns/meadows, then follow the maintained Yellow Aster Butte Trail back down to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a long single day: roughly 5-5.5 hours trailhead-to-summit and 4.5-5 hours back down, for a 10-11 hour car-to-car day over ~13-14 miles and ~4,800-5,600 ft of gain/loss. Parties wanting to split the effort can backpack in and camp at the Yellow Aster tarns (~5,580 ft) the evening before, leaving only the upper ridge/glacier/summit scramble for summit day.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line back to the maintained Yellow Aster Butte Trail; there are no fixed rappel anchors reported on route. If the exposed Class 3.5 slot near the summit feels too committing, parties can back off before it — the col and lower ridge/bench terrain below the glacier traverse offer easy, low-angle retreat at any point.
routes.turnaround: Given the long round-trip distance and elevation gain, set a firm turnaround time based on your pace, weather, and remaining daylight — the exposed Class 3 terrain near the summit and the off-trail ridge/bench route-finding below it are markedly harder and riskier to negotiate in fading light or a fast-moving storm.
routes.detailedRack: Not a technical rope climb for most parties in normal summer conditions; a light rack is optional rather than mandatory. Many parties carry a 30m glacier/scramble rope with a few slings and a picket or two to protect or lower packs through the short bench-glacier crossing and the exposed V-shaped Class 3.5 slot near the summit, particularly on the descent.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed anchors are reported on route; natural protection (slung horns, chockstones) is sufficient for the one short technical section if a party chooses to rope up.
gear: A few slings and a picket or two (optional)
Trekking poles for the maintained trail miles
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful/Interagency pass) for trailhead parking
Map/GPS — route-finding above the meadows is unmarked and off-trail
Sun protection for the long, exposed ridge
Extra water — limited sources above the tarns
Wind layers for the exposed ridge crest
proTips: Camp at the Yellow Aster tarns (~5,580 ft) to split the long day into two.
Stay on the top edge of the Tomyhoi Glacier bench when traversing to avoid the rock pinnacles lower on the ridge.
Carry an ice axe even in late summer for the moat/notch crossing below the summit block.
Parking is very limited at the trailhead (roadside pullouts) — arrive early on weekends.
watchOut: Hard snow/ice at the moat crossing below the summit — an ice axe (and possibly crampons) is recommended into early season.
The V-shaped Class 3.5 slot near the true summit is exposed and notably harder to downclimb than to ascend; some parties belay or lower packs.
Sections of glacier-polished, loose rock on the ridge and upper mountain.
Off-trail route-finding above the Yellow Aster meadows in poor visibility.
knownHazards: Exposed Class 3-3.5 scrambling on the summit block, with real fall consequences.
Minor glacier travel across the bench of the (unofficially named) Tomyhoi Glacier.
Snow/ice moat crossing below the final ridge, especially in June-early July.
Loose and glacier-polished rock in places on the ridge.
Mountain goats are commonly encountered near the summit and meadows.

pitchDetail.notes: Maintained Yellow Aster Butte/Gold Run Pass Trail to ~5,900 ft, then Yellow Aster Meadows Trail to tarns at ~5,580 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Meadows to col and lower ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Off-trail NNW over a hump, down to a col at ~6,266 ft, then up the broadening Tomyhoi Arm ridge.
Bypass the narrowing ridge on broad benches on the north side; traverse the top edge of the Tomyhoi Glacier to avoid rock pinnacles.
Cross a moat between rock and ice to gain a notch; hard snow common, ice axe useful.
Exposed scramble on solid but glacier-polished rock; V-shaped slot near the top is the crux, harder on descent.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Road 3065)
road.status: Rough, narrow gravel road; high-clearance recommended, generally passable to the trailhead in dry conditions
road.seasonalGate: Snow-closed in winter and spring; opening date depends on snowpack — check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before heading out
road.driveNote: From the Glacier Public Service Center on SR-542, drive east ~12-13 miles to the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance facility and turn left (north) onto FR-3065; continue ~4.5-4.7 rough miles to the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead. Parking is limited to roadside pullouts.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (Mount Baker area)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and drier, cooler summers typical of the west-slope North Cascades near the Canadian crest.
climate.spring: Heavy, lingering snowpack on the approach benches and summit moat commonly persists into June or July.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep) for climbing, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
climate.fall: Stable rock but shorter days and colder temperatures at the notch; first snow can return by October.
climate.winter: Road and trail are typically snowed in; not a winter objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Ranger District), Mount Baker Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful/Interagency Annual Pass) required for parking at the Tomyhoi Lake/Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead; Washington Discover Pass is NOT accepted at this Forest Service site.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Annual Pass
access.closures: Twin Lakes Road (FR 3065) is not maintained for passenger cars beyond the trailhead area and is closed by snow in winter/spring; check MBS National Forest alerts for current status before driving out.

timing.recommendedStart: Early/predawn given the long round-trip distance and elevation gain

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; 360-676-6911 non-emergency (Whatcom County Sheriff's Office)
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center / Mount Baker Ranger District, 10091 Mt. Baker Hwy, Glacier, WA 98244 — (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Haggen Family Emergency & Trauma Center, Level II Trauma), 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA — the only hospital in Whatcom County
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search & Rescue (WCSAR) volunteers respond to backcountry incidents in this area; cell coverage is unreliable on the approach trail and upper ridge/glacier.

blurb: Whitehorse Mountain (6,840–6,852 ft, sources vary) rises abruptly above the Sauk River valley town of Darrington on the western edge of the North Cascades, within the Boulder River Wilderness of the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Its north face gains roughly 6,000 feet in under two miles of horizontal distance — one of the steepest, most locally-prominent faces in the range — and still carries the Whitehorse and So-Bahli-Ahli Glaciers. The peak's first recorded ascent was in 1909 by USFS ranger/naturalist Nels Bruseth; it was named in 1894 by a local postmaster after a snow patch resembling a white horse.
hierarchyNote: None — name, coordinates (48.21°N, 121.68°W), elevation (~6,840-6,852 ft), and setting (Boulder River Wilderness / Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF, North Cascades, southwest of Darrington, Snohomish County WA) are all mutually consistent and confirmed across Wikipedia, Peakbagger-derived data, SummitPost, and multiple guidebook/trip-report sources.

routes.rappels: No fixed rappel route; a short single rappel (picket or rock-anchor sling) off the final summit block/steep rollover is sometimes used in place of downclimbing.
routes.season: Feb-Jun (best after a low-elevation snowstorm buries the brush; Apr-Jun most common)
routes.face: Northwest Shoulder (via Lone Tree Pass and High Pass)
routes.overview: The standard/easiest line on Whitehorse: a long, strenuous approach up the Niederprum climbers' path to Lone Tree Pass and High Pass, then a glacier/snow crossing to a short, exposed Class 3 scramble on the summit block.
routes.beta: Begin on Niederprum Trail #953/653, which quickly degenerates into a steep, brushy boot path gaining roughly 4,000 ft to an alpine bench near the old Niederprum mine. Continue on snow to Lone Tree Pass (~4,900 ft; the 'lone tree' is actually a cluster of three trees, not at the pass), traverse below the northwest ridge to High Pass (~6,000-6,030 ft), cross onto the Whitehorse Glacier, and climb a snow/ice ridge to the base of the summit block, finishing with exposed Class 3 scrambling to the true summit.
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 208 (Arlington/Darrington), drive east on SR 530 roughly 26 miles to Mine Road (387th Ave NE / FS Road 2030), about 5 miles west of Darrington. Turn right (south); the road is paved briefly then becomes gravel, with two left forks to stay on. The road historically continued to a car-accessible trailhead around 900-1,000 ft, but the Moose Creek bridge has been damaged/closed to vehicles in recent years per the Forest Service and current trip reports, so most parties now park near the bridge (~0.5-0.6 mi from the highway) and walk the closed road plus a boulder-barricade/temporary bridge crossing, adding roughly 1.75-2.6 miles round trip and about 400 ft of extra gain. From the trailhead, Niederprum Trail #653 switchbacks for about 1 maintained mile before becoming a rough, unmaintained route through brush and timber.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: down the glacier/snow to High Pass, traverse to Lone Tree Pass, then down the Niederprum climbers' path to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb (or make a short rappel off) the summit block, retrace the ascent line back across the glacier/snow ridge to High Pass, traverse below the northwest ridge back to Lone Tree Pass, and descend the boot path/brush back to the Niederprum Trail and trailhead. Trip reports note the bushwhack descent is noticeably faster than the ascent (roughly 1 hour) once back on the lower trail.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-day trip: Day 1 is the approach to a high camp near Lone Tree Pass or the Whitehorse Ridge platform (~4,800-4,900 ft, roughly 7 hours from the car per trip reports); Day 2 is the summit push and return (roughly 5 hours up, 2.5 hours down from camp). Strong parties have done it car-to-car in a single long day (~13-14 hours) with a pre-dawn start.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line down to Lone Tree Pass and the Niederprum path. The hardest places to reverse are the glacier crossing and the final summit-block scramble in poor visibility or icy conditions — don't commit to the glacier/summit block if weather is closing in.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on your pace to High Pass and the glacier; the long brushy approach, glacier crossing, and exposed summit scramble all become significantly harder to reverse after dark, in a whiteout, or once afternoon sun softens the descent snow.
routes.bestSeason: Most climbers go February through June, ideally right after a low-elevation snowstorm has buried the notorious lower brush and avalanche danger has settled — April through June is the most commonly cited window. By July the Whitehorse Glacier opens up with crevasses and a significant bergschrund, and by late summer the glacier can become nearly impassable, turning the route into a much more serious, dry, brushy scramble.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier rack: 2-3 snow pickets and a 30-50m rope for the party, used on the steeper snow/glacier sections and optionally for a short rappel off the summit block. No traditional rock rack is needed on the standard line.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is snow/ice-based rather than rock-based: pickets for glacier travel and any steep snow, with an occasional rock-anchor sling for a short rappel off the final summit outcrop if desired.
whatToBring: Approach/trail-running shoes for the lower brushy trail, swapped for mountaineering boots at snowline
Avalanche safety gear for early-season conditions
Extra water — no reliable water source above Snow Gulch/Ashton Creek
Bivy/camp gear if doing the standard 2-day itinerary
proTips: Time the trip for right after a low-elevation snowstorm so the lower brush is buried, per multiple trip reports.
The 'lone tree' at Lone Tree Pass is actually a small cluster of three trees, not a single tree at the pass — don't let the name mislead your navigation.
A high camp near Lone Tree Pass or the Whitehorse Ridge platform (~4,800-4,900 ft) splits the long day and is the standard approach for most parties.
watchOut: The Moose Creek bridge on the access road has been closed/damaged in recent years, adding mileage and a boulder-barricade crossing before the maintained trail even starts.
Route-finding to High Pass is a commonly cited crux — several trip reports describe ending up in the wrong gully or traversing below cliff bands.
The bergschrund and crevasses on the Whitehorse Glacier open up significantly from July onward.
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain on the steep snow traverses below Lone Tree Pass and High Pass, especially after new snow.
Crevasses and a major bergschrund on the Whitehorse Glacier, worst from mid-summer onward.
Dense brush (alder, devil's club, downed timber) on the lower approach when it is not snow-covered.

pitchDetail.notes: About 1 mile of switchbacks from the trailhead, roughly 900-1,900 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Boot path / brush to alpine bench
pitchDetail.notes: Unmaintained climbers' path gaining ~4,000 ft through timber and brush to the bench near the old Niederprum mine (~4,000-4,100 ft).

pitchDetail.pitch: Snow slopes to Lone Tree Pass
pitchDetail.notes: Ascend/traverse snowfields to Lone Tree Pass (~4,860-4,970 ft).
Descend/traverse below cliffs on the south side of the northwest ridge to High Pass (~6,000-6,030 ft).
Cross the Whitehorse Glacier, climb a snow/ice ridge to the summit block, finish with exposed Class 3 scrambling (some sources note a short low-5th-class step) to the true summit.

road.name: Mine Road / 387th Ave NE / FS Road 2030
road.status: Paved briefly, then gravel; rough in spots
road.driveNote: Moose Creek bridge along this road has been closed/damaged for vehicle traffic in recent years, forcing most parties to park earlier and walk an extra ~1.75-2.6 miles round trip plus ~400 ft of gain to reach the historic trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, snowy maritime Cascades climate; the peak's steep north face still holds the Whitehorse and So-Bahli-Ahli Glaciers.
climate.spring: Deep, sometimes unstable snowpack on approach slopes; the standard climbing window once brush is buried and avalanche danger has settled.
climate.summer: Snow recedes quickly; by July the Whitehorse Glacier opens with crevasses and a significant bergschrund, and by late summer it can be nearly impassable, exposing brush and loose rock below.
climate.fall: Reverts to a long, mostly dry, brushy scramble with limited snow cover; less commonly climbed.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger typical of the west-slope Cascades; a serious winter objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (Boulder River Wilderness)
access.fees: No specific fee reported at the Neiderprum trailhead itself
access.permit: No special permit required; standard Wilderness group-size and Leave No Trace rules apply within Boulder River Wilderness
access.closures: Moose Creek bridge on the access road closed to vehicle traffic in recent years, extending the approach on foot

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (roughly 4-5 AM) for a single car-to-car day; earlier if pushing from a high camp

waypoints.name: Neiderprum Trailhead / Moose Creek Bridge area

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff non-emergency: 425-407-3999 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF — (360) 436-1155, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (48-bed hospital with Level IV Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach with limited/no reliable cell coverage; carry a means of emergency communication (satellite messenger) given the long, technical retreat.

routes.rappels: A short rappel (single strand, picket or rock-anchor sling) is common off the steep icy rollover/summit block rather than downclimbing it, especially in firm early-season conditions.
routes.season: Feb-Jun (early/spring conditions strongly preferred for this variant)
routes.face: Northwest Face / Whitehorse & So-Bahli-Ahli Glaciers, finishing on the Northwest Shoulder
routes.overview: A more direct, glacier-committed version of the standard line: instead of a pure scramble, this variant crosses the Whitehorse and So-Bahli-Ahli Glaciers and climbs a steep (~50-55°), often icy snow rollover directly below the summit block, requiring roped glacier travel and picket protection.
routes.beta: Follows the same lower approach as the standard Northwest Shoulder (Niederprum Trail to Lone Tree Pass), but from the Whitehorse Ridge/Ashton Creek drainage stays on the glacier proper (climber's right to avoid the worst crevassing), ascends a steep icy rollover around 5,600 ft, then climbs the So-Bahli-Ahli Glacier toward a firm, icy summit block finished with Class 3-4 scrambling.
routes.approach: Identical to the standard Neiderprum Trail approach: from I-5 Exit 208, east on SR 530 ~26 miles to Mine Road/FS 2030, then the gravel road to the (currently vehicle-closed) Moose Creek bridge and Niederprum Trail #653, gaining the alpine bench near the old Niederprum mine before continuing toward Lone Tree Pass / Whitehorse Ridge and onto the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: back down the glacier and steep rollover to Lone Tree Pass, then the Niederprum climbers' path to the trailhead, staying alert for softened afternoon snow on the descent.
routes.descentText: Retrace the up-track through the crevassed sections of the glacier, favoring the same line used on ascent; one trip report recommends trending skier's-left below the avalanche debris on the way down. Downclimb or rappel the steep rollover, then descend to Lone Tree Pass and the brushy climbers' path back to the trailhead — the lower brush descent is reported as notably faster than the ascent.
routes.itinerary: Feasible as a single long day car-to-car (~13-14 hours, pre-dawn start around 4 AM) for fit, fast parties in good spring conditions, per multiple trip reports; many parties still prefer a high camp near Lone Tree Pass/Whitehorse Ridge (~4,800-4,900 ft) to split the effort and manage glacier timing.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier the way you came; the steep icy rollover and crevassed glacier sections are the most serious to downclimb if conditions deteriorate (softening snow, whiteout, or new snowfall raising avalanche risk) — don't push past the rollover if you're not confident retreating through it.
routes.turnaround: Given the roped glacier travel and steep icy sections, set a conservative turnaround time for reaching the base of the rollover; softening snow later in the day increases both avalanche and fall risk on the descent, and crevasse hazard is worse in low light/flat light conditions.
routes.bestSeason: This variant is best attempted in a narrow early-to-mid-spring window (roughly February-June) while the glacier and rollover are still consolidated, snow-covered, and crevasses remain well-bridged; conditions firm up (icier, more exposed) later in spring and the glacier becomes increasingly broken up and crevassed by summer.
routes.detailedRack: A glacier rack for the party: 2-3 pickets, a 30-50m rope for glacier travel and belaying/short-roping the steep rollover, and optionally a snow fluke or two for anchors on the steepest sections; a sling for a rock-anchor rappel off the summit block if the firm ice makes downclimbing unappealing.
routes.proNeeds: Almost entirely snow/ice protection: pickets and/or flukes for the glacier and the ~50-55° rollover below the summit, with an occasional rock sling anchor for the final short rappel.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the lower bushwhack, swapped for mountaineering boots at the glacier
Crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks/pulley) for roped glacier travel
Extra water — no reliable source above Snow Gulch/Ashton Creek
Sun protection for extended glacier exposure
proTips: Stay climber's right on the Whitehorse/So-Bahli-Ahli Glacier to avoid the worst crevassing, per a detailed 2020 trip report.
The steep rollover around 5,600 ft is best 'booted up' rather than climbed on crampon fronts alone when firm — plan for a slower pace here.
The summit block stays icy and firm even in spring; several parties describe it as comparable in character to a steep alpine snow finish, not a casual scramble.
watchOut: The steep rollover (~50-55°) below the summit is icy and has caught out parties who underestimated it in firmer spring conditions.
Crevasses on the glacier are 'mostly bridged' in spring but with open sections — this is genuine roped glacier travel, not a snow walk-up.

A documented 1970s spring ascent triggered a 0.4-meter slab avalanche on this general aspect after being traversed on the way up; the same slope would have swept climbers over a lower cliff band, underscoring real avalanche exposure on these slopes.
knownHazards: Crevasses and bergschrund on the Whitehorse/So-Bahli-Ahli Glacier, with open sections even in spring.
A steep, often icy snow/firn rollover (~50-55°) directly below the summit block.
Avalanche potential on the steep north-facing approach slopes, documented by at least one historical near-miss.
Firm, icy summit block requiring solid crampon/ice-axe technique.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach to Whitehorse Ridge / glacier edge
pitchDetail.notes: Same lower approach as the standard route via Niederprum Trail and Lone Tree Pass to the Whitehorse Ridge platform (~4,900 ft).
Cross the Whitehorse Glacier staying climber's right to avoid the worst crevassing.
Boot up the steep, sometimes icy rollover around 5,600 ft; crux of this variant.
Ascend the upper glacier to the firm, icy summit block, finishing with exposed Class 3-4 scrambling to the true summit.

road.name: Mine Road / 387th Ave NE / FS Road 2030
road.status: Paved briefly, then gravel; rough in spots
road.driveNote: Moose Creek bridge closed/damaged for vehicle traffic in recent years, adding roughly 1.75-2.6 miles round trip and ~400 ft of extra gain to reach the historic trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, snowy maritime Cascades climate; the north face retains the Whitehorse and So-Bahli-Ahli Glaciers year-round.
climate.spring: Prime window for this variant — glacier still well-bridged and the rollover consolidated, though avalanche danger must be actively managed.
climate.summer: Glacier opens up with more exposed crevasses; by late summer this variant becomes considerably more hazardous/broken up.
climate.fall: Not typically climbed as a glacier route this late; conditions revert to mostly bare, brushy terrain.
climate.winter: Full winter conditions with high avalanche hazard; a serious winter mountaineering objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (Boulder River Wilderness)
access.fees: No specific fee reported at the Neiderprum trailhead itself
access.permit: No special permit required; standard Wilderness group-size and Leave No Trace rules apply within Boulder River Wilderness
access.closures: Moose Creek bridge on the access road closed to vehicle traffic in recent years, extending the approach on foot

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (around 4-4:30 AM) to reach the glacier and rollover while snow is still firm/frozen and before avalanche/rockfall risk rises with the day's heating

waypoints.name: Neiderprum Trailhead / Moose Creek Bridge area

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff non-emergency: 425-407-3999 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF — (360) 436-1155, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (48-bed hospital with Level IV Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Remote glaciated terrain with limited/no reliable cell coverage; carry crevasse-rescue capability and a satellite messenger given the technical, roped nature of this variant.

blurb: American Border Peak (7,998 ft) rises in Whatcom County's North Cascades, just south of the US-Canada border in the Mount Baker Wilderness, with its twin summit, Canadian Border Peak, immediately across the international boundary to the north. It overlooks Tomyhoi Lake and the Silesia Creek valley near Mount Larrabee, and is known for notoriously loose, "rotten" rock that makes it less popular than nearby Slesse Mountain despite its steep, striking relief. The peak was first climbed September 14, 1930 by Alec Dalgleish, Tom Fyles, Stan Henderson, and R.A. Fraser; the now-standard Southeast Face route was pioneered separately by Dwight Baker, Fred Beckey, and John Dudra on September 21, 1952.

routes.overview: A long cross-country approach leads to a mostly class 3-4 scramble up loose red rock to a short, steep chimney system (the 'Great Chimney') that forms the technical crux, followed by an exposed final ridge traverse to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard/easiest line on the peak, first climbed by Baker, Beckey, and Dudra in 1952. From High Pass the route crosses talus, scree, and heather on Mount Larrabee's flank toward a saddle below the peak, then ascends slabby ledges and a snow gully to the base of the Great Chimney. Two short technical pitches (5.3 entry, 5.4 exit past a large chockstone, with a tight 'keyhole' squeeze) provide the crux; reported difficulty varies by party and conditions, with some describing sections up to 5.8. Above the chimney, a loose, dirty trough leads to the summit ridge and an exposed step-across move to the top. Rock quality is poor throughout and route-finding is nontrivial — helmets and route-finding experience are essential.
routes.approach: From the Twin Lakes Trailhead (5,200 ft, via Forest Service Road 3065 off Mt. Baker Highway SR 542, 12 miles east of Glacier, WA), start on the Winchester Mountain Trail 685. At 0.5 mile, bear right onto High Pass Trail 676, which crosses an avalanche chute beneath Winchester Mountain and climbs past Low Pass to High Pass (~5,960 ft) — steep, exposed snowfields on this stretch can linger into August and require an ice axe. From High Pass, descend toward the Gargett Mine basin (the last reliable water source) below Mount Larrabee's northwest side, then make a long, undulating cross-country traverse (talus, scree, heather) across Larrabee's western flank toward the saddle below American Border Peak to reach the base of the southeast face.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb the upper face, then make two rappels (~60m double-rope) through the Great Chimney's keyhole to regain scrambling terrain, and retrace the cross-country traverse back over High Pass and Low Pass to the Twin Lakes Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed upper ridge and slabby trough back to the top of the Great Chimney. Make two rappels (parties commonly use ~60m double-rope rappels) through the chimney's tight keyhole squeeze past its chockstones to regain 3rd/4th-class terrain below. From there, scramble back across the southeast face to the saddle, then reverse the long cross-country traverse (talus, scree, heather, and vegetation that becomes slick when wet or in fading light) back toward the Gargett Mine basin and up to High Pass. Follow High Pass Trail 676 back over Low Pass to the Winchester Mountain Trail junction and down to the Twin Lakes Trailhead. Parties have reported full-day-plus outings given the slow return traverse — budget generous extra time.
routes.itinerary: Most parties do this as a long single day from the Twin Lakes Trailhead with a pre-dawn start, or as an overnight with camp at High Pass, in the basin near Gargett Mine, or on the broad south saddle below the summit tower — climbing the face on day two and hiking out the same or following day.
routes.bail: Below the Great Chimney the route is straightforward 3rd/4th-class scrambling that can be reversed at almost any point. Above the chimney, the only reasonable retreat is to rappel back down the way you climbed — two double-rope rappels (~60m) get a party from the chimney/keyhole top back to walkable terrain; do not attempt to downclimb the chimney unroped.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and return traverse are long, slow, and largely cross-country, and the upper face and chimney are exposed and unwise to downclimb in the dark, set a hard turnaround time that gets your party off the technical chimney and summit ridge well before afternoon thunderstorms typically build, leaving enough daylight to complete the rappels and the multi-hour traverse back to High Pass before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the reliable window: the High Pass/Low Pass approach holds steep, exposed snow that can linger into August and requires an ice axe and snow travel experience, while the loose rock and chimney climb best and driest later in summer. Expect the route to be out of condition outside this window.
routes.fa: Dwight Baker, Fred Beckey, and John Dudra — September 21, 1952 (first ascent of the Southeast Face line specifically; the peak's overall first ascent was a different route, climbed September 14, 1930 by Alec Dalgleish, Tom Fyles, Stan Henderson, and R.A. Fraser).
routes.rappels: 2 rappels (~60m, double-rope) from the top of the Great Chimney/keyhole
objHaz: lingering snow in gullies through August
routes.face: Southeast Face
routes.detailedRack: A light rock rack suffices: small-to-medium nuts/stoppers and a handful of cams (finger-to-hand sizes) to protect the two short pitches through the Great Chimney's chockstone constrictions, plus a few slings/draws. Many parties carry two ropes (e.g., twin 60m) to speed the double-rope rappels on descent.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are modest — small-to-medium chocks and a few cams cover the chimney's constrictions and chockstone steps; a full trad rack is unnecessary given the route's short technical sections.
gear: light rack: small-medium nuts and cams
ice axe (through ~July, for High/Low Pass snow)
whatToBring: map/compass or GPS (route is unmarked, cross-country navigation)
extra water (last reliable source is Gargett Mine/snowmelt)
proTips: Fill water at Gargett Mine or lingering snowmelt patches — there's little reliable water beyond High Pass.
Ridge rock quality briefly improves near the summit tower before degrading again — test holds constantly regardless.
Carrying two ropes lets you make the chimney rappels in fewer stages and avoids getting a single rope stuck.
watchOut: Rockfall dislodged by your own party or others in the chimney and on the upper face
Losing the unmarked route across talus/heather traverses in fog or low visibility
Steep snow lingering in the approach gullies into August, requiring an ice axe
knownHazards: Notoriously loose, 'rotten' rock throughout the route, especially on the upper face and ridge.
Route-finding is difficult; the line to the Great Chimney is not obvious and cairns are sparse.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common over this exposed border ridge in late summer.
The return traverse over talus, scree, heather, and brush is slow and becomes treacherous when wet or in the dark.

pitchDetail.notes: Cross-country scrambling from the saddle/basin below the peak across talus, scree, and heather to the base of the southeast face and the Great Chimney.
Short 5.3 moves gain the base of the chimney, followed by easier chockstone steps.
5.4 face climbing past a large chockstone to a tight 'keyhole' squeeze at the top of the chimney; some parties report this section as harder (up to 5.8) depending on conditions/line.
Loose, dirty, slabby trough leads to the summit ridge, finishing with an exposed step-across move to the summit.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065)
road.status: Paved/gravel and passenger-car passable to the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake Trailhead at 4.5 miles; beyond that it is narrow, unmaintained for passenger vehicles, with few pullouts and possible mining truck traffic in summer.
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive 12 miles east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) to the WSDOT Shuksan maintenance yard and turn onto FS Road 3065. Continue 4.5 miles to the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake Trailhead (passenger-car parking), then 2 more rough miles to the Twin Lakes Trailhead (5,200 ft) if your vehicle has adequate clearance; otherwise park at Yellow Aster Butte and add ~2 miles each way on foot.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, snowy Cascade winters with a short, dry-ish alpine climbing season; the route is realistically climbable only a few months a year.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack persists on the approach trail and gullies typically into June/July.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep) for this route; afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
climate.fall: Weather cools and destabilizes quickly after September; early snow can return by October.
climate.winter: Deep snow and avalanche terrain; the access road is not maintained or plowed.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mount Baker Wilderness
access.fees: $5/day or $30/year day-use fee at the Twin Lakes Trailhead
access.permit: Self-issue wilderness permit typical at the trailhead; no advance reservation required for climbing in Mount Baker Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass (Washington State Discover Pass is NOT valid here)
access.closures: FS Road 3065 beyond the Yellow Aster Butte/Tomyhoi Lake Trailhead is unmaintained for passenger vehicles and subject to washouts; check Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF road alerts before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (alpine start) — the long cross-country approach, exposed chimney, and afternoon thunderstorm risk favor an early start.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office (dispatches Whatcom County Search & Rescue / Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council)
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker Ranger District, 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244 — (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center (Haggen Family Emergency and Trauma Center, Level II Trauma), Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unconfirmed/likely unreliable in this remote border-ridge terrain; no source-verified statement on coverage was found.

routes.corrections: The provided data left 'fa' blank; sources clearly distinguish the peak's overall first ascent (1930, Dalgleish/Fyles/Henderson/Fraser, via a different line) from the Southeast Face route's own first ascent (1952, Baker/Beckey/Dudra) — the route-specific FA was filled accordingly rather than the peak FA. No other supplied fields conflicted with sourced data.

blurb: Waterfall Basin sits at the head of the Squire Creek drainage in the Boulder River Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), tucked below the towering east face of Three Fingers near Darrington, Washington. Its granite/granodiorite features — the slabby, patina-edged Roan Wall, the east-facing Waterfall Buttress, the south face of the spire Salish Peak, and the overhanging boulder Martha's Place — were developed almost entirely by Chris Greyell and partners (Todd Karner, David Tower, Hanna) between 2005 and 2008. Reaching the basin requires a multi-hour approach with creek fords and brush through a landslide-damaged access road, rewarding climbers with sustained, high-quality multi-pitch face and slab routes in a remote, rarely-visited cirque.
hierarchyNote: Waterfall Basin is not a single summit — it is a cirque/basin at the head of Squire Creek below the east face of Three Fingers, containing several distinct climbing features (Roan Wall, Waterfall Buttress, Salish Peak, Martha's Place boulder) that the 5 listed routes ascend. All are correctly grouped under one Mountain Project "Waterfall Basin" area, itself a sub-area of "Squire Creek Wall" in the Darrington, WA climbing region — geographically consistent with a North Cascades/Darrington parent hierarchy. The elevationFt given below (2,659 ft) is the basin/approach-area elevation, not a summit height; individual routes top out at different elevations (e.g. Flight of the Falcon tops Salish Peak at 5,680 ft). No name/coordinate mismatch found — treat as confirmed, just note it's a multi-feature basin rather than a single peak.

routes.fa: Chris Greyell, Todd Karner, 2006
routes.rappels: Rappel from the top of Pitch 7 using 2x60m ropes (all belays bolted up to P7), or continue to the summit and walk off left/west (~40 min summit to packs).
objHaz: exposed Pitch 7 'Falcon's Perch' with ~500 ft vertical dropoff
creek crossing (log crossing of Salish/Squire Creek)
routes.face: Salish Peak, South Face
routes.overview: A 9-pitch, 800-foot Grade III-ish line up the steep south face of Salish Peak, a striking spire just northeast of Three Fingers, climbing ramps, corners and short steep crux sections at a sustained 5.9-ish grade with two harder 5.10 pitches.
routes.beta: One of the best remote alpine rock routes in the North Cascades (listed among the '15 best routes in the Cascades' in the guidebook Cascades Rock). Mostly sustained ~5.9 climbing with brief 5.10 cruxes on Pitches 3 and 6; 34 protection bolts total and bolted belay anchors up to Pitch 7. Pitch 6 is the main crux, face climbing up a green-tinted slab; Pitch 7 tops out at 'Falcon's Perch,' an exposed viewpoint. Pairs well with Roan Wall - Center Stage for a big alpine linkup day for fast parties.
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive to the end of Squire Creek Road (a 2002/2003 landslide destroyed ~200 yards of road; expect a footpath detour/road-walk). Start on the flat old roadbed (hikeable in the dark) for about 1 hour, reaching white boulders and descending into an alder grove after ~1.5 hours. Cross Salish/Squire Creek on a log (~2 hours in), then hike up the dry creekbed to Martha's Place, a large bivy boulder at the head of the basin. From there, push 200 ft through thick brushy alder to a rocky watercourse, climb two short approach pitches at the base of Roan Wall (a 5.8 bolted slab, then a low-5th-class mungy crack), then scramble left along the base of Roan Wall to reach the base of Salish Peak. Total approach is roughly 6 hours and 6+ miles with about 3,500 ft of gain to the base of the route.
routes.descent: Rappel from the top of Pitch 7 with 2x60m ropes, or climb to the true summit and walk off left/west.
routes.descentText: Two descent options exist. Most parties rappel from the anchor atop Pitch 7 ('Falcon's Perch') using double 60m ropes back down the route. Parties who continue to the summit can instead walk off to the left (west), a descent that takes about 40 minutes from the summit back down to packs at the base, navigating brushy, low-angle terrain.
routes.itinerary: Given the 6+ hour approach, parties typically either commit to a single very-early (pre-dawn) push (one reported round trip was about 17 hours 50 minutes car-to-car), or hike in during the afternoon/evening and bivy at Martha's Place boulder near the head of the basin, climbing the route the next day and hiking out — splitting the long round trip across two days.
routes.bail: The route is bolted with belay anchors up to Pitch 7, so parties can bail by rappelling from any belay with double ropes rather than committing to the summit.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach and exit both involve brushy, creek-crossing terrain that is far harder to navigate after dark or in rain, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin to complete the rappel or walk-off descent and re-cross Salish/Squire Creek before nightfall or any incoming weather.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed in July and August; the basin's approach terrain holds snow into early summer at this elevation and the brush is markedly worse when wet, so a stretch of settled, dry weather is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Wires, a single set of cams tips to #3, and about 12 quickdraws/slings; bring 2x60m ropes if rappelling.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly bolt-protected with some natural gear placements on lower pitches; a single rack to #3 plus wires covers the route.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suitable for brush and scrambling
Bivy gear if splitting the approach over two days at Martha's Place
proTips: Martha's Place boulder is a good bivy spot if you want to do the approach the evening before.
Fast, motivated parties can link Flight of the Falcon on Salish Peak with Center Stage on Roan Wall in a single big day.
The first hour on the old roadbed is flat and hikeable in the dark, making a pre-dawn start practical.
watchOut: The approach is long (6+ hours) and involves route-finding through brush, boulders, and a creek crossing.
Pitch 7 ('Falcon's Perch') is exposed with a roughly 500-foot vertical dropoff.
Wet brush and rock significantly slow the approach and climb.
knownHazards: Thick alder brush can be very wet and slow after rain.
Squire/Salish Creek crossing can be difficult at high flow.
Long, remote approach increases commitment if weather or injury forces a slow retreat.

pitchDetail.notes: Bolted slab approach pitch at the base of Roan Wall.
Low-fifth-class 'mungy' crack approach pitch.
One of two crux pitches.
Main crux: face climbing up a green-tinted slab.
Tops out at 'Falcon's Perch,' an exposed viewpoint with ~500 ft vertical dropoff; rappel anchor here.

road.name: Squire Creek Road (Darrington)
road.status: Drivable to a landslide washout point, then foot travel required
road.driveNote: From Darrington, take Darrington Street off the Mountain Loop Highway to the road end. A landslide (reported 2002/2003) destroyed roughly 200 yards of the road; a footpath crosses the slide but adds meaningful road-walking distance (reports range ~1/3 mile to several miles depending on year/washout state) before reaching the true trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate; high terrain holds snow into July and it returns by October.
climate.spring: Snow-covered approach and route; creek fords running high with snowmelt — not recommended.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Aug); brush and creek crossings are at their easiest.
climate.fall: Possible into September before snow returns; days shorten and weather grows less stable.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective; road and trail typically snow-covered.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (Boulder River Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required to park at the trailhead; no separate climbing fee.
access.permit: No permit required for day climbing; standard Wilderness regulations apply within Boulder River Wilderness.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful pass
access.closures: Squire Creek Road has a longstanding landslide washout requiring a footpath detour/road-walk; check current Darrington Ranger District alerts before going.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (alpine) start; the first hour of old roadbed is hikeable in the dark.

waypoints.name: Waterfall Basin (Roan Wall / Salish Peak approach base)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff / SNOCOM non-emergency dispatch: (425) 407-3999 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest — 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Level IV Emergency Dept.), 330 S. Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA
emergency.notes: No confirmed cellular coverage data for this specific drainage; treat this remote, forested valley below Three Fingers as having unreliable-to-no cell signal and carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

routes.corrections: Route length is reported as 800 ft (242 m) by Mountain Project vs. ~700 ft by the Steph Abegg trip report; Mountain Project (a required primary source) was used as the primary figure.

routes.fa: Chris Greyell, Hanna, 2008
routes.rappels: Rappel the route with 2x60m ropes; stay skier's-right during the lower-angle section to avoid the descent gully and creek.
objHaz: base commonly buried under residual avalanche snow much of the season
adjacent slab-waterfall gully/rockfall/water hazard on descent
routes.face: Prominent rib above Martha's Place, left of the major slab waterfall (Squire Creek Wall, Waterfall Basin)
routes.overview: A 6-pitch, 800-foot Grade III alpine rock route up the prominent rib above the Martha's Place bivy boulder, mixing bolted face climbing with thin cracks on quality granite.
routes.beta: Ascends the rib left of Roan Wall's major slab waterfall, which gives the route some protection from snow/debris shed from above — though the base is still commonly buried under residual avalanche snow for much of the climbing season. Facing east, it gets direct morning sun and afternoon shade. Climbing is a varied mix of bolted face and mostly-thin cracks; described as a modern Darrington-area classic with some climbing more reminiscent of Index or Yosemite than typical Darrington granite.
routes.approach: Share the shared Waterfall Basin approach from Darrington via Squire Creek Road (landslide detour), old roadbed, alder grove, and creek crossing to Martha's Place bivy boulder. From Martha's Place, hike directly up talus to the toe of the buttress, then traverse roughly 200 feet leftward along its base. Gain the start ledge via mid-fifth-class bouldering and some scrambling. Overall approach runs roughly 3 to 3.5 hours from the Squire Creek Road parking area.
routes.descent: Rappel the route with 2x60m ropes.
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the route with double 60m ropes. On the lower-angle section, stay skier's-right to avoid dropping into the adjacent gully and creek below the slab waterfall.
routes.itinerary: Feasible as a long single day from the Squire Creek Road trailhead (~3-3.5 hr approach each way plus climbing and rappel descent), or shorten the summit day by hiking in the evening before and bivying at Martha's Place, which sits directly at the base of the buttress approach.
routes.bail: All belays and rappel anchors on the route are bolted, allowing a clean bail/rappel from any point on the route with double ropes.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour brushy approach and the need to re-cross the creek and navigate the descent gully area, set a turnaround time that ensures the rappel descent and exit are finished well before dark or any deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best in mid-to-late summer (roughly July-September); the base of the route is commonly still buried under residual avalanche snow earlier in the season, and high terrain in the basin holds snow into July.
routes.detailedRack: Medium rack of cams and nuts to 2 inches, with extra pieces smaller than 1 inch. All belays and rappel anchors are bolted.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly bolt-protected face climbing supplemented by natural gear on the thin crack sections; a medium rack to 2" with extra small pieces covers it.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for talus and scrambling
Layers for an east-facing route with afternoon shade
proTips: The buttress is left of the major slab waterfall, which offers some (not complete) protection from debris shed above.
On the rappel descent, stay skier's-right on the lower-angle section to avoid the gully and creek.
watchOut: The base of the route is commonly buried under residual avalanche snow for much of the climbing season.
The approach requires creek crossing and brush navigation similar to the other Waterfall Basin routes.
knownHazards: Residual avalanche snow/debris can persist at the base into summer.
Descent gully near the slab waterfall carries rockfall/water hazard if the wrong line is taken.

pitchDetail.notes: Technical face and crack climbing, mainly thin cracks.
Easier, blocky terrain.
Most challenging section; airy climbing.
Optional short finishing pitch.

road.name: Squire Creek Road (Darrington)

road.driveNote: From Darrington, take Darrington Street off the Mountain Loop Highway to the road end; a landslide destroyed roughly 200 yards of the road, requiring a footpath detour before the true trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate; high terrain holds snow into July and it returns by October.
climate.spring: Route base typically still snow/avalanche-debris covered.
climate.summer: Primary season; base may still be partly buried early on.
climate.fall: Possible into September before weather deteriorates.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective.

routes.fa: Chris Greyell
routes.rappels: Descend by rappel/lowering; the 13 bolts are split with an anchor at a mid-route ledge to reduce rope drag across the 2 pitches.
objHaz: thin, technical footwork with fall potential onto a ledge ("don't fall or your shins are toast")
routes.face: Martha's Place boulder, downhill/lower side
routes.overview: A short, bolt-protected 2-pitch 5.11+ sport testpiece on the downhill side of the Martha's Place bivy boulder in Waterfall Basin, finishing atop the boulder.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 (5.11+, ~25 ft) is a very thin traversing pitch where precise footwork is essential — a fall risks hitting the ledge below. Pitch 2 (5.10+) heads straight up the slab to the arete and tops out on Martha's Place. Fully bolted with 13 bolts total; the belay is split at a ledge partway up to prevent rope drag. Noted for excellent bolt placement.
routes.approach: Shares the approach to Waterfall Basin/Martha's Place with the other routes in the basin (Squire Creek Road from Darrington, old roadbed, alder/creek crossing to the Martha's Place boulder). The route itself starts on the downhill side of the Martha's Place boulder.
routes.descent: Rappel or lower off the route's bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: A short route typically descended by rappel or lowering directly off the bolted anchor system; the mid-route ledge anchor allows a controlled two-stage descent if needed.
routes.itinerary: Usually climbed as a short add-on before/after approaching to Roan Wall, or while based at the Martha's Place bivy, rather than as a standalone full-day objective.
routes.bail: Short and fully bolted; easy to bail by lowering or rappelling from either bolted anchor.
routes.bestSeason: Same window as the rest of Waterfall Basin — roughly July through September, once the basin's approach terrain and creek crossings are manageable.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport route; a set of quickdraws (enough for 13 bolts) is sufficient, no trad rack needed.
routes.proNeeds: Bolt-protected throughout (13 bolts); no gear placements required.
whatToBring: Rock shoes suited to thin, technical footwork
proTips: Footwork is the key to Pitch 1 — precise, careful foot placements matter more than power.
Approach via the downhill side of the Martha's Place boulder; full approach beta is covered on the main Roan Wall/Waterfall Basin pages.
watchOut: The thin traversing first pitch has real fall consequences onto a ledge below — described locally as "don't fall or your shins are toast."
knownHazards: Traversing nature of Pitch 1 means a leader or follower fall can result in a pendulum onto rock/ledge features rather than a clean fall.

pitchDetail.notes: ~25 ft, very thin traversing pitch right; footwork critical, fall risk onto ledge below.

routes.fa: David Tower, Chris Greyell, 2005
routes.rappels: Rappel via the route's new double-ring stainless-steel bolted anchors at each belay (double ropes recommended).
routes.face: Roan Wall, center line split by a prominent corner system
routes.overview: A 10-pitch, roughly 1,000-foot Grade III route up the center of Roan Wall, a big red slab split by a prominent corner system, on very high-quality granite with patina edges, friction slab, and cracks higher up.
routes.beta: Climbs the middle of Roan Wall via a 5.8 bolted slab approach pitch, a 5.4 approach pitch, sustained 5.9-5.10 edge and face pitches, and a 5.11a crux slab pitch (P7), finishing with mixed 5.9-5.10- climbing. All belays feature brand-new double-ring stainless-steel bolted anchors. An enormously overhung boulder (Martha's Place) sits near the base and offers a good camp/bivy spot.
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive Squire Creek Road to the landslide-blocked road end (about 30-40 minutes on foot from the car, crossing the slide). Continue roughly 20-30 minutes to a large dry streambed with white boulders, then 30+ minutes through alder on a faint path to Squire Creek, fording the creek and continuing through sections of underground creek bed. Reach a thin, rocky watercourse leading to the base of Roan Wall, climbing two approach pitches (an 80 ft bolted 5.8 slab, then a 150 ft low-5th-class groove). Total approach is roughly 3 to 3.5 hours from the Squire Creek Road parking area.
routes.descent: Rappel the route on its bolted double-ring anchors.
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the route itself, using the new double-ring stainless-steel bolted anchors present at each belay station; bring double ropes.
routes.itinerary: Feasible in a single long day (~3-3.5 hr approach, full 10-pitch climb, rappel descent, ~3-3.5 hr exit), or split by bivying at the Martha's Place boulder near the base the night before to shorten the climbing day. Pairs with Flight of the Falcon on Salish Peak for a big linkup for fast, motivated parties.
routes.bail: New double-ring bolted anchors at every belay make it straightforward to bail and rappel the route from any pitch.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour approach and the need to re-ford Squire Creek and navigate brush on the way out, set a turnaround time that leaves ample margin to complete the rappel descent and exit before dark or worsening weather.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly July through September; the basin holds snow into early summer at this elevation and the creek ford/approach brush is easiest once the basin has dried out.
routes.detailedRack: About 14 quickdraws plus a single set of nuts and cams to 2 inches; all belays have new double-ring stainless steel bolted anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Predominantly bolt-protected slab and face climbing with some natural gear on crack sections; a single light rack to 2" plus ~14 draws covers it.
gear: Double 60m ropes for the rappel descent
whatToBring: Bivy gear if splitting the day at Martha's Place
proTips: Martha's Place, the huge overhung boulder near the base, is a good bivy spot the night before.
Fast parties can link Center Stage with Flight of the Falcon on Salish Peak in one big day.
All belays now have brand-new double-ring stainless anchors, making rappel descent straightforward.
watchOut: The approach is long and involves creek fording, brush, and route-finding through a watercourse before reaching the wall.
Pitch 7's crux slab is the technical crux of the route at 5.11a.
knownHazards: Creek ford can be difficult at high flow, especially early season.
Approach navigation through alder and the watercourse is not straightforward and benefits from prior beta.

pitchDetail.notes: Bolted slab, 30m.

Edge climbing.

routes.fa: Chris Greyell, Todd Karner, Hanna, David Tower, 2007
routes.rappels: Traverse to a narrow ledge, descend down and slightly left to the top of Center Stage, then rappel Center Stage's bolted double-ring anchors with double ropes.
objHaz: sustained slab climbing with friction cruxes
route-finding through the shared approach watercourse
routes.face: Roan Wall, right-hand slab (~200 ft right of the Center Stage start, between two left-facing corners)
routes.overview: A 10-pitch, roughly 1,400-foot Grade III route up the rightward slab of Roan Wall, offering stacked pitches of quality face climbing with friction cruxes, corner work, and a unique finishing pitch with an overlap crack and low-angle face climbing.
routes.beta: Shares its approach and lower access with Center Stage; before reaching Center Stage's start, traverse about 200 feet right to a pale/white slab between two obvious left-facing corners. Features a pale slab approach pitch, face pitches with friction cruxes, corner climbing, sustained slab sections with crimpy holds, and finishes on a unique pitch combining an overlap crack with low-angle face climbing. Climbs the same very high-quality granite as the rest of Roan Wall.
routes.approach: Identical to Center Stage's approach: from Darrington, Squire Creek Road to the landslide-blocked road end, old roadbed and dry streambed to white boulders, alder to Squire Creek, ford the creek, continue through the watercourse to Roan Wall's base (two approach pitches: 5.8 bolted slab, then a low-5th-class groove), then traverse right about 200 ft along the base to the Stage Right start. Roughly 3 to 3.5 hours total from the road-end parking.
routes.descent: Traverse to a narrow ledge, descend down-left to the top of Center Stage, then rappel Center Stage's bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: From the top of Stage Right, find a narrow ledge and work left and slightly downward to reach the top of the Center Stage route. From there, rappel Center Stage's bolted double-ring anchors (the face is described as clean and open) using double ropes.
routes.itinerary: Typically a long single day given the shared ~3-3.5 hr approach with Center Stage, full 10-pitch climb, and rappel descent via Center Stage's anchors; alternatively bivy at Martha's Place near the base the night before to shorten the summit day.
routes.bail: Because the descent route funnels onto Center Stage's bolted anchors, a party can bail from Stage Right by traversing to reach those anchors and rappelling from there, or, on lower pitches, retreating the way they came if within reach of gear/bolts.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour approach and the need to re-ford Squire Creek on the way out, set a turnaround time with enough margin to finish the traverse-and-rappel descent via Center Stage's anchors before dark or deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly July through September, matching the rest of Waterfall Basin's climbing window once the approach brush and creek ford are manageable.
routes.detailedRack: 10-12 quickdraws and a light rack of cams (small sizes up to 3 inches); double 60m ropes.
routes.proNeeds: Predominantly bolt-protected slab/face climbing supplemented by a light cam rack on the corner and crack sections; double ropes needed for the Center Stage rappel descent.
gear: Light cam rack, small sizes to 3"
whatToBring: Bivy gear if splitting the day at Martha's Place
proTips: Traverse about 200 feet right of the Center Stage start to a white slab between two obvious left-facing corners to find the Stage Right start.
The descent funnels onto Center Stage's new bolted anchors — know that route's top-out before committing.
watchOut: The approach is long and shares all the brush/creek hazards of the Center Stage approach.
The descent requires correctly locating the ledge traverse over to Center Stage's anchors.
knownHazards: Creek ford can be difficult at high flow, especially early season.
Sustained slab climbing with friction cruxes demands careful footwork, particularly if rock is damp.

blurb: Mount Buckindy is a 7,320-foot summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, in the remote Buckindy Range/Buckindy Group alongside Mounts Misch, Bruseth, and Snowking. The peak's rusty-red coloring comes from a mineralized breccia pipe in the rock, and its standard line is a class 4 scramble up the north slopes above Kindy Creek, past glacier remnants on the north and east flanks. It was first climbed August 28, 1955 by Don Grimlund, Dave Nicholson, and Win Trueblood, and remains one of the least-visited summits in Washington, reachable only by multi-day, largely off-trail ridge approaches.
hierarchyNote: Confirmed real, identifiable peak: Mount Buckindy, Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF, Darrington Ranger District), North Cascades, Skagit County, WA — coordinates 48.3514°N, 121.2061°W (USGS/Wikipedia), consistent with the "North Cascades" parent grouping. One data issue found: the given elevationFt (7,352) does not match sourced figures — Wikipedia (citing USGS/GNIS) and Peakbagger.com both give 7,320 ft; listsofjohn.com gives a slightly different LiDAR-based 7,340 ft. Recommend correcting elevationFt to 7,320 (or noting the 7,320/7,340 datum spread) rather than 7,352, which no source supports. No other hierarchy problems found.

routes.overview: Buckindy's standard route climbs class 4 rock on the peak's north slopes, passing near glacier remnants on the north and east flanks below the summit rocks — reached only after a long, remote ridge approach through the Buckindy Range in the heart of the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
routes.beta: The standard (and only non-technical) line on Buckindy climbs class 4 terrain on the north slopes, close by glacier remnants on the mountain's north and east flanks. There is no maintained trail to the peak itself — parties reach it via a multi-day, largely cross-country ridge traverse, most commonly starting from the Green Mountain Trailhead to the south via Suiattle River Road, or alternatively via the Kindy Ridge climbers' trail off Cascade River Road to the north, following the connecting ridgeline of the Buckindy Range. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, Vol. 1 (Columbia River to Stevens Pass) remains the primary printed route reference. Separate class 5 rock climbing exists on the southwest-facing 'Buckindy Crags,' outside the scope of this standard scramble route.
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive north on SR 530 roughly 7-8 miles, turning right onto Suiattle River Road (FR 26) just after crossing the Sauk River bridge. Follow FR 26 (pavement then gravel) about 20 miles, turn left onto FR 2680, and continue 6 miles to the Green Mountain Trailhead (~3,200 ft). Hike Green Mountain Trail #782 (about 4 miles, ~3,000 ft gain) through old growth and subalpine meadows to the Green Mountain Lookout area, then continue cross-country along the connecting ridge system east/southeast into the Buckindy Range, crossing intervening terrain before reaching Buckindy's north slopes and glacier remnants. IMPORTANT CURRENT CONDITION: as of 2026, FR 26 (Suiattle River Road) is closed to all motor vehicles at milepost 4 due to a ~75-ft-deep washout from an atmospheric river flood, under a USFS closure order in effect until January 1, 2028 (or earlier rescission) — the Green Mountain Trailhead is not reachable by vehicle, which adds substantial road-walking mileage to this approach. An alternate northern approach exists via Cascade River Road to Kindy Creek Road (FS 1570) and the Kindy Ridge climbers' trail (from a small pullout near 1,850 ft, climbing to a saddle near 4,800 ft south of Point 5116) — this trail is documented primarily as the approach to neighboring Snowking Mountain but connects toward the Buckindy Range along the same ridge system.
routes.descent: Reverse the class 4 north-slope scramble to the connecting ridge, then retrace the approach ridge back to the Green Mountain Trailhead (or out via Kindy Ridge if approached from the north).
routes.descentText: Descend the same class 4 terrain used on ascent back to the ridge system below the north slopes, then follow the connecting ridgeline of the Buckindy Range back toward Green Mountain Trail #782 for the hike out (Mazamas rates the round trip 'car to car in two or more days'). If approached from the Kindy Ridge side, descend the climbers' trail back to Kindy Creek Road. No fixed rappel stations or established descent gully are documented for this route — retreat is a reversal of the approach ridge in both directions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike Green Mountain Trail #782 to the lookout area and continue along the connecting ridge into the Buckindy Range, establishing a high camp. Day 2: Continue the ridge traverse to Buckindy's north slopes, cross the glacier remnants, and complete the class 4 scramble to the summit, returning to high camp. Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead (add extra time/mileage currently for the FR 26 closure).
routes.bail: There is no fixed bail-out line. Retreat means reversing the ridge traverse back toward Green Mountain (or Kindy Ridge if approached from the north). Because the approach itself is multi-day and largely off-trail, turning back partway up the mountain still commits a party to a long cross-country return — carry overnight gear even on a planned 'day' summit push from high camp.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, off-trail nature of the approach, the presence of glacier remnants, and the total absence of cell coverage, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to re-cross the glacier and reverse the ridge traverse to camp; this is not terrain to be caught on after dark or in deteriorating weather without full overnight gear.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, when the ridge approach and glacier remnants are most consolidated and snow-free travel is easiest; Mazamas grades it a viable objective (Grade C) in spring, summer, and fall but does not recommend it in winter. Heavy snowpack typically lingers into June on the approach ridge.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected anywhere along the approach ridge or on the peak itself — this is deep wilderness far from cell infrastructure. A satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised, particularly with the current road closure lengthening any self-rescue.
routes.face: North Slopes
routes.fa: Don Grimlund, Dave Nicholson, Win Trueblood — August 28, 1955 (peak's first ascent; this north-slopes line is the standard/first-ascent route)
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard class 4 line. Carry a basic glacier-travel kit — ice axe, crampons, and a rope with minimal crevasse-rescue hardware (pickets and/or a couple of screws depending on conditions) — for the glacier remnants on the north/east flanks, plus a helmet for the loose class 4 scrambling near the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier-travel protection (pickets, occasional screws) is the main consideration rather than rock protection; the class 4 rock is typically climbed unroped by parties comfortable with sustained exposed scrambling.
gear: Map/compass or GPS for off-trail ridge navigation
whatToBring: Overnight/bivy gear for the multi-day approach
Water treatment (no reliable water sources on the upper ridge)
Satellite communicator or PLB (no cell coverage)
Extra food/fuel buffer for weather delays given the remote, multi-day approach
proTips: This is one of Washington's most remote and rarely-climbed alpine summits — budget extra days for the approach alone, not just the climb.
Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, Vol. 1 remains the primary printed route reference; bring it or transcribed notes since online route beta is thin.

Check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF / Darrington Ranger District alerts before planning any approach via Green Mountain — the Suiattle River Road washout closure materially changes the approach length.
watchOut: Loose, exfoliating rock is typical of the reddish breccia-pipe geology on the upper class 4 terrain.
Glacier remnants on the north/east flanks can be crevassed, especially in early season.
Extremely remote setting — any injury requires a multi-day self-extraction or helicopter rescue.
knownHazards: Glaciers on Buckindy's north and east flanks can harbor hidden crevasses, particularly in early season.
The mineralized, reddish breccia-pipe rock that colors the peak is reported to be loose in places on the class 4 scrambling.
A significant portion of the approach is off-trail cross-country ridge travel, with real potential for route-finding error in poor visibility.

pitchDetail.notes: Trailhead to high camp via Green Mountain Trail #782 and the connecting Buckindy Range ridgeline (or via Kindy Ridge from the north) — multi-day, largely off-trail ridge travel.
Cross glacier remnants on the north/east flank below the summit rocks; rope up for crevasse hazard, especially early in the season.
Class 4 scrambling on the north slopes to the summit; watch for loose rock in the reddish breccia-pipe terrain.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (FR 26) to Green Mountain Trailhead
road.status: closed
road.driveNote: FR 26 is closed to all motor vehicles at milepost 4 due to a ~75-ft-deep washout from a 2026 atmospheric river flood event; USFS closure order in effect through January 1, 2028 unless rescinded sooner. Green Mountain and Suiattle River Trailheads, Downey Creek Trailhead, and Sulphur Creek/Buck Creek Campgrounds are all closed for the season as a result. An alternate approach exists via Cascade River Road and Kindy Creek Road (FS 1570) to the Kindy Ridge climbers' trail on the range's north side.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades climate; the Buckindy Range holds snow well into summer and sees frequent rain/cloud even in the warm season.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack typically persists into June on the approach ridge; full glacier-travel gear is essential and avalanche hazard can linger into late spring.
climate.summer: July-August is the most reliable window, with the most snow-free scrambling, though the north/east-flank glacier remnants persist year-round.
climate.fall: September can offer stable weather but with shortening daylight; the first significant snow can arrive by early October.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective — Mazamas rates it not recommended (no seasonal grade) in winter, and the approach road is normally snowed in even without the current washout closure.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest — Darrington Ranger District, within Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: A Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/annual) is normally required for trailhead parking on this side of the forest; currently moot at the Green Mountain Trailhead, which is closed by the FR 26 washout.
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit required within Glacier Peak Wilderness (normally available at the trailhead).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

Green Mountain Trailhead, Suiattle River Trailhead, Downey Creek Trailhead, and Sulphur Creek/Buck Creek Campgrounds are all closed for the season.

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start from high camp to allow a full day for the glacier crossing, class 4 scrambling, and the return ridge traverse.
