emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (covers the Darrington access corridor): 425-388-3393, or 911 for emergencies; Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency: 360-428-3211
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest — (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Level IV Emergency Dept.), Arlington, WA — closest to the Darrington access side; Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III Emergency Dept.), Mount Vernon, WA is the nearest higher-level trauma-capable ED.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage exists along the approach ridge or on the peak itself; expect a multi-day ground evacuation or helicopter rescue for any serious incident given the remoteness, compounded by the current Suiattle River Road closure.

routes.corrections: Two discrepancies found versus the given input: (1) Peak elevationFt was given as 7,352; Wikipedia (USGS/GNIS-sourced) and Peakbagger.com both give 7,320 ft, and listsofjohn.com gives 7,340 ft — no source supports 7,352, recommend correcting to 7,320. (2) The input route grade was given as 'Class 3 + glacier'; Wikipedia, Mazamas, and secondary references to Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide all describe the standard north-slopes line as a Class 4 scramble (not Class 3) with glacier remnants nearby — recommend the route's difficulty designation be upgraded to Class 4.

blurb: West McMillan Spire (8,038 ft, USGS/older surveys list ~8,004 ft) is the higher of the twin "McMillan Spires" in the Southern Picket Range, deep in the glaciated interior of North Cascades National Park. It is a granite alpine summit rising above the Terror Glacier and Terror Basin, considered one of the most remote and accessible-only-by-serious-approach peaks in the lower 48, first climbed by Fred and Helmy Beckey in 1940 during their pioneering exploration of the Pickets. Despite the technical rock character of neighboring Pickets summits, its West Ridge is regarded as the range's most attainable objective — a long glacier-and-scramble route rather than a sustained rock climb.
hierarchyNote: None — peak identity, coordinates (~48.773N, -121.278W), and Southern Picket Range / North Cascades NP placement are confirmed consistent across Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and Peakbagger. Note only a minor elevation-source spread worth flagging: older USGS-derived sources (Wikipedia, WTA) cite ~8,000-8,004 ft for this summit, while listsofjohn.com's newer LiDAR-based figure (8,041 ft) closely matches the DB's existing 8,038 ft — the DB value appears to already reflect the more recent survey and was left unchanged.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, August 29, 1940
routes.rappels: Typically down-climbed rather than rappelled — the route is descended via the same ascent line/notch gully; no fixed rappel stations are reported.
routes.face: West Ridge
routes.overview: The West Ridge is the standard and most popular line up West McMillan Spire, the higher of the twin McMillan Spires in the Southern Pickets: a long, view-filled outing that gains the Inspiration-McMillan col via a steepening snow/ice gully, then follows mostly 3rd-4th class ridge, scree and talus to a short, exposed step below the summit block.
routes.beta: From a high camp in Terror Basin, cross rocky benches and the Terror Glacier's outlet lake, then climb onto the glacier and pick a line to the base of the ridge. A steep snow/ice gully (steepening to roughly 40 degrees near the top) leads to the ~7,200 ft col between Inspiration Peak and West McMillan Spire. From the col, follow the west ridge over broken rock, heather, scree and talus — climbers can choose more solid rock steps or looser talus depending on preference — to a short, exposed class 3 (occasionally cited as low 5th on variations) move onto the summit block.
routes.approach: Park at the Goodell Creek climber's trailhead/group campground off Newhalem Creek Road (reached via SR-20 through Newhalem); a wilderness permit from the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center is required for overnight camping. Follow an unmaintained, log-choked path roughly 3.5-4 miles up old roadbed and forest to the base of a steep hillside, then climb a steep, brushy hill trail (comparable to the old Mailbox Peak trail) gaining about 3,000-4,000 ft in under 2 miles to reach heather benches. Continue on benches and slabs, trending slightly downhill, to a glacial lake outlet, cross it, and ascend granite slabs onto the Terror Glacier below the McMillan Spires; most parties camp at Terror Basin (~5,800 ft) before continuing to the route.
routes.descent: Reverse the West Ridge back to the Inspiration-McMillan col, then descend the notch/gully to Terror Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ridge to the col. The notch drops roughly 180 m from the saddle to the Terror Basin floor and takes most parties 10-15 minutes to descend. Without snow cover, the north side of the notch is steep, loose choss ('intimidating' per multiple trip reports); staying as far skier's-right as possible is more stable, and parties should climb the class 3 rock to the right of the worst rubble gully rather than in it. On the remaining snow below, trend climber's-left toward faint switchbacks and keep groups tight to avoid rockfall/debris onto climbers below. Reverse the approach benches and hillside back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from the Goodell Creek trailhead to Terror Basin camp (~5,800 ft), roughly 4-5 hours over brushy trail, a steep hill climb, and heather benches. Day 2: Climb from camp across the Terror Glacier, up the snow/ice gully to the Inspiration-McMillan col, along the West Ridge to the summit, then reverse the route back to camp (many parties report ~8-12 hours round trip from camp or car-to-car). Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The route has no fixed retreat protection; below the col, downclimb the ascent line back toward Terror Basin. Above the col on the ridge, retreat is by reversing the same 3rd-4th class terrain used on the way up — there is no shorter bail option once established on the upper ridge, so turn around early if weather, rockfall risk, or party pace suggest trouble.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, exposed approach and a descent gully prone to rockfall as the day warms and snow softens, parties should plan to be off the summit and through the notch/gully well before afternoon heating increases rockfall and loose-rock hazard, and before glacier travel becomes more hazardous on softened snow.
routes.bestSeason: Most climbed July through August, once seasonal snow has consolidated enough for glacier travel but before late-season melt-out exposes the steep gully to bare ice and maximizes loose rock and scree on the ridge; some parties climb into September in low-snow years. Earlier-season (June) ascents can offer faster, safer travel on continuous snow but require full glacier/crevasse-crossing skills and gear.
routes.comms: Deep wilderness location in the North Cascades National Park interior with no reliable cell coverage reported by climbers; several trip reports note carrying a satellite phone/communicator as a precaution given the remoteness and multi-day rescue timelines.
routes.detailedRack: A technical rack is optional rather than mandatory. Most parties carry an ice axe and crampons for the Terror Glacier crossing and the snow/ice gully to the col, plus a helmet for loose rock above. Some parties bring a light rope and a small rack of cams/nuts to protect the short exposed class 3 (occasionally low 5th) step below the summit block, but many climb the entire route unroped.
routes.proNeeds: No sustained technical protection is needed on the standard line. Gear is used opportunistically: crampons/axe for the glacier and gully, and, for some parties, a short belayed pitch or two for the exposed step near the summit and on the loose descent gully.
gear: Small trad rack for the summit block (optional)
whatToBring: Backcountry/wilderness permit (obtained at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center)
Overnight camping gear for Terror Basin
Water filter (streams near mile 3.5 and at Terror Basin)
Trekking poles for the brushy approach and talus
Sturdy mountaineering boots (extensive talus, scree, and snow)
proTips: Start the approach very early to beat both heat and the dense forest deadfall on the lower trail.
Climbing when the gully to the col still holds continuous snow is generally faster and safer than late-season, when it turns to scree and loose rock.
Camping at Terror Basin (~5,800 ft) breaks the outing into a much shorter, safer summit day than pushing car-to-car.
On the ridge, parties can choose a line on more solid rock steps versus looser talus depending on comfort with exposure.
watchOut: The loose red/rubble gully leading to the Inspiration-McMillan col is a rockfall funnel; climbing the class 3 rock to its right avoids the worst of it.
The descent on the north side of the notch is steep, loose choss without snow cover — stay skier's-right and keep parties tight to avoid dropping rock on climbers below.
The Terror Glacier can have bare ice and open crevasses late season; rope up and carry crevasse-rescue gear if conditions warrant.
The approach trail has significant blowdown/deadfall and is easy to lose in the forest section.
knownHazards: Unroped travel on a crevassed glacier during the approach to the ridge.
Loose rock and talus throughout the upper mountain, worsened by parties above dislodging debris.
A short but genuinely exposed class 3 (sometimes rated low 5th) step directly below the summit block.
Extreme remoteness — any injury or incident requires a lengthy, difficult NPS/county SAR response.

pitchDetail.notes: ~4-5 mi with roughly 5,000-6,000 ft gain to camp at ~5,800 ft; heavy blowdown in the forest section.

pitchDetail.pitch: Glacier crossing to base of ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Cross the glacial lake outlet, ascend slabs onto Terror Glacier, pick a line to the base of the West Ridge.
Steepens near the top; narrows to a few feet at the col between Inspiration Peak and West McMillan Spire.

pitchDetail.grade: Class 3-4, short 5.6 step reported by some sources
pitchDetail.notes: Mostly scree, talus and broken rock with route-finding choice between solid rock and looser talus; a short exposed step gains the summit block.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20) to Newhalem Creek Road / Goodell Creek
road.status: Paved and open year-round to Newhalem and the Goodell Creek trailhead, which lie well west of SR-20's seasonal high-pass closure.
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally over Washington/Rainy Pass (typically ~Nov-Apr) far to the east; this does not affect access to Newhalem/Goodell Creek from the west (Marblemount/Sedro-Woolley side).
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, follow SR-20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem; turn left onto Newhalem Creek Road, left again at the T-junction, then take the next right and continue to the Goodell Creek campground/climber's trailhead.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades alpine climate with a short weather-dependent climbing season; the Pickets are known for holding snow and generating their own localized weather later into summer than more accessible Cascades peaks.
climate.spring: Continuous but avalanche-prone snowpack; glacier and gully travel is fast but requires full winter mountaineering skills and gear.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Aug); snow bridges and the gully to the col are more consolidated early, transitioning to bare ice/scree by late summer.
climate.fall: Possible into September in low-snow years, but the gully and ridge become looser and rockier as snow recedes.
climate.winter: Not a viable season for this route given approach length, avalanche hazard, and North Cascades storm exposure.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex (National Park Service)
access.fees: No day-use entry fee for North Cascades National Park; overnight backcountry camping requires a permit fee.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for any overnight stay (e.g., camping in Terror Basin); obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA — $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from Terror Basin camp to allow a full day for the glacier, gully, ridge, and loose descent while snow is firm.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (per USGS/Wikipedia); accessed and administratively coordinated via Skagit County/Marblemount for North Cascades NP wilderness travel
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 — routes to Skagit County Sheriff Communications, which coordinates search-and-rescue into the North Cascades National Park interior in cooperation with NPS rangers
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245; NPS park emergency dispatch (360) 873-4590
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley (360-856-6021) is closest to the Marblemount/Newhalem access corridor; Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (360-424-4111) is the regional hospital.
emergency.notes: One of the most remote peaks in the lower 48 Cascades; any rescue typically requires an NPS/Skagit County SAR helicopter or multi-day ground team. Self-sufficiency, a satellite communicator, and a filed itinerary/permit are strongly advised.

routes.corrections: The peak's existing 'Grade III, 5.6' summary is only partly matched by primary sources: Mountain Project's official West Ridge route page (a required primary source) rates the standard line as 4th class, Grade II, 2,000 ft, and virtually every trip report (Climber Kyle, Jeffrey Hebert, Eric's Base Camp, Jess B.) describes the route as a 3rd-4th class scramble with at most a short, exposed low-5th step below the summit block — not sustained 5.6 climbing. Some guidebook citations (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide / Selected Climbs in the Cascades) reportedly grade harder ridge-crest/variation lines at II-III, 5.6-5.7. Commitment above is set to 'II' per Mountain Project; rockGrade '5.6' is retained to reflect the guidebook-cited optional crux/variation rather than the everyday scrambling line most parties actually climb — treat it as an upper bound, not the typical experience. First-ascent date/party (Fred and Helmy Beckey, Aug 29, 1940) is corroborated by both Mountain Project and independent trip-report citations of the Beckey history.

blurb: Spider Mountain (8,317 ft) is a North Cascades summit in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of Chelan County, Washington, rising 1.5 miles east of Mount Formidable across the well-known Spider-Formidable Col on the classic Ptarmigan Traverse. The peak is built of greenish schist that erodes readily into scree, giving it a reputation among traverse parties as one of the range's less pleasant summits to climb, though its glaciated flanks (the Spider Glacier on the north face, the Middle Cascade Glacier to the west) are dramatic. It was first climbed July 25, 1938 by Calder Bressler (also spelled Bessler), Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers, members of the original Portland Ptarmigan Climbing Club party that pioneered the Ptarmigan Traverse.
hierarchyNote: None — Spider Mountain (8,317 ft, ~48.4095 N / -121.0361 W) is a real, well-documented North Cascades summit in Chelan County's Glacier Peak Wilderness, adjacent to Mount Formidable and accessed via the Cascade Pass/Ptarmigan Traverse corridor from North Cascades National Park. Name, elevation, and location are consistent across SummitPost, Wikipedia, and multiple trip-report sources; no mismatch found.

routes.overview: The standard scramble line on Spider Mountain: gain the high, glaciated col between Spider and Mount Formidable, then climb broken, loose schist gullies and ribs on the north side of the summit ridge to the true (west) summit.
routes.beta: Reached via the Cascade Pass Trail and the start of the Ptarmigan Traverse, this route climbs scree/talus and the Middle Cascade Glacier to the Spider-Formidable Col (~7,300 ft), then works up Class 3-4 terrain of notoriously loose, eroding schist to the summit. Most Ptarmigan Traverse parties treat it as a side trip from a camp at Kool-Aid Lake; strong single-push parties have done trailhead-to-summit-and-back via a direct glacier line in roughly 7-9 hours. The rock quality is widely described as poor, and the peak is only lightly climbed (the 1968 summit register saw just one or two parties a year for decades) despite its position as the second-highest summit between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft, road-end of Cascade River Road, 23.1 mi/37 km from Marblemount and WA-20), hike the maintained Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 mi (1,700 ft gain) to Cascade Pass (5,560 ft). Leave the trail and follow the Ptarmigan Traverse tread south, climbing scree and talus benches past Mix-up Arm toward the toe of the Middle Cascade Glacier (~6,500 ft). Rope up and cross the glaciated basin (crevasses present, worse late-season) to gain the broad Spider-Formidable Col. Alternatively, multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse parties approach via Cache Col and a camp at Kool-Aid Lake (6,100 ft, about 6 miles/6-7 hours from the trailhead), then traverse south past Red Ledge and up the Middle Cascade Glacier to the same col.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the loose gully/rib system back to the Spider-Formidable Col, then re-cross the Middle Cascade Glacier and descend scree/talus to Cascade Pass and the trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same broken schist terrain back to the Spider-Formidable Col — there is no easier alternate way off the upper mountain. From the col, re-rope for the glacier crossing back over the Middle Cascade Glacier, then descend the scree/talus slopes to rejoin the Cascade Pass Trail near the pass. Parties camped at Kool-Aid Lake instead descend north from the col area past Red Ledge back to camp before hiking out via Cache Col and Cascade Pass the following day. Rockfall triggered by the party itself or others above is a persistent hazard on the descent through the gullies.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-3 day plan: Day 1 — Cascade Pass Trailhead to Kool-Aid Lake camp via Cascade Pass and Cache Col (~6-7 hrs, ~3,500 ft gain). Day 2 — climb Spider Mountain via Red Ledge, the Middle Cascade Glacier, and the Spider-Formidable Col to the summit and back to camp (or continue the Ptarmigan Traverse). Day 3 — hike out. Fit parties can also do it as a single long day (~7-9 hrs car-to-car) via a direct line from Cascade Pass up the Middle Cascade Glacier to the col without an established camp.
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the route: downclimb to the Spider-Formidable Col and re-cross the glacier toward Cascade Pass. There is no shorter bail option once established in the summit gullies — the col is the only feasible way off the upper peak.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting that accounts for glacier travel softening on the Middle Cascade Glacier through the day and for rockfall risk in the summit gullies, which increases as the rock warms and as other parties climb above; do not linger on the loose upper mountain into the afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most reliable weather and driest rock/scree; the Cascade River Road and high camps (Cascade Pass, Kool-Aid Lake) typically aren't accessible until late June or early July due to snowpack, and permitted reservations for high-country camps are unavailable for trips starting before July 1.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light mountaineering boots
Overnight/bivy gear if camping at Kool-Aid Lake
Sun protection for extended glacier/snow travel
Map and compass or GPS for navigation around Cache Col in poor visibility
routes.detailedRack: No fixed rock protection is used on the standard line — it is a Class 3-4 scramble on loose schist. Parties carry a single rope (around 30m) for glacier travel and short-roping through crevassed sections of the Middle Cascade (and, for Traverse parties, Cache) Glacier, along with a basic crevasse-rescue kit (pickets or ice screws, prusik cord, pulley).
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are glacier-travel oriented (pickets/screws, crevasse rescue gear) rather than rock protection; the summit scrambling itself is unprotected.
proTips: Wear a helmet — the schist sheds loose blocks constantly and rockfall (self-triggered or from parties above) is one of the mountain's defining hazards.
Camping at Kool-Aid Lake (6,100 ft) splits the approach into two easier days and lets you cross the Middle Cascade Glacier early while snow is firm.
The true summit is the higher west high point, not the benchmarked east point (8,286 ft) — don't stop at the register on the lower point.
watchOut: Loose, crumbly schist throughout the upper mountain makes rockfall a constant concern, worse as the day warms or with parties above.
Crevasses on the Middle Cascade and Cache Glaciers, especially later in the season as snow bridges deteriorate.
Cornices on the summit ridge near the col can persist into early summer.
Afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving fronts are typical of North Cascades summers even in the best months.
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose, eroding schist on the summit gullies and ridge
Crevasses on the Cache Glacier and Middle Cascade Glacier
Cornices on the summit ridge near the Spider-Formidable Col in spring/early summer
Exposure to fast-changing North Cascades weather, including afternoon thunderstorms

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first third, then gravel with narrow/steep sections near the road-end
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead parking lot by late June; in heavy-snow years it may not open until early July
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles (37 km) from State Route 20 at Marblemount to the road-end trailhead parking lot, roughly a 1-hour drive

climate.typical: Wet, unsettled weather is common outside the July-September window; even in summer, afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving fronts are typical of the North Cascades crest.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June/July; the road and high camps are often inaccessible until late June.
climate.summer: The best climbing window (Jul-Sep), though afternoon convection and occasional new snow can occur at any time.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows with increasing chance of early snow by late September/October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; the road is closed and the approach becomes a serious ski/snowshoe undertaking (the peak's North Face has seen documented winter ski descents).

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Glacier Peak Wilderness) for the peak itself; the approach begins in North Cascades National Park (Cascade Pass Trailhead / Stephen Mather Wilderness) before crossing into the Glacier Peak Wilderness near Cache Col.
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay within North Cascades National Park (day use to Cascade Pass needs none); permits are available via Recreation.gov or as walk-ups (about 40% of quota held for walk-up) from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount. No permit is required once the route crosses into the Glacier Peak Wilderness past Cache Col; camping is not allowed at or near Cascade Pass itself.
access.closures: High-country camps including Cascade Pass/Pelton Basin cannot be reserved for trips starting before July 1 due to snow; the Cascade River Road itself is gated/impassable in winter and early spring.

timing.recommendedStart: Start early enough to cross the Middle Cascade (and, via Cache Col, the Cache) Glacier while snow is still firm, and to be off the loose summit gullies before afternoon warming increases rockfall risk or thunderstorms build.

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); Skagit County (Cascade River Road / trailhead access)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: North Cascades NP Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37 after hours; Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-9450 as backup
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (nearest to the Cascade River Road corridor)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable to absent along Cascade River Road and throughout the backcountry approach; the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center and North Cascades NP dispatch are the primary points of contact for trip planning and emergencies.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed real, identifiable peak: Ghost Peak sits in the Northern Picket Range, North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA (48.8236°N, 121.3414°W per Wikipedia; 48.82374, -121.34141 per Mountain Project — independently corroborating coordinates), 0.30 mi NNE of Phantom Peak and 0.20 mi south of Crooked Thumb Peak. Parent hierarchy (North Cascades/Picket Range) is geographically correct. One minor data note: reputable sources (Wikipedia/Peakbagger-derived, Mountain Project) consistently describe the summit elevation as "8,000+ ft" (a contour-interval estimate — there is no precise USGS spot elevation for this minor sub-summit), which is slightly higher than the given 7,993 ft. I could not find a more authoritative precise figure to justify overwriting 7,993 ft, so it is left as-is; flagging only in case a LIDAR-derived source you have access to differs.
blurb: Ghost Peak is a small, sheer rock horn in the Northern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, tucked between Crooked Thumb Peak (just north) and Phantom Peak (just south) and cut by a huge, steep east face. Rock quality is described as generally solid "as the Pickets go." It was first climbed on July 17, 1970 by Joan and Carla Firey, Peter Renz, and David Knudson during an eleven-day traverse of the Northern Pickets, part of the Fireys' storied multi-decade project to complete first ascents of every named Picket Range summit.

routes.fa: Joan Firey, Carla Firey, Peter Renz, David Knudson — July 17, 1970 (part of an 11-day Northern Pickets traverse)
routes.face: South face to the south arête, then the west face, to the summit
routes.overview: The original and standard line on Ghost Peak: gain the notch south of the summit from the west, then follow the south face and south arête onto the west face to the top, staying on obvious weaknesses the whole way — a short, low-5th/Class-4 outing once you're on the mountain, though reaching it is a serious multi-day wilderness approach.
routes.beta: Ghost Peak is normally climbed as an add-on from a high camp shared with neighboring Crooked Thumb Peak and Phantom Peak, since all three summits are within a few tenths of a mile of one another in the Northern Pickets. Mountain Project notes access from camp is 'obvious' once you're established there. The first-ascent party's own account: 'The route was from the notch south of the peak which was gained from the west. We stayed on the south and west sides of the mountain following obvious weaknesses' — easy-to-moderate 4th/low-5th-class climbing on comparatively solid Pickets rock.
routes.approach: There is no Ghost-Peak-specific published approach; climbers reach it via the standard Northern Pickets approach shared with Crooked Thumb and Phantom Peak. From the Hannegan Trailhead (~3,100 ft, end of Ruth Creek Road/FR 32 off the Mt. Baker Highway), follow the Hannegan Pass / Chilliwack River trail system: Hannegan Pass to Boundary Camp to Copper Creek Camp, cross the Chilliwack River via the hand-hauled cable car, continue on the Brush Creek Trail to Whatcom Pass, then move onto snow/glacier terrain and travel cross-country toward the Crooked Thumb Glacier area, establishing a high camp from which Ghost Peak, Crooked Thumb, and Phantom are all climbed. (An alternative Northern Pickets approach exists via Access Creek from the Big Beaver Trailhead on Ross Lake, but published trip reports for that line do not mention Ghost Peak specifically.)
routes.turnaround: This route sits deep in the Stephen Mather Wilderness at the end of a multi-day glacier approach with no quick retreat option — commit only with a solid multi-day weather window, and turn back from the ridge/glacier terrain early if weather deteriorates, since a full storm-bound retreat through Whatcom Pass and the Chilliwack drainage can take a full day or more on its own.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the realistic window: the first ascent itself was made July 17, and the closest published trip reports for the adjoining Crooked Thumb/Phantom high camp (late June–early July) still describe soft, ankle-deep snow above 7,000 ft and active bergschrunds on the approach glaciers, so early-season attempts should expect full glacier travel conditions.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Picket Range interior; this is classic deep-wilderness NPS backcountry, so a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly advised for the multi-day approach and summit day.
gear: a short rope for the exposed Class 4 sections near the summit
whatToBring: bivy/camping gear for the multi-day approach
map/compass or GPS for cross-country glacier travel
knownHazards: Multi-day, remote cross-country glacier approach with no quick evacuation route.
Bergschrund/crevasse hazard on the approach glacier persists into July per nearby trip reports.

road.name: Ruth Creek Road / Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Gravel USFS road to the Hannegan Trailhead; drivable in normal passenger vehicles when open, but has had washouts near the trailhead in recent years — check current alerts before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed by snow in winter; typically opens late spring depending on snowpack.
road.driveNote: From the Mt. Baker Highway (WA-542) near Glacier, WA, turn onto Ruth Creek Road/FR 32 and follow it to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead (~3,100 ft). Check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts for washout status before the trip.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC / NWS 'West Slopes North' zone (covers Mt. Baker and the North Cascades crest including the Picket Range)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and a short, drier alpine climbing window in summer; the Picket Range holds snow later than most Washington ranges due to its interior, high-elevation, glaciated character.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists on approach routes and glaciers well into early summer.
climate.summer: The main climbing window, roughly late June/July through September, though bergschrunds and crevasses remain active on approach glaciers even in July per nearby trip reports.
climate.fall: Snow can return to the high peaks by October, shortening the season.
climate.winter: Heavy snow; access road gated and trails largely unused for climbing.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park Service Complex, Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: North Cascades National Park has no general entrance fee. Backcountry wilderness permits cost $10 per person plus a $6 reservation fee for advance bookings (walk-up permits still require the $10/person fee); climbers 15 and under are free.
access.permit: An NPS wilderness permit is required for all overnight backcountry camping (including climbing high camps). Obtain in advance via Recreation.gov or as a walk-up at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: No Northwest Forest Pass or Discover Pass needed inside the National Park; a wilderness camping permit is the operative requirement.
access.closures: No route-specific closures found; the trailhead access road is subject to seasonal gating and washout-driven closures — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (routes to Whatcom County Sheriff / NPS dispatch coordination for backcountry rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA 98267 — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Pkwy, Bellingham, WA — 24-hour ER, (360) 734-5400
emergency.notes: Deep wilderness location with no nearby road access and no cell coverage; NPS helicopter rescue staged out of Marblemount is the typical evacuation method for climbing accidents in the Pickets. Severe trauma cases are sometimes flown onward to Harborview Medical Center in Seattle.

routes.corrections: Enriched the bare '1970' first-ascent year with the specific date (July 17, 1970) and full party (Joan Firey, Carla Firey, Peter Renz, David Knudson), cross-checked across Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and the American Alpine Club's published account of the Fireys' Northern Pickets traverse. Existing grade fields (Grade III, Class 4 / PD+) were not contradicted by any source found and were left unchanged.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed real, single-identity peak: Mount Crowder, 7,082 ft, Picket Range, North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness), Whatcom County, WA — coordinates 48°47'52"N 121°21'07"W match the given elevation exactly (Wikipedia/USGS). One geographic nuance worth flagging for hierarchy placement: Crowder sits on the far WEST edge of the Picket massif, on the Baker River/Picket Creek divide, and is reached by a completely different (and far more obscure) approach corridor — up the Baker River valley from Baker Lake, over Ruth/Icy/Mineral/Easy Peak and Easy Ridge to Perfect Pass, then a notorious "Crowder Bypass" — rather than the standard Goodell Creek / Access Creek approaches used for the main Northern and Southern Picket summits. If this app's hierarchy has separate "Northern Pickets"/"Southern Pickets" buckets, Crowder logically belongs in neither; a general "Picket Range" or "North Cascades National Park" parent fits best. No other mismatch found.
blurb: Mount Crowder is a remote 7,082-foot summit at the western edge of the Picket Range in North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, Whatcom County, Washington, rising sharply above the headwaters of the Baker River and Goodell/Picket Creek drainages. Long known to climbers as "Old Brownie," it was renamed in 1970 to honor Dr. Dwight F. Crowder, the USGS geologist and mountaineer who spent twelve years mapping and documenting the North Cascades (and co-authored the region's classic climbing guide, "Routes and Rocks in the Mt. Challenger Quadrangle"). It is one of the least-visited peaks in the Cascades, guarded by a long off-trail approach and a formidable terrain "bypass" that keeps traffic minimal even by Pickets standards.

routes.fa: Cal Magnusson, Jack Ardussi, Don Mech, Don Schmechel — 1962 (peak's first ascent, made when the summit was still called "Old Brownie"; the SW Flank is the range's documented easiest/standard line, so it is very likely, but not explicitly confirmed in print, that this was the 1962 party's ascent route)
objHaz: The "Crowder Bypass" on the peak's west side — steep snow, trees, and gullies with no real alternative, and reported by multiple parties as the crux/most formidable obstacle of the whole approach
Extensive off-trail travel and bushwhacking through the Sulphide/Crystal Creek drainages
Loose rock and exposure on the connecting ridge terrain (Icy/Ruth/Mineral/Easy Peak, Easy Ridge)
Avalanche and steep-snow hazard on the bypass and approach slopes in early season
Wet vegetation and poor visibility make the bypass significantly more dangerous
routes.face: Southwest Flank
routes.overview: The Southwest Flank is the standard, easiest line to Crowder's summit: open scrambling terrain on the peak's southwest side. The climbing itself is straightforward — the real challenge of this route is simply reaching its base, via one of the longest, most obscure approaches in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: Once through the approach and the infamous 'Crowder Bypass,' the SW Flank itself is a moderate 3rd/4th-class scramble on open slopes and ledges to the summit — no technical rock climbing required. Parties doing a standalone ascent typically descend the way they came; parties continuing a larger Pickets traverse instead descend the far more serious NE Ridge toward Pickell Pass.
routes.approach: From the Baker River Trailhead (end of Forest Service Road 11/1168, off SR 20 near Concrete), follow the Baker River Trail (#606) into the Baker River valley to the Sulphide Creek confluence. From there the route leaves maintained trail, ascending the Sulphide/Crystal Creek drainages with significant bushwhacking, then climbs onto the high ridge over Icy Peak, Ruth Mountain, Mineral Mountain, and Easy Peak to Easy Ridge, reaching Perfect Pass as a natural high camp. From Perfect Pass, traverse toward Crowder's west side and negotiate the 'Crowder Bypass' — steep snow, trees, and gullies with essentially no alternative line — before gaining the base of the Southwest Flank for the final scramble to the summit. (Parties already deep in a Northern/Southern Pickets traverse instead reach Crowder from the east, via Wild Pass/Wild Lake toward Pickell Pass.) This is a multi-day, largely off-trail wilderness approach through some of the least-visited terrain in the Cascades.
routes.descent: Reverse the Southwest Flank scramble back to Perfect Pass and the approach ridge (standard for a standalone ascent).
routes.descentText: For an out-and-back ascent, downclimb the same SW Flank terrain used on the way up, then reverse the Crowder Bypass and the Perfect Pass/Easy Ridge approach ridge back to the Baker River Trail. Parties continuing a larger Pickets enchainment instead descend the peak's NE Ridge toward a camp at Pickell Pass — this alternative is notably more serious: grassy 3rd-class ledges give way to occasional 4th-class moves and one or two short 5th-class downclimbs, with rappelling often used for the exposure. A major chasm/fault-line feature splits the lower NE Ridge, creating consequential no-fall terrain; multiple parties have described this descent option as sketchy and better avoided unless already committed to a traverse.
routes.itinerary: A realistic plan runs 4-6 days given the remoteness: Day 1 — hike the Baker River Trail to a camp near Sulphide/Crystal Creek. Day 2 — ascend through the Icy/Ruth/Mineral/Easy Peak ridge to a high camp at Perfect Pass. Day 3 — negotiate the Crowder Bypass, climb the SW Flank to the summit, and return to camp (weather/conditions permitting). Days 4-5/6 — reverse the approach ridge and hike out the Baker River Trail, with a spare day built in for the bypass or weather.
routes.bail: Retreat from Perfect Pass or the Crowder Bypass by reversing the approach ridge (Easy Ridge/Easy Peak/Mineral/Ruth/Icy) back down to the Baker River Trail — there is no shorter bail option once committed to the bypass or the upper mountain.
routes.turnaround: Given the length and seriousness of the approach, plan turnaround times that leave enough daylight — and enough days of food/fuel — to fully reverse the route, not just the summit push; do not attempt the Crowder Bypass in wet conditions or low visibility, and be willing to sit out weather rather than push through it.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is the favorable window, once the approach ridge and the Crowder Bypass have shed most of their snow; earlier in the season the bypass's steep snow and gullies carry meaningfully higher avalanche and slide risk, and the route is not a realistic winter objective.
routes.comms: This is one of the most remote corners of North Cascades National Park; expect no reliable cell coverage for the majority of the approach and climb — carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the SW Flank scramble itself. An ice axe (and often traction) is standard for the steep snow sections of the Crowder Bypass and approach ridge. Parties planning the alternative NE Ridge descent (as part of a larger traverse) commonly carry a light rope and a small rack/slings for the short 4th/5th-class downclimbing and occasional rappel.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed for the standard SW Flank ascent/descent; a light rack and rope are only warranted if descending the technical NE Ridge toward Pickell Pass.
gear: Light rope (~30m) and small rack — only if descending via the NE Ridge
whatToBring: Bear canister / approved food storage (NPS wilderness requirement)
Map, compass, and GPS for extensive off-trail navigation
Several spare days of food and fuel for weather delays
proTips: Scout the Crowder Bypass conditions carefully before committing — steep snow, trees, and gullies are far more serious once wet
Perfect Pass makes a good staging high camp for the final push to Crowder
Expect no trail and very likely no other parties — this is one of the least-visited corners of the Pickets
watchOut: The Crowder Bypass has no real alternative line and becomes genuinely dangerous in wet or low-visibility conditions
Long stretches of off-trail travel and bushwhacking through the Sulphide/Crystal Creek drainages before you're anywhere near the peak
Loose rock and exposure on the connecting ridge terrain over Icy/Ruth/Mineral/Easy Peak
knownHazards: A major chasm/likely fault-line feature splits the lower NE Ridge of Crowder, creating serious no-fall terrain for parties descending that way
Steep snow and dense vegetation on the west-side Crowder Bypass, described by multiple parties as the crux of the whole outing
Significant avalanche/slide hazard on approach and bypass slopes in early season or after new snow

pitchDetail.notes: Baker River Trail to Perfect Pass via Icy/Ruth/Mineral/Easy Peak and Easy Ridge; mostly off-trail ridge travel
West-side traverse of steep snow, trees, and gullies; no real alternative, much worse when wet
Final scramble on open slopes/ledges to the summit

road.name: Baker Lake Highway (Forest Service Road 11) to FS Road 1168
road.status: Paved for most of its length; gravel/unpaved for roughly the last 6 miles to road's end
road.driveNote: From SR 20 near Concrete, drive ~16 miles east to milepost 82, turn north onto Baker Lake Highway (FS Rd 11), and continue about 25.5 miles to road's end at the Baker River Trailhead. Not recommended for low-clearance sedans on the final gravel miles; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts, as a washout has at times blocked the final ~0.25 mile of road (temporary parking is available below the washout in that case).

climate.forecastZone: No dedicated NWAC zone covers this specific area; the nearest general reference is NWAC's West Slopes North (Mount Baker area) zone
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy precipitation and a deep, persistent snowpack typical of the western North Cascades
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; the Crowder Bypass and approach ridge are typically still snow-covered
climate.summer: Best climbing window (Jul-Sep) as snow recedes from the bypass and ridge, though afternoon thunderstorms and wet brush remain common
climate.fall: Early-season storms can return by late September/October, quickly re-covering the route
climate.winter: Severe weather and heavy snowpack make this a realistically inaccessible winter objective

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park, Stephen Mather Wilderness (approach trail begins on Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest land)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass required at the Baker River Trailhead (day-use fee currently $5/day or $30/year); no separate NPS park entrance fee
access.permit: NPS wilderness backcountry permit required for any overnight stay in North Cascades National Park; reserve via recreation.gov or self-issue at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount when it's closed for winter
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass/Interagency Recreation Pass at the trailhead; NPS wilderness permit for camping
access.closures: Check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving in — the final approach road has at times been washed out near its end, and the parallel Baker Lake Trail (#610, a different trail from the same trailhead) has been closed to stock due to bridge damage.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit); the Baker Lake/Baker River approach corridor lies near the Whatcom/Skagit county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies. Per NPS guidance for this part of the park, incidents are routed to the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office; NPS non-emergency law enforcement line (360) 854-7255.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245; park headquarters/visitor info (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 24-hour ER (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: This is a very remote, largely off-trail objective with minimal party traffic and no cell coverage for most of the route — self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator/PLB are strongly advised given the multi-day evacuation time from the upper mountain.

blurb: Little Big Chief (7,225 ft, Alpine Lakes Wilderness) enriched with both catalog routes: Northwest Face / Falcon Route (IV 5.9, 15 pitches) and the Northeast Face scramble/snow route, cross-checked against Mountain Project, the AAC's American Alpine Journal, Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and multiple trip reports.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity is well confirmed: name, coordinates (47.52965, -121.25668), and 7,225 ft elevation cross-check consistently across Mountain Project, Wikipedia, and Peakbagger. One geographic note worth flagging: Little Big Chief sits in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness / Central Cascades (Snoqualmie Pass corridor), on the King/Kittitas county line — not in the North Cascades National Park complex proper. If this peak's parent hierarchy in the app is filed under a "North Cascades" region node specifically (rather than a broader "Cascades"/Alpine Lakes/Central Cascades bucket), that placement should be double-checked; geographically it belongs with the Snoqualmie Pass-area / Alpine Lakes peaks (near Summit Chief, Overcoat Peak), not the North Cascades Highway/Mt Baker area.

routes.fa: Martin Volken & Jeff Hansell, September 10, 2001
routes.rappels: 1-2 rappels during the descent, from the ridge crest near 5,400' down toward the valley
objHaz: Rockfall/loose rock on the approach talus and lower pitches
Remote, committing position with slow rescue access
Glacier-polished descent slabs, slick when wet/snow-covered
routes.face: Northwest Face
routes.overview: A long, sustained 15-pitch alpine rock route up the remote Northwest Face of Little Big Chief on excellent gneiss, graded IV 5.9; also known as the Falcon Route, it sees very few ascents due to its deep, cross-country approach into Summit Chief Valley.
routes.beta: Named for a falcon that swooped the first-ascent party on the exposed summit ridge, this route climbs roughly 700 feet of 4th/low-5th-class terrain up a left-trending gully system, then continuous 5.6-5.9 crack and groove climbing on sound gneiss to an easy, exposed final ridge pitch at the 7,225' summit. An earlier published account (American Alpine Journal, via the AAC) frames the technical climbing as 8 named pitches (5.7-5.9) above 900 feet of talus; a more granular 15-pitch breakdown is documented by later parties. Both accounts agree the crux is a steep 5.9 headwall roughly mid-route, and that while never desperate, the climbing is remote and demands solid alpine skills.
routes.approach: From the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Trailhead (FR 56, ~1,040'), hike the Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Trail up-valley for approximately 3 hours. At a steel bridge crossing, leave the maintained trail and head south cross-country into the seldom-visited Summit Chief Valley, a tributary drainage of the Middle Fork. Little Big Chief rises on the east side of this valley. Continue up-valley through talus and brush to a flat gravel wash at approximately 4,650' where camping is available below the face.
routes.descent: South along the connecting ridge to a pocket glacier, then glacier-polished slabs (trending climber's left) back to the ridge crest near 5,400'; 1-2 rappels, a goat-ledge traverse, and a forest descent return to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, travel south along the ridge for several hundred feet to a small pocket glacier below the face. Cross glacier-polished slabs, trending climber's left, to regain the ridge crest at approximately 5,400'. From there, make one to two rappels down broken terrain into the valley, then traverse exposed goat ledges before a final forest descent back to the Summit Chief Valley camp. The glacier-polished slabs can be wet, snow-covered, or icy well into summer.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike in from the Middle Fork Trailhead (~3 hrs to leave the maintained trail, plus additional cross-country travel) and establish camp at the ~4,650' gravel wash in Summit Chief Valley. Day 2: Climb the Northwest Face (Falcon Route) and descend via the south ridge/pocket glacier back to camp. Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The lower ~700 feet of 4th/low-5th-class terrain offers reasonable downclimbing/scrambling retreat back to the base talus. No established rappel line down the upper wall is documented, so above this point parties should generally plan to continue to the summit and use the standard descent rather than attempt to reverse the upper pitches.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent requires crossing a pocket glacier and glacier-polished slabs before regaining the ridge, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to finish both the climb and the multi-stage descent (glacier traverse, rappels, ledges, forest) before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, once the approach gully and glacier-polished descent slabs have shed most seasonal snow; given its remote, multi-day character the route sees very few ascents per year.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack (cams from finger- to hand-size plus a set of nuts) with a few small/thin pitons for seam placements on the lower pitches; a single rack is generally reported sufficient given the moderate, well-protected nature of the climbing.
routes.proNeeds: Good, continuous protection on gneiss; bring a standard alpine trad rack plus a few thin pitons for seams. Descent rappel anchors are typically natural (blocks/horns) or existing slings — carry rap rings/cord.
whatToBring: Overnight/bivy gear for the multi-day approach
Approach shoes for talus and cross-country travel
Ice axe (early-season glacier-polished slabs on descent)
Map/GPS for the unmarked cross-country approach into Summit Chief Valley
proTips: The approach leaves the maintained Middle Fork Trail at a steel bridge — mark this landmark for the return.
Camp at the ~4,650' gravel wash to shorten summit day.
The lower ~700 ft of 4th/low-5th-class terrain can be simul-climbed to save time.
watchOut: Route-finding on the cross-country approach into Summit Chief Valley is nontrivial — it's seldom visited and unmarked.
Glacier-polished slabs on the descent are slick, especially early season or when wet.
This is a remote, committing outing with slow rescue access — go with a strong, self-sufficient party.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the approach talus and lower pitches.
Exposed, unprotected final ridge pitch to the summit.
Potential residual snow/ice on the pocket glacier and polished descent slabs into midsummer.

pitchDetail.notes: ~700 ft up a left-trending gully system; can be simul-climbed
crux headwall
easy, exposed ridge traverse to the 7,225' summit

road.name: Forest Service Road 56 (Middle Fork Road)
road.status: Paved to the Middle Fork Trailhead; the corridor has a history of washouts and slumping in unstable riverside sections and is subject to periodic repair closures
road.driveNote: From North Bend, take I-90 exit 34, go north on 468th Ave SE, then right onto SE Middle Fork Road (FR 56) for about 11-12 miles to the Middle Fork Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascade climate; best conditions come once seasonal snowpack has melted off the approach gully and descent slabs.
climate.spring: Heavy residual snow likely on the approach gully and pocket glacier — not the rock-climbing season.
climate.summer: July-September is the primary climbing window, once snow has cleared.
climate.fall: Possible into early fall but with shortening days and increasing storm frequency.
climate.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; deep snow and avalanche hazard on the approach.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Skykomish Ranger District), within Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful Pass) required to park at the Middle Fork Snoqualmie Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for entry into Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.passRequired: NW Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: FR 56 (Middle Fork Road) has experienced periodic washout-related closures and repairs in unstable riverside sections — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving in.

emergency.county: King County (summit is on the King/Kittitas county line; this approach is from the King County side)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 206-296-3311; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, Snoqualmie, WA (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Search and rescue is coordinated by the King County Sheriff's Office with King County Search & Rescue Association volunteer teams.

routes.corrections: Given FA date of 'August 2001' conflicts with the American Alpine Club's published route account (American Alpine Journal, via publications.americanalpineclub.org), which documents the first ascent by Jeff Hansell and Martin Volken on September 10, 2001. Corrected fa field to reflect the AAC-published date. Route is also formally known as the Falcon Route on Mountain Project; 'Northwest Face' and 'Falcon Route' refer to the same climb (confirmed via a CascadeClimbers.com trip report titled 'Little Big Chief - Northwest Face / Falcon Route').

routes.rockGrade: 3rd-4th class (a low-5th-class crux on the alternate northeast-basin approach)
routes.rappels: No fixed rappel line reported; short roped sections are typically reversed via downclimb or a rappel from tree/sling anchors
objHaz: Variable snowfield conditions (soft, icy, or moat-riddled)
Exposed low-5th crux move on the alternate basin approach
routes.face: Northeast Ridge / East Face
routes.overview: The standard, non-technical line up Little Big Chief: heather, talus, and snowfield travel up the northeast ridge to a cirque below the east face, finished by two short belayed pitches of 3rd-4th class (occasionally low 5th) rock to the summit.
routes.beta: Approached via the PCT to Dutch Miller Gap, the route climbs about 500 feet of heather and rock steps up the northeast ridge to an open bench, then crosses a snowfield into a cirque below the east face. Two short (roughly 60-foot) belayed pitches of 3rd-4th class rock lead to the summit area; at the top of the first pitch, move climber's left to find the direct finishing pitch. Reported variations include the north ridge from the upper snowfield, or four pitches of roped class-4 slabs taken directly. A separate approach from Pete Lake/Summit Chief camp reaches Little Big Chief's northeast basin and a ridge notch guarded by a low-5th-class crux (a large vertical crack) that most parties rope up for.
routes.approach: Two documented approaches exist. (1) Via the PCT to Dutch Miller Gap (~5,000'), where the route start is described as obvious; climb heather and rock steps onto the northeast ridge. (2) Via the Pete Lake Trailhead: hike the Waptus Pass Trail system past Vista Lakes to a camp near Summit Chief Lake/Summit Chief Mountain (~5,700'), then traverse north into Little Big Chief's northeast basin to reach the ridge notch (this basin approach features a low-5th-class crux at a vertical crack, often protected with a rope).
routes.descent: Reverse the route: downclimb or rappel the short roped pitches back into the east-face cirque, then descend the snowfield and northeast-ridge heather/rock steps back toward Dutch Miller Gap (or, via the alternate approach, back into the northeast basin toward Summit Chief camp).
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the two short belayed pitches — using tree or sling anchors for any rappels — back down into the cirque below the east face. Cross the snowfield and descend the roughly 500 feet of heather and rock steps on the northeast ridge back toward Dutch Miller Gap. Parties who approached via Pete Lake/Summit Chief instead downclimb the ridge-notch crux (often on a rope) and drop into the northeast basin to regain camp.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as part of a 2-3 day trip, often combined with a neighboring peak (Summit Chief or Overcoat) from a backcountry camp near Dutch Miller Gap or Summit Chief Lake: Day 1 approach and camp, Day 2 climb Little Big Chief (and optionally an adjacent peak), Day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: The roped sections are short (each roughly 60 ft) and can be downclimbed or rappelled from tree/sling anchors; below that the route is walk-off heather and snow, so retreat is straightforward except during active snowfall or whiteout on the upper snowfield.
routes.turnaround: Since much of this route crosses snowfields and heather slopes with limited protection, set a turnaround time that accounts for slower travel in poor visibility or soft/refrozen snow, plus the long approach distance back to the trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly June through September; a mid-June ascent has been documented (party carried a rope for the low-5th crux and moat crossings), while July-September is generally recommended once snowfields have consolidated and seasonal hazards (loose rock, wasps in the basin) are reduced.
routes.detailedRack: A single rope, helmets, and slings for tree anchors or possible rappels are the standard kit; no dedicated cam/nut rack is typically reported as necessary for the short roped sections, though a few slings for natural protection are worth carrying.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal formal protection needed — the two roped pitches are typically belayed off natural features (trees, horns) rather than placed gear; carry slings/cord for anchors.
gear: Slings/cord for tree or natural anchors
whatToBring: Ice axe and possibly crampons for snowfield travel
Sturdy boots for talus and heather
Navigation tools (GPS/map) — route-finding through the basin and ridge is fuzzy in guidebook descriptions
Insect repellent (wasps have been reported in the approach basin)
proTips: At the top of the first belayed pitch, move climber's left to find the direct finishing pitch to the summit.
The Pete Lake approach lets you combine Little Big Chief with Summit Chief Mountain in one trip.
Carry a rope even though most of the route is scrambling — the ridge-notch crux and any moats are more comfortably protected with one.
watchOut: Snowfield conditions vary widely by season and year and can require an ice axe or crampons.
A wasp nest has been reported along the Pete Lake approach basin.
Route-finding in the basin below the east face is fuzzy in published descriptions — GPS tracks are recommended.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the heather/rock steps and ridge.
Variable snowfield conditions (soft, icy, or moat-riddled) depending on season.
Exposed low-5th-class crux move on the alternate northeast-basin approach.

pitchDetail.notes: ~500 ft of heather and rock steps up the NE ridge, then a snowfield to the east-face cirque
~60 ft, belayed; move climber's left at the top for the direct finish
direct pitch to the summit

road.name: Salmon La Sac Road / FR 46 / FR 4616 / FR 113 (Pete Lake approach) or FR 56 Middle Fork Road (PCT/Dutch Miller Gap approach)
road.status: Both are maintained Forest Service roads to their respective trailheads; sections can be subject to seasonal or washout-related closures
road.driveNote: Pete Lake approach: from Cle Elum take Hwy 903 north ~7.6 mi, continue on Salmon La Sac Rd ~9.2 mi, left on FR 46 for 4.7 mi, right on FR 4616 for ~0.5 mi, then FR 113 about 1 mi to the trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced Cascade climate on the ridge crest with a drier east-slope influence; snowfields on this route can persist well into summer.
climate.spring: Full snow cover typical on the ridge and cirque; a mountaineering/snow-climbing objective rather than a rock scramble.
climate.summer: June through September is the main window, with snowfield conditions easing as the season progresses.
climate.fall: Feasible into early fall but with increasing chance of new snow at this elevation.
climate.winter: Not a recommended winter objective given avalanche-prone approach terrain.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (west/Middle Fork-Dutch Miller Gap side) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (east/Pete Lake side); both within Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful Pass) required at both the Middle Fork and Pete Lake trailheads
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for entry into Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.passRequired: NW Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: Check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts for seasonal road/trail closures before driving in.

emergency.county: King/Kittitas county line (King County via the Middle Fork/Dutch Miller Gap approach; Kittitas County via the Pete Lake/Salmon La Sac approach)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 206-296-3311; Kittitas County Sheriff (via Kittcom): 509-925-8534; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger District (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF) or Cle Elum Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee NF), depending on approach
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, Snoqualmie, WA (west-side access) or Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA (east-side/Pete Lake access) — both 24-hour Emergency Departments
emergency.notes: Search and rescue coordination depends on which county the incident falls in; King County SAR (King County Sheriff's Office) covers the west side, Kittitas County Sheriff's Office the east side.

routes.corrections: The given data listed this route's first-ascent party as 'unknown'; research confirms no documented first-ascent party/date exists for this line specifically (distinct from the peak's overall 1939 first ascent by Fred Beckey, Wayne Swift, Joe Barto, and Campbell Brooks, which established the peak but not necessarily this exact NE Face/ridge line) — fa left null rather than fabricated.

blurb: Prusik Peak is an 8,008-ft granite spire at the west end of the Enchantment Lakes basin in the Stuart Range, part of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Leavenworth, WA — often called the signature summit of the Enchantments for its pyramidal profile above Gnome Tarn and Perfection Lake. Fred Beckey and Art Holben made the peak's first ascent in May 1948 (the mountain's name honors the Prusik knot used on that climb), and Beckey returned through the 1950s-60s to help establish several of its classic lines. The rock is unusually solid, clean granite, and the peak hosts some of the Cascades' best moderate-to-hard alpine rock climbing, from the easy Grade II West Ridge to sustained 5.11+ testpieces on the South Face.

routes.overview: A classic, moderate alpine rock ridge and one of the best easy multi-pitch climbs in the Cascades — mostly low-5th-class blocky terrain leading to a short, exposed 5.7 slab crux near the top.
routes.beta: From Prusik Pass, scramble to a large balanced rock, then climb broken, steepening ground left (north) of the ridge crest to a roughly half-rope-length 5.6 dihedral. Continue on ledges and easier cracks to the ridge crest, traverse with exposure to the base of a small slab (the 5.7 crux, protected by an old fixed pin), then finish via either a left-side offwidth (5.7) or a right-side layback crack to the summit area.
routes.fa: FA 1957 — Fred Beckey (partner not confirmed in available sources).
routes.rappels: 4 single-rope rappels down the north side of the ridge
objHaz: old/marginal fixed pin at the crux
routes.face: West Ridge
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (end of FR 7601 off Icicle Creek Road) hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail about 4 mi to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft). Continue past the lake's east side and climb the steep, unmaintained boulder/talus Aasgard Pass (~1.1 mi, ~2,200 ft gain) to the Upper Enchantment Basin (~7,800 ft). Traverse northwest through the basin past Isolation, Inspiration, and Perfection Lakes to Prusik Pass on the peak's west side, the start of the route (roughly a 5-6 hour approach from the trailhead). The longer, more gradual Snow Lakes Trail (~10 mi, 5,000+ ft gain) approaches from the north as an alternative.
routes.descent: 4 single-rope rappels off the north side, then walk back over Prusik Pass.
routes.descentText: From near the summit, make four single-rope rappels down the peak's north side using slung anchors, then walk/scramble back over Prusik Pass to regain the approach trail through the Enchantment Basin.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Stuart Lake Trailhead, hike via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass to camp in your permitted zone. Day 2: Climb the West Ridge round-trip from camp/Prusik Pass. Day 3: Hike out via Aasgard Pass and Colchuck Lake.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on the lower pitches by downclimbing/walking off before committing to the exposed ridge-crest traverse; above that, rappel the line of ascent using natural gear/slings to the north side.
routes.turnaround: Start early enough to be off the summit and through the rappels well before afternoon thunderstorms typically build over the Stuart Range; don't commit to the exposed crux traverse if weather is deteriorating.
routes.bestSeason: Best from July through September once Aasgard Pass and the approach trail are snow-free; May-June trips are feasible but expect lingering snow on the pass, and October offers cool, often stable rock weather with a higher chance of early storms.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin or on the peak; carry a satellite communicator for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of nuts and cams up to about 3 inches is sufficient; there are no bolts on the route. Slings serve as rappel anchors, and a 0.75-size cam is noted as useful protecting the crux slab.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack of nuts and small-to-medium cams; the route is mostly easy terrain with only the crux slab needing careful natural protection near an old fixed pin.
proTips: Most of the climbing is 5.5 or easier — many parties simul-climb or move together on the lower pitches, saving rope-work for the exposed traverse and the P3 slab crux.
The route is popular; start early to beat other parties to the crux pitch and the rappels.
watchOut: The crux slab is protected by a single old, possibly marginal fixed pin — back it up with natural gear where you can.
The ridge-crest traverse before the crux has significant exposure on both sides.
knownHazards: Loose rock is present on the lower, blocky sections of the ridge.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Cascades in summer and can build quickly.
The approach over Aasgard Pass is steep, loose talus that is more hazardous when wet or snow-covered.

pitchDetail.notes: Crack through blocky terrain up to the West Ridge crest, ending where the ridge flattens.
Follow a trail along the ridge's north side, then a short 15-ft move gains the south side with significant exposure leading to the crux.
The crux friction slab, protected by an old fixed pin; traverse exposed terrain, then drop to the north side to set up the final pitch.
Two finish options: left-side offwidth jamming or a right-side layback crack.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Road 7601 (Stuart Lake/Colchuck Trailhead)
road.status: Open in summer season (verify current conditions before driving)
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 is gated and closed to vehicles roughly November through May each winter.
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road ~8.4 mi, then turn onto FR 7601 and follow the rough, sometimes steep dirt road ~3.7 mi to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; high-clearance recommended late season.

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades / Stuart Range (see mountain-forecast.com Prusik Peak point forecast)
climate.typical: A high alpine granite peak at 8,008 ft with a short, dry climbing season, cold nights, and rapid weather changes typical of the Cascade crest.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the approach trail, Aasgard Pass, and shaded gullies well into June.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); warm days, cold nights, and frequent afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Cooler, often stable weather into early October, with increasing overnight freezes.
climate.winter: Deep snow and avalanche terrain on approaches, with the access road closed, make the peak essentially inaccessible to rock climbers.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River (Leavenworth) Ranger District; Alpine Lakes Wilderness / Enchantment Permit Area.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/year) required for trailhead parking; Enchantment Permit Area overnight permits carry a lottery/reservation fee via Recreation.gov.
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit for day use. Overnight camping in the Enchantment Permit Area requires an advance permit May 15-Oct 31, awarded through the Feb 15-Mar 1 lottery on Recreation.gov (limited daily walk-up permits also available).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass at the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Trailhead.
access.closures: FR 7601 is closed to vehicles roughly November-May each winter; occasional emergency closures of Enchantments access have occurred (e.g., search operations) — check current Forest Service alerts.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from camp to summit and begin rappels before afternoon storms build.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (RiverCom 911 dispatches Chelan County Sheriff Search & Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River (Leavenworth) Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-5815 (24/7 ED; serious trauma transferred to Wenatchee, Seattle, or Spokane)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin or on the peak; carry a satellite communicator. Chelan County SAR responds to dozens of Enchantments/Colchuck incidents each year.

whatToBring: bear-resistant food storage (Ursack acceptable) for camp

routes.overview: A striking, sustained 5.11a line up clean golden granite on the west side of Prusik's south face, widely regarded as one of the Cascades' best moderate-hard alpine rock pitches.
routes.beta: Starts just right of the outside corner on the left flank of the south face and climbs a right-leaning crack system to a stembox, a roof/hand-crack pitch, slabby/off-width terrain past a chimney (loose rock caution), and a final splitter corner crack, topping out on the West Ridge rather than the true summit.
routes.fa: FA 1989 — Wayne Wallace and Tim Olson.
routes.rappels: Single 60m rappel down the north side from the top of the route
objHaz: documented loose/rockfall-prone block near P4 (already dislodged by a past ascent)
rotten rock in the P3-P4 chimney exit
routes.face: South Face - west side, left flank
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead hike via Colchuck Lake (~4 mi) and up Aasgard Pass (~1.1 mi, ~2,200 ft) into the Upper Enchantment Basin, then traverse toward Gnome Tarn below Prusik's south face (about 20 minutes from the tarn to the base of the wall). Start just right of the outside corner on the left flank of the face.
routes.descent: Single 60m rappel down the north side, then a short scramble to the base.
routes.descentText: Most parties make one 60m rappel down the north side from the top of the route (which tops out on the West Ridge, not the true summit), then scramble roughly 30 minutes down to the base of the face. Parties wanting the true summit can continue up the remaining West Ridge terrain and descend as for that route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass to camp near Gnome Tarn/Enchantment Basin (per permit zone). Day 2: Climb Solid Gold and rappel back to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat by rappelling the line of ascent from any established belay; natural gear anchors are available at most stances.
routes.turnaround: Begin early enough to finish the route and rappels before afternoon thunderstorms typical of the Cascade summer; the loose block near P4 makes retreat in a hurry riskier — move deliberately.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most consistent dry rock; snow can linger into June on the approach and shaded start of the face.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin or on the face; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: A double set of finger-sized cams up through #2, plus one #3 Camalot, covers the route's crack and corner systems.
routes.proNeeds: Doubles in the smaller-to-mid cam sizes protect most pitches well; natural pro is generally good on this line.
gear: double rack of cams, finger size to #2
proTips: The route tops out on the West Ridge, not the true summit — continue up the ridge if you want to stand on top, or rappel straight from the top of the route.
Test suspect rock carefully near the P4 chimney exit; a large block has already come loose there on a prior ascent.
watchOut: A microwave-oven-sized block near the P4 chimney base has already been dislodged by a past party — handle this section carefully and warn anyone below.
The chimney exit on P3-P4 has notably rotten rock.
knownHazards: Documented rockfall/loose block hazard near the top of P4.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in Cascades summer.
Remote setting with a multi-hour approach complicates any rescue.

pitchDetail.notes: Crack to a stembox with a crux move; sustained corner past a large block to a roof belay (~100 ft).
Hard move under the roof into a hand crack; belay on a horizontal gear placement (~100 ft).
Off-width pinching to a mantle, part of a combined ~185-ft section with P4.
Arching seam through a chimney wall; caution for rotten rock at the exit.
Perfect corner crack; option to exit left at a tree or continue to the ridge top (~150 ft).

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Road 7601 (Stuart Lake/Colchuck Trailhead)
road.status: Open in summer season (verify current conditions before driving)
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 is gated and closed to vehicles roughly November through May each winter.
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road ~8.4 mi, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi (rough dirt) to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; high-clearance recommended late season.

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades / Stuart Range (see mountain-forecast.com Prusik Peak point forecast)
climate.typical: High alpine granite face with a short, dry climbing season, cold nights, and rapid weather changes.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the approach and shaded lower face well into June.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); warm days, cold nights, and frequent afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Cooler, often stable weather into early October.
climate.winter: Deep snow, avalanche terrain, and a closed access road make the route inaccessible.
