whatToBring: double rack to #2 plus one #3 cam
bear-resistant food storage (Ursack acceptable) for camp

routes.overview: The original, moderate line up Prusik's 600-ft south face — sustained, varied climbing on superb rock through a chimney, a chockstone tunnel, and a squeeze-chimney crux to the true summit; also known as the South Face - West Side (Beckey's CAG) or the Chockstone Route.
routes.beta: Start near the middle of the south face base. Climb a wide crack/chimney (5.6-5.8), exit right on knobs into a deep gully, continue up the gully to a chockstone, tunnel behind it through steep double cracks, then finish through a flaring squeeze chimney (crux, 5.9+) and a left-facing corner to the summit.
routes.fa: FA 1968 — Ron Burgner and Fred Stanley (South Face); inspired by Fred Beckey's 1962 ascent of the South Face (Beckey-Davis route) nearby.
routes.rappels: 5 rappels off slung belay stations on the north face (about 1 hour if no other parties present)
objHaz: squeeze-chimney crux is hard to protect
slung-block rappel anchors that don't always reach cleanly between stations
routes.face: South Face (central line)
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead hike via Colchuck Lake (~4 mi) and up Aasgard Pass (~1.1 mi, ~2,200 ft) into the Upper Enchantment Basin, then traverse toward Gnome Tarn below Prusik's south face. Start at approximately the middle of the base of the south face.
routes.descent: Walk east from the summit, then rappel the north face on slung anchors.
routes.descentText: From the summit, walk/scramble eastward, then make about 5 rappels down the north face using slung belay stations, with either a single or double rope. Route-finding between anchors takes care, as some slung stations don't reach cleanly to the next.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to camp near Gnome Tarn/Enchantment Basin. Day 2: Climb the South Face and descend the north-face rappels back to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Rappel the north-face descent line (slung stations) rather than the route itself if retreat is needed above the lower pitches; carry extra slings.
routes.turnaround: Start early to be off the summit and through the rappels before afternoon thunderstorms; the squeeze-chimney crux and chockstone tunnel are slow terrain to retreat through.
routes.bestSeason: July through September for the most consistent dry conditions; snow can linger into June on the approach.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin or on the face; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles to #3 Camalot, additional pro to 4 inches, and TCUs cover the crack and chimney systems; the route has little fixed gear.
routes.proNeeds: A fairly full rack with doubles through #3 and small TCUs for the flaring squeeze-chimney crux.
proTips: Pitches 1-2 and 3-4 link cleanly into roughly 180-ft pitches for parties who want to move fast.
Bring extra slings — several rappel anchors on the descent are slung blocks that need re-rigging.
watchOut: Multiple slung-block rappel anchors on the north-face descent don't always reach the next station cleanly.
The squeeze chimney crux is awkward to protect.
knownHazards: Loose rock present on some ledges and the gully sections.
Afternoon thunderstorms common in Cascades summer.
Remote location complicates any rescue.

pitchDetail.notes: Wide crack/chimney start (~180 ft); a #4 cam works at the crux, scramble to the tallest tree.
Crack exiting right on knobs to a deep gully (~100 ft).
Gully climbing to a belay below a chockstone (~180 ft).
Steep double cracks; tunnel behind the chockstone (~60 ft).
Cracks into a flaring squeeze chimney — crux for many climbers (~80 ft).
Left-facing corner to the summit (~180 ft).

whatToBring: 60m rope (or two ropes for full-length raps)
bear-resistant food storage (Ursack acceptable) for camp

routes.overview: A harder, more sustained testpiece 50 ft left of the Stanley-Burgner start, following steep splitter cracks and technical stemming through six pitches of sustained 5.10-5.11+ climbing to the summit.
routes.beta: Climb left-slanting cracks to a tipsy crux entering a 'stembox,' then a mental-crux knobby corner past a fixed pin, an undercling flake pitch ('the money pitch'), a committing crack-switch corner, a short corner-to-slab pitch, and finish with a steep, flaring-hands splitter and a chimney (loose block) to the summit.
routes.fa: FA c. early-mid 1990s — Brooke Sandahl, established over several seasons with visiting partners.
routes.rappels: 5 single-rope rappels down the north side (60m rope)
objHaz: known loose block in the P6 chimney/mantel finish
runout/PG13 sections relying partly on fixed bolts and a fixed pin
routes.face: South Face - just left (west) of the Stanley-Burgner start
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead hike via Colchuck Lake (~4 mi) and up Aasgard Pass (~1.1 mi, ~2,200 ft) into the Upper Enchantment Basin to a base camp near Gnome Tarn (about 20 minutes from the tarn to the base of Prusik's south face). The start is about 50 ft left of the standard South Face (Stanley-Burgner) route — look for the distinctive stembox feature.
routes.descent: 5 single-rope rappels down the north side.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make 5 single-rope rappels down the north side of the peak using a 60m rope, regaining the base of the face and the approach trail back through the Enchantment Basin.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to camp near Gnome Tarn/Enchantment Basin. Day 2: Climb Der Sportsman and rappel back to camp. Day 3: Hike out.
routes.bail: Rappel the line of ascent from fixed/bolted anchors on the lower pitches; retreat above the P3 ledge is more involved as some anchors are gear-built.
routes.turnaround: Start early to finish all 6 pitches and the descent before afternoon thunderstorms build; the sustained grade and PG13 sections make this a longer day than the other Prusik routes.
routes.bestSeason: July through September for the most consistent dry conditions; snow can linger into June on the approach.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantment Basin or on the face; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.detailedRack: RPs and extra draws for the bolt/fixed-pin protected sections, doubles in small cams through #2, one to two #3 Camalots, and an optional #4 for the P6 splitter/chimney finish.
routes.proNeeds: A technical rack weighted toward small gear (RPs, small cams) plus extra draws, since some pitches rely partly on fixed bolts and a fixed pin rather than continuous natural gear.
proTips: Bring extra draws — pitches 2 and 5 rely partly on fixed bolts and a fixed pin rather than natural gear.
P3 ('the money pitch') is widely considered the best pitch on the route — savor the undercling flake/crack climbing.
watchOut: The P6 chimney/mantel finish has a known loose block — climb it deliberately and avoid shock-loading it.
P2 is described as 'never dangerous, but definitely heady' due to exposure.
knownHazards: Documented loose block near the P6 chimney top-out.
Afternoon thunderstorms common in Cascades summer.
Remote location and long multi-pitch day complicate any rescue.

pitchDetail.notes: Left-slanting crack system to a tipsy crux guarding the stembox, then lieback/stem twin cracks to a two-bolt anchor (~50m).
Corner climbing, cut right on knobs past a fixed pin, stem further right to the base of an offset tips crack.
'The money pitch' — undercling flake/crack system to a broad ledge with a gear anchor (~35m).
Corner crack with a committing crack-switch right; traverse to a pillar with noticeable exposure (~30m).
Short corner crux moving right to a slab; two belay bolts, continue past a bulge to a gear anchor (~30m).
Steep, thuggish, flaring thin-hands splitter into a chockstone mantel and chimney (loose block) to the top-out (~30m).

whatToBring: technical rack with RPs and extra draws
bear-resistant food storage (Ursack acceptable) for camp
routes.corrections: Given data listed pitches as 5; both Mountain Project and StephAbegg.com's detailed trip report independently document 6 pitches (matching a ~600 ft route length), so pitches was corrected to 6.

blurb: Old Guard Peak (8,260 ft) is a granite/gneiss summit in the North Cascades of Chelan County, Washington, rising alongside its near-twin Sentinel Peak from the Le Conte Glacier within the Glacier Peak Wilderness. It lies along the classic multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak and is grouped by regional peak-baggers among the "Ptarmigan Pten." No first-ascent record for the peak or its routes was found in any checked source; it is most often climbed as a side-trip from a traverse camp via a glacier snow ramp on its northwest side, finishing with a short class 2-3 rock scramble to the summit.

routes.overview: A class 2-3 snow-and-rock scramble up Old Guard Peak's northwest side, gained by roped travel across the Le Conte Glacier from the Le Conte-Sentinel col to a snow ramp, finishing with a short scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: Old Guard is usually tagged as a side-trip from a Ptarmigan Traverse camp near the Le Conte-Sentinel col (roughly 7,300-7,400 ft). From the col, rope up and cross the Le Conte Glacier, skirting crevasses and a bergschrund, to reach a snow ramp on the peak's northwest side. Above the ramp the route eases into a straightforward but somewhat loose, indistinct class 2-3 rock scramble to the summit. Most parties climb neighboring Sentinel Peak from the same camp in the same outing.
objHaz: bergschrund crossing near the Old Guard-Sentinel col
loose/indistinct rock on the summit scramble
afternoon sun-softened ('sugar') snow on the glacier in late season
whiteout/poor visibility at the surrounding passes
routes.face: Northwest side, above the Le Conte Glacier
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from SR-20 at Marblemount (about 23 miles, paved then gravel, narrow and steep near the end) to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,600 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail (3.7 mi, ~1,700 ft gain) to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft), then continue on the Ptarmigan Traverse route via Cache Col, Kool-Aid Lake, and the Yang Yang Lakes area, descending to and crossing the Le Conte Glacier system to the Le Conte-Sentinel col. This is typically day 2-3 of the multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse rather than a standalone approach; a documented alternate approach climbs from Stehekin up the Flat Creek drainage to Le Conte Lake and the glacier's east shoulder.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back across the Le Conte Glacier to the Le Conte-Sentinel col.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 2-3 scramble terrain back to the top of the northwest snow ramp, then descend the snow/glacier back across the Le Conte Glacier to the Le Conte-Sentinel col, staying roped for crevasse hazard. No rappels are reported on the standard route; carry a rope, pickets/screws, and crevasse-rescue gear for the glacier crossing in both directions.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed within a 5-7 day Ptarmigan Traverse: Day 1 Cascade Pass Trailhead to a camp near Cache Col or Kool-Aid Lake; Day 2-3 continue via Yang Yang Lakes and the Le Conte Glacier to a camp at or near the Le Conte-Sentinel col; from that camp, climb Old Guard Peak (often paired with Sentinel Peak) as a several-hour round trip before continuing the traverse south toward Spire Point and Dome Peak.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this route: downclimb to the snow ramp and re-cross the glacier to the col. There is no fixed protection or established rappel line, so any bail must be done by reversing the ascent line while conditions (visibility, open crevasses, snow bridges) allow.
routes.turnaround: Because the route requires a roped glacier crossing exposed to afternoon sun on the Le Conte Glacier, start early enough to complete the glacier crossing while the snow is still firm, and set a turnaround that gets you back across the glacier before it softens into wet, crevasse-obscuring snow later in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed almost exclusively July-September once the Ptarmigan Traverse approach is snow-free enough for travel; a documented late-June ascent (a June 2025 survey trip) is possible in a low-snow year with firmer, more continuous snow on the glacier and passes. Late-season (August-September) visits face more open crevasses and thinner snow bridges on the Le Conte Glacier.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: ice axe, crampons, a rope per team, pickets or ice screws, and a full crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks, pulley, mechanical ascenders), plus a helmet for the scramble above the col. No technical rock rack is used on this class 2-3 route.
routes.proNeeds: None beyond glacier-travel protection - pickets/screws for crevasse rescue and a belay on the snow ramp if desired; the summit scramble itself is unroped class 2-3.
whatToBring: North Cascades NP backcountry permit if camping within the park boundary
proTips: Cross the Le Conte Glacier early in the day while the surface is still firm; multiple trip reports describe it turning to granulated 'sugar' snow over hard ice by afternoon in late summer.
Most parties combine Old Guard with neighboring Sentinel Peak (its near-twin in elevation) from the same Le Conte-Sentinel col camp.
At least one party rated the climb 'underrated' relative to its class rating, finding more sustained attention required on the rock scramble than expected.
watchOut: Crevasses on the Le Conte Glacier, including a bergschrund near the Old Guard-Sentinel col, require careful roped travel and route-finding.
Rock on the summit scramble is loose and indistinct in places.
Whiteout conditions and cornices have been reported at the surrounding passes on the Ptarmigan Traverse.
knownHazards: Glacier travel with real crevasse and bergschrund hazard between the col and the base of the peak.
Loose rock on the class 2-3 scramble above the snow ramp.
Afternoon sun softens the glacier surface, increasing crevasse risk and slowing travel later in the day.
Black bears have been reported in the area (one party saw a bear cross the glacier near their camp).

pitchDetail.notes: Roped crossing of the Le Conte Glacier from the Le Conte-Sentinel col, skirting crevasses and a bergschrund to reach the base of Old Guard's northwest snow ramp.
Snow couloir/ramp gains a short, somewhat loose rock scramble (class 2-3) to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for roughly the first third, then gravel, narrow and steep near the end; a high-clearance vehicle is recommended for the final miles to the Cascade Pass Trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Gated for winter near milepost 20 (Eldorado area); typically reopens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June or July depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: About 23 miles / roughly 1 hour from SR-20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,600 ft)

climate.typical: Alpine North Cascades climate: cool, wetter shoulder seasons and a short, drier climbing window in July-September; the Le Conte Glacier and surrounding passes hold snow into midsummer.
climate.spring: Still largely snow-covered; Cascade River Road may not yet be open to the trailhead.
climate.summer: The main climbing season; expect firm morning snow on the glacier softening by afternoon.
climate.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency; glacier crevasses more exposed as seasonal snow thins.
climate.winter: Road closed and deep snowpack make this a non-standard winter objective; avalanche and glacier hazard are significant.

access.landManager: Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (approached through North Cascades National Park Complex from the Cascade Pass Trailhead)
access.fees: No climbing fee; a Northwest Forest Pass is required to park at the Cascade Pass Trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit for the Glacier Peak Wilderness portion of the route; a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit (via recreation.gov or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount) is required for any overnight camping within the park boundary
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Cascade River Road is closed in winter and typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June or July

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from the Le Conte-Sentinel col camp, to cross the Le Conte Glacier while the snow surface is still firm

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); the Cascade Pass approach trailhead is in Skagit County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (Chelan/Douglas County dispatch): 911 or (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245
emergency.notes: Remote, glaciated terrain; NPS/SAR operations in this area have used helicopter short-haul rescue, typically staging through Marblemount before hospital transport.

routes.corrections: No source used the name 'Southwest Route'; all sources (SummitPost, Peakbagger, trip reports) describe the standard route ascending the peak's west/northwest side via a Le Conte Glacier snow ramp to the Old Guard-Sentinel col, then a class 2-3 scramble - treated here as the route matching the given routeId. Given grade (Class 3) and alpineGrade (PD) are consistent with sourced descriptions and were not changed. Given elevation (8,260 ft) is consistent with cross-checked sources (Listsofjohn 8,259 ft; a June 2025 professional survey measuring 8,259.6 ft NAVD88; other sources 8,259-8,261 ft) - not corrected.

blurb: M&M Wall — better known as Supercave Wall — is a steep, south-facing granite wall rising above the North Cascades Highway (SR-20) about 2 miles northeast of Liberty Bell, roughly 3 miles east of Washington Pass. It is one of the largest and cleanest roadside crags in Washington, known for bulletproof granite and a mid-height system of huge caves (the largest "Supercave" is roughly the size of a tennis court). Long ignored after a single 1969 aid ascent, the wall saw almost no further traffic for 40 years until Erik Lawson and Lee Arden Pete established Ellen Pea (2009), followed by a wave of hard free climbing from Blake Herrington and partners (The Tiger, 2014; free variation of the L&H Route, 2015) and Colin Moorhead/Tom Wright (Golden Age, 2016).
hierarchyNote: Peak name/coordinates check out: this is a rock wall (not a summit) at 48.54793, -120.63975, on the North Cascades Highway corridor 2 miles NE of Liberty Bell and ~3 miles east of Washington Pass, consistent across Mountain Project, Steph Abegg, and Blake Herrington's trip reports. Note it is a crag/wall feature rather than a discrete summit, so peak-style databases (Peakbagger, listsofjohn) have no listing for it — that is expected, not an error. No mismatch found otherwise.

routes.overview: An 11-pitch free line up the center of M&M Wall, widely considered one of the best free routes at Washington Pass, rivaling the Thin Red Line in quality and difficulty; climbs through a cave feature ("Tiger Belly's Lair") and a striking flake ("Tiger Flake") en route to the ridge.
routes.beta: Technical, slabby corners low on the route give way to steep, physical, and strenuous climbing higher up. All but one of the 11 pitches is rated 5.10 or harder, with a bouldery 5.12- crux (underclings under a roof) at pitch 7. Well-placed bolts protect the hardest moves, but some 5.11- sections require finicky natural gear.
routes.approach: Park at milepost 166 on Highway 20 (a few miles east of Washington Pass / the hairpin). Hike an easy third/fourth-class talus gully directly uphill (north) from the highway toward the base of the Supercave gully; a couple of exposed 5.0 moves and a fixed line assist the steeper sections. Approach takes about 40 minutes to 1 hour to the base ledge system.
routes.descent: Double-rope (70m) rappels back down the route.
routes.descentText: Descend via double-rope rappels down the line of The Tiger using 70m ropes; alternatively, traverse across to the Ellen Pea route line for a rappel descent, or walk off via the 3rd-class ledge system to the west/summit ridge.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible via rappel from most belay stations back down the route line with double ropes; the cave (Tiger Belly's Lair) around pitch 5 offers a natural, sheltered bail point.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in dry, settled conditions from mid-summer into September; the approach gully becomes hazardous (loose, wet rock) in bad weather, so this is explicitly "not a climb to do with iffy weather." Snow can linger in the gully earlier in the season; water is available near the base in early September.
watchOut: The approach gully is exposed to rockfall and becomes dangerous when wet — avoid climbing in or after rain.
Several 5.11- moves rely on finicky natural gear rather than bolts.
A fixed line assists a steep section of the approach gully but should not be blindly trusted; inspect it before weighting.
knownHazards: Loose talus and an exposed 5.0 section on the approach gully.
Rockfall potential in the gully, especially with other parties above or after rain.
South-facing aspect means full-day sun exposure and heat in summer.
proTips: Bring extra 5-inch and 6-inch pieces for pitch 10's giant flake ("Tiger Flake").
Offset RPs are useful for several of the thin, technical sections.
A direct 5.12+ variation exists on pitches 6-7 climbing straight out of the cave for parties seeking harder climbing.
whatToBring: Fixed-line-rated gloves or a sling for the gully handline
Extra water — south-facing wall gets full sun
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from tips/small cams up to 2 inches, plus a single set from 3 to 6 inches (an extra 5" and 6" piece is useful for the pitch 10 flake). Bring small wires with offset RPs for thin technical placements. Well-placed bolts protect the crux moves at 5.11c and harder, but several 5.11- sections require careful natural gear.
routes.proNeeds: A mix of bolted protection on the hardest, most sustained pitches and natural gear (thin cams/wires) on easier but less-protected terrain; some finicky, run-out placements should be expected on lower-grade pitches.
routes.rappels: Double-rope (70m) rappels down the route, or traverse to Ellen Pea for descent
routes.fa: Blake Herrington, Colin Moorhead, and Max Tepfer (Summer 2014)
routes.face: South face, center of M&M Wall

30m, left-facing corner, face moves, arete
35m, through the large cave ("Belly's Lair"), sustained technical climbing
15m, steep with positive holds; 5.12c "Eye of the Tiger" direct variation available
15m, crux: underclings under a roof with exposure
25m, layback corner, leftward hand traverse
30m, cracks, flakes, balancy twin thin cracks
30m, the giant "Tiger Flake" — sustained undercling/jamming
30m, broken cracks and blocky features to a final bulge on the ridge

routes.overview: The free version of the 1969 Langdon-Hargis aid line up M&M Wall, freed in 2015 by Blake Herrington and Max Tepfer; an 8-pitch outing on immaculate granite with a sustained 5.12 crux pitch widely regarded as one of the best pitches of 5.12 on granite anywhere in the state.
routes.beta: Shares its start with The Tiger before splitting into its own line. Quality 5.10 climbing (some sections still lichen-flecked) is interspersed with harder cruxes on immaculate rock; grades are described by the first free ascensionists as "theoretical" given the route has seen very few repeats. The original 1969 aid pitches used roughly 1.5 billion pitons and 1 bolt; the free ascent added 3 protection bolts (with FA-party Jim Langdon's permission) on sections that had been aided via upward-driven knifeblade pitons.
routes.approach: Same approach as The Tiger: park at milepost 166 on Highway 20, hike the talus/gully directly uphill to the treed ledge system at the base of the wall (roughly 40-60 minutes). The route starts from the same ledge as The Tiger — step right over a small tree to reach the first corner system.
routes.descent: Rappel the Ellen Pea route with two ropes, or walk off to the west
routes.descentText: From the top, rappel down the Ellen Pea line using two ropes (diagonal rappelling is required in places), or walk down via the 3rd-class terrain to the west.
routes.bail: The large treed ledge reached after pitch 3 offers a natural bail point with two descent options of varying difficulty back toward the base.
routes.bestSeason: Best in dry summer conditions; pitch 2's crux is noted as temperature-dependent (cooler, cloudier conditions were credited with helping the first free ascent), so early morning or overcast conditions can help on the hardest moves.
watchOut: Pitch 6 has easy but unprotected, run-out terrain below a roof.
Pitch 7's hand traverse is exposed and "not good for a timid second."
Significant rope drag reported on some of the easier variation options.
Pitch 8's finish involves hollow, grainy flakes — test holds before committing.
knownHazards: Old fixed pitons remain from the 1969 aid ascent in places; treat as unreliable.
Cave traversal on pitch 5 exposes climbers to loose rock and cold, damp rock.
Route grades are "theoretical" due to very few repeat ascents — expect route-finding uncertainty.
proTips: Triple small cams (purple C3 through 0.5") plus doubles of 0.75" and 1" cover the technical crux pitches well.
Cool, overcast conditions help the pitch 2 crux, which is temperature-sensitive.
Three fixed protection bolts and three fixed anchor bolts (added with FA-party consent) supplement gear on the hardest sections.
whatToBring: Two ropes for the Ellen Pea rappel descent
Approach shoes for the talus gully
routes.detailedRack: Single rack to #3 (BD size), with triples of the smaller cam sizes (purple C3 through 0.5"), doubles of 0.75" and 1", plus a standard set of stoppers and RPs. Three protection bolts and three anchor bolts were added on the free ascent with the original first-ascensionist's permission.
routes.proNeeds: A gear-intensive free line: small cams triple-racked for sustained thin sections, supplemented by a handful of fixed bolts on the hardest crux moves; several older fixed pitons remain from the original 1969 aid ascent and should not be trusted.
routes.rappels: Two-rope rappel down the Ellen Pea line
routes.fa: Jim Langdon, Meade Hargis, 1969 (FA) Blake Herrington, Max Tepfer, 2015 (FFA)
routes.face: South face, M&M Wall (shares start with The Tiger)

pitchDetail.notes: 210', left-facing corner from the Tiger's start, ending below a roof at a pin/gear anchor

190', traverse left to a crack system leading to a large treed ledge
100', standout pitch — elegant flake climbing and powerful moves on clean edges
150', face climbing with a lieback corner, traverses into the cave system
105', wandering pitch, unprotected/run-out terrain below a roof
130', memorable hand-traverse pitch connecting to the Ellen Pea route
180', finish via hollow, grainy flakes; rappel or scramble off

routes.overview: The wall's original modern free line: a sustained 7-pitch 5.11c up the right side of M&M Wall, intersecting the huge Supercave feature at mid-height — one of the most popular and (relatively) easiest routes at the crag.
routes.beta: A ground-up effort by two Spokane climbers over several days across a few seasons, completed in 2009. Excellent, squeaky-clean granite throughout, with a well-bolted crux pitch of pocket climbing and a steep exit from the cave regarded by many parties as the route's hardest pitch. The large Supercave itself offers a natural mid-route rest/shelter.
routes.approach: Park at milepost 166 on Highway 20 (about 3 miles east of the hairpin). Scramble up the creekbed/talus directly uphill (north) into the supercave gully, then walk in from the left along the lowest treed ledge on the wall. Approach takes roughly 45 minutes; figure about 1 hour to the lower ledge and 1 hour 10 minutes to the upper ledge.
routes.descent: Rappel the route with two ropes
routes.descentText: Descend via rappel using two ropes; expect some diagonal-rappel maneuvering to stay on anchors, or traverse off to a 3rd-class ramp partway down. Figure roughly an hour from the summit/top of the route back to the base.
routes.bail: The Supercave itself, reached partway up the route, is a natural, sheltered bail/rest point; rappel from there back down the lower pitches is straightforward.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable from mid-summer onward once snow and seep water clear from the lower pitches; a very early (pre-dawn) start is recommended in peak summer to beat the heat on the south-facing wall.
watchOut: The approach gully has been described by parties as "involved and somewhat dangerous" — stay on route-finding beta rather than freelancing up the gully.
Early-season attempts can be very wet; water seeps affect the lower pitches into early summer.
The south-facing wall gets brutally hot in full summer sun — an alpine start is recommended.
Pitches 2-5 are strenuous with tough onsight sequences and limited rest stances.
knownHazards: Loose talus/creekbed terrain on the approach.
Water/seepage on lower pitches persists into early summer in wet years.
Full south-facing sun exposure with no shade until the cave.
proTips: Bring long runners for the cave section to manage rope drag.
Start pre-dawn (as early as 4 a.m. in peak summer) to beat the heat on this south-facing wall.
The Supercave itself makes an excellent shaded rest stop roughly mid-route.
gear: Double cams from tips to #2 BD
whatToBring: Ample water — hot, sun-exposed approach and lower wall
Headlamp for an early alpine start
routes.detailedRack: Double cams from tips-sized up through #2 BD, a standard set of wires, and extra extendable draws/long runners (particularly useful through the cave section to cut rope drag).
routes.proNeeds: Well protected for a route of its grade — the crux pitch is well-bolted with pocket climbing — but standard trad gear covers the remaining pitches; extendable draws help manage drag through the wandering cave exit.
routes.rappels: Two-rope rappel down the route (~1 hr top to base)
routes.fa: Erik Lawson and Lee Arden Pete (2009)
routes.face: Right side of M&M Wall, through the Supercave feature

pitchDetail.notes: 40m warm-up pitch, left-facing corner to a stance below left-slanting twin cracks
50m diagonal twin cracks with an inset dihedral crux; squeaky-clean rock

20m, crux — short arch, well-bolted pocket climbing, exit move onto the Supercave
30m, steep exit from the left side of the cave via an overhanging wall; often called the hardest pitch
56m, steep face of flakes, corners and ramps to the wall top
optional 15m finish to a tree

routes.overview: A shorter, 3-pitch 5.11a trad route on M&M Wall reached via the Supercave approach gully; a quicker outing than the wall's big multi-pitch free lines.
routes.beta: Pitch 2 holds two distinct cruxes and can be French-freed at 5.10a for parties not wanting to commit to the full 5.11a; slab sections give way to steep, balancy overlap moves. A belay bolt marks the route's start on a small ledge.
routes.approach: Park at milepost 166 on Highway 20 (about 3 miles east of the hairpin). Ascend the talus directly uphill from the highway into the Supercave gully; hike up the gully until forced left, then cut back into the gully. A belay bolt on a small ledge marks the start.
routes.descent: Single 70m-rope rappel down the route
routes.descentText: The route is designed for a single 70-meter-rope rappel descent back down the line.
routes.bail: Being only 3 pitches, retreat is straightforward via rappel from any of the belay stations back to the ground.
routes.bestSeason: Dry summer months, consistent with the rest of the wall's south-facing granite.
watchOut: Pitch 2 has two distinct crux sequences — not a route to underestimate at its grade.
The approach gully requires route-finding (hike up, get forced left, then cut back into the gully).
knownHazards: Loose talus on the approach gully.
South-facing exposure — hot in full summer sun.
proTips: Pitch 2 can be French-freed at roughly 5.10a if you don't want to commit to the full 5.11a crux sequences.
A single 70m rope is sufficient for the descent — no need to carry two ropes for this shorter route.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the talus gully
routes.detailedRack: A standard trad rack up to 1 inch, plus quickdraws and runners; a mix of bolts and gear protects the two pitch-2 cruxes.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed bolt-and-gear protection; standard rack to 1 inch covers the route, with bolts backing up the crux moves on pitch 2.
routes.rappels: Single 70m-rope rappel
routes.fa: Thad Nelson, Mark Hanna, Daniel Reed
routes.face: M&M Wall, lower/approach-gully area

pitchDetail.notes: From the belay bolt, head up using cracks for small pro and bolts
Slab sections and steep overlaps with two distinct cruxes; can be French-freed at ~5.10a
Clip the first two bolts, climb the crack, head right over a small arete to the anchors

blurb: Spectre Peak (7,952 ft) is a remote summit in the Northern Pickets, a subrange of the Picket Range deep within North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, standing on the ridge between Swiss/Fury and Phantom Peak. Like the rest of the Pickets, it is built of biotite gneiss formed by metamorphism roughly 100 million years ago, and is reached only by a multi-day, off-trail approach via Easy Ridge and the Challenger Glacier corridor. The peak was first climbed in 1980 by its easiest south-side line, decades before its harder technical faces — Haunted Wall (2006) and Spirited Away (2022) — were established.

routes.face: South side of Spectre Peak
routes.overview: The South Route is the original, moderate line up Spectre Peak, following easier 5th-class terrain on the mountain's south side to the summit, in contrast to the peak's harder technical faces established decades later.
routes.beta: Established in 1980 as the original way to the summit of Spectre Peak, the South Route (Grade III, 5.7) climbs moderate rock on the peak's south side. It remains the standard, easiest way to tag the summit and is the line typically used by parties visiting Spectre as part of a Northern Pickets traverse or as a side objective from a camp near Phantom Peak or Easy Ridge.
routes.approach: Access is via the Hannegan Pass Trailhead at the end of Ruth Creek Road (Forest Road 32) off the Mt. Baker Highway. From Hannegan Pass, follow Easy Ridge east and south, dropping through the Imperfect Impasse and crossing a col near the Challenger Glacier (with Perfect Pass/Whatcom Pass just to the north) to reach the high basins of the Northern Pickets below Phantom and Spectre Peaks. This is a multi-day, off-trail approach involving glacier travel, and typically takes two full days each way.
routes.descent: Descend the South Route in reverse, downclimbing/rappelling moderate rock and snow back to the south-side basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the remoteness and multi-day commitment of the approach, parties should set a firm turnaround time based on weather and daylight, since retreat from high on the Northern Pickets crest is slow and exposed.
routes.bestSeason: Like the rest of the Northern Pickets, Spectre Peak is best climbed from mid-July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the Easy Ridge/Challenger approach and the Challenger Glacier crossing is more consolidated; the range is notorious for prolonged wet, socked-in weather outside this window.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Northern Pickets backcountry; a satellite messenger (e.g., inReach) is strongly recommended for emergency communication.
whatToBring: Glacier travel gear for the Challenger Glacier crossing (crampons, ice axe)
Bivy/camping gear for a multi-day approach
proTips: Many parties combine Spectre Peak with neighboring Phantom Peak or a full Northern Pickets traverse (Luna, East Fury, West Fury, Swiss, Spectre, Phantom, Crooked Thumb, Ghost, Challenger) rather than climbing it alone.

The Easy Ridge / Challenger Glacier / Imperfect Impasse approach is shared by every known route on Spectre Peak, so trip reports for the Haunted Wall and Spirited Away routes are useful for approach beta even when climbing the easier South Route.
watchOut: Loose, exfoliating biotite gneiss is common on the Pickets' south-facing terrain.
Crevasse hazard while crossing the Challenger Glacier on approach.
Weather in the Pickets can deteriorate quickly and the range is known for extended wet spells.
knownHazards: Remote location with slow potential rescue response.
Glacier travel exposure on the standard approach.
Loose rock on south-facing gneiss faces.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road / Ruth Creek Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: Gravel road, generally passable in season
road.seasonalGate: Snow-blocked in early season; typically clears by early-to-mid summer
road.driveNote: From the Mt. Baker Highway, follow FR 32 and take the left fork onto Ruth Creek Road at 1.3 miles, continuing 5.4 miles to the road's end at Hannegan Campground/Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Picket Range zone
climate.typical: Wet, snowy alpine climate typical of the North Cascades crest; the Pickets receive heavy winter snowfall and are prone to persistent summer cloud and precipitation.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack and high avalanche hazard; the approach road and Easy Ridge route are often still snow-covered.
climate.summer: The main climbing season, July through September, though weather windows remain unpredictable.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows with increasing snow by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and road closures; not a viable season for this route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS); the Hannegan Pass trailhead itself is in Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: NPS backcountry permit fees (per-person nightly fee plus a nonrefundable reservation fee); a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass is required at the trailhead
access.permit: NPS wilderness/climbing backcountry permit required for overnight stays, available via Recreation.gov or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatches Whatcom County Search & Rescue, including Bellingham Mountain Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (Level II Trauma Center; the only hospital in Whatcom County)
emergency.notes: Extremely remote area with no reliable cell coverage; rescue response times can be long given the Pickets' wilderness character. A satellite communicator is strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Older sources (Wikipedia's Picket Range peak table, several American Alpine Club route reports) list Spectre Peak's elevation as '7,880+ ft'; a higher-precision figure from Listsofjohn.com gives 7,953 ft, closely matching the database's 7,952 ft, so no change was made to elevationFt. Could not independently verify the individual climbers on the 1980 first ascent of the South Route — the only corroborating source found is Wikipedia's Picket Range peak table, which lists 1980 as Spectre Peak's overall first-ascent year with no party named; this is consistent with (though not a route-level primary-source confirmation of) the given 'South Route, FA 1980' data, so the value was kept as given rather than altered.

blurb: Eldorado Peak (8,873 ft) is the highest summit of the Eldorado Icecap group in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, WA, about 27 miles east of Concrete. Built almost entirely of Cretaceous Eldorado Orthogneiss (a biotite granodiorite), it is ringed by the Eldorado, Inspiration, and McAllister Glaciers — part of the largest contiguous non-volcanic icefield in the Lower 48 — and is renowned for its narrow, corniced summit knife-edge. It was first climbed August 27, 1933 by Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Wilson, and Arthur Winder via the East Ridge.
hierarchyNote: "Main Peak" is not a distinct summit — it is Mountain Project's own sub-area name for Eldorado Peak's true summit block (as opposed to Eldorado's other MP sub-areas like Dorado Needle or Early Morning Spire). Confirmed via MP breadcrumbs: every route below sits under "...> Eldorado Peak > Main Peak." However, three of the six given routes do NOT top out on Eldorado's own 8,873 ft summit — they are separate, well-documented peaks reached from the same Eldorado high camp and bundled into this MP sub-area because parties commonly link them together: Klawatti Peak (8,485 ft, its own peak ~1 mi east), Austera Peak (8,339 ft, ~1 mi further north), and Tepeh Towers (a subsidiary rock ridge/spire cluster, ~8,113 ft, visible across the Inspiration Glacier). Only "North Ridge," "West Ridge" (=West Arete), and "South Ridge" actually finish on Eldorado's own summit. This is Mountain Project's real, intentional grouping (not an import error), but highPointFt/waypoints below are set per-route to each route's ACTUAL summit, not uniformly to Eldorado's 8,873 ft — treat "Main Peak" as a shared-camp cluster area, not a single mountain.

routes.fa: unknown
routes.face: Northwest side, across the Inspiration Glacier from the East Ridge camp
routes.bestSeason: Best once the Inspiration Glacier moat below the tower opens enough to cross safely but before it becomes too wide late season; most parties combine it with an Eldorado/Klawatti/Austera traverse in July or August.
routes.overview: A very short, easy alpine rock add-on to an Eldorado Peak trip: one low-5th-class pitch and some scrambling to a minor summit visible across the icecap from camp.
routes.beta: Climbed almost exclusively as a quick side objective from Eldorado's East Ridge camp — glacier travel to the base, a moat crossing, one short low-5th pitch, then scrambling to the top; round trip from camp is well under 2 hours.
routes.approach: Follow the Eldorado Creek approach (log crossing of Eldorado Creek/Cascade River, steep climber's trail through timber and talus, then onto the Eldorado Glacier) to the East Ridge camp (~7,400-7,600 ft). From camp, travel roughly 45 minutes northwest across the glacier to about 48.5463, -121.1306, climbing up where the glacier meets rock.
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel back onto the glacier
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb or rappel from a fixed rappel station directly below the top, landing back on the glacier for the return traverse to camp.
routes.bail: The route is short enough that retreat is simply reversing the line at any point; the main constraint is the glacier moat crossing, which can become harder to reverse as the day warms.
routes.turnaround: Because the objective itself is short, the real time constraint is glacier travel to and from camp — turn back toward camp with enough daylight margin to recross the moat and any softening snow bridges before conditions deteriorate.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in the Inspiration Glacier basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A single light rack to 2 inches plus a handful of slings is enough for the one short pitch; the rest is glacier and scrambling gear.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed — one short low-5th pitch with a small rack.
knownHazards: Hidden crevasses on the Inspiration Glacier approach
Moat crossing at the base of the rock

watchOut: Moat width and difficulty change through the season — assess before committing to the crossing
proTips: Combine with Eldorado's East Ridge, Klawatti Peak, and/or Austera Peak from the same high camp to make the most of the approach effort

routes.fa: unknown
routes.face: East side of the north ridge (route follows clean rock mostly on the ridge's east flank)
routes.bestSeason: A rarely climbed line; best when residual snow still covers the northeast face, since parties report the route feels far less exposed with snow cover on that side.
routes.overview: A little-traveled technical alternative up Eldorado's north side, taking the path of least resistance on clean, solid rock mostly along the east flank of the ridge, ending just below the true summit pinnacle.
routes.beta: Start at a point of choice on the north ridge and climb the cleanest rock available, generally staying on the east side, finishing right below the summit block. Reported as looser and more serious than the standard East Ridge, with trip reports noting bad rock, rockfall, and the potential for unplanned bivouacs.
routes.approach: Same Eldorado Creek approach and glacier camp as the standard route (see East Ridge camp at ~7,400-7,600 ft), then glacier travel to gain the base of the north ridge.
routes.bail: Downclimbing back along the ridge to the point of ascent is the main retreat option; there is no established rappel line reported.
routes.turnaround: Given the reported loose rock and route-finding difficulty, turn back early if the party is moving slower than planned on the ridge — the consequence of getting benighted on unfamiliar, loose terrain here is significant.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in the Eldorado Icecap basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine protection for 5.7 terrain; exact rack size not documented in available trip reports.
whatToBring: Extra water (dehydration noted in trip reports)
knownHazards: Loose, poor-quality rock
Rockfall
Route-finding difficulty
Reports of unplanned bivouacs on this route
watchOut: Rock quality is notably worse than the standard East Ridge
Route-finding is non-obvious — 'path of least resistance' terrain with no fixed line
proTips: Snow cover on the northeast face reduces exposure — consider timing the climb for when that snow is still present

routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, Tom Gorton, 1940
routes.face: Southwest buttress, rising directly from Klawatti Col
routes.bestSeason: Standard North Cascades icecap season; best once the Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier approach and Klawatti Col are safely crossable, typically July through September.
routes.overview: A moderate alpine rock line up Klawatti Peak's southwest buttress from Klawatti Col — 2-3 short low-5th-class pitches leading to scrambling on the summit.
routes.beta: From Klawatti Col, climb the SW Buttress via 2-3 short low-5th-class pitches with easier scrambling above to the summit; rock quality requires testing holds carefully.
routes.approach: Follow the Eldorado Creek approach to the East Ridge camp, then continue via glacier travel toward Klawatti Col/camp; the buttress starts just minutes from established camping spots near approximately 48.5529, -121.1062.
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel the ascent route
routes.descentText: Either reverse the ascent line, or descend north through a gully toward a rock feature known as The Smokestack, circle it clockwise to grassy ledges, descend the north ridge to the Klawatti Glacier, and follow glacier travel to the McAllister Glacier rappel station.
routes.bail: The route is short (3 pitches); retreat by downclimbing or rappelling the line climbed is straightforward at any point.
routes.turnaround: Given the loose rock, turn back or slow down if holds feel questionable rather than pushing through — this is a route where rockfall/handhold failure, not time pressure, is the main hazard driver.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in the Inspiration/Klawatti Glacier basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A single light rack to 2 inches with several slings for anchors covers the 2-3 short pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack to 2 inches; test holds carefully due to loose rock on the buttress.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the buttress — test holds carefully
Crevasses on the glacier approach to Klawatti Col
watchOut: Rock quality is inconsistent; treat holds with suspicion
proTips: Camp near Klawatti Col to combine this with Austera Peak and/or Eldorado's other routes in one trip

routes.fa: Richard Emerson and Walter Gove (Aug. 29 1969)
routes.face: West arête, above treeline to the summit
routes.bestSeason: Best mid-to-late season when the arête is snow-free and days are long, since it is a long, loose, committing route poorly suited to short daylight windows.
routes.overview: A long, narrow, steep rock arête also known as 'West Arete,' rising from above treeline to Eldorado's summit, mixing 4th and 5th class climbing with variable, often loose rock and serious commitment.
routes.beta: Known both as 'West Ridge' and 'West Arete.' From camp, traverse the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers and cross Dorado Col, descending loose 4th-class terrain into the Marble Creek cirque to reach the route's base (~2 hrs from camp). The lower section is loose 5.0-5.7 climbing through confusing, sandy gullies; the middle section crosses multiple gendarmes on improving but still loose rock; the crux is a 5.8 exposed face traverse protected by an in-situ piton, bypassing one of the two major gendarmes; upper pitches involve chimneys and ridge climbing (5.0-5.7), finishing with simulclimbed pitches to the summit. Trip reports describe up to ~19 shorter roped sections in total versus Mountain Project's 10-pitch listing, reflecting how parties vary their rope-up points on this ridge.
routes.approach: From the East Ridge high camp (~7,400-7,600 ft, reached via the Eldorado Creek trail and Eldorado Glacier), traverse the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers, cross Dorado Col, then descend loose 4th-class terrain into the Marble Creek cirque to the base of the arête — roughly 2 hours from camp.
routes.descent: Reverse via Eldorado's East Ridge
routes.descentText: Descent is not down the arête itself — after topping out, descend via Eldorado's standard East Ridge route, which is described as straightforward and far safer than reversing the ascent line.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: approach from the Eldorado Creek Trailhead to high camp (5-7 hrs, 3,500-5,500 ft gain). Day 2: traverse glaciers and Dorado Col to the route base (~2 hrs), then climb the West Arete to the summit and descend via the East Ridge back to camp (full, long day — reported round trip from the route's base alone is about 17 hours). Day 3: descend to the trailhead (2-4 hrs).
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once committed above the lower gendarmes — downclimbing the loose terrain is hazardous and there is no established rappel line; because descent mid-route is effectively not viable, the route is only appropriate when the party is confident in completing it to the summit and descending via the East Ridge.
routes.turnaround: Because retreat from partway up the ridge is not a safe option, this route requires committing early: if the party is not moving efficiently through the lower loose sections well before midday, turn back before crossing Dorado Col rather than midway up the arête.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in this basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
gear: Ice axe and crampons for glacier travel
routes.detailedRack: A single rack from .2 to 3 inches, doubling up .5-1 inch pieces, plus a set of nuts; a 70m rope is preferred over 60m given the route's length.
routes.proNeeds: Protection quality is inconsistent on loose rock; the crux face traverse relies partly on a fixed piton.
whatToBring: Extra food/water for a 17+ hour push from the route base
knownHazards: Extremely loose rock throughout, especially the lower gullies
Poor protection quality in places
Serious commitment — descent mid-route is essentially not possible
Significant exposure on the tower/gendarme section
Bivouac risk if the party is slow
watchOut: Two major gendarmes bar the ridge; one is bypassed via an exposed, highly loose slab traverse
Route-finding through the lower sandy gullies is confusing
proTips: Plan for a very long day — 17 hours round trip from the route's base alone is typical
The crux 5.8 face traverse has an in-situ piton for protection
Descend via the East Ridge, not back down the arête

pitchDetail.notes: Unbelievably loose terrain with confusing route-finding through sandy gullies
Multiple towers with improving but still loose rock quality
Exposed slab traverse bypassing a gendarme, protected by an in-situ piton
Chimney climbing and ridge traversing
Final pitches simulclimbed to the summit

routes.fa: unknown
routes.face: South ridge skyline as seen from the glacier
routes.bestSeason: Standard Cascades alpine rock season; the glacier flank on approach is steep, exposed firn early season, so crampons should be expected regardless of month.
routes.overview: A moderate 4-pitch rock line up Eldorado's south ridge — a crack system to a notch, then knife-edge ridge climbing — followed by broad, mellow scrambling to the true summit.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 climbs an obvious crack up the face to the 'U-notch' on the ridge; pitch 2 follows excellent cracks on the far side; pitches 3-4 are classic exposed knife-edge ridge ('B-Basin knife riding') ending in a short downclimb, after which the ridge broadens into mellow scrambling to the summit.
routes.approach: Ascend the flank of the glacier toward the ridge's left (south) skyline; this slope is steep, exposed firn, and crampons are mandatory.
routes.descent: East Ridge
routes.descentText: Descend via Eldorado's standard East Ridge route back toward the glacier camp.
routes.bail: After the short downclimb below the knife-edge section, the ridge broadens and easier terrain offers straightforward retreat options; below that, downclimb the crack pitches.
routes.turnaround: The steep, exposed firn approach slope is the main early-turnaround trigger — if crampons and firm snow conditions aren't confirmed, don't commit to the approach slope.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in this basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack to 2 inches covers the crack pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad protection to 2 inches for the crack-and-ridge pitches.
knownHazards: Steep, exposed firn on the glacier approach slope
Loose scree/snow conditions after the snow melts out
watchOut: The approach slope to the ridge is steep and exposed — crampons mandatory
proTips: Descend via the East Ridge rather than reversing the South Ridge

pitchDetail.notes: Obvious crack up the face to the U-notch on the ridge
Excellent cracks on the opposite side of the notch
Classic exposed knife-edge ridge climbing ('B-Basin knife riding'), ending in a short downclimb
Ridge broadens; mellow scrambling to the true summit

routes.fa: unknown
routes.face: Summit tower, reached via the north ridge off the Klawatti/McAllister col
routes.bestSeason: August is the typically referenced window; the route requires crossing the Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti Glaciers, so timing depends on those glaciers being safely negotiable.
routes.overview: A glacier mountaineering traverse to a rugged summit poking out of the Eldorado Icecap, finishing with a short technical scramble/climb up a loose summit tower.
routes.beta: From Eldorado camp, traverse the Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti Col, pass through the col, and follow the north ridge along the McAllister Glacier to a break at about 7,900 ft, where climbers rappel 75 ft to the Klawatti Glacier. From there, 4th and easy 5th class climbing for about 150 ft gains the sub-summit; a further short pitch (roughly 50 ft, well-protected 5.5 per one trip report) on the loose summit tower reaches the true summit.
routes.approach: Starting from Eldorado camp (~7,600 ft), traverse the Inspiration Glacier to Klawatti Col, continue through the col, and follow the north ridge along the McAllister Glacier to the ~7,900 ft break point.
routes.descent: Return via the same route
routes.descentText: Reverse the route: climb back up the ~75 ft rappel pitch (well-protected 5.5), navigate the moat, retrace the north ridge/McAllister Glacier traverse back through Klawatti Col, and cross the Inspiration Glacier back to camp.
routes.bail: Below the rappel point, retreat by reversing the glacier traverse is straightforward; above it, downclimbing the 75 ft pitch and moat is the only way back without leaving gear for extra rappel anchors.
routes.turnaround: Given the multiple glacier crossings involved (Inspiration, McAllister, Klawatti), turn back if visibility drops or if any of the crossings show signs of deteriorating snow bridges rather than pushing on toward the tower.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent in this basin; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack from 0.3 to 2 inches plus a few long slings covers the summit tower pitch; a 30-60m rope is sufficient given the short technical sections.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — a light rack for the ~150 ft of 4th/low-5th climbing to the sub-summit and the final loose summit tower pitch.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Inspiration/McAllister/Klawatti Glacier traverses
Moat crossing near the rappel point
Loose rock on the final summit tower
Exposed climbing on the closing pitches
watchOut: The summit tower itself is loose — only about 50 ft of technical climbing but on poor rock
proTips: Combine with Klawatti Peak SW Buttress and/or Eldorado's other routes from the same high camp to make full use of the long glacier approach

pitchDetail.notes: ~150 ft to gain the sub-summit after the rappel/glacier traverse
~50 ft of well-protected but loose-rock climbing to the true summit
