routes.corrections: The 'Eldorado camp' waypoint reuses Eldorado's Main Peak area coordinate (48.53755, -121.13436) only as a rough camp-area reference at ~7,600 ft per Mountain Project's route text — it is not Austera Peak's own summit; Austera Peak's real summit is a separate point at 48.56846, -121.1015054 (8,339 ft), about 1 mi north of Klawatti Peak per Wikipedia.

blurb: Vesper Peak (6,214 ft) rises above the Mountain Loop Highway corridor in Snohomish County, about 18 miles south of Darrington and 21 miles east of Granite Falls in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. The peak is composed of biotite-quartz diorite intruding metamorphosed Mesozoic ribbon cherts (an Oligocene stock related to the Index batholith), and its gentle south/east talus slopes contrast sharply with a steep, solid north face that hosts several excellent moderate multi-pitch rock routes. The peak was likely first climbed by prospectors before 1918 (confirmed by a 1918 Mountaineers outing); the north face itself was first climbed in 1968 by Bruce Garrett and Jim Langdon, with the modern rock routes established decades later.
hierarchyNote: None — Vesper Peak's name, coordinates (48.01293, -121.51801 per Mountain Project), and Mountain Loop Highway/Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest location are consistently confirmed across Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, and WTA, and the Glacier Peak Wilderness-adjacent North Cascades placement is geographically correct.

routes.overview: A 6-pitch 5.7 alpine rock route on the right side of Vesper Peak's north face, following shallow corners, flakes, and low-angle slabs before finishing along an exposed arête to the summit; established in 2013, it is one of the most popular moderate multi-pitch outings in the Mountain Loop Highway corridor.
routes.beta: Ragged Edge climbs the right (east) side of Vesper's north face on solid granite/quartz diorite, mixing crack, slab, and face climbing across 6 pitches (5.5-5.7). The route is accessed via a heather ledge system that traverses the north face at roughly 5,760-5,800 ft, reached from a col/saddle between Vesper and Wolf/Sperry peaks. It is frequently crowded even on weekdays and is often paired with, or used as an alternative to, the neighboring True Grit (5.8) route.
routes.approach: From Granite Falls, drive the Mountain Loop Highway east approximately 27-28 miles, then turn onto Sunrise Mine Road (Forest Road 4065) and follow the gravel road about 2.3-2.5 miles to its end at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead (~2,400 ft, Trail No. 707; Northwest Forest Pass required, pit toilet on site). Hike the Sunrise Mine Trail about 2.7-3 miles, gaining roughly 2,600 ft, to Headlee Pass (4,720 ft). Continue on trail around Sperry Peak's south flank into the Vesper Creek/lake basin between Vesper and Sperry, then follow the main summit trail up talus and slabs to about 5,600-5,700 ft. From there head north/northwest across slabs to the saddle/col between Vesper and Wolf Peak (two large cairns mark the pass near 48.01375, -121.51451, with Copper Lake visible to the northwest). Third-class slabs about 30 ft above the cairns lead onto heather-covered ledges on Vesper's north face; traverse these ledges roughly 800 ft eastward to the original route start (approx. 48.01376, -121.51736), reached via 4th-class moves. An alternative start via a small notch/col overlooking the Vesper Glacier is also used. Allow 3+ hours for the approach; early-season parties (June) should expect steep snow on the ledge traverse requiring ice axe and crampons.
routes.descent: Walk off to the southeast/east side of Vesper Peak.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via a walk-off down the southeast/east side of Vesper Peak, rejoining the standard hiking route back down through talus and slabs to the lake basin and Headlee Pass, then out the Sunrise Mine Trail to the trailhead. No rappels are required; parties report the descent to the car taking roughly 2 hours in good conditions (total car-to-car time for the route is commonly cited around 8-12 hours, with one trip report logging 11 hours).
routes.bail: The route can be escaped by walking off along easier terrain to climber's right/east toward the summit walk-off slopes rather than continuing up the arête; because the route follows low-angle ledge and slab terrain rather than a sustained wall, retreat by downclimbing or traversing off to easier ground is generally more practical than rappelling.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent is a walk-off (not a rappel line) and the approach traverses exposed heather ledges and talus that become hazardous in the dark or in a storm, parties should set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight, weather, and party pace so the ledge traverse and any snow sections are crossed while visibility and footing are good.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly June through September; the Mountain Loop Highway/Sunrise Mine Road typically opens after seasonal repairs in late spring (often around Memorial Day) and closes again with the first heavy snow in late fall. Early-season (June) ascents commonly encounter lingering steep snowfields on the approach ledges and just below the summit, requiring an ice axe and crampons; by mid-to-late summer the route and approach are typically snow-free.
routes.fa: Darin Berdinka and Gene Pires, August 18, 2013
objHaz: steep snow on approach ledges in early season
high exposure on the upper arête
crowding leading to rockfall/rope-management hazard from parties above
routes.face: North Face (right/east side)
routes.detailedRack: A single 60m rope is sufficient. Recommended rack: doubles of cams from about 0.5-1 in, singles of 0.2-0.4, 2, and 3 in, plus a set of small nuts; the route also has some fixed protection (bolts on the pitch 2 traverse, fixed pins on the pitch 3 arête). Several trip reports note a single standard cam rack is adequate and doubling up is not strictly necessary.
routes.proNeeds: Moderate trad rack (small-to-mid cams and nuts) supplemented by a handful of fixed bolts and pins; belays are reported as sometimes marginal, so care in anchor building is warranted.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the talus/heather approach
map/compass or GPS for the cairned col and ledge traverse
proTips: The route is popular and can be crowded even on weekdays; start early to beat other parties to the base
True Grit (5.8) on the same face is a good backup objective if Ragged Edge is occupied
A single standard rack of cams and nuts is enough; doubling up on cam sizes is not required by most parties
watchOut: The approach ledge traverse and 4th-class downclimb to the alternate start become sketchy/hazardous when wet
Pitch 4 has loose and semi-loose stacked blocks
Rockfall has been documented on the route (September 2024)
The final arête pitch has significant exposure despite its modest grade
knownHazards: Loose rock on pitch 4's stacked blocks
Documented rockfall event in September 2024
Steep, exposed snow traverse on the approach ledges in early season (June)
High exposure along the summit arête pitch

pitchDetail.notes: 160-170 ft; layback flake and groove up a flaky gully (a variation start is also used)
60-90 ft; dyke traverse protected by bolts, exits a gully; often cited as the crux slab pitch
95 ft; arete climbing with fixed pins, fun dihedral
95 ft; stacked blocks — loose rock hazard
80 ft; exposed face traverse, considered the crux by many parties
130 ft; long, easy arete romp to the summit

road.name: Sunrise Mine Road (Forest Road 4065)
road.status: Gravel forest road, seasonally maintained
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens in late spring (often around Memorial Day) after winter repairs and closes again with the first significant snowfall in late fall; the Mountain Loop Highway north of Barlow Pass is also unpaved and often does not open until mid-to-late summer
road.driveNote: From Granite Falls, drive the Mountain Loop Highway east about 27-28 miles, then turn right onto FR 4065 and follow it roughly 2.3-2.5 miles to the road-end Sunrise Mine Trailhead (~2,400 ft); check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts before driving, as closures are common

climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades weather; snow lingers into early summer on north-facing approach ledges and shaded gullies
climate.spring: Road/trailhead access frequently still closed or newly opened; significant snow likely on approach and route
climate.summer: Prime climbing season; route and approach are typically snow-free by mid-to-late summer
climate.fall: Good conditions into September before early snow; road closes for the season once heavy snow arrives
climate.winter: Road and trailhead inaccessible; route not in condition

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District; the former Verlot Ranger District office now operates as the Verlot Public Service Center)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required to park at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead
access.permit: No climbing permit required
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065) and the upper Mountain Loop Highway are subject to seasonal closure; check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts (fs.usda.gov) before traveling

timing.recommendedStart: Early (predawn/first light) given crowding on the route and a 3+ hour approach

waypoints.name: Saddle/col between Vesper and Wolf Peak (cairned pass)
Ragged Edge route start (north face ledge)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office (via Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue, SCVSAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Verlot Public Service Center, 33515 Mountain Loop Highway, Granite Falls, WA 98252, (360) 691-7791 (open Fri-Mon; administered under the Darrington Ranger District, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in the Mountain Loop Highway corridor and on the mountain; a notable multi-week SAR search occurred here in August 2018 for a missing hiker. Carry a satellite communicator/PLB and download maps in advance; do not rely on cell coverage for emergency contact.

routes.corrections: Given data listed alpineGrade as 'Grade III' and routeFt as 800; Mountain Project's first-ascent page and multiple corroborating trip reports (climberkyle.com, bcadventureguides.com) instead give Grade II (bcadventureguides: 'II+ 5.7') and a total route length of approximately 630 ft (Mountain Project's pitch-by-pitch sum) to 700 ft (StephAbegg trip report) — corrected to Grade II / 630 ft here. The peak's summit elevation is also more consistently sourced as 6,214 ft (Mountain Project, SummitPost, WTA, USGS-derived hiking sources) to 6,221 ft (Wikipedia infobox) rather than the 6,226 ft in the given peak data; highPointFt is set to 6,214 ft as the best-corroborated figure (2+ independent sources).

blurb: Poltergeist Pinnacle is the southernmost of several rocky subsidiary summits strung between Mount Challenger and Crooked Thumb Peak, deep in the remote Northern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Washington. It was named to fit the area's Ghost/Phantom-themed nomenclature (alongside Mount Terror, Phantom Peak, Mount Fury and Ghost Peak) and is distinguished by a large shield-like rock face at its base streaked with hundreds of intersecting dikes. The pinnacle's only documented technical climbing route, the East Face (IV 5.9), was first climbed by Dan Aylward and Forrest Murphy on July 4, 2004, reached via a multi-day cross-country approach and a crossing of the Challenger Glacier.

routes.overview: A six-pitch alpine rock route up the striking, dike-streaked East Face shield of Poltergeist Pinnacle, gained after a multi-day approach and a glacier crossing; the pinnacle's first-ascent line and its only documented technical route.
routes.beta: The route starts at the edge of a huge shield feature at the base of the pinnacle's east face, crisscrossed by hundreds of rock dikes. It climbs a dihedral onto the shield, traverses across parallel dikes on an unprotectable section, then follows corners and ledges up the lower face before simul-climbing the easier upper ridge crest to the summit. Rock is generally sound and takes gear well.
routes.fa: Dan Aylward and Forrest Murphy, July 4, 2004
routes.rappels: No rappels required on the route itself; descend by downclimbing/simul-climbing the connecting ridge north to Mt. Challenger's summit, then down Challenger's standard glacier route.
objHaz: unprotectable ~40 ft runout traverse on P1 (5.9 R)
crevasse hazard on the Challenger Glacier approach/descent
bergschrund below Mt. Challenger's summit encountered on descent
loose rock in sections off the main dike features
routes.face: East Face
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Trailhead (end of Forest Service Road 32, off the Mt. Baker Highway/SR 542, ~5.3 miles from the highway), follow the Hannegan Pass Trail over Hannegan Pass and down into the Chilliwack River valley. Pick up the Easy Ridge climbers' route, which climbs to the site of the former Easy Ridge Lookout (6,000+ ft) and follows the ridge crest with sweeping Picket Range views. Near Whatcom Peak the ridge is broken by a steep notch known as the 'Imperfect Impasse' (or 'Perfect Impasse'), requiring a roughly 1,000-ft descent and a brushy class 2-3 climb back up to regain the ridge and reach Perfect Pass (~6,300 ft), the standard base camp for Mt. Challenger objectives. From Perfect Pass, make a rising traverse across the Challenger Glacier, avoiding crevasses, for about 3 miles south to the base of Poltergeist Pinnacle's East Face shield, where the technical climbing begins.
routes.descent: Simul-climb or belay easy 5th class along the connecting ridge north to Mt. Challenger's summit, then descend Challenger's standard Challenger Glacier route back to Perfect Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb or simul-climb north along the connecting ridge crest (mostly low 5th class) to Mount Challenger's main summit. From there, descend Challenger's standard route across the Challenger Glacier, negotiating a bergschrund just below the summit that has turned back parties in late season, then retrace the glacier traverse back to Perfect Pass.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 3-4 days: Day 1 - hike from the Hannegan Trailhead over Hannegan Pass and along Easy Ridge toward the Imperfect Impasse or a camp short of it. Day 2 - negotiate the Imperfect Impasse, reach Perfect Pass, and traverse the Challenger Glacier to a high camp near Poltergeist Pinnacle. Day 3 - climb the East Face (6 pitches) and descend via the connecting ridge and Mt. Challenger's standard route back to camp. Day 4 - hike out via Easy Ridge and Hannegan Pass.
routes.bail: The lower three pitches are gear-protected corners and ledges that can be downclimbed or rappelled from natural anchors back toward the glacier; the rock takes protection well throughout. Above the technical pitches, the ridge crest to Challenger's summit is the only realistic way off, so deteriorating weather should prompt retreat before committing to the glacier crossing or the upper pitches.
routes.turnaround: As with any glaciated, multi-day Picket Range objective, set a firm turnaround time based on weather, glacier/crevasse conditions, and the party's pace back across the Challenger Glacier and over the Imperfect Impasse — rescue response in this terrain can take hours to days, so a conservative retreat decision matters more than reaching the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early September is the typical window: by early July most low-elevation snow has melted enough for the Easy Ridge approach, while the Challenger Glacier still holds solid snow bridges, making the crossing to the pinnacle straightforward. As the season progresses into August the glacier opens up with more exposed crevasses and moats, and the bergschrund below Mount Challenger's summit (encountered on descent) becomes more difficult to negotiate. Conditions can hold into early October in a favorable year, but glacier travel becomes progressively trickier as the season goes on.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable throughout this remote, glaciated part of North Cascades National Park; the NPS notes reception is occasionally possible from high points but should never be relied on for rescue, and any response can take hours to days.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack: cams from small to about 3 inches, a set of nuts, and extra slings for the corner and ledge pitches; rock is generally sound and holds gear well throughout, including on the dike-streaked shield. Add glacier travel gear (rope, crevasse-rescue kit, ice axe, crampons) for the Challenger Glacier crossing on approach and descent.
routes.proNeeds: Gear anchors throughout — the rock is sound and protects well. Fixed anchors (bolts) are prohibited across the surrounding Stephen Mather Wilderness, so retreat and belays rely entirely on removable protection.
whatToBring: multi-day backpacking/bivy gear for the approach
bear canister (required for overnight backcountry stays in the park)
map/compass/GPS for the cross-country Easy Ridge and Imperfect Impasse route-finding
extra layers and weather protection for exposed ridge and glacier travel
proTips: Go early in the season (July) while the Challenger Glacier still has solid snow bridges — the 3-mile glacier crossing gets progressively trickier through August as crevasses and moats open up.
The rock is sound for gear anchors throughout, even where the dike-streaked shield looks complex.
The upper ridge pitch (mostly low 5th class) can be simul-climbed to save time before dropping to Challenger's standard descent.
watchOut: An unprotectable ~40-foot runout traverse on Pitch 1, rated 5.9 R
Crevasse hazard crossing the Challenger Glacier, worse later in the season
The 'Imperfect Impasse' detour on Easy Ridge involves a steep, brushy class 2-3 descent and reclimb
The bergschrund below Mt. Challenger's summit can be difficult to negotiate by late summer, on the descent
knownHazards: Extremely remote location means any rescue response can take hours to days — plan for full self-sufficiency
Glacier travel exposes parties to crevasse-fall hazard on both approach and descent
Rapid Cascades weather changes can turn a straightforward glacier crossing dangerous quickly
No fixed anchors are permitted in the surrounding Stephen Mather Wilderness

pitchDetail.notes: Climbs a dihedral onto the dike-streaked shield, then traverses right across parallel dikes for an unprotectable ~40 ft (5.9 R) to the edge of the face; ~180 ft.
Corner climbing up the lower face to its top; ~200 ft, gear anchors.
Ledge traverse leading into a rock gully toward the connecting ridge; ~200 ft.
Simul-climb or belay the ridge crest (mostly low 5th class) roughly 800 ft to the pinnacle's summit.

road.name: Forest Service Road 32 (Hannegan Pass Road)
road.status: Maintained gravel road, generally passable to standard vehicles in season
road.seasonalGate: Not gated but snowed-in and unmaintained outside the summer/fall season; used as a groomed winter snow-play/ski area when closed to vehicles
road.driveNote: Travel east on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) to about 12.5 miles east of the Glacier Public Service Center, then follow FS Road 32 for 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead. A Northwest Forest Pass is required for parking.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC "West Slopes North" / NWS "West Slopes North Cascades" (Mount Baker zone)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced Cascades alpine climate with heavy winter snowpack and a short, weather-variable summer climbing season.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June on the approach trail and glacier; not a viable climbing window for this route.
climate.summer: Best window; early-mid summer has more reliable Challenger Glacier snow bridges, while conditions can be warm and settled but also subject to sudden Cascades storms.
climate.fall: Climbable into September, sometimes early October in good years, but glacier crevasses and the bergschrund below Challenger's summit are more exposed.
climate.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; approach road and trail become snow-covered and effectively inaccessible for this route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (Stephen Mather Wilderness) for the peak and Picket Range terrain; Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest for the Hannegan Trailhead and access road.
access.fees: No park entrance fee; a Northwest Forest Pass is required to park at the Hannegan Trailhead.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays in North Cascades National Park, available from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (360-854-7200) or, in summer, the Glacier Public Service Center (Thursdays/Fridays, late June-late September).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Fixed anchors (bolts/hangers drilled into rock) are prohibited throughout the Stephen Mather Wilderness, which covers essentially all of this route.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or Whatcom County Sheriff non-emergency 360-384-5390. North Cascades NP dispatch: 360-856-5700 ext. 515 (after hours 360-873-4590 ext. 37, or Skagit County Sheriff 360-336-9450).
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA 98267, 360-854-7200; also Glacier Public Service Center, 10091 Mt. Baker Hwy, Glacier, WA 98244 (Thu/Fri, late June-late September).
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA — roughly a 1.5-2 hour drive from the Hannegan Trailhead.
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in this remote, glaciated terrain; NPS advises against relying on a cell phone for evacuation and warns that rescue response may take hours to days given the multi-day approach.

routes.corrections: The existing DB entry listed this route as 'North Route, Grade III, 5.7' with no pitch count or length. Every primary source found (Mountain Project, AAC Publications, the 2004 CascadeClimbers trip report) documents only one technical climbing route on Poltergeist Pinnacle: the East Face, first ascended July 4, 2004 by Dan Aylward and Forrest Murphy, graded IV 5.9 (not III 5.7) with 6 pitches and about 1,200 ft of climbing. The 5.7 figure in the original entry matches only the route's easiest, uppermost ridge pitch (P4+), not its overall difficulty — no source documents a separate, distinct 'North Route.' Renamed routeName to 'East Face' (the name used in AAC Publications) and corrected commitment III->IV and rockGrade 5.7->5.9; pitches and routeFt were previously null and are now sourced. FA year (2004) in the original entry was confirmed correct.

blurb: Sharkfin Tower is an 8,120 ft granite spire in North Cascades National Park (Skagit County, WA), set midway on the high ridge connecting Forbidden Peak and Boston Peak above Boston Basin, with the Quien Sabe Glacier below its south side and the Boston Glacier below its north side. Its clean, high-quality granite — regarded as among the best in Washington State — and narrow, dramatic summit make it a popular short technical objective from a Boston Basin camp. First climbed in August 1947 by Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, and Joseph Vance, whose line became the Southeast Ridge.

routes.fa: Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, Joseph Vance — August 1947
routes.rappels: 2 raps (single 60m rope) down the ridge back to the notch at the route's base; the approach gully below can also be downclimbed or rappelled to the glacier if wet/icy.
routes.face: Southeast Ridge — the ridge crest between the summit notch and the top, with the Boston Glacier below to the north and the Quien Sabe Glacier below to the south
routes.overview: A short, moderate (5.0) alpine rock climb straight up the crest of Sharkfin Tower's southeast ridge, reached via the Quien Sabe Glacier and a loose approach gully, finishing on one of the most exposed, photogenic summits in Boston Basin.
routes.beta: Three short, low-fifth-class pitches follow the crest of the ridge from the notch at its base to the small summit, with huge exposure over the Boston Glacier for most of the climb. The rock is solid, high-quality granite and protection is generally good. It's a popular quick technical add-on to a Boston Basin trip (often combined with Sahale Peak or Boston Peak), but the glacier crossing and approach gully carry real objective hazard.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead pullout on Cascade River Road (~mile 22, opposite Johannesburg Mountain), follow the steep, eroded climbers' trail up through forest, crossing several streams, to the designated low camp (~5,700 ft) in Boston Basin — about 3.5 miles and roughly 2,500 ft of gain. From camp, follow the standard Quien Sabe Glacier approach used for Sahale: ascend snowfields on the climber's-left side of the glacier, gain the glacier itself relatively high up, rope up, and traverse climber's-right (east) across the top of the glacier until directly beneath Sharkfin Tower. A gully breaches the cliff band from the glacier up to a sloped terrace below the tower — snow-filled and roughly 35° in early season, loose class 2-3 scree by late season. Confirm the correct gully: a similar-looking neighboring gully was the site of a fatal July 2005 rockfall accident. From the terrace, continue to the notch at the base of the Southeast Ridge, where the roped climbing begins.
routes.descent: 3 rappels: scramble back to the P2 anchor, rappel to the P1 belay, then scramble to a large block above the notch and rappel again to the notch; reverse the approach gully and glacier back to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble back to the top of the second pitch. Rappel that pitch (one 60m rope) to the first-pitch belay, then scramble over to a large block above the notch and make a second rappel down to the notch, where the roped climbing began. From the notch, downclimb (or rappel if wet/icy) the approach gully back onto the Quien Sabe Glacier — this gully is loose and often running with water, so rockfall is a real hazard on the way down too. Rope up for the glacier crossing back toward camp, reversing the traverse and snowfield descent into Boston Basin.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan is a 2-3 day trip: Day 1 hike in to Boston Basin low or high camp (3.5 mi, ~2,500 ft gain, 3-5 hrs); Day 2 climb Sharkfin Tower via the Quien Sabe Glacier and Southeast Ridge (roughly 8-12 hrs round trip from camp, often paired with Sahale Peak or Boston Peak from the same camp); Day 3 hike out. Strong parties have also linked trailhead-to-trailhead in a single long day (one solo report logged just over 9 hours car-to-car).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward anywhere on the ridge: downclimb or set a quick rappel back to the notch at the base of the route. From the notch, the approach gully can be downclimbed (or rappelled if wet/icy) back onto the Quien Sabe Glacier and toward camp.
routes.turnaround: The approach gully is loose and prone to running water and rockfall, and North Cascades summer afternoons regularly build unstable weather. Climb the gully and glacier early while conditions are still frozen/consolidated, and turn around if the gully is actively shedding rock, if weather is deteriorating, or if the remaining time/energy margin doesn't clearly cover the rappels and the reversal of the glacier and gully.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through September is the working season, once Cascade River Road has opened and the Boston Basin approach has melted out enough to camp. Early-season ascents (June-early July) find more continuous snow in the couloir/gully (useful for crampons but adds glacier hazard); by August-September the gully is mostly bare, loose scree and the glacier is more crevassed with weaker snow bridges.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the route; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergency contact.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams/nuts from small to about 2.5 in, 4-6 shoulder-length slings, a few lockers, and a single 60m rope covers the 3 short pitches and the 2 rappels back to the notch. Add crampons and a general mountaineering axe for the Quien Sabe Glacier crossing and the approach gully, which can hold snow into early summer.
routes.proNeeds: A light single rack to about 2.5 in with a handful of slings is adequate; protection is generally good on the ridge-crest pitches, though the first pitch's dihedral and the rappel anchors near the notch sit on blocky, sometimes loose rock — back up aged tat before trusting it.
gear: Rock shoes or approach-capable mountaineering boots
whatToBring: Bear-resistant food storage (required in Boston Basin)
Sun protection — extended glacier and ridge exposure
Extra insulating layer for the exposed summit
proTips: Start early to cross the Quien Sabe Glacier and climb the approach gully while snow/rock is still frozen and rockfall risk is lowest.
Combine with Sahale Peak or Boston Peak for an efficient multi-summit day from the same Boston Basin camp.
Bring rock shoes for the pitches but keep boots and crampons handy for the glacier and gully approach.
watchOut: Multiple gullies breach the cliff band below the tower — confirm you're in the correct one; a nearby look-alike gully was the site of the fatal July 2005 rockfall accident.
Water often runs through the approach gully in warm weather, increasing rockfall risk — climb it early before the sun softens things.
Rappel anchors near the notch sit on loose, blocky terrain; inspect and back up any aged slings.
knownHazards: Loose, wet approach gully with significant rockfall potential.
Crevasses on the Quien Sabe Glacier, worse late-season as snow bridges weaken.
A look-alike gully near the correct route was the site of a fatal July 2005 rockfall accident that killed three Mountaineers members — the park's worst multi-fatality mountaineering incident.
Loose, blocky rock around the notch and rappel stations.

pitchDetail.notes: From the notch, skirt climber's right on large blocks, then a short dihedral leads up a couple of moves to the ridge crest and a belay.
Solid rock directly on the ridge crest with good gear; a steeper alternative line lies just left of the crest for those wanting more challenge.
Scramble at or slightly right of the ridge crest to the summit; often simul-climbed.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal gravel/paved NPS-Forest Service road from Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead; the Boston Basin trailhead pullout sits near mile 22, opposite Johannesburg Mountain.
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead parking area by late June, sometimes not until early July after a heavy snow year; gated/closed through winter and spring.
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles total from Marblemount (SR-20); paved for the first ~10 miles, then rough gravel, with the final 3 miles to Cascade Pass narrow with steep drop-offs. Vehicles over 22 ft or towing trailers are prohibited past milepost 18. The road is also subject to unscheduled closures for washouts/storm damage — check current NPS road-status postings before driving.

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone forecast: Cascades of Whatcom and Skagit Counties, WA
climate.typical: Cool, changeable alpine weather typical of the North Cascades crest; afternoon convective buildups/thunderstorms are common in summer.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; road and basin access frequently still snowed-in into June.
climate.summer: The main climbing season (Jun-Sep); mornings often clear with cloud or thunderstorm buildup possible by afternoon.
climate.fall: Cooler, more stable dry spells but shorter days and increasing chance of early-season snow at elevation by late September/October.
climate.winter: Road closed/gated; deep snowpack and avalanche terrain — not a normal climbing season for this route.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for an overnight backcountry/wilderness permit; no fee for a car-to-car day climb.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for overnight camping in the Boston Basin cross-country zone; obtain via recreation.gov reservation or as a walk-up at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, (360) 854-7245.
access.passRequired: None at the Boston Basin trailhead pullout itself (inside the national park boundary) — no Northwest Forest Pass required there.
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated and closed each winter/spring; it is also subject to unscheduled closures for washouts or storm damage — check current NPS road-status postings before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from camp (pre-dawn/early morning) to cross the glacier and climb the approach gully while snow is frozen and rockfall risk is lowest.

waypoints.name: Boston Basin Trailhead (Cascade River Road pullout)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 428-3211; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — (360) 856-6021 (24/7 ED, critical access hospital)
emergency.notes: Search and rescue is coordinated by North Cascades National Park (NPS) and Skagit County Search and Rescue. The July 10, 2005 Sharkfin Tower rockfall accident (3 Mountaineers members killed, 1 injured after the party entered the wrong gully) remains the park's worst multi-fatality mountaineering incident and prompted lasting safety education by The Mountaineers.

routes.corrections: The given grade/rockGrade of 'Grade II, 5.4' with 4 pitches is not what the primary cross-checked sources support: Mountain Project (route pages for Southeast Ridge), StephAbegg's trip report, and Wikipedia's climbing-history summary all consistently give the Southeast Ridge as YDS 5.0 (Grade II), 3 pitches, ~200 ft, FA by Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, and Joseph Vance in August 1947. Two guide-service marketing pages (North Cascade Mountain Guides, BC Adventure Guides) list it as 5.4 (one calling it 'III 5.4, 6 pitches' under the name 'Northeast Ridge'), likely rounding up or folding in approach scrambling/glacier terrain — these appear to be outliers versus the guidebook/route-database consensus, so the route has been corrected to 5.0 / II / 3 pitches / 200 ft. The given elevationFt of 8,120 matches Wikipedia and is retained (a couple of sources list 8,160 ft as a secondary figure).

blurb: Southeast Mox Peak ("Hard Mox," 8,504 ft) is the higher and far more difficult of the twin Mox Peaks in the Chilliwack Range of the North Cascades, within North Cascades National Park about four miles south of the Canadian border in Whatcom County, WA. It is built of notoriously loose, banded gneiss and is reached only by a remote multi-day approach that crosses on foot into Canada and back; it is widely rated among the hardest summits on Washington's Bulger list. Fred and Helmy Beckey made the first ascent via the west ridge on June 21, 1941, a climb Fred later called "the most difficult climb we had ever done."

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, June 21, 1941
routes.rappels: Single rope sufficient for the rappel/downclimb off the summit tower back toward the notch and Ridge of Gendarmes; exact rappel count not documented in available sources.
objHaz: very remote location with slow evacuation
routes.face: West Ridge
routes.overview: The Beckey Route follows Southeast Mox's west ridge and the Ridge of Gendarmes to a notch just below the summit, then climbs three short, loose 4th-to-low-5th-class pitches to the top — the technical crux of an otherwise long, remote alpine approach.
routes.beta: The standard/easiest way up Hard Mox: from a high camp near the Redoubt Glacier/Depot-Redoubt divide, traverse the glacier to the saddle between the Mox Peaks and Mt. Redoubt, descend into the 'Col of the Wild,' gain the Ridge of Gendarmes, then follow ledge/gully systems (West Ledges, Gendarme Notch, East Ledges, Southeast Gullies) to a high notch just left of the summit tower. From the notch, three pitches of loose 4th-to-low-5th-class climbing lead to the top. Per Beckey: most of the climbing is 'more intimidating than technically difficult; if a party finds the climbing above 5.5 they are probably off route.' The route is not sandbagged but is terrifically chossy.
routes.approach: Access is via Canada: from Trans-Canada Hwy 1 at Chilliwack, BC, follow Chilliwack River Road ~31.7 miles to the unsigned Depot Creek Road turnoff (about 200 yards before the bridge over Depot Creek); park in the large turnout as the road quickly becomes rough and brushy. From Depot Creek Road near Milepost 1.5 (~2,350 ft), follow the Depot Creek climbers' path roughly 3 miles through old-growth forest, using a handline and Class 3 scrambling past Depot Creek Falls (~967 ft drop, one of Washington's largest waterfalls), to Ouzel Lake. From Ouzel Lake continue up past the toe of the Redoubt Glacier to a high camp around 7,150-7,300 ft on the Depot-Redoubt divide/Ridge of Gendarmes saddle. This walk-in crosses the international border on foot between British Columbia and North Cascades National Park.
routes.descent: Reverse the west ridge/Ridge of Gendarmes line back through the notch and gully systems to the Redoubt Glacier saddle, then retrace the approach back down Depot Creek to the road.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/rappel the three short pitches back to the notch, then reverse the Southeast Gullies-East Ledges-Gendarme Notch-West Ledges sequence along the Ridge of Gendarmes to the Col of the Wild. Regain the saddle between the Mox Peaks and Mt. Redoubt and cross back onto the Redoubt Glacier to the high camp. From camp, retrace the approach down past Ouzel Lake and Depot Creek Falls (handline) to Depot Creek Road. The full round trip from the high camp has taken documented parties roughly 8-9 hours summit-to-summit on the technical portion alone, with the multi-day glacier/forest approach adding substantially more time.
routes.itinerary: Typical 4-5 day plan used by documented parties: Day 1 - drive to Depot Creek Road and hike in past Depot Creek Falls to camp at Ouzel Lake (~3,550 ft gain from the vehicle). Day 2 - move camp up past the Redoubt Glacier to a high camp on the Depot-Redoubt divide (~7,150-7,300 ft). Day 3 - climb NW (Easy) Mox as an acclimatization/reconnaissance day. Day 4 - climb SE (Hard) Mox via the Beckey Route (West Ridge), typically an 8-9 hour round trip from high camp. Day 5 - break camp and hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Below the summit notch, retreat by reversing the gully/ledge system to the Ridge of Gendarmes and the Col of the Wild without needing to complete the final pitches; from the Redoubt Glacier saddle, the entire route can be abandoned by descending back to Ouzel Lake and out Depot Creek. There is no quick bail once committed to the upper mountain — retreat requires reversing the same complex terrain used on the ascent.
routes.turnaround: Given the long glaciated approach, complex route-finding, and loose, exposed summit tower, parties should set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather rather than push for the summit — the descent through the gully/ledge system and back across the glacier takes as long unroped-4th-class routefinding in the dark or in deteriorating weather significantly raises rockfall and routefinding risk.
routes.bestSeason: The route is typically in condition from July through September, once the Redoubt Glacier approach and the Depot Creek Falls scramble sections have melted out enough for safe passage; documented ascents cluster in mid-to-late July. Early season (June) retains more snow/ice on the glacier and approach gullies, while conditions can deteriorate again with new snow by late September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the remote Chilliwack/Redoubt Glacier basin; a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is strongly recommended and has been used to coordinate a real helicopter rescue from this route.
routes.detailedRack: A light standard alpine rack: a small set of nuts, small-to-medium cams, several slings/runners, and a single rope for the roped pitches and rappel/downclimb off the summit tower, plus crampons and an ice axe for the Redoubt Glacier crossing and any lingering snow in the approach gullies.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rack is sufficient for the three roped pitches; per Mountain Project, be careful when placing protection and watch for loose blocks, as the rock is notoriously chossy gneiss with unreliable placements and anchors.
whatToBring: multi-day bivy/camping gear for the approach
bushwhacking-durable clothing for the Depot Creek forest approach
proTips: Camp at the ~7,150-7,300 ft saddle on the Redoubt Glacier/Depot-Redoubt divide to shorten summit day for both Mox Peaks.
Beckey noted that if the climbing feels harder than 5.5, you are likely off-route — favor the easier line.
A satellite communicator is essential given the remoteness; parties have needed helicopter evacuation from this route.
The handline past Depot Creek Falls and the Class 3 scrambling above it are a common crux of the approach itself, independent of the technical climbing higher up.
watchOut: Extremely loose, chossy gneiss throughout — test every hold and avoid pulling on blocks.
Rockfall risk is elevated when parties are stacked on the ridge or in gullies.
The approach crosses the Canada-US border on foot via Chilliwack River Road (BC) into Depot Creek (WA); be aware of both countries' entry/reporting requirements.
The Redoubt Glacier crossing has crevasse hazard and requires roped travel.
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose gneiss blocks on the ridge and summit tower.
Crevasses on the Redoubt Glacier traverse.
Long, complex retreat if weather deteriorates — no quick bail from the upper mountain.
A documented 2018 trip report describes a dislodged rock during a rappel causing a climber's knee lacerations/bruising and a subsequent helicopter evacuation on this peak.

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse right through loose rock, then climb upward to a distinctive white column.
About 140 feet, trending up and to the right.
Loose scramble to the summit.

road.name: Chilliwack River Road (BC) to Depot Creek Road
road.status: Chilliwack River Road is a maintained road to the Depot Creek turnoff; Depot Creek Road itself is a narrow, unsigned, increasingly rough and overgrown dirt spur not suited to low-clearance vehicles.
road.driveNote: From Trans-Canada Hwy 1 at Chilliwack, BC, take Exit 119A toward Chilliwack/Sardis, then follow Chilliwack River Road about 31.7 miles to the unsigned Depot Creek Road turnoff (~200 yards before the bridge over Depot Creek). Park in the large turnout, as the spur road quickly becomes rough and brushy.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, maritime alpine climate typical of the west-slope Chilliwack Range/North Cascades, with heavy winter snowpack and frequent summer cloud and rain even in the core climbing season.
climate.spring: Still largely snowbound; the glacier and approach gully lines are mostly unformed and unsafe for the standard route.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep) once the Redoubt Glacier approach and the Depot Creek Falls scramble section melt out enough for safe passage.
climate.fall: Climbing window narrows quickly; early-season snow can return to the upper mountain by late September.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; the route is not in climbable condition.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS), Chilliwack River backcountry unit
access.fees: Backcountry camping permit: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee; youth 15 and under free.
access.permit: NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight camping, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA, or — for the Chilliwack/Mt. Baker Hwy access area — the Glacier Public Service Center (Thursdays/Fridays, late June through late September) or by email to noca_wilderness@nps.gov.
access.closures: The approach crosses the Canada-US border on foot between Chilliwack River Road (BC) and Depot Creek (WA); climbers should be aware of both countries' re-entry and reporting requirements for this wilderness border crossing.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (roughly 6:30-7:15 AM) departure from the high camp near the Redoubt Glacier/Depot-Redoubt divide, consistent with documented ascents.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (360) 676-6911 (call 911 for active emergencies).
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA: (360) 854-7245; for Chilliwack-area permits, the Glacier Public Service Center on the Mt. Baker Highway.
emergency.nearestHospital: Nearest hospital-level emergency care is via Skagit Regional Health facilities in the Sedro-Woolley/Mount Vernon area; given the route's remote multi-day approach, evacuations are typically by helicopter.
emergency.notes: This route requires multi-day glacier travel with no reliable cell service; a satellite communicator is strongly advised. A documented 2018 incident on this peak required helicopter evacuation after a rockfall injury during descent.

routes.corrections: Given rockGrade of 5.6 is not supported by sources — Mountain Project and Beckey's own account give 5.5 for the crux climbing ('if a party finds the climbing above 5.5 they are probably off route'). The given routeName 'Southeast Rib / Standard' does not match the established name in any source; Mountain Project and secondary sources identify this line as the 'Beckey Route (West Ridge),' first climbed by Fred and Helmy Beckey along the west ridge (not a southeast rib) on June 21, 1941. The given peak elevationFt of 8,506 is close to but not identical to sourced figures (8,504 ft per Peakbagger/Wikipedia/Stevensong; 8,509 ft per Mountain Project); 8,504 ft was used as the most consistently cited figure across independent sources.

blurb: South Twin Sister is the high point (roughly 7,000 ft) of the jagged Twin Sisters Range, a subrange of the North Cascades in the Mount Baker Wilderness about 10 miles southwest of Mount Baker in Whatcom County, Washington. The range holds the largest exposed mass of olivine (dunite) in the Western Hemisphere, giving its rock a distinctive orange-yellow color and famously high-friction, "50-grit sandpaper" texture. The peak was first climbed on August 1, 1891, by J. M. Edson, E. A. Hegg, and P. J. Parris.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity checks out: South Twin Sister (Twin Sisters Range, North Cascades, Mount Baker Wilderness, Whatcom County WA) is a real, distinct summit from North Twin (6,644 ft) in the same range, and is unrelated to similarly-named "South Sister"/"Twin Sisters" peaks in Oregon, Idaho, or Colorado that surface in generic searches. However, the given elevationFt of 6935 appears low: Mountain Project (GPS-derived, 48.70484, -121.98725), Wikipedia ("above 7,004 feet"), SummitPost ("7,000ft/2134m"), and Peakbagger-derived search snippets all independently place the summit at approximately 7,000-7,004 ft. Recommend correcting the peak elevation to ~7004 ft; flagged per-route via highPointFt and corrections below rather than silently overwritten.

objHaz: Exposure along the ridge crest with limited rest stances
Loose rock/choss in the gully variants (has caused rockfall/pinning injuries)
Seasonal snowfields on the approach talus and summit gullies into early summer
Confusing logging-road network on the approach
routes.overview: South Twin Sister's standard route: a very long logging-road/bike approach to Dailey Prairie and Orsino Creek Basin, followed by a sustained Class 2-4 (locally low-5th) scramble up the west ridge crest on famously sticky olivine (dunite) rock to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Middle Fork Nooksack gate, bike/hike logging roads around Dailey Prairie to Orsino Creek, then follow a brushy climbers' path and red talus slopes into the basin between North and South Twin. Gain a notch near ~5,200 ft and follow the west ridge crest — mostly Class 2-3 with route-finding required, alternating between the crest and both flanks. A short Class 4 (low 5th on the direct line) step near a prominent notch below the summit is the technical crux; it can be avoided by traversing south across gullies and a seasonal snowfield. One of the longest continuous ridge scrambles in the Cascades, on rock so high-friction it's often compared to sandpaper or elephant hide.
routes.approach: Drive Middle Fork Nooksack Road (Forest Road 38) to a gated bridge over the Middle Fork Nooksack River (private industrial timberland — permit required). From the gate, continue by mountain bike up the steep Dailey Quarry Road around Dailey Prairie (roughly 7 miles of logging road), then stash bikes and hike a brushy old road/climbers' path through an old cutblock and older forest, past old log-cabin ruins, up red talus to Orsino Creek Basin between North and South Twin. Orsino Creek is the last reliable water source. Total one-way distance to the summit is on the order of 12.5 miles combining vehicle, bike, and foot travel; the logging-road network has many unmarked offshoots, so GPS/map navigation is strongly advised.
routes.descent: Reverse the West Ridge scramble; parties uncomfortable with the Class 4 crux traverse south onto gullies and a seasonal snowfield to bypass it, or drop north off-route via a steep dirt/scree gully back toward Orsino Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the ridge crest back toward the notch below the top, using the south-side gully/snowfield bypass to avoid the exposed Class 4 step if desired. Continue down the ridge to the basin below, then retrace the talus, climbers' path, and logging roads back to the bike stash and gate. Several trip reports describe descents finishing after dark, so a headlamp and conservative turnaround time are recommended. Loose rock and steep dirt in the bypass gullies have caused injuries (a party reported a boulder pinning a climber's thigh during descent); expect significant fingertip abrasion from the coarse dunite.
routes.itinerary: Most parties complete the route car-to-car in a single very long day (9-15 hours reported); some split it into two days, camping at Orsino Creek Basin or near Dailey Prairie and climbing North Twin the following day as part of a Twin Sisters linkup.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward back down the ridge crest the way you came; the main obstacle to reverse is the Class 4 downclimb near the notch below the summit, which can be avoided by traversing onto the south-side gullies/snowfield instead of down-climbing the crest directly.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach (on the order of 12+ miles one-way with significant biking and hiking) and fully exposed ridge scrambling with no fixed anchors, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin for the descent — multiple trip reports describe finishing the descent well after dark when this wasn't respected.
routes.bestSeason: Most reliably climbed July through September, once winter snowpack has cleared from the approach roads and upper talus/scramble terrain. Snow can linger on the road and north-facing gullies into June or even July in heavy-snow years; climbing after mid-August carries increased new-snow/early-storm risk.
routes.detailedRack: Most parties climb the route unroped in sturdy boots or approach shoes. Some parties bring a rope and a light alpine rack (small-to-mid cams, nuts, slings) for the short Class 4/low-5th sections near the ridge crest crux, especially if staying on the direct line rather than the south-side bypass.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed for most of the route; a light rack only comes into play on the short technical crux near the summit notch if climbing the direct ridge-crest line rather than the easier south-side bypass.
gear: Mountain bike (strongly recommended for the logging-road approach)
whatToBring: Mountain bike for the multi-mile logging-road approach
Map/GPS — the logging roads have many confusing unmarked junctions
Sun protection for the exposed talus and ridge
Extra water — Orsino Creek is the last reliable source
Headlamp — many parties finish after dark
proTips: Bike the logging-road approach — it saves hours and lets you coast out on the return
Orsino Creek is the last reliable water source before the summit push; fill up on the way up and again on the way down
Cairns mark a standard line but many sub-variations exist on the ridge — route-finding matters as much as scrambling ability
Staying just off the crest is sometimes easier than climbing directly on top of it
watchOut: The Class 4 downclimb near the summit notch is the technical crux and feels more serious in descent
Loose rock/choss in the gully variants has caused rockfall and pinning injuries
Logging roads have many unmarked offshoots — GPS navigation is strongly advised
Access crosses private industrial timberland requiring a paid recreational permit and may be gated
knownHazards: Extended unroped Class 3-4 scrambling with significant, sustained exposure along the ridge crest
Loose rock and choss, especially in gully sections off the main crest
Seasonal snow/ice on the approach talus and summit gullies into early summer
A very long, physically demanding day (~12+ miles, ~5,000+ ft gain) that raises the risk of finishing after dark

pitchDetail.notes: Bike-able logging roads from the gated Middle Fork Nooksack bridge around Dailey Prairie to Orsino Creek Basin
Talus/scree to a notch near ~5,200 ft, then follow the ridge crest, alternating sides as needed for the easiest line
Short downclimb/scramble near a prominent notch below the summit; bypass via south-side gullies/snowfield at Class 2-3 if desired
Final scramble to the true summit

road.name: Middle Fork Nooksack Rd (Forest Road 38) to Dailey Quarry / Middle Fork logging roads
road.status: Gated at the Middle Fork Nooksack River bridge; private industrial timberland beyond (Hampton Family Forests, on former Weyerhaeuser Columbia Timberlands) requiring a paid recreational access permit
road.driveNote: Park at the gated bridge over the Middle Fork Nooksack River; roads beyond are closed to public motor vehicles — continue by bike or on foot.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades climate with heavy winter snowpack; the range sits close enough to Mount Baker's storm track that upper terrain often holds snow into June or July
climate.spring: Significant snow persists on the approach roads and upper talus/ridge into June
climate.summer: Driest, most stable window (July-September), though afternoon thunderstorms are possible
climate.fall: Early season snow can return by October along with rapidly shortening daylight
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche terrain; approach road effectively inaccessible

access.landManager: Private industrial timberland (Hampton Family Forests / Hampton Lumber, on former Weyerhaeuser Columbia Timberlands) for the lower approach roads; Mount Baker Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, USFS) for the upper mountain
access.fees: Fee recreational access permit required for Hampton's ~79,000-acre North Cascades forestland spanning Whatcom, Skagit, and Snohomish counties, administered via myoutdooragent.com (limited to 600 motorized / 150 non-motorized permits)
access.permit: Wilderness self-issue permit typically required once inside the Mount Baker Wilderness boundary
access.passRequired: Hampton recreational access permit for the timberland approach; Northwest Forest Pass/America the Beautiful pass may apply at any USFS trailhead facilities
access.closures: Access road has historically been gated with 'No Trespassing / Permit Required' signage by the timberland owner; confirm current gate and permit status before approaching

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn / early morning given the length of the day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 360-676-6911 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA — the only hospital in Whatcom County, roughly 20-25 road miles away
emergency.notes: Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council is the primary technical SAR team dispatched by Whatcom County for the Twin Sisters Range. A multi-week, multi-agency search (Whatcom County SAR, Bellingham Mountain Rescue, Whatcom 4x4, US CBP Air and Marine) for a climber missing on South Twin in summer 2026 underscored the remoteness and difficulty of the terrain.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevationFt (6935) is likely low — Mountain Project (GPS 48.70484, -121.98725), Wikipedia, and SummitPost all independently report ~7,000-7,004 ft; used 7004/7000 as highPointFt/summit elevation here rather than overwriting the peak record directly (see hierarchyNote).

objHaz: Glacier travel / bergschrund crossing on the Sisters Glacier approach
Rockfall reported near Arrowtip Tower on the lower ridge
routes.overview: A connecting ridge linking South Twin's north side toward North Twin across the Sisters Glacier — moderate Class 3-4 scrambling with a short low-5th step, most often climbed as part of a Twin Sisters linkup/traverse rather than a stand-alone route.
routes.beta: The ridge is gained from either its north or south side via moderate scrambling between South Twin's true summit and a subpeak about a quarter mile northeast. Climbers moving between North and South Twin cross the Sisters Glacier southbound to a bergschrund below the ridge, then climb up (favoring snow over rock where possible) to the ridge crest and follow it, mostly Class 3, with some low-5th variations, to the summit. An older (1983) trip report notes good rock climbing on 'Arrowtip Tower' on the lower part of the ridge for parties looking for extra pitches.
routes.approach: Most commonly reached either directly from North Twin by crossing the Sisters Glacier, or via the Green Creek drainage (Elbow Lake Trail to a climbers' trail up Green Creek to a cirque, then the Green Creek Arete) as part of the broader Green Creek Circuit / Twin Sisters Traverse.
routes.descent: Most parties continue over the South Twin summit and descend via the standard West Ridge scramble rather than reversing the glacier crossing.
routes.descentText: From the ridge crest near the subpeak, continue south along easy 3rd class terrain to South Twin's true summit, then descend the West Ridge (bypassing its Class 4 crux via the south-side gullies/snowfield if desired) back to Orsino Creek Basin and the logging-road approach.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as one leg of a longer single-day Twin Sisters linkup (e.g. the Green Creek Circuit: river crossing, Green Creek Arete, Sisters Glacier traverse, this ridge, then West Ridge descent) rather than an isolated day route.
routes.bail: Retreat back across the Sisters Glacier toward North Twin if conditions deteriorate, or continue south along the ridge to join the easier West Ridge for the descent.
routes.turnaround: Because this route involves glacier travel with a bergschrund crossing in addition to exposed low-5th scrambling, build in extra time margin for changing glacier conditions and set a turnaround that allows a full glacier/ridge retreat in daylight if needed.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed early-to-mid summer while the Sisters Glacier still holds snow coverage over the bergschrund; late-season conditions expose a more open, potentially more hazardous bergschrund crossing.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack of small-to-mid cams, nuts, and slings for the short low-5th step, plus standard glacier travel gear (rope, crampons, ice axe) for the Sisters Glacier crossing.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rock protection beyond the crux step; the main technical consideration is glacier travel gear and bergschrund management.
whatToBring: Crampons and ice axe for the Sisters Glacier crossing
Helmet — rockfall reported near Arrowtip Tower
Map/GPS — route-finding across the glacier and subpeak is complex
proTips: Cross the Sisters Glacier early season, while the bergschrund is still well snow-bridged
Often linked with the Green Creek Arete and West Ridge as part of a full Twin Sisters Traverse day
watchOut: The bergschrund crossing becomes more difficult and potentially hazardous later in the season
Loose rock reported near Arrowtip Tower on the lower ridge
knownHazards: Glacier travel and bergschrund/crevasse hazard on the Sisters Glacier
Rockfall potential near Arrowtip Tower
Exposed low-5th climbing with limited natural protection

pitchDetail.notes: Cross the Sisters Glacier southbound to the bergschrund below the ridge
Optional harder rock climbing on Arrowtip Tower for parties seeking more technical terrain
Follow the ridge crest past a subpeak ~1/4 mile NE of the true summit to the top

routes.corrections: Route-identity note: Mountain Project lists an '800 ft, Grade II' route on South Twin called the 'Northeast Ridge' (connecting the true summit to a subpeak ~1/4 mile NE, low-5th variations), which appears to be the same physical feature that older trip reports and SummitPost-derived sources call the 'North Ridge' connecting South Twin toward North Twin via the Sisters Glacier — used to fill routeFt/beta here, but flagging the naming/grade discrepancy (MP: Grade II vs. this listing's Grade III) rather than silently reconciling it. Also see peak-level elevation correction (~7004 ft vs. given 6935 ft) in hierarchyNote.

objHaz: Loose rock/scree in the bypass gullies
Seasonal snowfield on the upper gullies below the summit notch
Confusing logging-road network on the approach
routes.overview: The standard non-technical line up South Twin Sister: follow the West Ridge approach to a high notch, then bypass the ridge crest's Class 4 step by traversing south across gullies and a seasonal snowfield to the summit, keeping the entire route to Class 2-3 on the range's namesake sticky olivine (dunite) rock.
routes.beta: Shares its approach with the West Ridge route: bike/hike logging roads from the gated Middle Fork Nooksack bridge around Dailey Prairie to Orsino Creek Basin, then ascend talus to a notch near ~5,200 ft below the ridge. Rather than following the ridge crest through its Class 4 step, traverse south onto gullies and a seasonal snowfield (present into mid-summer) to reach the summit at a sustained Class 2-3.
routes.approach: Identical to the West Ridge approach: Middle Fork Nooksack Road (FR 38) to the gated bridge, then bike the logging roads around Dailey Prairie, hike a brushy climbers' path and talus to Orsino Creek Basin between North and South Twin. Orsino Creek is the last reliable water source.
routes.descent: Reverse the same south-side gully/snowfield line back to the notch and down the approach talus and logging roads.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the south-side gullies and seasonal snowfield back to the notch below the ridge, then retrace the talus slopes, climbers' path, and logging roads to the bike stash and gate. An ice axe is useful for the snowfield crossing even in mid-summer; loose rock in the gullies is the main hazard.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single very long car-to-car day; some parties camp at Orsino Creek Basin or near Dailey Prairie to split the trip over two days.
routes.bail: Simply reverse the gully/snowfield line back to the notch and descend the approach — there is no technical climbing to down-lead or rappel on this variant.
routes.turnaround: Given the very long approach (on the order of 12+ miles one-way) and full exposure on the upper talus and gullies, set a turnaround time that guarantees a daylight descent margin.
routes.bestSeason: Most reliably climbed July through September, once winter snowpack has cleared from the approach road; the seasonal snowfield in the summit gullies can persist into July and is easier with an ice axe.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is used on this variant; it is a non-technical Class 2-3 scramble throughout.
gear: Mountain bike (recommended for the logging-road approach)
whatToBring: Mountain bike for the logging-road approach
Map/GPS for the many confusing logging-road junctions
Sun protection for the exposed talus
Extra water — Orsino Creek is the last reliable source
proTips: Staying south of the ridge crest in the gullies avoids essentially all of the technical climbing on the standard line
An ice axe is worth carrying for the seasonal snowfield below the summit notch even in mid-summer
This variant is a good option for parties wanting the summit without the West Ridge's Class 4/5th exposure
watchOut: Loose rock and scree in the bypass gullies
The snowfield can be firm or icy in early season
Long logging-road approach with many unmarked offshoots
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus on the south-side gully bypass
Seasonal snow/ice in the gullies below the summit notch
A very long, physically demanding approach that increases exposure to fatigue and afternoon weather
