climate.fall: Early season snow can return by October
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall; approach road effectively inaccessible

access.landManager: Private industrial timberland (Hampton Family Forests / Hampton Lumber, on former Weyerhaeuser Columbia Timberlands) for the lower approach roads; Mount Baker Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, USFS) for the upper mountain
access.fees: Fee recreational access permit required for the Hampton timberland approach (myoutdooragent.com, ~79,000 acres across Whatcom/Skagit/Snohomish counties)
access.permit: Wilderness self-issue permit typically required within the Mount Baker Wilderness boundary
access.passRequired: Hampton recreational access permit; Northwest Forest Pass/America the Beautiful pass may apply at USFS trailhead facilities
access.closures: Access road has historically been gated with 'No Trespassing / Permit Required' signage by the timberland owner; confirm current gate and permit status before approaching

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn / early morning given the length of the day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 360-676-6911 (call 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA — the only hospital in Whatcom County, roughly 20-25 road miles away
emergency.notes: Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council is the primary SAR team dispatched by Whatcom County for the Twin Sisters Range.

routes.corrections: This route is treated as the well-documented south-side gully/snowfield bypass of the West Ridge (described on SummitPost/Mountain Project as avoiding the ridge crest's Class 4 step); no source uses the exact name 'Olivine Scramble,' but the description and difficulty (Class 3, non-technical) match this variant closely. Elevation: see peak-level correction (~7004 ft vs. given 6935 ft) in hierarchyNote.

blurb: Trapper Mountain is a 7,530-foot summit in the North Cascades of Chelan County, Washington, rising southeast of Cascade Pass on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Its slopes drain into Trapper Lake and the Stehekin River, and its Class 3 South Slopes line — the peak's original and easiest route — was first climbed in 1949 by George Bell, Andrew Griscom, Harry King, and W. V. Graham Matthews. It remains a lightly-visited, off-trail objective, seeing more traffic today as a ski-mountaineering target (its North Couloir) than as a summer scramble.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity and placement check out: Trapper Mountain is a real, distinct summit (not to be confused with the more popular "Trappers Peak" near Thornton Lakes) in Chelan County on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness, southeast of Cascade Pass — geographically consistent with a North Cascades parent hierarchy. One correction: the given elevation of 7,541 ft does not match any source found. Four independent sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Wikidata/GNIS, Mountain-Forecast.com) all give 7,530 ft (2,295 m). Recommend correcting elevationFt to 7530. Precise summit coordinates per Wikidata/GNIS: 48.43180°N, -121.01814°W (matches Peakbagger).

routes.fa: George Bell, Andrew Griscom, Harry King, W. V. Graham Matthews, 1949 (the peak's first ascent, made via this — its easiest — Class 3 line)
objHaz: Steep, brushy, unmaintained terrain between the ridge above Pelton Basin and Trapper Lake
Loose rock on the upper slopes
Spring wet-slide avalanche activity on nearby north-facing slopes toward Hurry-up Peak (mainly relevant to early-season/ski ascents)
routes.face: South Slopes
routes.overview: A remote, entirely off-trail Class 3 scramble up Trapper Mountain's south-facing slopes, reached only after a long, brushy descent-and-reascent between the Pelton Basin trail system and Trapper Lake — one of the more obscure summits in the Cascade Pass area.
routes.beta: The South Slopes is Trapper Mountain's original and easiest line, first climbed in 1949. There is no maintained trail to the summit. Parties leave the Cascade Pass Trail system at Pelton Basin, cross rough, partly-bushwhacked terrain over the ridge north of Trapper Mountain, drop several hundred feet to the Trapper Lake basin (4,170 ft), and then climb the open south-facing slopes and gullies above the lake to the 7,530-ft summit, staying on Class 2-3 terrain and avoiding steeper rock bands.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road east from Marblemount (WA-20 milepost 106) about 23 miles to its end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (roughly 3,600 ft; paved for about the first 10 miles, then gravel with potholes and washboard — low-clearance cars are discouraged, and RVs/trailers over 22 ft are prohibited). Follow the Cascade Pass Trail approximately 3.7 miles and about 1,700 ft of gain to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). From the pass, descend east toward the Pelton Basin backcountry camp. From Pelton Basin, an unofficial but well-used route climbs a boot path to a small campsite and switchbacks up onto the ridge above Trapper Mountain; the tread then fades and the drop to Trapper Lake (4,170 ft) becomes a steep, brushy bushwhack. From the lake basin, climb the open south-facing slopes to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the South Slopes to Trapper Lake, then re-climb the brushy ridge back to the Pelton Basin trail system and out via Cascade Pass.
routes.descentText: Descend the same south-facing slopes used on the ascent back to the Trapper Lake basin (4,170 ft). From the lake, regain the ridge to the north via the same steep, brushy line used on approach — this short re-ascent is one of the trip's more tedious sections. Once back on the boot path above Pelton Basin, follow it down to the Pelton Basin camp and rejoin the maintained Cascade Pass Trail for the walk out to Cascade Pass and the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat Trapper Mountain as a long single push from a Pelton Basin backcountry camp, or as a very long car-to-car day from the Cascade Pass Trailhead given the total distance, elevation gain, and sustained off-trail travel.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at essentially any point on the south slopes or the connecting ridge simply by reversing the line back toward Trapper Lake and then to Pelton Basin; the standard route has no technical downclimbing or rappelling.
routes.turnaround: Because the return trip includes a steep, brushy re-ascent from Trapper Lake back up to the ridge above Pelton Basin before the walk out even begins, build in generous time for the exit and turn around early enough to complete that reascent and reach the trailhead before dark or before afternoon thunderstorms build, which are common in the North Cascades in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared the south-facing slopes and Cascade River Road / the Cascade Pass Trailhead are fully open; the road typically doesn't open until late June or early July in heavy-snow years.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed for the Class 3 South Slopes; most parties carry an ice axe and, depending on early- or late-season conditions, light traction for residual snow on the approach ridge and lower slopes.
gear: Map, compass and/or GPS for off-trail navigation
whatToBring: Backcountry camping permit if overnighting at Pelton Basin
Approved food storage per NPS backcountry regulations
Extra water — no reliable water source on the open slopes above Trapper Lake
proTips: Camp at Pelton Basin (permit required) to break the long approach into two days rather than attempting Trapper Mountain as a single push from the trailhead.

The route down to Trapper Lake from the ridge above Pelton Basin is easy to lose — note landmarks carefully on the way in, since the boot path fades quickly in brush and the same ground must be re-climbed on the way out.
watchOut: No maintained trail beyond Pelton Basin — expect sustained off-trail travel and route-finding on brushy, sometimes loose terrain.
North-facing slopes near Hurry-up Peak, close by, are known to produce large wet-slide avalanches in spring; most relevant to early-season or ski ascents rather than the summer scramble.
knownHazards: Steep, brushy, unmaintained descent/reascent between the ridge above Pelton Basin and Trapper Lake.
Loose rock and route-finding challenges on the upper south-facing slopes.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades in summer, with little shelter on the open slopes.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal, check current NPS postings before driving)
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens late June to early July depending on snowpack; historically subject to washout closures after major storm events
road.driveNote: About 23 miles from Marblemount (WA-20 MP 106) to the Cascade Pass Trailhead; paved for roughly the first 10 miles, then gravel with potholes and washboard to the end. No RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft; low-clearance sedans are discouraged.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Washington East Slopes Cascades (NWS Seattle)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy orographic precipitation as Pacific storms rise over the Cascade crest.
climate.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack persists on north-facing slopes into June; wet-slide avalanche hazard on nearby slopes.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep) and the recommended climbing season; afternoon thunderstorms are possible.
climate.fall: Shortening days, with early-season snow possible by late September or October.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall; the peak becomes a ski-mountaineering objective (its North Couloir) rather than a scramble destination.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (trailhead and approach); the summit sits on the shared boundary with the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest)
access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for any overnight camping in North Cascades National Park, including Pelton Basin; obtain in advance via recreation.gov or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Cascade River Road and the Cascade Pass Trailhead are seasonal and close with winter snow; check current NPS road-condition postings before driving in.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn if attempting as a single very long day from the trailhead, given the off-trail travel and the elevation loss-and-regain between Pelton Basin and Trapper Lake.

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit); road/trailhead access is via Marblemount in Skagit County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 667-6851; dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: The peak sits on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness, so incident response may involve NPS rangers, Chelan County SAR, and/or USFS depending on exact location.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (7541) does not match any source; verified elevation is 7,530 ft (2,295 m) per Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Wikidata/GNIS, and Mountain-Forecast.com (all agree). Recommend updating the peak's elevationFt to 7530 and route highPointFt accordingly.

blurb: Whatcom Peak (7,574 ft) is a glaciated summit in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, standing as an outlying peak of the Picket Range immediately north of Mount Challenger and separated from it by Perfect Pass, at the head of the Little Beaver Creek drainage south of Whatcom Pass. The mountain's core rock is Skagit Gneiss, and it carries the rapidly retreating Whatcom Glacier on its northeast/east flank. It was first climbed in 1936 by Fred Berry and Lawrence Buchanen.

routes.overview: A glacier-and-scramble line to Whatcom Peak's summit: traverse the mountain's east side below the retreating Whatcom Glacier from Whatcom Pass to Perfect Pass, then climb a moderate snow slope and a short, exposed Class 3 ridge to the true (northeast) summit.
routes.beta: From Whatcom Pass the standard approach descends on a traversing line to about 5,200 ft to round the base of Whatcom Peak's NW rib, then crosses polished slabs below the Whatcom Glacier on the peak's east side to reach Perfect Pass at the south side of the mountain. From Perfect Pass the summit is 'an easy grab' — a snow slope of moderate angle followed by a short but exposed Class 3 ridge scramble to the higher, northeast summit point. The Whatcom Glacier is shrinking quickly and can present bare ice and shallow crevasses in late season, so many parties treat the crossing as roped glacier travel. This route is also reachable/exited via Easy Ridge and 'Imperfect Impasse' from the Chilliwack side, or combined with an ascent of Mount Challenger from Perfect Pass.
routes.approach: From the Hannegan Pass Trailhead (~3,100 ft) on Ruth Creek off Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542), follow the Hannegan Pass Trail roughly 4 miles to Hannegan Pass, then descend into the Chilliwack River valley on the Chilliwack River Trail, fording or hand-tramming the Chilliwack River and passing Brush Creek camp. Continue up-valley to Whatcom Pass (~5,200 ft) — about 8 miles and 3,300 ft of gain total from the trailhead. Leave the trail at Whatcom Pass and descend on a traverse to about 5,200 ft to round the peak's NW rib, then cross the slabs below the Whatcom Glacier on the east side to reach Perfect Pass.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the south-side snow slope to Perfect Pass, then re-cross the Whatcom Glacier traverse back to Whatcom Pass and out via the Chilliwack River and Hannegan Pass trails.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed Class 3 south ridge crest back to the snow slope above Perfect Pass. Re-trace the Whatcom Glacier traverse below the peak's east side back to Whatcom Pass, watching for softened snow bridges and bare ice in the afternoon, then descend the Chilliwack River Trail and climb back over Hannegan Pass to the trailhead. Parties continuing on to Mount Challenger instead descend from Perfect Pass toward the Challenger Glacier.
routes.itinerary: Typical 3-day plan: Day 1 — Hannegan Pass Trailhead to a camp near Whatcom Pass or Brush Creek (~8 mi, ~3,300 ft gain). Day 2 — traverse the Whatcom Glacier to Perfect Pass, camp, and climb Whatcom Peak via the south side. Day 3 — reverse the glacier traverse and trail back to the trailhead. Parties linking Easy Ridge/Imperfect Impasse or an ascent of Mount Challenger typically extend to 4-5 days.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ascent line back to Whatcom Pass; there is no shortcut bailout once committed to the glacier traverse, so turn back early if weather or visibility deteriorates.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that leaves enough daylight and weather margin to re-cross the Whatcom Glacier traverse and descend to Whatcom Pass before conditions deteriorate — the glacier crossing and exposed summit ridge are markedly more hazardous in poor visibility, on softened afternoon snow, or after refreeze turns slopes to hard ice.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once the Chilliwack River crossing and trail to Whatcom Pass are in, but before the Whatcom Glacier traverse degrades late season into bare ice and open crevasses; solid snow cover on the south-side summit slope also makes the final scramble more secure earlier in the window.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service is expected anywhere along this remote North Cascades National Park route; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergency contact.
routes.fa: Fred Berry, Lawrence Buchanen, 1936
routes.face: South side / east-side Whatcom Glacier traverse
objHaz: icefall/rockfall from the retreating Whatcom Glacier
remote, committing terrain with no quick bailout
knownHazards: The Whatcom Glacier is shrinking rapidly, and large chunks of ice periodically calve onto the slabs below it.
Crevasses and bare ice can be encountered on the glacier traverse in late-season conditions, requiring roped travel and crevasse-rescue readiness.
The final south-ridge scramble to the true summit is exposed with real fall consequences despite its low Class 3 rating.
watchOut: Time the glacier traverse for firm morning snow; afternoon conditions expose bare ice and open moats.
The true (higher) summit is the northeast point, not the closer point visible first from Perfect Pass.
This is remote North Cascades National Park terrain far from any road; a rescue would take a long time to reach you.
proTips: Approaching via Whatcom Pass avoids the tricky 'Imperfect Impasse' rock step on the Easy Ridge approach, but substitutes glacier-crossing exposure instead.
Perfect Pass makes a scenic, sheltered high camp for the summit push and for continuing on to Mount Challenger.
Solid snow cover greatly eases both the glacier traverse and the summit snow slope; conditions deteriorate markedly by late summer.

pitchDetail.notes: Descending traverse from Whatcom Pass (~5,200 ft) around the base of Whatcom Peak's NW rib; crosses polished slabs below the retreating Whatcom Glacier. Bare ice and shallow crevasses reported in late season; roped travel recommended.
Continue the low traverse on the east/south side of the peak to Perfect Pass (~6,200 ft).
A moderate-angle snow slope leads to a short, exposed Class 3 scramble along the south ridge crest to the higher (northeast) summit.

routes.detailedRack: A light glacier-travel and scrambling kit rather than a rock rack: a rope for the Whatcom Glacier crossing, crevasse-rescue hardware (pulleys, prusiks or mechanical ascenders, spare slings/cord), ice axe and crampons for the glacier slabs and summit snow slope, and a helmet for the exposed Class 3 ridge.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal since this is a glacier-travel and scramble route rather than a roped rock climb, but a rope, pickets as conditions dictate, and a full crevasse-rescue kit are essential for the Whatcom Glacier crossing.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Service Road 32)
road.status: Generally open in summer season; sections have experienced storm/flood damage in recent years — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving
road.seasonalGate: Not formally gated by a fixed calendar date, but access beyond the maintained section is snow-dependent; the road corridor is groomed for cross-country skiing in winter
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA on Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542), turn onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS 32) and follow it to its end at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead / Shuksan Picnic Area (~3,100 ft). A Northwest Forest Pass is required to park.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mount Baker / West Slopes North zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy winter snowfall and a relatively dry, stable window in mid-to-late summer.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; river crossings running high with snowmelt.
climate.summer: The primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); warm days, though glaciated terrain still requires standard glacier travel precautions.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing chance of early snow above treeline from late September.
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; a serious winter mountaineering undertaking, rarely climbed in this season.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS) for the climbing route; Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest for the Hannegan Pass Trailhead approach.
access.fees: NPS backcountry permit fee of $10/person plus a $6 reservation fee (youth 15 and under free).
access.permit: NPS backcountry permit required for any overnight stay; reserve in advance via Recreation.gov or obtain a walk-up permit (about 40% of sites are held for walk-ups).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking, plus the NPS backcountry permit for overnight travel.
access.closures: Hannegan Pass Road/Trailhead access can be affected by storm damage or washouts; check Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before the trip.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from a high camp at Whatcom Pass or Perfect Pass, to cross the glacier traverse and exposed summit ridge on firm snow before afternoon softening

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line (360) 676-6911, which coordinates Whatcom County Search & Rescue
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA, (360) 854-7245; seasonal Glacier Public Service Center, Glacier, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (Level II trauma center)
emergency.notes: Extremely remote glaciated terrain with no reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator/PLB and leave a detailed itinerary/backcountry permit with the park.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,579 ft) conflicts with Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and MountainProject, which all list 7,574 ft; corrected highPointFt to 7,574 ft. The FA (Fred Berry & Lawrence Buchanen, 1936) is documented as the peak's overall first ascent; no source specifically confirms it followed this exact Whatcom Glacier/south-side line, though it is the mountain's historically standard, easiest line and most likely candidate. This route is also referred to as the 'South Side' route or, on MountainProject, the 'South Spur' (4th class), consistent with the given 'Southwest Route (Whatcom Glacier)' name.

blurb: Colchuck Peak (8,705 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, part of the Cretaceous-age Mount Stuart batholith, rising directly above Colchuck Lake near Leavenworth, WA. Its north/northeast side holds the receding Colchuck Glacier, whose moraine and couloirs feed several popular moderate alpine lines, while the peak's parent, Dragontail Peak, sits just 0.5 mi to the east. The first recorded ascent was on August 11, 1948 by Elvin R. Johnson, Norma Johnson, William A. Long and Kathy Long via the glacier route now called the Colchuck Glacier (or "East") Route.

routes.fa: Elvin R. Johnson, Norma Johnson, William A. Long, Kathy Long — August 11, 1948 (this glacier line, called the "East Route" by Fred Beckey, was also the first ascent of the peak)
objHaz: Crevasses (few, but present, especially late season)
Rockfall on upper glacier and moraine
Bare, harder ice by late summer
routes.season: Jun-Aug (best early-to-mid season while continuous snow covers the glacier; by late summer it turns to bare, more involved ice)
routes.face: North side / Colchuck Glacier, east lobe
routes.overview: The standard, easiest route on Colchuck Peak: a moderate glacier ascent up the shrinking Colchuck Glacier's east lobe, with one short steep snow section near the Colchuck-Dragontail saddle and 3rd-class scrambling to the summit plateau.
routes.beta: Beckey's 'East Route,' almost universally known today as the Colchuck Glacier Route. From the base of the glacier, ascend easy snow on the left (east) lobe past the base of Dragontail's Serpentine and Backbone Ridge routes. The angle increases near the Colchuck-Dragontail saddle to roughly 100 ft of 40-degree snow, then eases into 3rd-class sandy scrambling to the 8,500 ft summit plateau and on to the true 8,705 ft summit. With good snow cover it's an easy 3-4 hour climb from a camp at Colchuck Lake, or is readily done car-to-car in a long day.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road (FR 7600) about 8.4 miles to the FR 7601 junction near Bridge Creek Campground, then follow the rough, steep FR 7601 for 3.7 miles to Stuart Lake Trailhead (high-clearance recommended). Hike the Stuart Lake Trail; at about 2.5 miles branch onto the Colchuck Lake Trail and reach Colchuck Lake (5,574 ft) at roughly 4-4.5 miles and about 2,200 ft of gain. From the lake's north shore, gain the crest of the lateral moraine as soon as possible to avoid loose scree and talus, and follow it to the toe of the Colchuck Glacier — about 5.5 miles total from the trailhead to the start of the technical climbing. FR 7601 is gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring, adding about 3 miles and 1,000 ft to the off-season approach.
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route.
routes.descentText: Reverse the 3rd-class scramble off the summit plateau, downclimb or plunge-step the short ~40-degree snow section near the saddle, then descend the glacier and moraine back to Colchuck Lake and out the trail.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long day trip car-to-car, or as a short climb (about 3-4 hours round trip) from a backcountry camp at Colchuck Lake (overnight permit required in season) as part of a broader Enchantments trip.
routes.bail: Retreat by descending the ascent line at any point; the only sustained steeper section is the ~100 ft of 40-degree snow near the saddle, which can be down-climbed facing in or belayed if icy.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if the glacier surface has gone to bare, crevassed ice beyond your party's comfort or gear, if the summit plateau is socked in, or if you can't be off the upper glacier before afternoon sun softens the snow.
routes.bestSeason: Best roughly June through August, once FR 7601 opens and while the glacier still holds continuous snow cover; later in summer the glacier bares out to harder ice and the climb becomes noticeably more involved.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service through the Icicle Creek canyon or in the Colchuck Lake/Enchantments basin; carry a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard line; many parties carry an ice axe and crampons only, with a picket or two for extra security on the short steeper pitch near the saddle.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — a solid ice-axe self-arrest is normally sufficient; a picket adds security on the ~40-degree section near the Colchuck-Dragontail saddle.
gear: Rope (optional, for glacier travel if crevasses are open)
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the moraine/scree approach
Extra water — no reliable source above the lake
proTips: Gain the lateral moraine crest immediately from the lake to avoid loose scree and talus.
Climb early-to-mid season (June) while the glacier is still snow-covered — it turns to bare, more technical ice by late summer.
Stay on the left (east) side of the glacier to minimize crevasse exposure.
watchOut: The final ~100 ft below the Colchuck-Dragontail saddle steepens to about 40 degrees, enough to require real crampon technique.
The glacier is rapidly receding and thinning; year-to-year surface and crevasse conditions vary.
knownHazards: A handful of crevasses can open on the glacier, especially late season.
Some rockfall has been reported on the upper glacier and moraine.
Loose scree/talus off the lateral moraine if you stray from its crest.

pitchDetail.notes: Gain the lateral moraine crest quickly from the lake's north shore to avoid loose scree and talus.
Follow the moraine crest to the base of the Colchuck Glacier.
Ascend the east lobe past the base of Dragontail's Serpentine/Backbone Ridge; steepens to ~100 ft of 40° snow near the Colchuck-Dragontail saddle.
Sandy 3rd-class scrambling from the saddle area to the 8,500 ft summit plateau and on to the true summit (8,705 ft).

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road/FR 7600) to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.status: Rough, steep gravel road; high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring to protect the roadbed and prevent travel on steep, snow-covered sections
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road ~8.4 mi to the FR 7601 junction near Bridge Creek Campground, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead. Icicle Road itself has also seen temporary storm-damage closures — check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF alerts before driving.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central zone
climate.typical: East-slope Cascades rain-shadow climate: drier and sunnier than the western Cascades, with cold, snowy winters and warmer, mostly dry summers.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack often persists into May-June; glacier surface is typically at its best continuous-snow condition.
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry with possible afternoon thunderstorms; the glacier degrades to bare, harder ice by mid-to-late summer.
climate.fall: Cool and increasingly unstable; the season's first significant snow typically closes FR 7601 by October.
climate.winter: Cold with heavy snowfall and significant avalanche hazard; FR 7601 is gated, adding mileage to any winter approach.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass required for trailhead parking; day-use wilderness permit is free and self-issued at the trailhead.
access.permit: Overnight camping within the Enchantment Permit Area (Colchuck zone) requires a permit May 15-Oct 31, allocated via a recreation.gov lottery plus limited walk-in permits; day climbs need only the free self-issued day-use permit.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass
access.closures: FR 7601 is gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring; check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF alerts for any additional storm-damage closures on Icicle Road.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early alpine start recommended to be off the glacier before afternoon sun softens the snow

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 non-emergency: (509) 663-9911 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826 — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-5815 (24-hr ER)
emergency.notes: The Chelan County Sheriff's Office (with Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue) handles wilderness rescues. Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in Icicle Creek canyon and the Colchuck Lake basin; carry a satellite communicator.

routes.fa: Skip Edmunds, Clark Gerhardt, Paula Kregel, Greg Markov — January 1975
objHaz: Short cliff bands / icy sections on entry and exit
routes.season: Jan-May (a winter/spring steep snow-and-ice climb; sources explicitly describe it as a winter route rather than a summer one)
routes.face: Northeast Face, higher on the Colchuck Glacier than the North Buttress Couloir and below the Northeast Buttress
routes.overview: A moderate, aesthetic alpine snow/ice couloir on Colchuck Peak's northeast face that climbs from the Colchuck Glacier to a steep headwall, exits along its base, and finishes on steep snow to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Follow the couloir off the Colchuck Glacier as it winds upward and steepens; the gully hits a steep wall, so continue along the base of this wall to climber's left to find the exit, finishing on steep snow/ice to 60+ degrees that gains the summit ridge. Protection is a mix of snow gear and a small alpine rock rack including pitons. Regional guides describe it as roughly Grade II with an elevation gain of about 1,500 ft from the glacier to the ridge; some secondary sources grade it up to Grade III depending on conditions.
routes.approach: Follow the shared Colchuck Peak approach: from Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road ~8.4 mi to FR 7601, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead; hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail (~4-4.5 mi, ~2,200 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake. Climb the lateral moraine to the toe of the Colchuck Glacier, then angle up and across the glacier toward its middle — the Northeast Couloir is the deep gully higher on the glacier than the North Buttress Couloir, below the Northeast Buttress. In winter, when FR 7601 is gated, parties instead approach via Eightmile Road/Mountaineer Creek Trailhead, adding several miles and about 1,000 ft.
routes.descent: Descend via the Colchuck Glacier route.
routes.descentText: Most parties do not reverse the couloir; from the summit ridge, descend onto the Colchuck Glacier and follow its standard route and the moraine back down to Colchuck Lake.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed from a car camp near the trailhead or a permitted backcountry camp at Colchuck Lake, with a pre-dawn alpine start so the couloir is climbed on firm, cold snow/ice before solar warming increases avalanche and rockfall hazard.
routes.bail: Below the exit wall, downclimb the couloir the way you came, or where terrain allows, traverse onto the adjacent, lower-angle Colchuck Glacier route to escape the couloir's terrain traps.
routes.turnaround: This is avalanche and rockfall terrain — turn around for any recent avalanche activity, rapid warming, new snow loading, or elevated avalanche danger in the NWAC East Central zone forecast; do not commit under uncertain conditions.
routes.bestSeason: A winter-spring line, roughly January through May, when the couloir is continuously snow/ice-filled; regional climbers describe it as a winter route rather than a summer climb.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service through Icicle Creek canyon or in the Colchuck Lake/Enchantments basin; carry a satellite communicator for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of snow and ice protection (a few ice screws, 2-3 pickets) plus a small alpine rock rack including several pitons for the headwall exit; a 60m rope is typical.
routes.proNeeds: Snow and ice protection through the lower/mid couloir, transitioning to thin rock/mixed protection (pitons, small cams/nuts) near the headwall exit.
gear: Small alpine rock rack including pitons
whatToBring: Extra insulating layers — the couloir stays shaded and cold
proTips: A cold, stable morning greatly reduces the couloir's avalanche and rockfall hazard — this is a winter/spring route, not a summer one.
At the headwall, continue along its base to climber's left to find the exit.
Check the NWAC East Central zone forecast the day before; this couloir has produced deadly avalanches.
watchOut: A slab avalanche killed three climbers here on Feb 19, 2023, after the lead climber triggered a release that swept the party of four about 1,000 vertical feet — the couloir's terrain traps and cliff bands turned a moderate-sized slide fatal.
Narrow, constricted sections and short cliff bands add fall/entrapment consequence on top of the avalanche hazard.
Loose rock is present along the exit wall.
knownHazards: NWAC's accident report notes the couloir's steep slope angles, frequent terrain traps, and large height differences between landforms make it particularly susceptible to avalanche hazard even from small slides.
Icy sections alternate with powder/wind slab depending on recent weather.
Exposure and rockfall on the entrance pitch and headwall.

pitchDetail.notes: Enter the couloir off the Colchuck Glacier and climb moderate snow as it winds upward.
Couloir steepens and hits a steep rock/ice wall; traverse along its base to climber's left.

pitchDetail.grade: Steep snow/ice to 60°+, AI3 with pin protection
pitchDetail.notes: Look for the exit past the wall; steep snow/ice gains the summit ridge.
Short ridge scramble to the summit.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road/FR 7600) to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.status: Rough, steep gravel road; high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring; winter ascents typically approach via Eightmile Road instead
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road ~8.4 mi to the FR 7601 junction near Bridge Creek Campground, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead when open. In winter, park near Bridge Creek Campground and approach via Eightmile Road/Mountaineer Creek Trailhead instead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central zone
climate.typical: East-slope Cascades rain-shadow climate with cold, snowy winters; this couloir is climbed specifically during that snowy/icy winter-spring window.
climate.spring: Often the couloir's best, most continuous condition before it starts to melt out.
climate.summer: The couloir typically melts out and is not considered a summer climb.
climate.fall: Increasingly unstable early-season snowpack; avoid climbing on unconsolidated early-season snow.
climate.winter: Cold, heavy snowfall, and significant avalanche hazard — the route's primary season but also its primary hazard window.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass required for trailhead parking once the road/gate is open; day-use wilderness permit is free and self-issued.
access.permit: Overnight camping within the Enchantment Permit Area (Colchuck zone) requires a permit May 15-Oct 31; outside that window, self-issued day-use/winter camping rules apply per current Forest Service guidance.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass
access.closures: FR 7601 is gated mid-fall through spring, which is precisely this route's climbing season — plan the winter approach via Eightmile Road accordingly.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start, well before sunrise, to be through the couloir before solar warming increases avalanche/rockfall hazard

emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-5815 (24-hr ER)
emergency.notes: This route was the site of a fatal avalanche on Feb 19, 2023 (3 deaths); the Chelan County Sheriff's Office and Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue respond to incidents here. Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent in the approach canyon and basin; carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Given data lists this route as 'Grade III, AI2-3.' Mountain Project's own route page states 'Grade II alpine snow climb' with ~1,500 ft of gain, and other secondary sources describe it as Grade II-III depending on conditions. iceGrade AI3/alpineGrade AD are consistent with sourced descriptions and were kept, but commitment was set to 'II' per Mountain Project as the best-sourced primary reference — flagging the II vs. III discrepancy for review rather than asserting either with full confidence.

routes.fa: Ray Lilleby and Jim Wickwire — July 1962
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 (when snow thins later in the season)
objHaz: Crevasses on the Colchuck Glacier descent
Poor protection in mixed sections when snow thins
routes.season: Mar-Jun (best in spring as soon as trailhead/road access allows, while continuously snow-filled)
routes.face: North Buttress, rising directly out of the Colchuck Glacier's moraine
routes.overview: The most direct couloir rising out of the Colchuck Glacier's moraine on Colchuck Peak's north side — a popular introduction to steep snow and easy mixed climbing, with conditions ranging from a straightforward snow slog to thin mixed rock-and-ice.
routes.beta: From the far side of Colchuck Lake, hike uphill to gain the moraine, follow it to the right, and drop into the large couloir at the edge of the Colchuck Glacier. In winter and early season it is continuous snow the entire way; later in summer, Class 4 rock can be exposed in a few short steps. The route can be climbed minimalist and fast, or fully protected with rock gear, pickets, and screws. Descent is via the Colchuck Glacier route on the peak's far side.
routes.approach: Follow the shared Colchuck Peak approach: from Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road ~8.4 mi to FR 7601, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead; hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail (~4-4.5 mi, ~2,200 ft gain) to Colchuck Lake. From the lake's far (south) side, hike up and gain the moraine, follow it to the right, and drop into the couloir — it is the first major gully reached rising out of the toe of the Colchuck Glacier's moraine.
routes.descent: Descend via the Colchuck Glacier route.
routes.descentText: Descend the backside of the mountain onto the Colchuck Glacier and follow its standard route and the moraine back to Colchuck Lake; in soft, late-day snow this descent has been reported as slow and exhausting.
routes.itinerary: Usually climbed from a car camp near the trailhead or a permitted backcountry camp at Colchuck Lake, with an early alpine start to climb the couloir on firm snow before it softens and before any summit cornice becomes more hazardous to breach.
routes.bail: Downclimb the couloir back to the moraine, or continue over the top and descend via the standard Colchuck Glacier route if conditions deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Turn around for recent avalanche debris in or above the couloir, increasing rockfall/icefall as the sun hits the gully, or if the party can't move fast enough to clear the couloir and any summit cornice with margin before conditions deteriorate.
routes.bestSeason: Best in spring, roughly March through June, as soon as trailhead/road access allows, when the couloir is continuously snow-filled; by mid-to-late summer it can bare out to Class 4 rock.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service through Icicle Creek canyon or in the Colchuck Lake/Enchantments basin; carry a satellite communicator for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: Two pickets, two ice screws (14-22 cm), a small cam rack (roughly 0.5-2 in) and a few small pitons/nuts covers most conditions; parties climbing fast and light often carry little to no gear.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs scale with conditions: firm early-season snow wants pickets and screws, while a thinner, mixed/rock finish later in the season wants a light rock rack.
gear: Light rock rack (cams ~0.5-2 in, small pitons, nuts)
whatToBring: Sun protection — the couloir gets afternoon sun and sheds rock/ice
proTips: Climbing as soon as the trailhead/road opens usually finds the couloir in its best, most continuous snow condition.
Two pickets and two ice screws cover most parties' needs; a light rock rack backs up sections where snow has thinned.
Watch for sun-loosened rock and ice fall by mid-morning.
watchOut: Conditions swing from a straightforward snow slog to thin, technical mixed climbing depending on season and recent weather.
A cornice can guard the top and has taken over an hour to surmount in some conditions.
Soft, punchy snow on the Colchuck Glacier descent has caught out tired parties late in the day.
knownHazards: Rockfall and ice debris shed from sun-warmed rock above the couloir.
Occasional serious cornice at the couloir's top.
Crevasse hazard on the Colchuck Glacier during the descent.

pitchDetail.notes: From the far side of Colchuck Lake, hike up and gain the moraine, follow it right to the couloir entrance at the glacier's edge.
Climb steep snow filling the gully.
As snow thins later in the season, a few short Class 4 rock steps are exposed.
Top-out can be guarded by a cornice that has required over an hour to breach in some reported conditions.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road/FR 7600) to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.status: Rough, steep gravel road; high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring; the route is often climbed as soon as this gate opens
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road ~8.4 mi to the FR 7601 junction near Bridge Creek Campground, then FR 7601 ~3.7 mi to Stuart Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central zone
climate.typical: East-slope Cascades rain-shadow climate with cold, snowy winters and a spring melt window that this route is timed around.
climate.spring: Prime season — the couloir is typically continuously snow-filled and 'in great shape' in April per trip reports.
climate.summer: Snow recedes and the route can expose Class 4 rock steps; considered less in-condition.
climate.fall: Early-season snow can be thin/unconsolidated; road access (FR 7601) also closes by fall.
climate.winter: Cold with heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard; FR 7601 is gated, complicating access.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass required for trailhead parking; day-use wilderness permit is free and self-issued.
access.permit: Overnight camping within the Enchantment Permit Area (Colchuck zone) requires a permit May 15-Oct 31, allocated via a recreation.gov lottery plus limited walk-in permits.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass
access.closures: FR 7601 is gated and closed to vehicles mid-fall through spring; many parties time this climb to just after the gate opens.

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start to climb the couloir on firm snow before it softens

routes.corrections: Given data's top-level grade text is 'IV,' but Mountain Project, SummitPost, and multiple trip reports (a Mountaineers program page and independent trip-report blogs) describe the North Buttress Couloir as a moderate Grade II-III steep-snow/easy-mixed couloir, not a serious Grade IV commitment route — one secondhand reference to Beckey's guide even calls upper terrain west of the crest 'Grade II, Class 3&4.' No primary source checked confirms Grade IV, so commitment was left null rather than asserting a specific number; alpineGrade='D'/iceGrade='AI3' were kept as plausible and not clearly contradicted. Recommend a human check against Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide directly.

hierarchyNote: This is not a standalone summit but a small shaded rock-climbing sector ("Ice Box, Left Side") within the "Hairpin Crags" formation at Washington Pass, North Cascades — confirmed via Mountain Project's location tree (Washington > Northwest Region > Hwy 20 & North Cascades National Park > N Cascades > Washington Pass > Hairpin Crags > Ice box left side) with GPS 48.51344, -120.64594 at ~5,490 ft, consistent with Washington Pass's documented elevation (~5,477 ft, Wikipedia) on the Chelan/Okanogan county line. No name/location mismatch found. Peakbagger/SummitPost have no dedicated entry for it because it is a minor crag sector rather than a named summit — expected at this scale, not an error.
blurb: Ice Box (Left Side) is a small, permanently shaded granite crag on the Hairpin Crags formation at Washington Pass in the North Cascades, reached via a steep dry-creek/slab approach above the highway's hairpin turn on SR-20. Bryan Burdo (with Gabe Grayum credited on Iceman) established its three moderate single-pitch routes, documented in the Burdo/Grayum Mazama Granite guide; the shade makes it a good warm-weather complement to the sunnier faces nearby, with climbs ranging 5.9–5.10c.

routes.fa: Bryan Burdo, Gabe Grayum (first ascent credit uncertain/unconfirmed per Mountain Project)
objHaz: Smooth, slippery approach slabs above the dry creek bed (fall hazard, worse when wet)
Loose rock possible on unfeatured alpine granite
routes.face: Ice Box (Left Side), Hairpin Crags, Washington Pass
routes.overview: A one-pitch 5.10a/b line up the shaded Ice Box (Left Side) buttress, the 'dead middle' finger-crack/roof line splitting sport routes Route C and Route D.
routes.beta: Climbs a steep face on good holds to a bolt guarding a thin finger-crack move, then finishes on horizontal cracks into an awkward but enjoyable left-facing corner. Mixed protection (3 bolts plus small-to-mid cams) makes it the trad line of the three Ice Box routes.
routes.approach: From the obvious hairpin turn on SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) near Washington Pass, follow the climbers' trail toward Quicksilver Slab and Spire Gully, then cut right into a dry creek bed. Ascend a series of granite slabs (one fitted with a fixed hand-line) to reach the shaded Ice Box (Left Side) buttress. The lower slab has no hand-line and both slabs are smooth and slippery, especially when wet or dewy — exercise caution.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel from the anchor back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the top anchor, lower on a single strand or rappel with a 60m rope directly back to the base of the route; no walk-off is required for this single-pitch line.
routes.itinerary: A single-day cragging objective, typically combined with Route C and/or Route D at the same crag: drive to the hairpin turn on SR-20, approach via the slab/creek-bed trail, climb, then lower/rappel and retrace the approach to the car.
routes.bail: Straightforward retreat on a single-pitch route — lower or rappel off the highest gear/bolt placed at any point.
routes.turnaround: Start early enough to be off the exposed approach slabs and rock before afternoon thunderstorms, which are common in the North Cascades in summer; treat the slick granite slabs as more hazardous in rain.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, matching the broader Washington Pass climbing season; the Ice Box's shaded, north-facing character keeps it cooler than sunnier Washington Pass walls and can hold wet rock or residual snow later into the early season.
routes.comms: No cell phone service is available at Washington Pass or along this stretch of the North Cascades Highway corridor per NPS and WSDOT; do not rely on a signal for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: 3 fixed bolts protect the crux face and finger-crack section; supplement with a small rack of cams from about BD #0.3 to #1 for the horizontal-crack and corner finish. A single 60m rope and standard quickdraws/alpine draws are sufficient for this one-pitch route.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed protection — 3 bolts plus a handful of small-to-medium cams (0.3-1 BD equivalent) placed in the horizontal cracks and finishing corner.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suited to slick granite slabs
proTips: Good handholds on the initial face make the opening moves easier than they look — save gear placements for after the bolt-protected finger-crack crux.
Combine with neighboring Route C and Route D for a full day at this shaded, cooler crag.
watchOut: The approach slabs are smooth and slippery, and the first slab encountered has no hand-line to grasp.
Finger-crack crux section is a pumpy, sustained move — read it before committing.
knownHazards: Slick approach slabs pose a fall hazard on the hike in, independent of the climbing itself.
Alpine granite setting means occasional loose rock off-route.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep face on good holds to a bolt protecting a thin finger-crack section, then horizontal cracks into a left-facing corner finish.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Paved two-lane state highway; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closed roughly early December through mid/late April between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP134) and the Silver Star/Early Winters gates (MP171/178) due to snowpack and avalanche hazard; closure points can shift to lower elevations (Newhalem/Early Winters gates) later in the season.
road.driveNote: Parking is a roadside pullout at the obvious hairpin turn on SR-20 near Washington Pass, reached from Mazama/Winthrop to the east or Newhalem/Marblemount to the west.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Washington Pass corridor (East Slopes Northern Cascades)
climate.typical: Dry, warm alpine summers with afternoon thunderstorm risk; the shaded Ice Box stays notably cooler than sun-exposed Washington Pass faces.
climate.spring: Highway typically still gated or freshly reopened (mid-to-late April); snow and wet rock likely at the crag into early summer.
climate.summer: Best climbing window, late June through September; dry granite but watch for afternoon convective storms.
climate.fall: Climbable into September/early October with shortening days and increasing storm risk before the highway's winter closure.
climate.winter: SR-20 is gated closed; the area is inaccessible by road.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: No fee documented specifically for the hairpin turn roadside pullout; nearby Forest Service trailheads (Blue Lake, Cutthroat Lake, Rainy Pass) require a Northwest Forest Pass or $5 Recreation.gov day pass.
access.permit: No permit reported required for this roadside-pullout approach; unclear whether a Northwest Forest Pass applies at the hairpin pullout itself (unlike the marked trailheads nearby) — carry one if uncertain.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful pass accepted at official Forest Service trailheads in the area
access.closures: Access is entirely dependent on SR-20 being open; the highway is gated closed in winter (see road status).

routes.rappels: 1 lower/rappel from chain anchors (60m rope)
routes.fa: Bryan Burdo (first ascent credit uncertain/unconfirmed per Mountain Project)
objHaz: Smooth, slippery approach slabs above the dry creek bed (fall hazard, worse when wet)
routes.face: Ice Box (Left Side), Hairpin Crags, Washington Pass — third bolt line right of the prow
routes.overview: A fully bolted one-pitch 5.10b/c sport route, the third bolt line right of the prow at Ice Box (Left Side).
routes.beta: Juggy steps lead into a slappy, very strenuous left-facing corner to a rest ledge, then a steep face on good holds finishes the pitch.
routes.approach: From the obvious hairpin turn on SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) near Washington Pass, follow the climbers' trail toward Quicksilver Slab and Spire Gully, then cut right into a dry creek bed. Ascend a series of granite slabs (one fitted with a fixed hand-line) to reach the shaded Ice Box (Left Side) buttress. The lower slab has no hand-line and both slabs are smooth and slippery, especially when wet or dewy.
routes.descent: Lower off the chain anchors.
routes.descentText: Lower on a single strand from the fixed chain anchor at the top of the route, or rappel with a 60m rope; no walk-off is required.
routes.itinerary: A single-day cragging objective, commonly linked with Route C and/or Iceman at the same crag: drive to the hairpin turn on SR-20, approach via the slab/creek-bed trail, climb, then lower off and retrace the approach to the car.
routes.bail: Lower off any bolt at any point — fully bolted sport route with straightforward retreat.
routes.turnaround: Start early enough to be off the exposed approach slabs before afternoon thunderstorms, common in the North Cascades in summer; the slick granite slabs are more hazardous in rain.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, matching the broader Washington Pass season; the Ice Box's shaded character keeps it cooler and can hold wet rock later into early season than sunnier walls nearby.
routes.comms: No cell phone service is available at Washington Pass or along this stretch of the North Cascades Highway corridor per NPS and WSDOT; do not rely on a signal for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted with 9 bolts to chain anchors — carry roughly 9-10 quickdraws and a 60m rope; no traditional gear rack needed.
routes.proNeeds: Sport-bolted line; no trad gear required beyond quickdraws.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suited to slick granite slabs
proTips: The corner section is short but very strenuous for the grade — commit through it rather than pausing on it.
Pairs well with Route C on the same wall for a two-route day.
watchOut: The left-facing corner is described as 'very strenuous' relative to its grade — don't underestimate it.
Approach slabs are smooth and slippery, and the lower slab has no hand-line.
knownHazards: Slick approach slabs pose a fall hazard on the hike in, independent of the climbing itself.

pitchDetail.notes: Juggy start into a slappy, very strenuous left-facing corner to a rest ledge, then a steep face on good holds to the chain anchor.

routes.beta: A well-bolted, slightly steep climb on jug holds. Climbers can follow the bolt line straight to the chains, or after clipping the 5th bolt, traverse right onto a solid horn and back left for a mellower ledge variation.

routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted with 8 bolts to chain anchors — carry roughly 8-9 quickdraws and a 60m rope; no traditional gear rack needed.
routes.proNeeds: Sport-bolted line; no trad gear required beyond quickdraws.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suited to slick granite slabs
proTips: Good introductory grade for the crag — jug-hauling makes it approachable for climbers newer to steep terrain.
Use the traverse-right variation after bolt 5 for a mellower finish if the direct bolt line feels stiff.
watchOut: Approach slabs are smooth and slippery, and the lower slab has no hand-line.
Slightly steep terrain on jugs can still pump out less-conditioned climbers.
knownHazards: Slick approach slabs pose a fall hazard on the hike in, independent of the climbing itself.

pitchDetail.notes: Well-bolted, slightly steep climb on jugs; either follow the bolt line straight to the chains, or after bolt 5 traverse right onto a solid horn and back left for a mellower ledge variation.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Paved two-lane state highway; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closed roughly early December through mid/late April between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP134) and the Silver Star/Early Winters gates (MP171/178) due to snowpack and avalanche hazard; closure points can shift to lower elevations later in the season.
road.driveNote: Parking is a roadside pullout at the obvious hairpin turn on SR-20 near Washington Pass, reached from Mazama/Winthrop to the east or Newhalem/Marblemount to the west.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Washington Pass corridor (East Slopes Northern Cascades)
climate.typical: Dry, warm alpine summers with afternoon thunderstorm risk; the shaded Ice Box stays notably cooler than sun-exposed Washington Pass faces.
climate.spring: Highway typically still gated or freshly reopened (mid-to-late April); snow and wet rock likely at the crag into early summer.
climate.summer: Best climbing window, late June through September; dry granite but watch for afternoon convective storms.
climate.fall: Climbable into September/early October before the highway's winter closure.
climate.winter: SR-20 is gated closed; the area is inaccessible by road.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: No fee documented specifically for the hairpin turn roadside pullout; nearby Forest Service trailheads (Blue Lake, Cutthroat Lake, Rainy Pass) require a Northwest Forest Pass or $5 Recreation.gov day pass.
access.permit: No permit reported required for this roadside-pullout approach; unclear whether a Northwest Forest Pass applies at the hairpin pullout itself — carry one if uncertain.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful pass accepted at official Forest Service trailheads in the area
access.closures: Access is entirely dependent on SR-20 being open; the highway is gated closed in winter.

emergency.county: Chelan/Okanogan county line (Washington Pass straddles the two)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff non-emergency: 509-422-7232 / 800-572-6604; Chelan County Sheriff: 509-667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA; (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA, or Mid-Valley Hospital, Omak, WA (both roughly 30 mi from Winthrop, the nearest town)
emergency.notes: No cell coverage at Washington Pass — plan for a satellite communicator; the highway is remote with no services in the immediate area.

blurb: South Face is a broad, SSW-facing apron of clean, low-angle granite slab on Squire Creek Wall, a remote big-wall formation in the Boulder River Wilderness above Darrington, Washington. It sits at the eastern end of the wall's south side, which is divided into several large, low-angled buttresses reached only by an unmaintained trail and a multi-hour bushwhack/scramble approach. Its routes were established between 2012 and 2019 by local developers David Whitelaw, Bill Enger, Yale Lewis, Jim Daubert, and Jake Larson, who hand-drilled all protection bolts because the wall lies within designated Wilderness where power drills are prohibited.
hierarchyNote: "South Face" is a directional sub-wall (an SSW-facing buttress complex; Mountain Project lists its own reference point at ~3,249 ft) of the larger Squire Creek Wall massif — it is not itself a separate named summit. The true high point of the Squire Creek Wall formation is documented at 4,958 ft by Peakbagger and ListsOfJohn (coordinates ~48.209, -121.630, near the shared trailhead/pass area). The given parent hierarchy (Squire Creek Wall > Darrington & Mountain Loop Hwy > Darrington, WA, Boulder River Wilderness, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF) is geographically correct and matches Mountain Project's area tree — no placement error found. Flagging only that elevationFt here reflects the South Face sub-wall's own reference elevation, not the massif's true 4,958 ft summit, since routes describing a "summit crest" finish likely top out on a buttress crest below the true high point rather than the actual peak.

routes.fa: 8/21/2017, Bill Enger, Yale Lewis, Jim Daubert
routes.rappels: Rappel the route (5 pitches) with two 60m ropes
objHaz: hand-drilled bolts can be hard to spot in shaded corners
routes.face: South Face, Squire Creek Wall
routes.overview: A 5-pitch, 700-ft moderate slab line up the South Face with two distinct crux pitches sandwiched between easier friction climbing above and below.
routes.beta: Pitch 2 is a lieback/stemming crux; pitch 3 is a delicate smearing finger traverse; the upper two pitches ease into pleasant smearing and cruising to the top. Protection mixes hand-drilled bolts on the faces with gear in the dihedral/crack sections.
routes.approach: From the Squire Creek trailhead, follow Squire Creek Trail #654 (FS Rd 2040 access) to a ford of Squire Creek, then climb through old-growth forest via two gullies to a grassy saddle below the South Face (~3 hrs). From the saddle, head left/upward toward the Skeena26 line, then negotiate a class-4 scramble (smearing moves, grooves) up to a white tree stump near the route start. Total approach is roughly 4 hours from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Rappel the route
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the line of ascent using the fixed hand-drilled bolt anchors; two 60m ropes recommended.
routes.turnaround: There is no quick bail on this route once established above the saddle scramble; begin early enough to finish the climb, rappel, and reverse the multi-hour approach before dark, and turn back if thunderstorms are building given the exposed, low-angle slab.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer into early fall (roughly July-September); the Boulder River Wilderness holds snow at elevation into July and can see new snow return by October, per USFS trail data.
routes.detailedRack: Single standard rack to 4 inches, about a dozen quickdraws plus a few longer/extended ones, and two 60m ropes for the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Protection alternates between hand-drilled 3/8" stainless bolts on the friction/face sections and natural gear (cams/nuts) in the dihedral and crack features; no power-drilled bolts exist here since the route lies within Wilderness.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suited to a rough, brushy 3-4 hr approach with a creek ford
proTips: Bolts are hand-drilled per Wilderness rules and can be hard to spot in shaded corners — carry a topo or route photo
Combine the approach with Skeena26 or Doorway Flake beta since all share the same grassy-saddle start
watchOut: Class-4 scramble section above the saddle exposes climbers before the roped pitches begin
Squire Creek ford can be hazardous at high water
knownHazards: Steep, brushy, poorly maintained approach trail with stream crossings and old avalanche/rockslide debris
Remote location extends any self-rescue or evacuation timeline

pitchDetail.notes: Lower-angle start climbing off the saddle scramble
Crux pitch: lieback and stemming moves
Crux pitch: delicate smearing on an exposed finger traverse
Pleasant smearing and easier cruising to the top

routes.routeName: Concerto in C for Drill and Hammer
routes.fa: 9/7/2014, David Whitelaw, Bill Enger, Jake Larson
routes.rappels: Rappel the route; several 60m rappels, with parties generally rapping to the Pitch 13 chain near the top
objHaz: long, sustained friction-slab climbing with runout sections
hand-drilled bolts can be hard to spot in shaded corners
routes.face: South Face, Squire Creek Wall
routes.overview: A long, 14-pitch alpine slab climb from a bivy bench up to the summit ridge, featuring sustained 5.8 friction on several pitches and a crusty ridge-running finish above Buckeye Basin.
routes.beta: Begins on a wide granite apron with pleasant knobs and good crack protection low down; the crux 'Middle C' section (roughly pitches 7-10) climbs sustained 5.8 friction slab, including a runout quartz-dike pitch with small stair-step edges. Above, the route passes the 'Bubba Compactor' ledge and an 'Inverted Prow' dihedral before ridge-running to the top, with views over Buckeye Basin to Darrington.
routes.approach: From the grassy saddle reached via Squire Creek Trail #654 and the standard South Face approach (Squire Creek ford, old-growth gullies, ~3 hrs from the trailhead), scramble down granite ramps 20-30 m across the apron, then walk the 'sidewalk' to a two-bolt anchor at a short vertical wall; the distinctive Africa Flake marks the route line about 35 m upslope.
routes.descent: Rappel the route
routes.descentText: Rappel the line of ascent on fixed chain/bolt anchors; several rappels require a 60m rope, and parties typically rappel to the Pitch 13 chain anchor near the summit before continuing down.
routes.turnaround: With 14 pitches and a multi-hour approach, this is a full-day undertaking with no fast bail; start early enough to top out, rappel, and hike out before dark, and retreat at the first sign of building thunderstorms on the exposed upper ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Best mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September); the Boulder River Wilderness holds snow into July and can see new snow by October.
routes.detailedRack: Standard single rack to 3 inches, supplemented by hand-drilled 3/8" stainless-steel wedge bolts with modern hangers on the friction sections; anchors are mostly fixed stainless chain (some tree anchors on the upper pitches).
routes.proNeeds: A mix of gear placements in cracks low on the route and bolt protection through the sustained friction-slab crux pitches (7-10); the quartz-dike pitch is noted as runout between bolts.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for a rough, 3+ hr approach with a creek ford
Extra water — no reliable water source on the upper wall
proTips: The Africa Flake, about 35 m above the two-bolt base anchor, marks the true start of the route
Plan on 60m rappels for the descent and expect to rap to the Pitch 13 chain near the top
watchOut: Runout, small-hold friction climbing on the quartz-dike pitch (pitch 7)
Rope drag noted on pitch 14 — manage rope management on the ridge-running finish
knownHazards: Sustained low-angle friction slab is unforgiving if wet
Steep, brushy, poorly maintained approach with stream crossings and old rockslide/avalanche debris

pitchDetail.notes: Wide apron start with pleasant knobs and good crack protection
Sustained friction slab, described as 'stellar friction moves'
Clean thin crack usable with nuts and small cams
Crux 'Middle C' section: runout quartz dike with small stair-step edges
Continuation of sustained friction-slab crux climbing on small knobs
Inverted Prow dihedral feature, one key bolt placement
Ridge-running finish to the top; tree anchors historically used, later upgraded; rope-drag on pitch 14

routes.fa: 9/17/2012, David Whitelaw, Bill Enger, Yale Lewis
routes.rappels: Rappel the route; 50m ropes suffice for most pitches, 60m ropes allow combining pitches 5-6
objHaz: bolts moderately spaced and hard to spot in shade
water/seepage reported on pitch 7 even in season
routes.face: South Face, Squire Creek Wall
routes.overview: A 12-pitch, 1,500-ft friction climb on immaculate, knobby grey granite, with a polished 5.9 crux on pitch one easing into mostly 5.4-5.6 climbing above, finishing on clean splitter cracks overlooking Whitehorse Mountain.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 climbs a narrow 'whale's back' of stone between facing corners on polished friction, protected by nine bolts, and holds the route's crux. Subsequent pitches ease to 5.4-5.6 with occasional 5.8 sections. The final pitch features clean splitter cracks (5.7) finishing on the buttress crest with views over Whitehorse Mountain.
routes.approach: Via the standard South Face approach (Squire Creek Trail #654, ford, old-growth gullies to the grassy saddle, ~3 hrs), then continue left (west) along the wall for about 5 minutes to a smaller saddle, and scramble up grass into a minor gully to the start of the route.
routes.descent: Rappel the route
routes.descentText: Rappel the line of ascent; 50-meter ropes are adequate for most pitches, while 60-meter ropes allow combining pitches 5 and 6 to speed the descent.
routes.turnaround: With 12 pitches and a multi-hour approach, treat this as a full-day objective with no quick bail; start early enough to finish and hike out before dark, and back off if water/seepage or building weather make the polished friction pitches unsafe.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September); trip reports note lingering water/seepage on pitch 7 even in-season, and the Boulder River Wilderness holds snow at elevation into July.
routes.detailedRack: A light single rack to 3 inches: BD Stoppers (#5-8), Black Diamond Camalot C3 (#1-2), Camalot C4 (0.5 to #3), plus 5 quickdraws and 5 alpine draws; hand-drilled stainless-steel wedge bolts protect the crux friction sections.
routes.proNeeds: Bolt-protected on the polished pitch-1 crux (9 bolts) and other friction sections, with natural gear placements filling in on lower-angle pitches and the final splitter-crack pitch.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for a rough, 3 hr approach with a creek ford
proTips: Bolts are moderately spaced (10-20 ft) and can be extremely difficult to spot in shade — scout ahead and carry a topo
50m ropes work for most pitches; bring 60m to combine pitches 5-6 on the descent
watchOut: Polished friction holds on the pitch-1 crux, especially when damp
Water still running on pitch 7 has been reported even in season
knownHazards: Steep, brushy, poorly maintained approach with a creek ford and old rockslide/avalanche debris
Remote location extends any self-rescue or evacuation timeline

pitchDetail.notes: Crux: polished friction along a narrow whale's-back of stone between facing corners, 9 bolts
Easier friction climbing on brilliant white, knobby granite
Can be combined on rappel with 60m ropes
Water/seepage reported here even in-season per trip reports

routes.fa: 6/19/2019, Bill Enger, Yale Lewis
routes.rappels: Rappel the route (5 pitches) with two 60m ropes
objHaz: hand-drilled bolts can be hard to spot in shaded corners
routes.face: South Face / Far Western Wall area, Squire Creek Wall
routes.overview: A 5-pitch, 700-ft route on the Far Western Wall reaching a distinctive hanging flake, with sculpted-knob face climbing low down and a bolt-protected 5.9 face crux on pitch two.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 (5.8) climbs sculpted knobs to a hanging flake with stemming and crack moves. Pitch 2 (5.9) is the crux — bolt-protected face climbing. Pitch 3 (5.7) follows a clean crack system, and pitches 4-5 (5.6) ease through dihedrals and slab to the top.
routes.approach: From the South Face grassy saddle (reached via Squire Creek Trail #654, the creek ford, and old-growth gullies, ~3 hrs from the trailhead), traverse left along the wall and climb 4th-class terrain to reach the Far Western Wall; the flake sits about 6 m above the bivy site across a talus field. Total approach is roughly 4 hours from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Rappel the route
routes.descentText: Rappel the line of ascent using the fixed hand-drilled bolt and Fixe chain anchors; two 60m ropes recommended.
routes.turnaround: There is no quick bail once on route above the 4th-class approach scramble; start early enough to finish the climb, rappel, and reverse the multi-hour approach before dark, and back off if thunderstorms threaten the exposed face pitch.
routes.bestSeason: Best mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September); the Boulder River Wilderness holds snow into July and can see new snow by October.
routes.detailedRack: Single standard rack to 4 inches, about a dozen quickdraws (plus a few longer/extended ones), and two 60m ropes for the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Bolt protection on the pitch-2 face crux, with gear placements in the flake, crack, and dihedral sections on the remaining pitches; all bolts are hand-drilled per Wilderness rules.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for a rough, 4 hr approach with a creek ford and 4th-class scrambling
proTips: The 4th-class approach traverse to the Far Western Wall is exposed — move carefully with packs on
Bring a topo; hand-drilled bolts can be hard to spot in shade
watchOut: Exposed 4th-class scrambling before roping up
Bolt-protected crux face pitch (2) — inspect conditions before committing
knownHazards: Steep, brushy, poorly maintained approach with stream crossings and old rockslide/avalanche debris
Remote location extends any self-rescue or evacuation timeline

hierarchyNote: This is a small bolted sport-climbing wall (not a standalone summit) inside Hairpin Crags, a roadside crag area at Washington Pass on the North Cascades Highway (SR-20). Confirmed via Mountain Project's breadcrumb (Washington > Northwest Region > Hwy 20 & North Cascades National Park > N Cascades > Washington Pass > Hairpin Crags > Ice box right side) and consistent GPS clustering with the sibling "Ice box left side" wall and the parent Hairpin Crags area, all within ~300m of one another near the SR-20 hairpin switchback. Because it is a crag/wall rather than a named summit, it has no dedicated SummitPost or Peakbagger page — those sites instead cover the nearby named peaks (Liberty Bell Group) in the same Washington Pass corridor, which corroborates the general location. The parent hierarchy (North Cascades / Washington Pass) is geographically correct; no mismatch found.
blurb: Ice Box right side is a compact granite wall within Hairpin Crags, a roadside sport-climbing area at Washington Pass on the North Cascades Highway (SR-20) in the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. Reached via a short trail and slab scramble into a shady, moisture-holding chasm above the highway's namesake hairpin turn, the crag's name reflects how much longer it retains dampness than surrounding rock. All five routes here — rough, water-polished granite bolted lines — were established by Gabe Grayum in 2017.

routes.fa: Gabe Grayum, September 2017
routes.face: Ice Box right side (farthest-left route on the wall)
routes.overview: The four-star testpiece of the crag: a steep, technical single-pitch sport line up water-polished granite on the left side of the Ice Box right side wall.
routes.beta: Starts on water-polished rock, pulls a small roof, works through a thin crux, then hangs on through a leftward traverse on good holds to the bolted anchors.
routes.approach: From the SR-20 hairpin switchback just east of Washington Pass, follow the use-trail up toward the obvious chasm/drainage below Hairpin Crags, staying right of the drainage. Cross left below the slab, then scramble 3rd-class up along the left margin of the slabs into the Ice Box amphitheater. About 15 minutes from the car.
routes.descent: Lower off the bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: Single-pitch sport route; lower directly from the two-bolt anchor at the top of the pitch back to the base. No walk-off or rappel needed.
routes.bestSeason: This wall holds moisture longer than most of the crag and doesn't fully dry out until late summer; plan for July through September, later than nearby left-side routes, for best conditions.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage along the SR-20 corridor at Washington Pass; do not count on a phone signal.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted; no natural gear needed.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted single pitch; a standard sport rack of about 8-10 quickdraws is sufficient to protect the 60 ft (18 m) route, plus a rope long enough to lower a climber that distance.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suitable for a 3rd-class slab scramble
Extra layer — the wall stays shady/cool
proTips: Check recent conditions before an early-season trip — this route is one of the last at the crag to dry out.
watchOut: Route can still be wet/mossy into early summer at this shady wall.
knownHazards: Water-polished holds can be slick when damp or in shade.

pitchDetail.notes: Water-polished start, small roof pull, thin crux, leftward traverse on good holds to anchors.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Paved two-lane highway; closed seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed roughly mid/late-November through April between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and Silver Star Gate (MP 171), spanning Rainy Pass and Washington Pass; exact dates vary yearly with snowpack (historically as early as Oct 17 and as late as Jan 3).
road.driveNote: Park in the roadside pullout at the prominent hairpin switchback on SR-20 just east of Washington Pass.

climate.typical: High mountain-pass climate at roughly 5,300 ft in the North Cascades; warm, dry summer days, cool nights, and a chance of afternoon thunderstorms; heavy snow and highway closure in winter.
climate.spring: SR-20 is often still closed or just reopening through Washington Pass; snow and moisture can linger on the approach and wall.
climate.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); this shady wall dries out later than surrounding rock in the same area.
climate.fall: Good conditions into Sept until the highway closes for the season.
climate.winter: Inaccessible — SR-20 is closed at Washington Pass.

access.passRequired: No pass reported for the roadside hairpin pullout (unlike the Blue Lake/Cutthroat Lake/Rainy Pass trailheads on the west side of the pass, which require a Northwest Forest Pass or Recreation.gov day pass).
access.closures: Inaccessible in winter when SR-20 closes through Washington Pass.
