emergency.nearestHospital: Mid-Valley Hospital, Omak, WA (~29 mi) or Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (~31 mi)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent along SR-20 through Washington Pass; Okanogan County Search and Rescue is dispatched via 911 through the Sheriff's Office.

routes.fa: Gabe Grayum, August 2017
routes.face: Ice Box right side
routes.overview: A moderate single-pitch sport route up the Ice Box right side wall, starting off a boulder in the creek bed right of an arching corner feature.
routes.beta: A thin crux comes in the first couple of bolts, with the third bolt set higher than ideal due to hollow rock near that stance. Above this, the climbing eases with slightly spaced bolts leading to the top of the wall.
routes.approach: From the SR-20 hairpin switchback just east of Washington Pass, follow the use-trail up toward the obvious chasm/drainage below Hairpin Crags, staying right of the drainage. Cross left below the slab, then scramble 3rd-class up along the left margin of the slabs into the Ice Box amphitheater. About 15 minutes from the car.
routes.descent: Lower off the bolted anchors.
routes.descentText: Single-pitch sport route; lower directly from the anchor at the top of the pitch back to the base. No walk-off or rappel needed.
routes.bestSeason: Best from July through September; the route was first established during smoky, late-summer wildfire-season conditions, so check regional air-quality/smoke conditions for late-summer trips.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage along the SR-20 corridor at Washington Pass; do not count on a phone signal.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted sport climbing; no natural protection needed.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted single pitch; roughly 10-12 quickdraws are enough to protect the 80 ft (24 m) route, plus a rope long enough to lower that distance.
whatToBring: Approach shoes suitable for a 3rd-class slab scramble
Consider a stick clip for the high third bolt
proTips: The third bolt is set higher than usual due to hollow rock at that stance — be ready for extra runout low on the route or bring a stick clip.
watchOut: High third bolt increases fall potential in the lower section before it.
knownHazards: Route sits in a shady, moisture-holding drainage; late-summer wildfire smoke has affected the area in the past (route was first climbed during smoky conditions).

pitchDetail.notes: Starts off a boulder in the creek bed right of an arching corner; thin crux in first couple bolts, high 3rd bolt due to hollow rock, then easier climbing on spaced bolts to the top.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Paved two-lane highway; closed seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed roughly mid/late-November through April between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and Silver Star Gate (MP 171), spanning Rainy Pass and Washington Pass; exact dates vary yearly with snowpack (historically as early as Oct 17 and as late as Jan 3).
road.driveNote: Park in the roadside pullout at the prominent hairpin switchback on SR-20 just east of Washington Pass.

climate.typical: High mountain-pass climate at roughly 5,300 ft in the North Cascades; warm, dry summer days, cool nights, and a chance of afternoon thunderstorms; heavy snow and highway closure in winter.
climate.spring: SR-20 is often still closed or just reopening through Washington Pass; snow and moisture can linger on the approach.
climate.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep); can be affected by wildfire smoke in late summer.
climate.fall: Good conditions into Sept until the highway closes for the season.
climate.winter: Inaccessible — SR-20 is closed at Washington Pass.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.passRequired: No pass reported for the roadside hairpin pullout (unlike the Blue Lake/Cutthroat Lake/Rainy Pass trailheads on the west side of the pass, which require a Northwest Forest Pass or Recreation.gov day pass).
access.closures: Inaccessible in winter when SR-20 closes through Washington Pass.

blurb: Cutthroat Creek Wall — nicknamed "Little Liberty Bell" — is a small granite formation in the Cutthroat Creek drainage on the east side of Washington Pass in the North Cascades, informally named for its resemblance to the much larger Liberty Bell Mountain visible from the highway. It shares the Cutthroat Trailhead approach with the neighboring, larger Cutthroat Wall but is a distinct, far less-visited crag with only two documented technical routes as of 2020.
hierarchyNote: This is an informal, minor rock formation, not an officially named USGS summit — there is no Peakbagger or SummitPost entry for "Little Liberty Bell" (those sites only cover the true, much larger Liberty Bell Mountain several miles away in the same Washington Pass group). Its name also creates real confusion risk with two other unrelated same-drainage/same-prefix features that must not be conflated with it: Cutthroat Peak (a genuine named summit with its own Mountain Project area, id 109326648) and Cutthroat Wall (a separate, larger granite buttress with 6-8 pitches, its own Mountain Project area id 113436052, reached via the same Cutthroat Trailhead). One secondhand CascadeClimbers.com forum comment referred to this specific wall by the alternate nickname "First Light Wall"/"First Light Spire," but this is unconfirmed by any primary source and should be treated as low-confidence. The area's coordinates (48.54975, -120.6435) and ~6,263 ft elevation (Mountain Project's area pin) are geographically consistent with a small crag on the Cutthroat Creek approach east of Washington Pass, so the hierarchy placement itself looks correct — the flag here is about the peak's informal/non-summit status and name-collision risk, not a location error.

routes.fa: Chris Wright & Mike Pond, 2009
routes.rappels: 4 double-rope (60m) rappels down the route line
routes.overview: A 4-pitch, 5.11 crack-and-corner testpiece up the previously unclimbed formation known as Little Liberty Bell, following a central left-facing arch and corner system to a treed exit near the top of the wall.
routes.beta: High-quality North Cascades granite with a strenuous pinch-crux corner low, sustained thin/technical face and stemming through the middle, and an easier blocky finish. Described on Mountain Project as "the only line on the wall" prior to the 2020 Narcos route, and as seldom repeated since its 2009 first ascent.
routes.approach: Drive Highway 20 to about 4.5 miles east of Washington Pass and turn onto Forest Road 400 (signed for Cutthroat Creek Trail); follow it 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead (parking and restroom, ~4,500 ft). Leave the maintained trail and hike up through open woods with no path, gaining elevation quickly, then traverse left across open forest and slabby benches to reach a shoulder at the base of the wall's right side — roughly 1 to 1.5 hours from the car.
routes.descent: Rappel the route: 4 double-rope raps on 60m ropes back down the line.
routes.descentText: From the top, descend via 4 consecutive double-rope rappels (60m ropes) directly down the Wright-Pond line, using the bolted belays established at the P1/P2 anchors along with intermediate stations.
routes.bail: Bolted anchors at the ends of P1 and P2 allow retreat by rappel from either station without needing to reach the top; the same anchors are used for the full descent.
routes.turnaround: No fixed turnaround time is published for this route. Because the approach and descent are unmarked, brushy, and route-find-y, and the line has few recorded repeats, parties should set their own turnaround based on remaining daylight, weather, and how comfortable they are down-climbing or rappelling unfamiliar, lightly-traveled terrain after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed late June through September, matching the season window for the adjacent, similarly-elevationed Cutthroat Wall crag reached from the same trailhead; snow/ice can linger on ledges into early season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service at the wall or on the approach; the nearest dependable coverage is generally back down near the North Cascades Highway corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles to a #3 Camalot, with an emphasis on small cams and wires; RPs are noted as useful for thin seams. Bring two 60m ropes for the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Gear-intensive corner and crack climbing throughout; small-to-mid cams (doubles to #3 Camalot) and a full range of wired stoppers cover most placements, with RPs helpful on the thinnest sections of the P1 arch/corner.
proTips: Small cams and wires see the most use — don't over-rack large gear.
Carry RPs specifically for the thin seams on the P1 arch/corner crux.
watchOut: PG13 protection rating — expect some balancy, less-protected face climbing, notably on the P2 bolted traverse.
Route is seldom repeated; expect route-finding uncertainty and possible loose rock/lichen off the beaten path.
knownHazards: Runout sections rated PG13
Unmarked, brushy approach and descent with no established trail

pitchDetail.notes: 55m. Climb the obvious left-facing arch into a corner with a strenuous pinch-move crux to a bolted belay at a ledge.
50m. Ascend a large flake into an open book with stemming and thin finger work, then traverse bolted face climbing on small edges and pockets to a bolted belay.
50m. Technical right-facing corner transitions into left-facing crack work, finishing at a treed ledge belay.
45m. Blocky corner/chimney leads to low-angle slab and a hand/fist crack exit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Seasonal — closed in winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically closes early-to-mid December at the Ross Dam (MP 134) and Silver Star (MP 171) gates and reopens in mid-spring; exact dates vary year to year with snow/avalanche conditions.
road.driveNote: From Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 about 28 miles (or 1.5 miles west of Lone Fir Campground) and turn right on Forest Road 400; follow it 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

climate.typical: East-slope North Cascades rain-shadow climate near Washington Pass: warm, generally dry summer days with cool nights.
climate.spring: Snow and wet rock likely persist into June at this ~6,000+ ft elevation.
climate.summer: Prime season, late June through September — warmest and driest window.
climate.fall: Climbable into early-mid October in dry years, but nights turn cold.
climate.winter: Not accessible — SR-20 is gated for the season roughly December through April/May.

access.permit: No climbing permit required; day-use fee applies at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or other Interagency recreation pass (Digital Day Pass accepted via Recreation.gov)
access.closures: Trailhead and approach are inaccessible whenever SR-20 is gated for winter.

waypoints.name: Cutthroat Creek Wall / Little Liberty Bell (area pin)

emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (critical access hospital serving the Methow Valley)

routes.fa: Michael Telstad, solo — July 2020
routes.overview: A 5-pitch, mostly-independent 5.9 line up low-angle ramps and corners on the same wall as Wright-Pond, joining that route's final two pitches to reach the top; documented on Mountain Project and CascadeClimbers as a "partial new route" since its upper section repeats existing climbing.
routes.beta: Starts up clean, low-angle crack systems on a big left-trending ramp, then works through bushy corners and a chimney/arete before merging into Wright-Pond's upper corner and finishing on that route's final blocky pitch. Rock quality is described as good, but cracks were noted as still bushy/dirty when first climbed, suggesting an early-ascent, not-yet-fully-cleaned line.
routes.approach: Access is via the Cutthroat Trailhead approach (shared with Wright-Pond and the adjacent Cutthroat Wall): from SR-20 about 4.5 miles east of Washington Pass, turn onto FR-400 and drive 1 mile to the trailhead. Walk the old roadbed about 1/4 mile (parallel to the Cutthroat Lake Trail) to a double cairn, then leave the road and climb up through the woods, staying on lower-angle terrain toward the base of the wall.
routes.descent: No separate descent line is documented; since Narcos's final two pitches join the Wright-Pond route, parties descend via Wright-Pond's rappel line.
routes.descentText: Because P4-P5 merge into Wright-Pond's corner and final pitch, the standard descent is to rappel down the Wright-Pond line: 4 double-rope (60m) raps back to the base.
routes.bail: The upper pitches share bolted belay anchors with Wright-Pond (its P1/P2 stations), giving bail-out rappel options if needed; the lower, Narcos-specific pitches use gear belays only, so retreat there requires building gear anchors.
routes.turnaround: No fixed turnaround time is published. Given the unmarked approach, still-bushy cracks, and shared descent with a route that requires 4 rappels, parties should set a turnaround based on remaining daylight and comfort with route-finding and rappelling unfamiliar terrain in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed successfully in mid-July; likely best from late June through September like the adjacent Cutthroat Wall crag on the same approach, though early-season parties should expect bushier, dirtier cracks that clean up with more traffic.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service at the wall or on the approach; nearest dependable coverage is generally back down near the North Cascades Highway corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Double rack of micro cams up to #2, single #3 and #4 (optional #6 useful for pitch 3), a single set of medium nuts, and a wide piece ("Crack Pipe"-style device) for the chimney/offwidth sections. Two 60m ropes.
routes.proNeeds: Micro-to-mid cam doubles cover most placements; singles of #3/#4 (and optionally a #6) protect the wider chimney sections, backed up by a set of medium nuts.
whatToBring: A small digging/cleaning tool — cracks were noted as still bushy and benefit from cleaning
proTips: Bring a small tool for cleaning dirt/vegetation out of the cracks, especially early in the route's climbing life.
The final two pitches share Wright-Pond's line, so gear needs lighten up near the top.
watchOut: PG13-rated sections on P3 and P4 with limited protection.
Bushy, dirt-filled cracks, particularly lower on the route.
knownHazards: Vegetated/dirty cracks, especially in less-traveled lower pitches
Runout PG13 terrain on P3-P4
Loose rock possible given the route's limited traffic

pitchDetail.notes: Clean cracks up a big left-trending ramp to a tree; gear belay.
Traverse right along horizontal breaks, then climb edges and cracks to a finger crack; gear belay.
Bush-choked corner into flakes, then a chimney/arete passage; protected by one fixed piton.
Arching corner to flakes, then a right-facing corner that joins the Wright-Pond route; bolted belay.
Finishes via Wright-Pond's final pitch: blocky corner/chimney to low-angle slab and a hand/fist crack.

hierarchyNote: Old Snowy Mountain (7,929 ft) is confirmed as a real, well-documented peak by Wikipedia, PeakVisor, ListsOfJohn, and multiple trip-report sources — but it sits in the Goat Rocks Wilderness, part of the SOUTHERN Washington Cascades between Mt. Rainier and Mt. Adams (Lewis/Yakima county line, Gifford Pinchot National Forest), not the North Cascades. If this peak is filed under a "North Cascades" parent bucket in the DB hierarchy, that placement is geographically wrong and should be corrected to a South/Southern Cascades or Goat Rocks Wilderness parent. Given elevation (7,929 ft) matches ListsOfJohn and other sources closely and required no correction.
blurb: Old Snowy Mountain (7,929 ft) is a heavily-eroded remnant of an old shield/stratovolcano at the heart of the Goat Rocks Wilderness in Gifford Pinchot National Forest, on the Lewis–Yakima county line between Mt. Rainier and Mt. Adams. It carries the small McCall Glacier on its east flank and the Packwood Glacier just to its northwest, and the Pacific Crest Trail traverses its western slopes at over 7,600 ft — its high point in Washington. It is the most-visited summit in the wilderness thanks to a short, mostly non-technical scramble spur off the PCT, though the final ~50 ft to the summit block involves loose, exposed rock.

routes.face: South ridge / summit block above the PCT high point
routes.overview: An out-and-back scramble that follows the Pacific Crest Trail to its highest point in Washington (~7,650 ft) on Old Snowy's west flank, then climbs an unsigned boot path up the south side of the ridge to the loose, mildly exposed summit block.
routes.beta: From the Snowgrass Flats trailhead, hike ~4.7 miles through forest and meadows to the PCT, then follow the PCT north/west around the mountain's flank to its high point. Leave the PCT on a boot path signed (as of 2013) that switchbacks up scree/talus on Old Snowy's south side to the summit; the last ~50 feet is loose Class 2–3 rock with real but manageable exposure. An alternate line gains the col between Old Snowy and Ives Peak and follows the ridge crest/just-south-of-crest to the top.
routes.approach: Drive I-5 to Hwy 12, east ~62 miles to the Forest Road 21 junction (west of Packwood). Turn south on FR-21 and follow it uphill ~13 miles to FR-2150 (signed "Chambers Lake"); follow FR-2150 ~2 miles to the Snowgrass Flats trailhead (Trail #96), elev. ~4,700 ft. Fill out a free self-issued wilderness permit at the trailhead signboard, then hike Trail #96 through ~4 forested miles of gentle uphill, through meadows and pygmy forest, to join the PCT around mile 4.7 (~7,100 ft). Follow the PCT north as it climbs successive benches toward Old Snowy's west side.
routes.descent: Descend the same boot-path/PCT route back to Snowgrass Flats trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the loose Class 2–3 summit block carefully (rock is worse going down), rejoin the boot path down to the PCT high point (~7,650 ft), and follow the PCT south back to the Snowgrass Flat Trail junction (~mile 4.7), then descend Trail #96 back to the trailhead. Some parties instead continue north along the ridge on a knife-edge traverse toward Elk Pass (~6,700 ft) and loop back via the PCT for variety, adding mileage and exposure.
routes.bail: The route is entirely on/near the PCT until the final scramble; retreat at any point is simply reversing the boot path to the PCT and walking the PCT/Trail #96 back to the trailhead. There is no technical descent required to bail from the summit block.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting and stick to it — the exposed ridge and loose summit block are meaningfully more hazardous in afternoon thunderstorms, high wind, or when early-season snow is present, and the long trail approach means a late retreat can mean hiking out in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Snowgrass Flats sits at high elevation and typically doesn't fully melt out until mid-July; the route is normally in good condition mid-July through September, with early-season parties often finding lingering snowfields along the PCT and on the final approach that make poles or an ice axe useful. Late-season (into Oct) travel is possible but with more variable weather.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is used on the standard route — it is a trail hike to a Class 2–3 scramble finish. Trekking poles are commonly carried for the snowfields and loose talus.
whatToBring: Ice axe if snow lingers on the PCT or final approach (early season)
Map/GPS — the boot path to the summit is unsigned/informal
Plenty of water — no reliable water source at elevation
proTips: The 2013-signed spur trail off the PCT high point is the standard line — don't wander the ridge looking for a better line before checking there first.
The final summit block is loose in both directions; go slow and test holds, especially descending.
Views from the summit take in Rainier, Adams, and St. Helens on a clear day — worth timing for good visibility.
watchOut: Loose, exposed rock on the last ~50 feet below the summit
Lingering snowfields on the PCT and final approach into July
Afternoon thunderstorms typical of Cascades summer weather
Heavy trail traffic and fragile alpine meadows — stay on trail
knownHazards: Rockfall/loose talus on the summit scramble
Exposure on the knife-edge ridge toward Elk Pass if that variant is used
Snow/ice on shaded sections early in the season
No reliable water above the meadows

pitchDetail.notes: ~4.7 mi, gentle forest and meadow hiking to the Pacific Crest Trail.
Follow PCT around Old Snowy's west/south flank, gaining benches with Adams/Goat Rocks views.
Unsigned spur path off PCT high point; loose rock, mild exposure in the final ~50 ft to the true summit.

road.name: Forest Road 21 (Johnson Creek Rd) to FR-2150 (Chambers Lake)
road.status: Seasonal — open summer through early fall, closed by snow in winter/spring
road.seasonalGate: High-elevation approach; road/trailhead access is generally not reliable until snow clears, typically by mid-to-late June/July depending on year
road.driveNote: From Packwood, west ~3 mi on Hwy 12 to FR-21, then south/uphill ~13 mi to FR-2150 (signed "Chambers Lake"), then ~2 mi to the Snowgrass Flats trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC South Washington Cascades avalanche zone (winter); no avalanche forecast issued in normal summer climbing season
climate.typical: Typical Pacific Northwest high-Cascades summer weather: clear mornings with a chance of afternoon buildup/thunderstorms; can be windy and cold at the PCT high point even in summer.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack persists into early summer at this elevation; trail typically not accessible until snow recedes.
climate.summer: Prime season (mid-July–Sep); warm days, cool nights, possible afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: Good conditions into September/early October with more variable, colder weather and shorter days.
climate.winter: Road and trail inaccessible by vehicle; deep snowpack, avalanche terrain on approach slopes.

access.landManager: Gifford Pinchot National Forest (Cowlitz Valley Ranger District), Goat Rocks Wilderness
access.fees: No day-use fee reported at Snowgrass Flats trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass required at the alternate Berry Patch trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit, available at the trailhead signboard
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass not required at Snowgrass Flats trailhead (required at Berry Patch alternate trailhead)
access.closures: Seasonal road/trail closure by snowpack; check current Forest Service conditions before driving in

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, to be off the exposed summit before afternoon thunderstorms build

emergency.county: Lewis County (trailhead/approach side); summit sits on the Lewis–Yakima county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Lewis County Sheriff non-emergency: (360) 748-9286; Lewis County 911 Dispatch: (360) 740-1105
emergency.rangerStation: Cowlitz Valley Ranger District, 10024 US-12, Randle, WA — (360) 497-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Morton General Hospital (Arbor Health), 521 Adams Ave, Morton, WA 98356
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Goat Rocks backcountry; nearest towns with services are Packwood and Randle, both well below the trailhead on Hwy 12.

routes.corrections: None — the given elevation of 7,929 ft matches ListsOfJohn.com and other sourced figures (7,929–7,930 ft range across sources) closely; no change made. Wikipedia's rounded "7,880+ ft" figure is a contour-based approximation and less precise than the surveyed/LiDAR-derived value already in the DB.

blurb: Dorado Needle is an 8,440+ ft granite/gneiss spire in North Cascades National Park, about one mile north of Eldorado Peak, standing as the western high point where the McAllister and Inspiration Glaciers meet. Composed of granodioritic orthogneiss, it offers several moderate multi-pitch alpine rock routes (Northwest Ridge, East Ridge, Southwest Buttress) reached via a remote glacier approach from an Eldorado high camp. The peak was first climbed via its Northwest Ridge in July 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, and Tom Gorton.

routes.overview: A moderate, remote alpine rock ridge climb on a striking granite spire in the heart of the Eldorado Ice Cap, reached by crossing the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers from an Eldorado East Ridge high camp, then finishing on two pitches of clean 5.7 crack climbing to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Eldorado East Ridge high camp (~7,400 ft), rope up and cross the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers to the col between Pt. 8386 and Dorado Needle. The route starts with a loose, thinly-protected 5.5 pitch off the col, eases into several ropelengths of class 3-4 ridge terrain (with a glacier traverse and moat crossing to regain the ridge partway up), then several ropelengths of 3rd-to-low-5th-class climbing to a sub-summit tower. Two short rappels drop into a notch below the true summit, followed by two sustained, high-quality 5.7 pitches to the top. Most parties take 8-12 hours round trip from high camp to summit and back, descending via the technical Northwest Ridge.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from Marblemount about 20 miles to the Eldorado Trailhead (~2,100 ft; first 10 miles paved, remainder rough gravel, high-clearance recommended). Hike the Eldorado Trail through forest, cross a creek, and climb steeply to the Eldorado Glacier, then follow the East Ridge of Eldorado Peak to a high camp around 7,400 ft (roughly 4 miles and ~6,000 ft of gain). From camp, rope up and traverse the Inspiration Glacier onto the McAllister Glacier to reach the col at the base of Dorado Needle's East Ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb and rappel the Northwest Ridge back to the McAllister/Inspiration Glacier col, then retrace the glacier traverse to high camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, avoid old/misleading rappel tat and head toward the Northwest Ridge. Locate a wrapped-block rappel anchor a short distance above a stance; rappel roughly 30m (100 ft) to another stance, then scramble easy ledges to a further, uncomplicated rappel that lands in a moatless notch. From there, navigate steep crevasses on climber's right to regain the McAllister-Inspiration col and the glacier route back to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from the Cascade River Road trailhead, cross a creek, and climb steeply via the Eldorado Glacier to the East Ridge of Eldorado Peak for camp setup and gear/technical review (~7,400 ft camp). Day 2: Traverse the Inspiration Glacier to the McAllister Glacier, descend to the base of the East Ridge, climb to the summit of Dorado Needle, and descend the Northwest Ridge back to high camp. Day 3: Descend to the trailhead, including the final creek crossing, typically finishing by early evening.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward low on the route by downclimbing/rappelling back to the col before committing above it; once past the sub-summit rappels, the quickest way off is to continue over the top and descend the Northwest Ridge rather than reversing the upper pitches.
routes.turnaround: This is a long day (roughly 8-12 hours round trip from high camp) with a technical, easy-to-misroute descent — start early enough that the team is not negotiating the Northwest Ridge's rappels and moat crossings in the dark or in deteriorating weather, and turn back if not established on the upper 5.7 pitches by early-to-mid afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbable July through September, once glacier snow bridges and moats on the Inspiration/McAllister approach have stabilized enough for safe crossing but before early-season storms return; guide services report the most consistent, driest conditions in mid-to-late summer.
routes.fa: East Ridge first ascent by Joan Firey, Joe Firey, Hans Hoesli, Dave Knudson, and Peter Renz on July 4, 1971 — distinct from the peak's overall first ascent via the Northwest Ridge in July 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, and Tom Gorton.
routes.rappels: 2 short rappels (~30m each) into the notch below the summit tower en route; the Northwest Ridge descent adds 1-2 further rappels (~30m) plus downclimbing back to the McAllister-Inspiration col
objHaz: Crevasses crossing the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers
Bottomless moats where the glacier meets the rock, especially on the ridge-access ledge
Loose, sometimes unattached rock and blocks on the initial pitch off the col
Misleading rappel tat on the Northwest Ridge descent that can lead toward difficult moat crossings
routes.face: East Ridge
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams to 3 inches (e.g. doubles from 0.5-2 with a 3 and small tricams per one party's report) and a single 60m rope covers the technical pitches; bring crampons and an ice axe (or two tools) for the glacier approach and any firm snow on the ridge.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is thin and sometimes suspect on the lower 5.5 pitch off the col — test every placement to ensure it's attached to the mountain; gear improves and cracks are better higher up on the two 5.7 pitches near the summit.
whatToBring: Rock shoes for the crux pitches
Overnight camp gear for the Eldorado high camp
Sun protection for extended glacier travel
proTips: A horn with an existing sling sits about 100 ft up the first pitch off the col and can serve as a natural pro/belay point — inspect it before trusting it
An alternate start beneath the 'cannonball hole' on the right side reportedly offers better climbing than the direct col start, but requires crossing a moat to reach it
Watch for the ledge system marked by bands of quartzite when linking back onto the upper ridge after the mid-route glacier traverse
watchOut: Loose, sometimes unattached rock and blocks on the initial pitch off the col
Bottomless moats where the glacier meets the rock, especially near the ridge-access ledge
Misleading rappel tat on the Northwest Ridge descent that can lead toward difficult moat crossings
Crevasse hazard crossing the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers
knownHazards: Remote, heavily glaciated approach with real crevasse-fall potential on the Inspiration and McAllister Glaciers
Loose rock low on the route demands cautious, tested protection placements
A complex, easy-to-misroute Northwest Ridge descent with moat and rappel-anchor hazards

pitchDetail.notes: From the col between Pt. 8386 and Dorado Needle. Loose, poorly protectable rock — check every placement. A horn with an existing sling sits ~100 ft up; an alternate start beneath the 'cannonball hole' to the right avoids this section but requires a moat crossing.

Multiple ropelengths along or just below the ridge crest; rock quality improves. Descend into a notch, then traverse the glacier to a ledge marked by quartzite bands (watch for a bottomless moat) to regain the ridge.
Several ropelengths of easier climbing lead to a tower below the true summit.
Two short rappels down into the notch between the sub-summit tower and the summit pyramid.
Two sustained pitches of good-quality 5.7 crack climbing to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal gravel/paved mountain road; typically opens by late June and can be delayed to early July by snow or storm damage; subject to periodic closures at various mileposts for washouts, maintenance, or emergencies (e.g. wildfire) — check current NPS road conditions before driving
road.seasonalGate: Winter/off-season gate; road access to the Eldorado Trailhead area (near MP 20) is closed outside the open season
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road roughly 20 miles to the Eldorado Trailhead parking area (~2,100 ft). The first 10 miles are paved; miles 10-20 are rough gravel; the final few miles are narrow with steep drop-offs and washouts requiring a high-clearance vehicle. No trailers or vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide are allowed past milepost 18.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North
climate.typical: Marine west coast mountain climate — wet, snowy winters and drier but changeable summers, with real afternoon thunderstorm and rapid-weather-change risk on the glaciated approach
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; Cascade River Road is frequently still gated or impassable to the trailhead
climate.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers become more open and crevassed with thinner snow bridges as the season progresses
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency by October, with early season snow possible
climate.winter: Road closed near the trailhead; deep snowpack and high avalanche hazard make the approach essentially inaccessible

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS), with Cascade River Road partly on Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest land
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass for trailhead parking on National Forest land; free NPS wilderness permit for overnight backcountry camping
access.permit: Free backcountry/wilderness permit required for overnight stays in the Eldorado cross-country zone; reservable via recreation.gov or obtained in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Cascade River Road has seasonal gate closures (typically opens late June/early July) and can close at various points for washouts, maintenance, or emergencies — check the NPS road conditions page before travel

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine/early start from high camp to cross glacier terrain on firm snow and leave a safety margin for the technical Northwest Ridge descent

waypoints.name: Col between Pt. 8386 and Dorado Needle (East Ridge start)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Emergencies: 911. Skagit County Sheriff after-hours line (360) 336-9450; Skagit 911 non-emergency (360) 428-3211
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245 / noca_wilderness@nps.gov. NPS North Cascades main line (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 (business hours); after-hours Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (24/7 Level III Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: Remote, heavily glaciated terrain with no reliable cell coverage in the backcountry; carry a satellite communicator/PLB and leave a detailed itinerary tied to your wilderness permit for search-and-rescue purposes.

routes.corrections: The existing record listed grade 'III, 5.4' with rockGrade 5.4 and pitches 3 — this matches Dorado Needle's Northwest Ridge (the peak's easiest route: 5.4, ~3 pitches per Wikipedia/Peakbagger), not the East Ridge. Cross-checking Mountain Project, the Alpine Institute route profile, BC Adventure Guides, and multiple trip reports, the East Ridge is consistently 5.7 (one guide service lists 5.8) at Grade III, roughly 800 ft of technical climbing across on the order of 14 total ropelengths of mixed 3rd-5th class terrain including two sustained 5.7 pitches near the summit. Corrected rockGrade to 5.7 and pitches to 14 accordingly; commitment grade III added. alpineGrade (PD+) was left unchanged since no source was found to confirm or contradict it.

blurb: Himmelhorn is a 7,880-ft summit of Skagit Gneiss in the Southern Pickets (Crescent Creek Spires), a remote sub-group of North Cascades National Park's Picket Range, rising just west of Mount Terror above the small Mustard Glacier. Originally named Himmelgeisterhorn ("Horn of the Sky Spirit"), it was first climbed on September 8, 1961 by Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan and Joe Firey, and George Whitmore, who found a class-4 line up the north face after the peak "did not look at all promising" from either flanking notch. It remains one of the range's least-visited summits, seeing roughly one recorded ascent every other year.
hierarchyNote: Two things to flag. (1) Elevation conflict: the given elevationFt (7,901) does not match published figures — Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and the AAC's 2015 "Himmelhorn, South Face, Stonehenge" writeup all independently list the summit at 7,880 ft. I corrected the route's highPointFt to 7,880 accordingly. (2) Route identity concern: no source I could find (Mountain Project, Wikipedia, the primary 1962 AAJ first-ascent account, SummitPost/Picket Range page) documents a named "Southeast Route" on Himmelhorn or a 5.8-rated line on that aspect. The only historic/standard route matching this entry's first-ascent year (1961, Cooper-Denny-Firey-Firey-Whitmore) is consistently described as ascending the north face from the west (Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle) notch — i.e. a north/northwest-facing class-4 line, not southeast. I corrected aspect/face to match the sourced description but left routeName, rockGrade, alpineGrade, and commitment as given since I found no clearly-better-sourced replacement values for those specific fields — this route entry may be worth a second look against a physical Beckey/Nelson-Molenaar guidebook, which I could not access online. Geography and identity of the peak itself (location, range, coordinates) are otherwise well-corroborated and not in question.

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Glen Denny, Joan Firey, Joe Firey, George Whitmore — September 8, 1961 (this class-4 north-face line was the peak's first-ascent and remains its standard/easiest route)
objHaz: Loose rock and choss in the approach gully to the Otto–Himmel col
Icy snow and chossy moats in the gully by late season
Rockfall near the col and summit block
Glacier/snow travel near the Mustard Glacier on approach
routes.face: North Face, reached via the Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle (west) notch
routes.overview: The original 1961 first-ascent line climbs class-4 terrain up Himmelhorn's north face, gained from the Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle notch after a multi-day approach through Crescent Creek Basin and over the Otto–Himmel col. It remains the peak's easiest and most-traveled route, though the range's remoteness keeps traffic to a handful of parties per year.
routes.beta: First ascensionists Cooper, Denny, and the Fireys found the peak unpromising from either flanking notch, then located a class-4 route by following a ledge onto the north face that wasn't visible from below. The line is mostly scrambling with short steeper steps; conditions and difficulty vary considerably with season and snow coverage in the approach gully.
routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Trailhead on SR 20 near Newhalem, follow the climbers' 'stump hollow' path up through old growth and talus into Terror Creek/Crescent Creek Basin below the Southern Pickets. From upper Crescent Creek Basin, climb the narrow gully to the Otto–Himmel col — a straightforward snow climb with a little scrambling near the top in early season, but icy snow and chossy moats by late season, sometimes requiring a class 3/4 bypass around an overhanging boulder near the top. From the col, work around/up toward the Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle notch to reach the base of the north-face line.
routes.descent: Reverse the north-face line to the west (Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle) notch, then down-climb/rappel back to the Otto–Himmel col and retrace the approach gully to Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: Parties using the Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle notch on other objectives have down-climbed class 2-3 terrain on the west side of the notch, then made two rappels with 60m ropes past vertical slabs before continuing through grass and sloping rock — the same notch and general character apply when reversing this route. Expect loose, chossy terrain throughout the descent.
routes.itinerary: Typical trips are 2-4 days: Day 1, hike from the Goodell Creek Trailhead to a camp in Terror/Crescent Creek Basin (a long, off-trail approach day). Day 2, climb the gully to the Otto–Himmel col, ascend the north-face route to the summit, and return to the col/camp. An extra day is commonly built in for weather or for combining Himmelhorn with neighboring objectives (Ottohorn, Mount Terror, West Twin Needle) given the difficulty of the approach.
routes.bail: The class-4 terrain can generally be down-climbed back toward the west notch; from there, retreat to the Otto–Himmel col and down the approach gully. No fixed rappel anchors are documented on this line — parties should be prepared to build improvised anchors off horns/blocks if deteriorating snow or weather forces a rope retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach is a multi-day, off-trail crossing of glaciated and loose terrain with no easy bail options once committed to the gully and col, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight, current weather, and the gully's exposure to afternoon rockfall/wet-snow release — don't push into the col or the summit scramble against a closing weather window.
routes.bestSeason: Recorded ascents cluster from late July through September. Earlier in the season (June–early July) the approach gully to the Otto–Himmel col is typically a continuous, more secure snow climb; by late summer it degrades into icy snow and choss-filled moats. A few October ascents are documented but are colder and icier.
gear: 30-60m rope for short technical sections and rappels
Light rock rack (a few cams/nuts) for protecting low 5th-class moves
whatToBring: Bear-resistant food canister (required in North Cascades National Park cross-country zones)
Multi-day backpacking/camping kit for the approach
Map/compass/GPS for off-trail navigation through Crescent Creek Basin
North Cascades National Park wilderness permit
proTips: Approach-gully conditions change fast through the season — a straightforward snow climb in June/early July becomes icy, chossy moats by late summer, sometimes with a class 3/4 boulder bypass near the top.
Carry two 60m ropes if you expect to rappel the Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle notch on the way out.
watchOut: Loose, chossy rock in the approach gully and near the summit block
Icy snow and moats in the gully by late season
Very limited trip-report beta and route-finding cues on this line compared to Wild Hair Crack
knownHazards: Rockfall in the narrow approach gully to the Otto–Himmel col
Glacier/snow travel hazards near the Mustard Glacier
Extreme remoteness inside North Cascades National Park backcountry, with slow potential rescue response

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Goodell Creek Trailhead (~MP 119) sits on the western, non-gated portion of SR 20 near Newhalem and is accessible essentially year-round.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes seasonally (roughly November–April/May) due to avalanche danger between Ross Dam Trailhead (~MP 134) and points east; the Goodell Creek TH is well west of this closure.
road.driveNote: From I-5, take SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) east through Marblemount to the Goodell Creek Trailhead/Campground near Newhalem (~milepost 119).

climate.typical: Wet, unsettled North Cascades weather is common even in midsummer, since the range sits directly on the Cascade crest and catches heavy Pacific moisture.
climate.summer: Late July through September brings the most stable weather and driest approach-gully conditions.
climate.fall: Documented October ascents exist but are colder, with more ice in the approach gully.
climate.winter: SR 20's seasonal closure and heavy snowpack make winter access to this peak effectively impractical.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (National Park Service)
access.fees: Summer backcountry permit fee (mid-May–early October): $10/person plus a $6 nonrefundable reservation fee; no charge from early October–mid-May; no charge for youth 15 and under.
access.permit: A North Cascades National Park wilderness/backcountry permit is required for all overnight stays, including cross-country zone camping in the Southern Pickets (Terror/Crescent Creek basins).
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) closes seasonally each winter well east of the Goodell Creek Trailhead; the trailhead itself remains accessible year-round.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line: (360) 676-6911; call 911 for active emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.notes: Deep, off-trail cross-country zone inside North Cascades National Park; cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent and SAR response can take a full day or more — carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (7,901) does not match published figures — Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and the AAC's 2015 Stonehenge first-ascent writeup all independently list Himmelhorn's summit at 7,880 ft; corrected highPointFt to 7,880. Given aspect implied by the route name ('Southeast Route') conflicts with the only documented 1961-era route on Himmelhorn: the primary 1962 AAJ first-ascent account (corroborated by Wikipedia and Mountain Project) describes it ascending the north face from the west (Himmelhorn–West Twin Needle) notch — a north-facing class-4 line, not southeast — so aspect/face were corrected accordingly. No source found documents a formally named 'Southeast Route' or confirms the 5.8 rock grade for this line (the primary account rates it 'class IV' scrambling); rockGrade, alpineGrade, and routeName were left as originally given since no clearly-better-sourced replacement was found, but this entry likely merits a check against a physical Beckey or Nelson/Molenaar guidebook, which was not accessible online.

hierarchyNote: Peak identity and hierarchy check out: West Twin Needle is a real, well-documented spire in the Southern Picket Range, North Cascades National Park (Stephen Mather Wilderness), Washington — geographically consistent with a North Cascades parent hierarchy, sitting ~0.55 mi west of Mount Terror. One correction found: the given peak elevation of 7,927 ft conflicts with Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Peakery, and onehikeaweek.com, which independently and consistently list West Twin Needle (the higher of the two "Twin Needles" spires) at 7,936 ft. Recommend updating the peak's elevationFt to 7,936. The single listed route (South Route, Grade III 5.7, FA 1932) is corroborated as the line taken on the peak's actual first ascent.
blurb: West Twin Needle is a 7,936-foot spire of Skagit Gneiss in the remote Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, rising just west of Mount Terror above Crescent Creek Basin. It forms a matched pair with the slightly lower East/North Twin Needle and is a minor objective along the celebrated Southern Picket Traverse, with the Mustard Glacier below its north slopes. It was first climbed on August 17, 1932 by William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg — one of the earliest recorded ascents in the Pickets' pioneering era.

routes.overview: A moderate line up the south/east side of the peak above Crescent Creek Basin, climbing broken class 3-4 gully terrain with short low-5th-class pitching (crux 5.7) through chockstone-choked gullies to reach the Eye Col area before easier ground to the summit; this was the line of the peak's 1932 first ascent and remains the standard way up outside of the full Southern Picket Traverse.
routes.beta: Approached from a multi-day camp in Crescent Creek Basin (reached via the Goodell Creek / Terror Creek / Barrier approach), the route crosses roughly a mile of basin terrain before climbing a chockstone-filled gully system rated up to 5.7 (logged elsewhere as 'S. Face - West Twin Needle, II-5th class'). Loose rock and route-finding are the main challenges; parties commonly climb this as a side objective from a Southern Pickets base camp rather than a stand-alone trailhead-to-summit day.
routes.approach: From SR 20 at Newhalem, turn north onto Newhalem Creek Road (signed for Goodell Creek Campground) and follow it to a T-junction, turning left onto a gravel road to a small pullout/trailhead just before the campground (start elevation ~600 ft). Follow the unmaintained Goodell Creek climbers' trail (old roadbed, ~4.5 miles) paralleling Goodell Creek, then leave it where a cairn marks the turn toward Terror Basin; instead continue north along the fainter trail beside Terror Creek, descending to cross Terror Creek at a large logjam, then continue via the 'Barrier' approach into Crescent Creek Basin, gaining a bench camp (~6,050-6,400 ft) below Mount Terror used as a staging camp for West Twin Needle and other Southern Pickets objectives. From camp, traverse roughly a mile north across Crescent Creek Basin toward the peak's south side to reach the base of the climbing gullies.
routes.descent: Downclimb the class 2-3 west/south slopes back toward Crescent Creek Basin, or rappel via the Himmelhorn-West Twin Needle col.
routes.descentText: Parties descending toward the Himmelhorn-West Twin Needle col report making two rappels through a left-hand gully below the col to bypass a section of vertical slabs, before continuing down snow/talus back into Crescent Creek Basin. Alternatively, the class 2-3 west face/slope can be downclimbed directly back into the basin on the south side, avoiding the col rappels.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan given the remote, multi-day approach: Day 1 - hike in via Goodell Creek/Terror Creek/Barrier to a camp in Crescent Creek Basin (~6,050-6,400 ft). Day 2 - climb the South Route to the summit and return to camp same day (full days reported, with summits often reached in early-to-mid afternoon). Day 3 - hike out to the Goodell Creek trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally back down the same gully system climbed on the way up; because the route follows chockstone-filled gullies, downclimbing mid-route is difficult and may require building rappel anchors in loose rock, so most parties commit once above the lower pitches. From upper sections near the Eye Col, dropping to the Himmelhorn-West Twin Needle col and rappelling into Crescent Creek Basin is an alternative bail line.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, multi-day approach and the fully unpatrolled, off-trail nature of the Southern Pickets, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather margin before starting the technical gully pitches — parties have reported not topping out until mid-afternoon even with an early start, leaving little room for delay on the descent.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is favored, once seasonal snow has cleared from the approach gullies and the Mustard Glacier-fed slopes below the peak; August is frequently cited as the prime month for the wider Southern Pickets. Earlier-season attempts (June) are feasible but should expect lingering snow/ice in the gullies and colder, less-settled weather.
routes.comms: The Southern Pickets cirque, including Crescent Creek Basin, is extremely remote with no reliable cell coverage and no NPS climbing ranger presence in this cross-country zone; a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is strongly advised for trip safety and emergency contact.
routes.fa: William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg — August 17, 1932 (first ascent of the peak; this South Route is the line of that ascent).
routes.rappels: Up to 2 rappels through a left-hand gully below the Himmelhorn-West Twin Needle col on descent, per trip reports; consult current conditions/anchors.
objHaz: Loose, chossy rock in the lower approach gullies
Route-finding difficulty in narrow, disorienting south-side gullies
Limited/unreliable water along the approach ridge
Lingering snow/ice on approach slopes into early summer
routes.face: South/East Face above Crescent Creek Basin
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (small-to-mid cams and nuts) covers the low-5th-class pitches and 5.7 crux; carry a few extra slings/quickdraws for the chockstone-choked gully sections and glacier travel gear (axe, and crampons if snow lingers) for the Crescent Creek Basin / Mustard Glacier approach depending on season.
routes.proNeeds: Standard rock rack to ~2-3 inches is sufficient for the technical pitching; two 60m ropes are commonly carried both to link pitches efficiently and to make the col rappels on descent.
whatToBring: Multi-day pack for the Crescent Creek Basin camp
Extra water capacity (sources scarce on the approach ridge)
proTips: Stage from the ~6,050-6,400 ft bench camp below Mount Terror in Crescent Creek Basin, commonly used for West Twin Needle and other Southern Pickets objectives.
Carrying two 60m ropes lets you link the short technical gully pitches and speeds the rappel descent through the Himmelhorn-West Twin Needle col.
watchOut: The lower gully sections shed loose rock onto parties below — helmets mandatory, keep the party tight, and be deliberate about where you stand.
Published beta for the south-side line is scarce compared to the more commonly traveled Southern Picket Traverse route, and the narrow gullies can be genuinely disorienting to navigate.
knownHazards: Loose, chossy rock throughout the approach gullies below the Eye Col area.
Limited and unreliable water sources along the approach ridge, especially late season.
Lingering snow/ice on the Mustard Glacier-side approach slopes into early summer, requiring snow travel gear.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach gullies to Eye Col area
pitchDetail.notes: Chockstone-filled gullies with loose rock; roped short-pitching recommended through the steepest chockstones
Short crux section on the south/east face; logged elsewhere as grade II overall for the route
Easier scrambling terrain finishing to the summit

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Newhalem Creek Road / Goodell Creek
road.status: Paved highway open year-round to Newhalem (west-side access, below the seasonal high-pass closure); gravel spur road to an unmarked trailhead pullout
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes seasonally at Washington Pass/Rainy Pass on the east side (typically mid-Nov to spring) due to avalanche danger, but this does not affect west-side access via Newhalem/Goodell Creek
road.driveNote: From Newhalem on SR 20, turn north (signed toward Goodell Creek Campground) onto Newhalem Creek Road, follow it to a T-junction, then turn left onto a gravel road to a small pullout just before the campground — the start of the unmaintained Goodell Creek climbers' trail.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park backcountry / NWS North Cascades point forecasts (the Southern Pickets lie outside standard NWAC avalanche-forecast zone coverage)
climate.typical: Wet, weather-exposed maritime alpine climate typical of the North Cascades, with rapidly changing conditions
climate.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack into June/July; approach gullies and basin slopes typically still snow-covered
climate.summer: Prime climbing window July-September; afternoon thunderstorms and rapid weather changes possible
climate.fall: Early snow possible by late September/October, shortening weather windows for approach and climbing
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and avalanche terrain; not a winter climbing objective

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park Complex, within the Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: No park entrance fee (North Cascades NP is fee-free); no separate route fee
access.permit: Backcountry/wilderness permit required for any overnight stay, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or reserved in advance via recreation.gov
access.closures: No new fixed anchors permitted anywhere in designated wilderness (covers ~99% of the park); clean-climbing ethic (removable pro only) required

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start from the Crescent Creek Basin camp to maximize daylight for the technical gully pitches and the descent

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit area); approached from the Skagit County side (Newhalem/Marblemount)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: North Cascades NP Communications Center (360) 873-4590 ext. 37 (after-hours); Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 336-9450 if unreachable; 911 for life-threatening emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote, off-trail cross-country zone with minimal-to-no cell coverage and no climbing-ranger presence; self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator are strongly advised for any Southern Pickets objective.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,927 ft) conflicts with Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and Peakery, which all independently list West Twin Needle at 7,936 ft; route highPointFt corrected to 7,936 accordingly (flagged for the peak record too, see hierarchyNote). Existing alpineGrade ('D') and the FA year (1932) were not contradicted by any source and were retained/confirmed; alpineGrade specifically could not be independently re-verified from a French-alpine-grading source but no conflicting data was found either, so it was left as given.

blurb: Sentinel Peak is an 8,257–8,266 ft summit on the crest of the North Cascades, straddling the Snohomish–Chelan county line within the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie/Wenatchee National Forests). It sits between the South Cascade Glacier (west) and LeConte Glacier (northeast) and is built from the metamorphic/plutonic rock of the North Cascades' crystalline core (Skagit Gneiss Complex — banded gneiss, tonalite and granodiorite). The peak is climbed almost exclusively as a waypoint on the multi-day Ptarmigan Traverse rather than as a standalone objective, and no documented first-ascent party/date was found in the sources checked.

routes.overview: A glacier-approach scramble up Sentinel's south face/southwest ridge, reached only via 1-2 days of off-trail glacier travel on the Ptarmigan Traverse; the final push is a moat-and-ramp system across the south face leading to easy summit scrambling.
routes.beta: Sentinel is 'most often climbed, probably almost exclusively climbed, by parties doing the famed Ptarmigan traverse' (SummitPost/Mountaineers). From a high camp near the LeConte–Sentinel col or on the South Cascade Glacier, parties ascend a slope to a moat on the right side of a ledge/ramp that crosses the south face; the ramp 'wasn't difficult but will keep you on your best behavior,' and the final section to the summit is straightforward Class 2-3 scrambling on snow and rock. A steeper variation exists via the west ridge, involving a steep snow ramp near 7,400 ft and a short low-5th-class rock step into a gully below the final ridge crest — this is harder than the standard line and should be treated as an alternate finish, not the default.
routes.approach: From the end of Cascade River Road (gravel past mile 10, elev. ~3,660 ft, 23 mi from Marblemount), hike the Cascade Pass Trail ~3.7 mi to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). Continue north on the Ptarmigan Traverse: climb to Cache Col, descend past Kool-Aid Lake, and cross the exposed 'Red Ledge' traverse (a boot track is often kicked in by earlier parties in peak season, but do not count on it). Continue onto the South Cascade Glacier, cross to a notch above White Rock Lakes, descend to the lakes, then climb the Dana Glacier onto Itswoot Ridge to a high camp at the LeConte–Sentinel col (~7,300 ft) or on the South Cascade Glacier itself. One trip report logged 12.7 miles from the Cascade Pass trailhead to this camp, typically covered over 1-2 days.
routes.descent: Reverse the south-face moat-and-ramp line back to the South Cascade Glacier/col camp, or continue south along the Ptarmigan Traverse toward Spire Point and Dome Peak and exit via the Downey Creek trailhead instead of returning the way you came.
routes.descentText: Most parties simply downclimb the ascent line (moat and ramp) back to camp on the South Cascade Glacier side. An east-ridge descent has been used by at least one party but is not recommended as a default: it was described as 'steep, rotten rock' transitioning into '60-degree snow patches,' bad enough that the party abandoned that plan. If continuing the full Ptarmigan Traverse rather than backtracking, the route continues south past Old Guard, Itswoot Ridge/Spire Point, and Dome Peak toward the Downey Creek trailhead (a multi-day exit, not a same-day descent).
routes.itinerary: Typical plan when tagging Sentinel as part of the Ptarmigan Traverse (not a standalone day trip): Day 1 — hike in from Cascade Pass trailhead (~12-13 mi) via Cascade Pass, Cache Col, Red Ledge, and the South Cascade Glacier to a high camp near the LeConte–Sentinel col (~7,300 ft). Day 2 — summit Sentinel Peak (and often neighboring LeConte Mountain from the same camp), then move camp south toward Spire Point/Yang Yang Lakes. Day 3+ — continue the traverse south over Dome Peak and exit via Downey Creek (~10 mi out), or reverse the route back to Cascade Pass if not continuing the full traverse.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the moat/ramp back to the South Cascade Glacier camp; from there the return to Cascade Pass trailhead retraces the approach (Dana Glacier, White Rock Lakes notch, South Cascade Glacier, Red Ledge, Cache Col, Cascade Pass). There is no short bail option once committed to the glacier crossings — any retreat is a multi-hour to multi-day off-trail glacier traverse back to a trailhead.
routes.turnaround: This is remote, off-trail, glaciated terrain many hours from any road, so set a hard decision point at camp or at the base of the moat/ramp rather than a fixed summit clock time. Whiteout has stranded parties navigating near crevassed terrain on this route ('zero visibility... dense mists had forced me to stop altogether'); if visibility drops or the glacier crossings/Red Ledge look unsafe to reverse later in the day, turn back to camp.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed almost exclusively July through early September, once Cascade River Road is open and the traverse's glaciers, snow ramps, and the Red Ledge crux are consolidated enough to travel safely; mid-July to mid-September is the window most consistently cited for good conditions and lower snow hazard.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage along the route — NPS notes reception in the North Cascades backcountry is inconsistent even at high points and shouldn't be relied on for a plan. Carry a satellite communicator (inReach/SPOT). If you can get any signal near the North Cascades NP portion of the approach, Park Dispatch (360-873-4500 ext. 37) reaches North Cascades NP rescue faster than 911 or the county sheriff, who would have to relay to the park anyway.
routes.face: South face / southwest ridge (moat-and-ramp line), approached from the South Cascade Glacier side of the Ptarmigan Traverse; an alternate, harder line climbs the west ridge/west face.
routes.rappels: None typically needed — parties downclimb the ascent line, or continue south along the Ptarmigan Traverse rather than reversing the route.
objHaz: crevasses (South Cascade, LeConte/Middle Cascade, and Dana Glaciers)
loose rock on the moat/ramp system
exposed Red Ledge traverse on the approach
steep snow on the southwest approach ramp
routes.detailedRack: This is a glacier-travel and scrambling objective, not a technical rock route — a full rack is unnecessary. Most parties carry a single lightweight rope (30-30m class) split among a 2-3 person rope team for glacier crevasse rescue and to protect the short low-5th-class step if the west-ridge variation is used, plus a few slings/runners rather than cams or nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal. Prioritize a crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks, pulley, a couple of ice screws) over rock protection; a few slings are enough to back up the one short 4th/low-5th-class step encountered on some lines.
gear: 30m glacier travel rope per rope team
whatToBring: Backpacking/camping gear for 1-3+ nights (tent, stove, sleeping system)
Bear canister (required for backcountry camping in North Cascades NP)
Map/compass/GPS — route is off-trail glacier travel and navigation, not a marked trail
Satellite communicator (inReach/SPOT) given the lack of cell coverage
proTips: Sentinel and neighboring LeConte Mountain are commonly climbed together from the same LeConte–Sentinel col camp since both are short pushes from there.

Don't assume a boot track will be kicked into the Red Ledge traverse — earlier-season or early-in-the-season parties may find it unconsolidated even though many trip reports describe following in others' steps.
The south-face moat/ramp stays snow-filled well into midsummer; check recent trip reports for current conditions before committing to a specific line.
Because this is a stop on a longer traverse, plan camp locations (South Cascade Glacier, LeConte–Sentinel col, Yang Yang Lakes) around water and flat ground rather than trying to day-trip the peak.
watchOut: Real crevasse hazard on the South Cascade, LeConte/Middle Cascade, and Dana Glaciers — rope up for all glacier travel.
Whiteout/fog is a documented hazard on this route and has forced parties to stop moving near crevassed terrain.
The east-ridge descent variation has rotten rock and sections of 60-degree snow — avoid it as a default descent.
Cascade River Road has a history of storm-driven washouts and multi-week closures; verify current road status before driving in, since a closure can add miles of extra road walking to the approach.
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier travel on three separate glaciers en route (South Cascade, LeConte/Middle Cascade, Dana).
Loose, rotten rock on some descent/alternate lines (east ridge).
Whiteout conditions on the South Cascade Glacier plateau have stranded parties mid-crossing.
Remote, off-trail terrain far from any trailhead — any rescue is a multi-hour to multi-day effort.

pitchDetail.notes: Rope up to cross the South Cascade Glacier and Dana Glacier to the base of the peak; real crevasse hazard, especially in low-snow years or late season.
Ascend a slope to a moat on the right side of a ledge/ramp system crossing the south face; exposed but not technically difficult with careful footwork.
Straightforward scrambling from the top of the ramp to the summit.
A steep snow ramp near 7,400 ft leads to a short low-5th-class rock step into a gully, then ~600 ft of ridge scrambling to the summit; more sustained than the standard south-face line.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open as of this research (gravel and rough with potholes/washboard past mile 10; not suitable for RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft, or low-clearance sedans)
road.seasonalGate: No fixed seasonal closure dates published; State Route 20 (the main access highway to Marblemount) typically closes for winter Nov/Dec–Apr/May depending on snowpack, and Cascade River Road itself is periodically closed by storm washouts independent of season.
road.driveNote: 23 miles from Marblemount to the Cascade Pass trailhead at road's end (elev. ~3,660 ft); paved to about mile 10, gravel with potholes/washboard the rest of the way. The road has a documented history of major storm washouts (including a 15 ft deep, 60 ft wide chasm in one event) that have closed it for extended periods — check the NPS road-conditions page before driving in.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC 'West Slopes Central' (Skagit River to south of I-90) — most relevant in shoulder season; the route is normally climbed in summer, outside the core avalanche season.
climate.typical: Wet, maritime North Cascades weather with fast-changing conditions; whiteout/fog on the glaciers is a documented hazard on this route even in summer.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack still covers most of the approach and the road may not yet be clear; not the normal season for this route.
climate.summer: The primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers and the Red Ledge/moat-ramp system are most consolidated and safely passable, though afternoon whiteout and thunderstorms can still occur.
climate.fall: Conditions can remain climbable into September, but earlier freeze and new snow shorten the window and re-glaze crevasses/slopes.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective; Cascade River Road access and route conditions are unsuitable for casual winter travel.

access.landManager: Approach (Cascade Pass, Cache Col) is within North Cascades National Park (NPS); the peak itself sits in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, managed by the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Wenatchee National Forests (USFS).
access.fees: No entrance fee for North Cascades National Park.
access.permit: A free NPS backcountry permit is required for overnight camps within North Cascades National Park; obtained in person (day-of or day-before) at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, with advance reservations available in peak season via recreation.gov. Group size is capped (12) in this area.
access.passRequired: No pass required at the Cascade Pass trailhead itself (it's within the national park); a Northwest Forest Pass may apply at alternate USFS trailheads used to exit the traverse (e.g., Downey Creek).
access.closures: Cascade River Road is subject to storm-related washouts and closures that can last weeks; verify current status with NPS before travel. Standard NPS wilderness camping regulations apply (e.g., camp 100+ ft from lakes).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning departure from high camp, to complete glacier crossings and the Red Ledge/moat-ramp sections while snow is firm and before afternoon whiteout risk increases — no source gives a specific clock time for this route.

emergency.county: Skagit County (Cascade River Road/Marblemount approach side); the summit sits on the Snohomish–Chelan county line.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency: (360) 416-1911 (or Skagit 911 dispatch (360) 428-3211). For incidents within North Cascades NP, NPS Park Dispatch — (360) 873-4500 ext. 37 — is faster than routing through 911/county sheriff.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245; park headquarters (360) 854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111, roughly 50 miles from the Marblemount/Cascade River Road access point.
emergency.notes: This route is deep, off-trail glacier terrain with no reliable cell coverage; expect any rescue to require a satellite-communicator SOS and a multi-hour-to-multi-day response. Carry a satellite communicator and be prepared for self-rescue or extended evacuation for injuries far from the trailhead.

routes.corrections: Existing peak data lists elevation as 8,257 ft. Two independently cross-checked sources (Wikipedia, citing USGS/NAVD88, and PeakVisor) both give 8,266 ft (2,518-2,519 m), while listsofjohn.com gives 8,256 ft. These differences (under 15 ft) are consistent with normal survey/datum rounding rather than a data error; highPointFt above uses 8,266 ft as the figure backed by two cross-checked sources.

blurb: Sherpa Peak (8,630 ft) is a granite satellite summit of Mount Stuart in the Stuart Range, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Washington. Formed from Cretaceous-age granodiorite of the Mount Stuart batholith, it has enough independent relief (~400+ ft prominence) to rank as its own peak and is best known for a striking ~20-ft balanced-rock obelisk near its summit, first climbed by Dave Mahre with Gene and Bill Prater in 1955. Its three standard ridge lines (West, East, North) offer some of the best moderate alpine rock in the range, though loose rock and long, partly cross-country approaches are typical.

routes.fa: Fred Dunham, Ray Lilleby, and James Wick, August 13, 1961
routes.rappels: 4 single-rope rappels down the ascent line back to the Stuart-Sherpa col (50-60m rope)
routes.season: Typically climbed June through September; an ice axe is recommended for early-season parties due to snow lingering in the approach gully/basin
routes.face: South side of the peak, climbed from the Stuart-Sherpa col
routes.overview: The peak's most popular and 'classic' line: a 3-pitch, mostly low-5th-class ridge climb from the Stuart-Sherpa col, with a short 5.4-5.5 crack exiting a cave belay as the crux, finishing with an airy 4th-class scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: From the col between Mount Stuart and Sherpa Peak, ascend the ridge generally trending climber's left. With a 60m rope you can reach the first belay in a cave-like feature with a platform large enough for several climbers; the crux is the crack leading out of the cave (rated 5.4-5.5 depending on source). A second pitch leads to another anchor, after which a short 4th-class scramble gains the notoriously airy summit block. Descent is 4 single-rope rappels down the ascent line.
routes.approach: Drive to the Esmeralda Trailhead (4,269 ft) via I-90 Exit 85 to WA-970, then Teanaway Road / North Fork Teanaway Road (FR-9737) — paved for roughly the first 13 miles, then gravel for about 9.5-9.6 miles to the trailhead. From there, hike the Longs Pass Trail (or Ingalls Way Trail) roughly 4.5 miles over Longs Pass and down into the Ingalls Creek drainage to a high camp (options around 6,200 ft and 7,400 ft). On summit day, ascend the boulder-filled basin to the Stuart-Sherpa col (~8,100 ft), crossing a talus/meadow shoulder near 5,800 ft along the way.
routes.descent: Rappel the West Ridge (4 single-rope raps) back to the Stuart-Sherpa col, then descend the ascent approach back to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/rappel back to the notch below the summit block, then make 4 single-rope rappels down the ridge to the Stuart-Sherpa col. From the col, descend the boulder-filled basin and retrace the approach gully back toward the high camp; parties frequently finish this descent by headlamp given the length of the day.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Esmeralda Trailhead, hike ~4.5 mi over Longs Pass and down to a high camp near Ingalls Creek (6,200-7,400 ft). Day 2: Pre-dawn start, climb to the Stuart-Sherpa col, complete the 3-pitch ridge to the summit, rappel the route, and hike out to the trailhead (full days on this route commonly run 12-15 hours car-to-car from high camp).
routes.bail: The route can be rappelled from nearly any point along the ridge back down to the Stuart-Sherpa col using the same anchors established on the way up.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround time based on remaining daylight for both the technical descent (4 rappels) and the multi-hour hike back to camp/trailhead; afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Cascades in summer, so being off the exposed ridge crest by early-to-mid afternoon is prudent.
routes.detailedRack: A small-to-mid-size alpine rack: quickdraws, slings, a set of finger-sized-to-mid cams, and one set of nuts covers the route's protection needs.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine trad rack (finger-to-hand-size cams and a nut set) plus a small amount of extra webbing for the crux crack and rappel anchors.
whatToBring: overnight camping gear (bivy or tent) for the standard 2-day itinerary
map/compass or GPS for the cross-country basin/col approach
proTips: Two established high-camp options exist (6,200 ft and 7,400 ft) — the higher camp shortens summit day but carries water further
Build rappel anchors on the way up so the descent can retrace the exact line
Early-season parties should carry an ice axe for lingering snow in the approach basin
watchOut: The summit block is notoriously airy and exposed
Loose rock is present throughout, especially near the cave belay and on descent
Water sources can be scarce or buried under boulders at high camp
knownHazards: Loose/blocky rock along the ridge and descent
Afternoon thunderstorm exposure on an unshaded ridge
Long, partly cross-country approach with route-finding challenges

pitchDetail.notes: From the Stuart-Sherpa col, ascend the ridge trending climber's left to a cave-like belay platform large enough for several climbers.
Climb the crack exiting the cave; graded 5.4 in older guides, 5.5 on Mountain Project's route page — most reports describe it as sustained crack climbing.
Short scramble along the ridge crest to the exposed, airy summit block.

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road (FR-9737) to Esmeralda Trailhead
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 13 miles from Teanaway Road, then maintained gravel forest road to the trailhead
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 85 (or Cle Elum via WA-970 ~7-8.5 mi), turn onto Teanaway Road, follow North Fork Teanaway Road/FR-9737 to its end at the Esmeralda Trailhead (~22-23 miles total from the highway).

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual pass at Esmeralda Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass)
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for Alpine Lakes Wilderness entry
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass
access.closures: Dogs prohibited on the Ingalls Way and Longs Pass Trails; camping prohibited within 0.5 mile of Lake Ingalls

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from high camp

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittcom (Kittitas County 911 dispatch) / Kittitas County Sheriff
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA; KVH Urgent Care in Cle Elum for non-emergencies
emergency.notes: Long, remote approach through the Ingalls Creek/Teanaway drainage; expect no reliable cell coverage and plan for self-rescue.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's dedicated route page lists the crux as YDS 5.5 (French 4b / UIAA IV+), while the existing 5.4 rating (and most trip reports, which describe the crux as '5.4-5.5') is also well supported — kept as given rather than overwritten. Pitch count to the summit is sometimes described as 5-6 short pitches in trip reports depending on rope length/simul-climbing style, but Mountain Project's official route page (used here) counts 3 pitches.

routes.rappels: 2 rappels down the West Ridge plus downclimbing into the south gully to descend
objHaz: exposed traverse near the balanced rock (can be snowy/wet)
route-finding on a partly cairned, cross-country ridge
routes.season: Jun-Sep for the standard rock ascent; a winter/spring ascent has also been documented
routes.face: South side of the peak, ridge running past the balanced-rock obelisk
routes.overview: The generally easier of Sherpa's two south-side ridge routes, following the east ridge past the famous balanced-rock obelisk to the summit, with mostly 4th- and low-5th-class climbing and a short 5.7 crux move at/near the balanced rock.
routes.beta: From a basin camp around 6,800 ft, follow the east ridge toward the summit; cairns and fixed slings/webbing along the ridge aid route-finding. The route passes around the balanced rock from the north side, which can involve a sketchy traverse over snow or wet rock in early season. Beckey describes the balanced-rock move itself as a shoulder-stand or rope-throw over a pedestal point to gain its east side, then bolt-protected friction climbing to the top, rated 5.7, with a rappel bolt atop.
routes.approach: Access via the Lake Ingalls/Longs Pass Trailhead (Esmeralda Trailhead). Hike to Longs Pass, descend to Ingalls Creek, and travel east on the Ingalls Creek Trail before leaving the trail at the first clearing to ascend a long buttress on Sherpa's southern slopes, reaching a camp around 6,800 ft west of the peak's south basin.
