routes.descent: Descend via 2 rappels down the West Ridge plus downclimbing into the south gully back toward the approach basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/traverse to the top of the West Ridge and make 2 rappels down that line, then downclimb into the south gully and traverse back toward the basin camp; expect loose rock and, in early season, snow patches on this descent.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally via downclimbing/rappelling back along the ridge the way you came, or dropping into the south gully toward the basin if already past the balanced rock.
routes.turnaround: Plan to be off the ridge and past the balanced-rock traverse before afternoon storms build; the descent's rappels and loose-rock gully take real time, so budget accordingly for a daylight finish.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer once seasonal snow has cleared the ridge and the balanced-rock traverse, roughly June through September; a documented winter ascent shows the route is possible off-season for well-equipped parties but with added snow/ice hazard.
routes.proNeeds: A rope, helmet, and small-to-mid trad rack for the ridge's low-5th-class sections and the balanced-rock move.
whatToBring: overnight camping gear for the standard 2-day approach
proTips: Follow the cairns and existing slings/webbing along the ridge crest for route-finding relief
Combine with a visit to the balanced rock obelisk since the route passes right by it
watchOut: The traverse around the balanced rock can be sketchy over new snow or wet rock
Loose rock throughout the ridge
knownHazards: Exposed, partly cross-country ridge with variable rock quality
Snow/wet-rock traverse near the balanced rock in shoulder seasons

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road (FR-9737) to Esmeralda Trailhead
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 13 miles from Teanaway Road, then maintained gravel forest road to the trailhead
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 85 (or Cle Elum via WA-970 ~7-8.5 mi), turn onto Teanaway Road, follow North Fork Teanaway Road/FR-9737 to its end at the Esmeralda Trailhead.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual pass at Esmeralda Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass)
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for Alpine Lakes Wilderness entry
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass
access.closures: Dogs prohibited on the Ingalls Way and Longs Pass Trails; camping prohibited within 0.5 mile of Lake Ingalls

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from basin camp

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittcom (Kittitas County 911 dispatch) / Kittitas County Sheriff
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA; KVH Urgent Care in Cle Elum for non-emergencies
emergency.notes: Remote, partly cross-country approach; expect no reliable cell coverage and plan for self-rescue.

routes.corrections: No source confirms a distinct first-ascent party for the East Ridge line itself, so 'fa' is left null. The only documented first ascent tied to this ridge is for the balanced-rock obelisk it passes — climbed by Dave Mahre with Gene and Bill Prater in 1955, per Fred Beckey's guide — which is a separate feature from the ridge route as a whole and should not be conflated with a route FA.

routes.fa: Rick La Belle and Pat Derr, 1971
routes.rappels: Descent involves 2 short rappels (roughly 30 ft and 20 ft) plus downclimbing to the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle
objHaz: long sustained ridge with route-finding challenges
routes.face: North side of the peak, via the Sherpa-Argonaut basin/Sherpa Glacier area
routes.overview: A long, sustained Grade III alpine rock route up Sherpa Peak's north ridge, broken into three sections by deep notches, with two 5.8 pitches guarding the final approach to the summit; regarded as one of the better moderate alpine rock outings in the Stuart Range.
routes.beta: The route comprises three ridge sections separated by deep notches. The lower section is mostly class 2-4 terrain, commonly simul-climbed, staying just on the east side of the crest (harder climbing results if the crest itself is followed). After descending to the second notch, two 5.8 pitches (a dirty 5.8 chimney and a 5.8 crack pitch, per trip reports) lead toward the summit, separated by class 2-3 sections, with large detached blocks making protection difficult in places. A gendarme partway up offers the route's best technical climbing directly, or can be bypassed via a ~75-ft rappel to easier terrain west of the ridge.
routes.approach: Two approach options exist. South approach (shared trailhead with the West/East Ridge routes): from the Esmeralda Trailhead, hike over Longs Pass and into the Sherpa-Argonaut basin, then contour up and climber's right to the base of the route's lower notch, scrambling easy slabs before roping up. North approach: from the Colchuck Lake/Stuart Lake Trailhead (Icicle Creek Road, near Leavenworth), follow the boot path up Mountaineers Creek, take the cairned path through talus on the western slope, then drop down and head south toward the Argonaut/Sherpa saddle, ascending steeply up talus near 5,800 ft to reach the north ridge.
routes.descent: Rappel/downclimb southwest off the summit into a gully, then follow benches toward the Sherpa-Argonaut col and descend scree slopes back to the basin.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel southwest, then work left/south into a gully below the summit. A ~30-ft rappel from the west ridge is followed by class 3 downclimbing in the gully heading southeast, then a further ~20-ft rappel over large blocks. Traverse below ribs around 7,000 ft toward the Sherpa/Argonaut saddle, then descend roughly 1,000 ft of loose scree/talus to treeline; expect significant loose rock throughout this descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a basin camp (Sherpa-Argonaut basin from the south, or a Mountaineers Creek camp from the north). Day 2: Early start up the ridge, roping up at the base of the lower notch, climbing the three ridge sections to the summit, then rappelling/downclimbing back to the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle and out.
routes.bail: The lower two-thirds of the ridge is largely class 2-4 and can be downclimbed; from higher on the route, retreat is via downclimbing to the nearest notch and dropping into the gully system toward the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's length (13 pitches/1,400 ft) and loose, protection-poor sections, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight for the multi-stage descent and long walk-out; simul-climbing the lower section is common specifically to bank time for the harder upper pitches.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed once snow has cleared the ridge and approach basin, typically June through September.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack to 3 inches with doubled smaller cams (0.5-1 inch) plus long runners for horns and blocks; approach shoes suitable for both hiking and climbing work well given the mixed terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is noted as difficult in places due to large detached blocks; doubled small-to-mid cams and long slings help on the two crux 5.8 pitches.
gear: single rack to 3 inch with doubled smaller sizes
proTips: Simul-climb the lower class 2-4 section and stay just on the east side of the crest to save time
The gendarme offers the route's best direct climbing, or can be skipped via a ~75-ft rappel to easier ground to its west
watchOut: Large detached/loose blocks throughout, especially near the two 5.8 pitches
Rockfall hazard on the descent gully — reports describe close calls with falling rock
knownHazards: Loose rock and difficult protection on a sustained, mostly unbolted ridge
Long descent through a loose scree/talus gully to the Sherpa-Argonaut saddle

pitchDetail.notes: Simul-climbed by most parties; stay just east of the ridge crest to avoid harder terrain on the crest itself.
A dirty chimney pitch containing an old fixed ice axe wedged in a crack, per trip reports.
Climbs cracks, some containing loose blocks.
The route's best technical climbing directly over the gendarme, or bypass via a ~75-ft rappel to easier terrain west of the ridge.
Two 5.8 pitches with loose blocks lead to easy scrambling and the summit.

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road (FR-9737) to Esmeralda Trailhead (south approach); Icicle Creek Road to Colchuck/Stuart Lake Trailhead (north approach)
road.status: South: paved then gravel forest road to Esmeralda TH. North: paved/gravel Icicle Creek Road to the Stuart Lake Trailhead near Leavenworth.
road.driveNote: South approach shares the Esmeralda Trailhead access with the West/East Ridge routes; north approach starts from the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead off Icicle Creek Road west of Leavenworth.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Alpine Lakes Wilderness) — Cle Elum Ranger District (south approach) or Wenatchee River Ranger District (north approach via Colchuck)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass required at both trailheads
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead for Alpine Lakes Wilderness entry
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn start to allow time for the route's 13 pitches plus a multi-stage descent

emergency.county: Kittitas County (south approach) or Chelan County (north approach via Colchuck); summit itself in Chelan County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County: Kittcom/Kittitas County Sheriff. Chelan County (Leavenworth side): Chelan County Sheriff.
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District (509) 852-1100 (south); Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth (north)
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Ellensburg, WA (south approach); Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth, WA (north approach)
emergency.notes: Remote, sustained ridge climb with no reliable cell coverage on either approach; plan for self-rescue given the route's length and loose rock.

routes.corrections: An initial broad web search attributed the North Ridge's first ascent to Fred Beckey and Steve Marts (1963), but this appears to conflate Sherpa Peak's North Ridge with Mount Stuart's separate, more famous North Ridge (which Beckey and Marts did first climb in 1963). Mountain Project's dedicated Sherpa Peak North Ridge route page — one of the required primary sources — credits Rick La Belle and Pat Derr with the 1971 first ascent, which is used here. Pitch count (13) and route length (1,400 ft) were previously null and are now filled from Mountain Project.

hierarchyNote: FLAG — this entry likely misrepresents what Sitkum Spire actually is. Cross-checking Peakbagger/PeakVisor/peakery data (elevation 2,851 m / ~9,355 ft, prominence only ~28 m / ~92 ft) shows Sitkum Spire is a very low-prominence rock outcrop ("an obvious rock finger," per SummitPost) on the WEST SUMMIT RIDGE of Glacier Peak (true summit 10,541 ft) in the Glacier Peak Wilderness — not an independent peak with its own climbing history. The given elevation (9,412 ft) is close to but does not match the sourced 9,355 ft (2,851 m) figure used consistently by Peakbagger/PeakVisor/peakery; recommend re-verifying against a DEM/GNIS point.  More importantly: no source (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountaineers, willhiteweb, or any trip report found) documents an independent technical route up Sitkum Spire's own summit. Mountain Project's only route in this immediate area, "Sitkum Glacier" (Grade III, Easy Snow, ice axe + crampons required), is filed with an elevation of 10,541 ft — i.e. it is the standard non-technical route up GLACIER PEAK's true summit, and multiple independent sources (SummitPost, willhiteweb, Mountaineers, Mazamas, Daniel Arndt trip report) confirm that route climbs the glacier to a saddle "just east of Sitkum Spire" and continues up Glacier Peak's pumice summit ridge — it passes near/below Sitkum Spire, it does not go over or summit it. The catalog's listed grade for this entry ("Class 4 / low 5th, glacier," alpine grade AD) is not supported by any source; it does not match the documented Class 2–3/"Easy Snow" character of the only route that exists in this location. Recommend the team decide whether "wa_sitkum_spire" should be merged into an existing Glacier Peak entry, re-scoped as a minor named sub-feature with no independent route, or removed — rather than carrying a fabricated technical grade for a summit that the documented route does not actually reach. I still returned the best-sourced, honestly-scoped approach/access/gear/season information for the area below, since that is genuinely useful regardless of how the hierarchy question is resolved.
blurb: Sitkum Spire is a minor, low-prominence rock outcrop (~2,851 m / 9,355 ft, ~28 m prominence) on the west summit ridge of Glacier Peak, a glaciated stratovolcano in the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), Snohomish County, Washington, at the southern end of the North Cascades volcanic arc. It is composed of the same volcanic andesite/dacite as the rest of Glacier Peak's upper flanks and sits beside the Sitkum Glacier. No independent first-ascent record or dedicated climbing route for Sitkum Spire itself was found in any source checked; it functions as a landmark passed near the saddle on the standard Sitkum Glacier route to Glacier Peak's true 10,541 ft summit.

routes.overview: No independent route up Sitkum Spire's own summit is documented in any source checked. The only established climbing route in this immediate area is the Sitkum Glacier route, the standard non-technical way up Glacier Peak's true 10,541 ft summit, which climbs the Sitkum Glacier to a saddle just east of Sitkum Spire and continues up Glacier Peak's pumice summit ridge — passing near Sitkum Spire without summiting it.
routes.beta: From Boulder Basin (the standard base camp, ~5,500-6,000 ft), travel southeast onto the Sitkum Glacier and climb NE toward a saddle at roughly 9,500 ft just east of Sitkum Spire. From the saddle, follow the pumice Sitkum ridge toward Glacier Peak's summit, staying right of and slightly below the summit block, then move into a snow chute that leads to the top. Standard glacier travel gear (ice axe, crampons, rope, harness) is required; Mountain Project rates the overall line Grade III, 'Easy Snow.'
routes.approach: Historically: from the White Chuck River Trailhead near Darrington, hike 5.5 miles to the former Kennedy Hot Springs site (~3,300 ft, buried by the 2003 flood), then 1.5 miles further to Sitkum Creek and the Pacific Crest Trail junction (~3,850 ft). Follow the PCT north (left) about 1/2 mile to a forested campsite (~4,100 ft), then take the steep, unmaintained climbers' path up a ridge south of Sitkum Creek to Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), the standard base camp — roughly 9-10 miles one-way to camp. IMPORTANT CURRENT ACCESS NOTE: the White Chuck River Trail has had no vehicle or maintained trail access since the 2003 flood destroyed the trail and buried Kennedy Hot Springs, and Forest Service Road 23 (White Chuck Road) has additional closures from 2025-2026 storm damage; verify current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts before planning this approach, as the historical route described above may not be currently passable.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down the pumice summit ridge and snow chute to the saddle, then down the Sitkum Glacier to Boulder Basin.
routes.descentText: Descend by reversing the ascent route: down-climb or glissade the snow chute and pumice ridge back to the ~9,500 ft saddle east of Sitkum Spire, then descend the Sitkum Glacier (roped for crevasse hazard, especially later in the season) back to Boulder Basin camp, and retrace the approach trail/climbers' path back toward the White Chuck drainage.
routes.itinerary: Historical 3-day plan (subject to current access closures — see approach note): Day 1 — hike in via the White Chuck River Trail/PCT to Boulder Basin base camp (~9-10 mi). Day 2 — alpine start, climb the Sitkum Glacier past Sitkum Spire's saddle to Glacier Peak's summit and return to camp. Day 3 — hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent at any point: descend back to the saddle east of Sitkum Spire and down the Sitkum Glacier to Boulder Basin; there is no technical rock climbing on this route to complicate a bail.
routes.turnaround: As with any glaciated Cascade volcano route, set a turnaround based on remaining daylight and weather margin rather than a fixed clock time — deteriorating visibility, softening snow bridges over crevasses, or incoming weather on the exposed upper glacier and pumice ridge are all reasons to turn back before reaching the summit.
routes.bestSeason: July is generally considered the best month: enough of the winter snowpack remains to bridge crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier, while the heavy rains of June have typically eased. By mid-to-late August the glacier tends to become significantly more crevassed as snow bridges deteriorate, increasing route-finding difficulty and hazard.
routes.comms: This is a remote wilderness approach and glacier route in the Glacier Peak Wilderness with no maintained infrastructure; cell coverage is not reliable in the White Chuck valley approach or on the glacier itself. A satellite communicator is advisable given the remoteness and current access difficulties.
routes.face: West summit ridge of Glacier Peak, between the Sitkum Glacier/Boulder Basin and the summit dome
whatToBring: free self-issue wilderness permit (obtain at trailhead/ranger station)
map and compass or GPS — Boulder Basin approach is an unmaintained climbers' path, not a trail
extra food/time buffer given current trailhead access difficulties
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is required for the documented route. Standard glacier travel kit: ice axe and crampons for the ascent, a rope with harnesses for glacier travel, several snow pickets for crevasse-rescue anchors and belays on the steeper sections near the saddle, and wands to mark the return route across the featureless upper glacier and pumice ridge in case of whiteout.
routes.proNeeds: Crevasse rescue protection (pickets, rope, prusiks) rather than rock protection; the route is glacier travel and steep snow/pumice, not technical rock climbing.
proTips: Aim for July: enough snow remains to bridge crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier without the deep, unstable spring snowpack of June, and before the glacier becomes heavily crevassed by mid-to-late August.
Boulder Basin is the standard base camp for this route, reached via a steep, unmaintained climbers' path leaving the Pacific Crest Trail about 1/2 mile north of the Sitkum Creek/PCT junction.
Wand your route across the upper glacier and pumice ridge — the terrain near the saddle and summit block is featureless and easy to lose in flat light or fog.
watchOut: Sitkum Spire itself is bypassed, not summited, by this documented route — climbers pass near a saddle east of it en route to Glacier Peak's true summit, so treat any claim of a distinct technical 'Sitkum Spire route' with caution.
Crevasses on the Sitkum Glacier open up significantly by mid-to-late August as snow bridges deteriorate.

Current trailhead/road access to the historical approach (White Chuck River Trail, FS Road 23) has been damaged/closed since 2003 flooding, with additional 2025-2026 storm closures — verify access before planning.
knownHazards: Glacier travel hazard (crevasses), increasing through the climbing season
Loose pumice and scree on the summit ridge above the saddle
Remote wilderness terrain with limited cell coverage and long rescue response times
Currently degraded/closed trailhead and road access requiring route-finding on an unmaintained path

road.name: White Chuck River Road (Forest Service Road 23)
road.status: Closed / no reliable vehicle access
road.driveNote: The historical White Chuck River Trailhead approach has had no vehicle or maintained trail access since the 2003 flood destroyed the White Chuck River Trail and buried Kennedy Hot Springs under debris; FS Road 23 has an additional temporary closure (from milepost 3.7 to its terminus, and FS Road 27 from milepost 4) due to December 2025 storm damage that made the road unsafe. Check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts before planning this approach.

climate.typical: Wet, cool conditions common outside a narrow July-August climbing window; substantial winter/spring snowpack persists into early summer on the Sitkum Glacier.
climate.summer: Best climbing conditions typically in July, tapering as the glacier becomes more crevassed by mid-to-late August.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District), Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass may be required at some developed Forest Service trailheads in the area
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead/ranger station for travel in Glacier Peak Wilderness; group size limited to 12 people/stock combined
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: White Chuck River Trailhead/Trail has had no vehicle or trail access since the 2003 flood; FS Road 23 (White Chuck Road) has an additional closure from milepost 3.7 to its terminus due to December 2025 storm damage — verify current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before planning

emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, 330 S. Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA — (360) 435-2133
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness area; Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR) supports the Snohomish County Sheriff's Office with wilderness/mountain rescue in this area.

routes.corrections: The catalog's listed grade for this route ('Class 4 / low 5th, glacier'; alpine grade AD) is not supported by any source found (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountaineers, willhiteweb, Mazamas, or trip reports). The only documented route in this location — Mountain Project's 'Sitkum Glacier' — is rated Grade III, 'Easy Snow' (ice axe + crampons only, no roped rock climbing), and is filed at elevation 10,541 ft, i.e. it is the standard route up Glacier Peak's true summit, not a distinct technical route up Sitkum Spire (9,355 ft, per Peakbagger/PeakVisor/peakery, vs. the catalog's 9,412 ft). This route passes near a saddle east of Sitkum Spire but does not summit it. No route-specific or Sitkum-Spire-specific first ascent was found; left null rather than reporting Glacier Peak's unrelated FA history.

hierarchyNote: The Triad is a real, well-documented three-summited peak (west/middle/east summits) in North Cascades National Park, sitting on the southwest ridge extension of Eldorado Peak — the given North Cascades parent hierarchy is geographically correct and confirmed by Wikipedia, Peakbagger, PeakVisor, and ListsOfJohn. However, those same independent sources consistently place the mountain's highest points (middle and east summits) at approximately 7,520-7,540 ft — not the 7,570 ft in the given peak record — with the separate west summit at ~7,440+ ft. Recommend rechecking/adjusting the peak's elevationFt; see the route-level correction note for detail.
blurb: The Triad is a rugged, rarely-visited three-summited peak (west, middle, and east tops) on the southwest ridge extension of Eldorado Peak in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, roughly 12 miles east of Marblemount. Composed of granodioritic orthogneiss dating to the Late Cretaceous-Eocene, it rises above Hidden Lake and Sibley Creek and is crossed by a small unnamed glacier on its north side. First climbed in 1949 by Dick Eilertsen, Dick Lowery, Dick Scales, and Don Wilde — whose three shared first names inspired the party's original nickname "Three Dicks" before the more decorous "Triad" was adopted — it remains a serious, lightly-traveled objective reached via the Hidden Lake Trail.

routes.overview: The standard East Ridge line on Triad's East Peak follows a heather ridge and short glacier crossing to the base of the ridge, then climbs a moderate 4th-class section to a final exposed low-5th-class (5.4) summit pinnacle — a long, remote day out with real exposure despite its modest technical grade.
routes.beta: From the Hidden Lake Trail, leave the maintained tread to gain a heather-covered ridge, cross a small unnamed glacier on the peak's north side, and descend a steep, loose gully nicknamed the 'Drainage of Despair' to reach the base of the East Ridge. Climb ramps and a slabby section up and right to the ridge crest (an old rappel anchor marks easier ground above), then a short, exposed low-5th-class pinnacle pitch with sparse protection gains the true summit. The technical crux is a committing 4th-class move used to gain the ridge itself from a ledge system below.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from Marblemount about 10 miles to the FS Road 1540 (Sibley Creek/Hidden Lake Road) junction, then continue 4.5 miles up the steep, rough spur (high-clearance/4WD recommended) to the Hidden Lake Trailhead. Hike Hidden Lake Trail #745 through forest and wildflower meadows into heather and rock benches; about 1 hour up-trail, leave the maintained trail at a ridge turnoff and climb roughly 15 minutes to a saddle, then follow the heather ridge toward The Triad for about 30 minutes. Descend to the small glacier on the peak's north side, then down the loose 'Drainage of Despair' gully to the base of the East Ridge (about 4.5 hours car-to-saddle in trip reports).
routes.descent: Reverse the East Ridge with 1-2 rappels off the summit pinnacle, then downclimb back to the loose gully and glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel the low-5th-class pinnacle step (parties report two roughly 30m rappels off aging fixed anchors — bring a tagline so you can rappel at full rope length rather than doubled), downclimb the ramps and slabby section back to the ridge crest, then reverse the steep, loose 'Drainage of Despair' gully back onto the small glacier and heather ridge to regain the Hidden Lake Trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (single push, common approach): Drive to Hidden Lake Trailhead, start pre-dawn (~4-5 AM). Hike ~1 hour to the ridge turnoff, ~15 min to the saddle, ~30 min along the heather ridge, then down onto the glacier and through the 'Drainage of Despair' gully to the East Ridge base (~4.5 hrs car-to-saddle). Climb the East Ridge to the summit pinnacle and top out (~1-1.5 additional hours). Descend via rappel and reverse the approach (~4-4.5 hrs), for a car-to-car day of roughly 10-11 hours.
routes.bail: Retreat below the summit pinnacle is straightforward — downclimb the ramps/slabs back to the ridge crest and reverse the approach. Above the pinnacle's committing 4th-class move, rappel from the existing fixed/old anchor near the crux rather than attempting to downclimb it.
routes.turnaround: This is a long day (commonly 10+ hours car-to-car) over exposed ridge, a loose descent gully, and glacier terrain, so start pre-dawn and set a firm turnaround time that guarantees a full-daylight descent — don't push for the summit pinnacle if you're running significantly behind a mid-morning pace to the ridge saddle.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, after the approach trail and heather ridge melt out but before early-season snow returns. The small glacier crossing is firmest (and safest) in the early morning and tends to be most straightforward in mid-to-late summer; a lingering early-season snowpack can simplify some talus travel but adds crevasse and avalanche considerations, and warm afternoons have been observed triggering wet, loose debris on south-facing slopes near the peak.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service should be expected on the approach or route; North Cascades National Park backcountry generally has little to no coverage, though isolated high ridgelines occasionally catch a weak signal. Do not rely on a phone for emergency communication — a satellite messenger or PLB is strongly recommended.
routes.rappels: 1-2 rappels off the summit pinnacle, reported at roughly 30m each; carry a tagline to rappel at full rope length rather than doubling the lead line
objHaz: loose rock in the descent gully ('Drainage of Despair')
crevasses on the small unnamed glacier
wet, loose rock/debris shed from south-facing slopes on warm afternoons
sparse protection and exposure on the summit pinnacle pitch
bears reported on the approach ridge/trail
routes.face: East Ridge
routes.detailedRack: A minimal rack suffices: a handful of small-to-medium cams and a few nuts for the short low-5th-class pinnacle pitch, plus a couple of slings for the existing (aging) fixed rappel anchors near the ridge crest. Carry a single rope with a lightweight tagline, or a thin double-rope setup, so the roughly 30m rappels off the summit block can be run at full rope length.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse on the crux pinnacle pitch — natural gear placements are limited despite generally solid rock, so the party should be comfortable on runout, low-5th-class terrain.
gear: Light alpine rack (small-to-medium cams and nuts)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass for trailhead parking
proTips: Start pre-dawn (parties commonly leave the car around 4-5 AM) to cross the glacier while it's still firm and to leave a full margin for the exposed, loose descent.
Bring a lightweight tagline in addition to your lead rope so you can make full-length rappels off the summit pinnacle instead of doubling a single rope.
Aluminum crampons and a light axe are sufficient for the short glacier crossing in typical summer conditions.
Check FS Road 1540's status before driving — a washout partway up the spur has repeatedly closed the road short of the trailhead, adding miles of extra approach on foot.
watchOut: Don't mistake this for a simple scramble — the East Ridge has real 4th/low-5th-class climbing with an exposed, committing move to gain the ridge.
Old, weathered rappel anchors exist near the ridge crest; inspect them carefully before trusting them for a rappel.
Bears have been encountered on the approach trail/ridge in late summer — store food properly.

FS Road 1540 (the Hidden Lake Trailhead spur) is prone to washouts and has been closed to vehicles at milepost 2.6 in recent seasons — confirm current status with the Mt. Baker Ranger District before you drive up.
knownHazards: The short glacier crossing on the peak's north side can conceal crevasses, particularly later in the season; a rope and basic glacier travel gear are recommended.
The gully below the East Ridge, nicknamed the 'Drainage of Despair,' is loose rock and dirt over steep rock and heather, and has produced stressful, sketchy descents for multiple parties.
South-facing approach terrain has been observed shedding wet, loose debris during warm afternoons.
Protection on the summit pinnacle pitch is sparse despite its modest 5.4 grade, and several parties have chosen to rope up and rappel it rather than downclimb.

pitchDetail.notes: From the Hidden Lake Trail ridge turnoff, follow the heather-covered ridge, then descend onto the small unnamed glacier on the peak's north side (rope/light glacier gear advisable, especially early season).
Down the steep, loose 'Drainage of Despair' gully (loose rock/dirt over steep rock and heather) to gain the base of the East Ridge.

Ramps lead up and right to a slabby section that switches back near the ridge crest; the crux is a committing 4th-class move to gain the ridge from a ledge below. An old rappel anchor marks the crest.
A short, exposed low-5th-class pitch on generally good holds but sparse protection gains the true East Peak summit; most parties rope up for this section and rappel it on descent.

road.name: Cascade River Road to FS Road 1540 (Sibley Creek/Hidden Lake Road)
road.status: FS Road 1540 is closed to vehicles at milepost 2.6 due to a washout (per Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alert current as of July 2026); Cascade River Road itself has also experienced washout-related closures/repairs in recent years.
road.seasonalGate: FS 1540 is a rough, high-clearance spur that is only reliably snow-free and driveable in summer; check with the Mt. Baker Ranger District for the season's opening date and current washout status.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount on Hwy 20, drive Cascade River Road roughly 10 miles to the FS 1540 junction, then continue 4.5 miles up the steep, rutted Sibley Creek/Hidden Lake spur (high-clearance/4WD strongly recommended) to the trailhead. As of the current washout closure at mile 2.6, parties must add roughly 2+ miles of road walking each way until repairs are completed.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades); NWAC backcountry mountain-weather/avalanche forecasts also cover this part of the North Cascades
climate.typical: Cool, wet winters and springs with a heavy maritime snowpack giving way to a shorter drier window from roughly July through September.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack persists into early summer; glacier travel and avalanche hazard are significant considerations on the approach.
climate.summer: The most reliable climbing window; the trail and heather ridge are typically snow-free by mid-to-late July, and this is when nearly all recorded ascents have taken place.
climate.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency; the first significant snowfall can arrive by late September or October, quickly closing the technical window.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard turn the approach into a ski/snowshoe undertaking, and the access road is typically unplowed and effectively closed.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (the climb itself) / Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach trail and access road)
access.fees: No NPS entrance fee; a trailhead parking pass is required on the Forest Service portion of the approach.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit from North Cascades National Park is required for any overnight stay in the park (e.g., camping en route); a single-day car-to-car climb requires no permit.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Interagency Pass at the Hidden Lake Trailhead.
access.closures: FS Road 1540 is currently closed to vehicles at milepost 2.6 due to a washout (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alert, current as of July 2026), lengthening the approach; confirm status with the Mt. Baker Ranger District, (360) 854-2553, before driving up.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn, roughly 4-5 AM, to cross the glacier while it's still firm and to ensure a full-daylight round trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: (360) 336-9450 — Skagit County Sheriff, the after-hours emergency contact for North Cascades NP search and rescue
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7200; NPS Communications Center/SAR — (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 (business hours) or (360) 873-4590 ext. 37 (after hours)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (Skagit Valley Hospital in Mount Vernon and Cascade Valley Hospital in Arlington are further alternatives)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is minimal to nonexistent in this part of the North Cascades backcountry; carry a satellite messenger or PLB. In any life-threatening emergency, call 911 first.

routes.corrections: The peak record lists elevationFt 7,570, but Wikipedia, Peakbagger, PeakVisor, and ListsOfJohn all independently place The Triad's highest points (middle and east summits, which are essentially co-equal) at approximately 7,520-7,540 ft, with ListsOfJohn's LiDAR-informed figure at 7,540 ft — used here as this route's highPointFt. The separate west summit is lower still, at roughly 7,440+ ft. No route-specific first ascent could be confirmed for the East Ridge/East Peak line specifically; the documented 1949 first ascent (Eilertsen, Lowery, Scales, Wilde) is attributed to "The Triad" as a whole and is not confirmed as an East Peak-specific route FA, so 'fa' is left null rather than assumed.

blurb: Vasiliki Ridge is a roughly three-mile-long crest of Golden Horn batholith granite in the Methow Mountains (a North Cascades sub-range) in Okanogan County, Washington, plainly visible from Washington Pass along the North Cascades Highway. Its high point — Ares Tower, at 8,203 ft — is one of several named granite spires strung along the ridge (with Charon Tower, The Acropolis, Vasiliki Tower, the Juno-Jupiter Towers, Aphrodite Tower, and Baccus Tower), first climbed by Fred Beckey and Herb Staley on May 31, 1952; Beckey named the ridge after a woman he met and briefly dated that year.
hierarchyNote: Name/location/elevation check out: this is a real, correctly identified summit. Peakbagger's recent USGS LiDAR survey pins Ares Tower's true summit at 8,202.8 ft ± 0.1 ft (NAVD88) — matching the given 8,203 ft exactly and confirming it as "the highest point referred to as Ares Tower in the Beckey guidebook." One thing worth flagging for the catalog generally: there is a separate, lower, easily-confused summit on the same ridge called "Vasiliki Tower" (~7,663-7,940 ft depending on source), about 1/2 mile away. It is NOT this peak — if "Vasiliki Tower" ever appears elsewhere in the DB as its own area, don't merge it with this one (Ares Tower/Vasiliki Ridge high point).

routes.overview: Follows Vasiliki Ridge's crest north from Burgundy Col to Ares Tower: 4th-class granite scrambling to a notch just below the true summit, then one short, exposed low-5th-class step (rope recommended) to the top.
routes.beta: This is the standard line up the ridge's true high point, first climbed by Fred Beckey and Herb Staley in 1952. From Burgundy Col, work north along/just below the ridge crest on 4th-class rock to a notch on the north side of the summit block, then scramble south around the west side of a large summit boulder to the base of a short low-5th-class step that gains the true summit. An old fixed rappel anchor sits at the top for the descent. Most parties do it as a long day from the Washington Pass pullout, and many combine it with the neighboring Acropolis and Niki Tower to the south for an efficient multi-summit day on the ridge.
routes.approach: Park at the informal pullout on the east side of SR-20 at roughly 4,300-4,310 ft, about 3.75 miles east (downhill) of the Washington Pass summit. A faint but noticeable boot path drops down loose talus to the east and becomes a real trail once in the trees. Cross Early Winters Creek (log crossing), then follow the unmaintained but well-defined climbers' trail, with switchbacks, up through forest and past a bivy site known as "The Ledge" about two miles in. Snow often still covers the trail above roughly 6,200 ft into early summer. The trail continues above the bench, traversing scree gullies, to Burgundy Col (about 7,770 ft) roughly 3 miles and ~3,500 ft above the creek crossing. From the Col, continue north along the ridge toward Ares Tower.
routes.descent: Downclimb the 4th-class terrain back to the notch, then rappel the short low-5th-class step off the old fixed anchor near the summit (inspect/back it up) before reversing the approach.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, rappel the short low-5th-class step using the old fixed anchor near the top (back it up — it is aged gear) to regain the notch. From there, downclimb/scramble the 4th-class terrain back south along the ridge to Burgundy Col. Retrace the approach trail down through the scree gullies and forest switchbacks, past The Ledge bivy site, back down to and across Early Winters Creek, and up the talus/boot path to the SR-20 pullout.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (or single push): Drive to the Washington Pass-area pullout (~4,300 ft) on SR-20. Descend to and cross Early Winters Creek, then climb the approach trail ~3 miles / ~3,500 ft to Burgundy Col, passing The Ledge bivy around the 2-mile mark. Continue along the ridge crest to Ares Tower, climb the 4th-class terrain to the notch, then the short roped low-5th-class step to the true summit. Optionally continue south to tag The Acropolis and Niki Tower before descending. Rappel/downclimb back to the Col and retrace the approach to the car. Parties splitting the trip over two days often camp at The Ledge or a bench camp near the Col.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point below the summit step: downclimb the 4th-class terrain back to the notch and Burgundy Col. The single technical move (the low-5th-class step to the true summit) can be rappelled from the fixed anchor if conditions or ability dictate turning back.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone is a long, unmaintained 3-mile, ~3,500 ft trail before any technical climbing begins, and the North Cascades crest is prone to fast-building summer afternoon thunderstorms. Don't commit to the roped summit step if storms are building, and don't linger on the exposed summit block or ridge crest once weather starts to turn.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep is the reliable window. Snow can linger on the approach trail above ~6,200 ft and near Burgundy Col well into June even in an average year (the 1952 first ascent was climbed May 31 with snow present), so early-season parties should be prepared for snow travel on the approach. Check recent trip reports for current conditions before counting on a dry scramble.
routes.comms: No cell service through the Washington Pass corridor / North Cascades Highway backcountry — this is a well-documented dead zone with no services of any kind at the pass. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB; do not rely on a phone signal for a call-out.
routes.detailedRack: Only one short section is technical: a single 30-40m rope and a minimal rack (a handful of slings plus a few small-to-mid cams/nuts) are enough to protect the low-5th-class step to the true summit. The rest of the route is unroped 4th-class scrambling. An old fixed rappel anchor is present at the summit for the descent — inspect it and back it up rather than trusting it outright.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed overall — the route is mostly exposed but unroped scrambling, with a single short roped move near the top.
gear: approach shoes or light scrambling boots
whatToBring: Ice axe and microspikes/crampons for lingering snow near the approach trail and Burgundy Col in early season
Sun protection — long exposed hours on scree/talus and open ridge
Bivy/camp gear if splitting the trip over two days or linking Acropolis/Niki Tower
Map/compass or GPS track — the approach is an unmaintained boot path with a creek crossing
Satellite communicator/PLB — no cell coverage anywhere on route
proTips: Link Ares Tower with the neighboring Acropolis (a fun 4th-class ridge scramble to the south) and Niki Tower for an efficient day tagging multiple Vasiliki Ridge summits.
The Ledge, a bivy site about two miles up the approach trail, makes a good base if splitting the trip over two days.
Snow frequently lingers near Burgundy Col into early summer even after the lower trail is dry — check recent conditions reports first.
watchOut: Loose, blocky granite typical of the Wine Spires group — test holds carefully, especially on the 4th-class approach.
The fixed rappel anchor at the summit is old gear — inspect and back it up rather than trusting it blindly.
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly along the North Cascades crest in summer, with no quick escape from the exposed ridge/summit block.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock on the 4th-class terrain below the summit.
Exposure on the low-5th-class summit step.
Lingering snow/ice on the approach trail and near Burgundy Col into early summer.
No cell coverage anywhere on the approach or climb — self-rescue/satellite comm planning required.

pitchDetail.notes: From Burgundy Col, work north along/just below the ridge crest on 4th-class granite to the notch just north of the true summit.
Short, exposed roped move out of the notch (scramble around the west side of a large summit boulder) to gain the true summit; old fixed rap anchor at the top.

routes.rappels: 1 rappel from an old fixed anchor near the summit to retreat down the short low-5th-class step; the remainder of the route downclimbs on 4th-class terrain.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Herb Staley, May 31, 1952 (first ascent of Vasiliki Ridge's high point, Ares Tower)
routes.rockGrade: low-mid 5th class (4th class terrain plus one short low-5th-class/~5.4 step to the true summit)

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Open seasonally; paved highway to the trailhead pullout
road.seasonalGate: Gated near Ross Dam (west) and the Early Winters area (east) each winter, typically closing in early December and not reopening until mid-April at the earliest, depending on snowpack and avalanche conditions
road.driveNote: Trailhead is an unmarked, informal roadside pullout on the east side of Washington Pass at roughly 4,300-4,310 ft, about 3.75 miles east of the pass summit — no water, services, or cell coverage at the pullout.

climate.typical: Maritime-influenced Cascades climate with heavy winter snowpack from orographic lift; this east-of-crest terrain (Golden Horn batholith) is somewhat drier than the west-slope Cascades but still holds substantial snow into early summer.
climate.spring: SR-20 typically doesn't reopen until mid-April; snow lingers on the approach trail and near Burgundy Col into June.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window (Jun-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms are common along the Cascades crest.
climate.fall: Often stable, dry weather into September/October before the highway closes for winter (typically early December).
climate.winter: SR-20 is gated and unplowed through this section, making the approach essentially inaccessible by vehicle.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required. The Burgundy Col approach starts from an informal roadside pullout, not a developed fee trailhead (unlike nearby Blue Lake, Cutthroat Lake, or Rainy Pass, which do require a Northwest Forest Pass/Interagency pass or day-use fee).
access.closures: SR-20/North Cascades Highway closes seasonally (roughly early December to mid-April) making the drive-in approach inaccessible in winter.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff/911 Dispatch — 911 for emergencies; non-emergency line 509-422-7232 (or 800-572-6604 within the 509 area code)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 W Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA 98862 — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (down the Methow Valley from Winthrop); Methow Valley's Confluence Health clinic in Winthrop provides closer but non-hospital urgent/primary care
emergency.notes: This is a remote, unmaintained-trail approach off a seasonally-gated highway with no cell coverage — carry a satellite communicator/PLB and file a trip plan; Okanogan County SAR covers the North Cascades Highway corridor.

routes.corrections: No correction needed — the given elevationFt (8,203) already reflects Peakbagger's precise, LiDAR-based survey of Ares Tower's true summit (8,202.8 ft ± 0.1 ft, NAVD88), which is more accurate than the older, rounded 8,190 ft figure commonly cited on Wikipedia/USGS topo-derived sources.

blurb: Summertime Crag is a small bolt-protected sport-climbing cliff at Washington Pass in the North Cascades, reached by a short off-trail approach from the Mazama side of Highway 20 that crosses Early Winters Creek. Carved from the granite of the Golden Horn batholith (the same rock as nearby Liberty Bell and the Early Winters Spires), it offers steep (~80°) face climbing on mostly short (40-70 ft) single- and two-pitch sport lines plus one longer five-pitch trad/sport line, with views across the valley to Liberty Bell and South Early Winters Spire. It holds shade until mid-afternoon, making it a popular cooler alternative when Mazama and the sun-exposed Washington Pass walls get too hot; most of its routes were established by local climber Jason Keith ("JMK") around 2015.
hierarchyNote: Confirmed real and correctly placed: Summertime Crag is a genuine, minor bolted sport-climbing cliff at Washington Pass in the North Cascades (Mountain Project and theCrag both list it; GPS 48.52006, -120.6398, elev. ~5,231 ft matches the Washington Pass / Mazama parent area). One data-quality flag outside this enrichment's scope: every route here is tagged discipline "alpine" in the DB, but all reputable sources describe this uniformly as a bolt-protected sport/toprope face-climbing crag (short flat approach, chain anchors, 40-400 ft routes) rather than an alpine venue — worth a discipline-classification review. Also note: neither SummitPost nor Peakbagger has a dedicated page for this crag (it is not indexed as a summit/peak on either site, as expected for a minor sport crag rather than a mountain); Mountain Project is the authoritative source here, cross-checked against theCrag's area listing confirming the crag's existence and location within the Washington Pass climbing area.

routes.fa: Jason Keith
routes.face: far left side of the crag
routes.rappels: Lower/rappel from chain anchors at the top of each pitch (single-rope capable given route length).
routes.overview: The best-regarded line at Summertime Crag: a technical, reachy bolted face pitch on the far-left side of the cliff, followed by a short 5.10 second pitch to a ledge.
routes.beta: P1 climbs left past several bolts ('pinch the peanut and step up right into the dish'); the crux reaches right up a buttress-like feature past more bolts to chains. P2 is a shorter ~50 ft 5.10 pitch above the belay to a ledge with more chains.
routes.approach: From Mazama, park before the Spires Gully pullout on SR-20 (Washington Pass area). Descend from the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it downstream briefly, then climb the slope toward the crag, contouring left; about 20-25 minutes one-way.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel from the chain anchors back to the ground.
routes.descentText: From either belay/anchor, lower or make a single-rope rappel back to the base of the route; reverse the approach (descend to the creek, cross, climb back up to the road) to return to the car.
routes.bail: Fully bolted to chains at both pitch anchors — lower off from either belay at any point if needed.
routes.turnaround: Given the short approach and route, the main turnaround trigger is deteriorating weather or losing daylight/route-finding on the unmarked forest approach on the way out.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly late June through September, matching the general Washington Pass season; the crag's shade until mid-afternoon makes it attractive on hot summer days.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through the Methow Valley/Washington Pass corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport line; bring 12-14 quickdraws and a 60m rope (70m gives more lowering margin).
routes.proNeeds: No trad gear required — bolts only.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the creek crossing and off-trail forest approach
A layer for belaying in the shade
proTips: Highest-rated route at the crag (MP average ~3.3 stars) — worth prioritizing if choosing one line.
The crag stays shaded until mid-afternoon, so it's a good midday/hot-weather pick.
watchOut: Off-trail approach is only informally marked — easy to lose the line, especially descending in low light.
Early Winters Creek can run high in early season.
knownHazards: Unmaintained, off-trail approach with a creek crossing.
Lightly-trafficked crag — confirm current bolt/anchor condition before climbing.

pitchDetail.notes: Climbs left past bolts ('pinch the peanut, step up right into the dish'); crux reaches right up a buttress feature past bolts to chains.
~50 ft second pitch above the belay to a ledge with more chains.

routes.fa: JMK
routes.face: 4th route from left (shares its first 2-3 bolts with the 2nd and 3rd routes)
objHaz: Route length means more exposure to weather than the crag's shorter lines
routes.overview: The crag's namesake and longest route: a 5-pitch, Grade II trad/sport line that 'meanders around following the easiest line' up the tallest formation, with a bolted-plus-small-gear crux roof pitch (5.11c) partway up.
routes.beta: P1 follows bolts at 5.10 to chains, then trends left past additional anchor sets. The next section is a corner with traditional gear and a slab. The standout crux pitch is bolted with small trad gear, trending up-right through corners and over a roof (5.11c) to an anchor, after which easier 5.10 slabby climbing leads to the top of the cliff.
routes.approach: From Mazama, park before the Spires Gully pullout on SR-20 (Washington Pass area). Descend from the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it downstream briefly, then climb the slope toward the crag, contouring left; about 20-25 minutes one-way.
routes.descentText: Descent method beyond the crux pitch is not detailed in available sources; anchors/chains are passed at several points along the route, suggesting parties can lower/rappel pitch-by-pitch from these, or continue to the top. Confirm current beta before committing to the full 5-pitch line.
routes.bail: Multiple chained anchors are passed along the route (after P1 and at the crux pitch), allowing a lower/rappel bail from partway up.
routes.turnaround: As the longest route at the crag, weather changes or slowing pace partway up are the main reasons to bail via one of the intermediate chain anchors rather than push to the top.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly late June through September, matching the general Washington Pass season.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through the Methow Valley/Washington Pass corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted low down, but bring a small trad rack (small cams and wires) for the corner sections and the crux roof pitch, plus 12+ quickdraws and a 60-70m rope.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly bolted; described as 'bolts with smallish trad gear' on the crux pitch and 'mixed gear and bolts above' — bring a light trad rack in addition to draws.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the creek crossing and off-trail approach
Extra time buffer given it's the longest route on the wall
proTips: This is the tallest, most involved line at the crag (Grade II) — budget more time than for the crag's single-pitch routes.
watchOut: Off-trail approach is only informally marked.
Route length means more commitment than the crag's shorter sport lines — confirm the bail/anchor plan before starting.
knownHazards: Unmaintained, off-trail approach with a creek crossing.
Mixed bolt/trad protection requires carrying and placing gear, unlike the crag's fully-bolted routes.

pitchDetail.notes: Bolts to chains, then trends left past additional anchor sets.
Corner pitch with traditional gear leading into a slab section.
Bolts with small trad gear, trending up-right through corners and over a roof to the anchor.
Easier, slabbier climbing to the top of the cliff.

routes.fa: JMK
routes.face: 5th route from left; a continuation pitch above Summertime's first pitch
routes.overview: A short continuation pitch that climbs directly out of the top of Summertime's first pitch, following a seam/crack past a flake and finishing over a roof.
routes.beta: From the top of Summertime P1, continue up a seam/crack with small gear, then move up and right past a flake with bolts and over a roof (5.11c) to the anchor.
routes.approach: From Mazama, park before the Spires Gully pullout on SR-20 (Washington Pass area). Descend from the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it downstream briefly, then climb the slope toward the crag, contouring left; about 20-25 minutes one-way. Reaching the start of this route also requires climbing (or being lowered to) the top of Summertime's first pitch.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel from the anchor back to the ground.
routes.descentText: Lower or make a single-rope rappel from the top anchor back to the base; reverse the approach to return to the car.
routes.bail: Short, bolted finish — the anchor at the top is reachable in one pitch; there is no intermediate bail point below the roof.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly late June through September, matching the general Washington Pass season.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through the Methow Valley/Washington Pass corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Small gear (small cams/wires) for the seam/crack start, plus a handful of quickdraws for the bolted flake-and-roof finish.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed: small trad gear low, then bolts through the crux roof.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the creek crossing
watchOut: Starting point requires accessing the top of Summertime's first pitch rather than the ground.
Off-trail approach is only informally marked.

routes.fa: JMK
routes.face: rightmost start at the crag
routes.overview: The crag's hardest-rated line, starting from the rightmost position: a bolted 5.10+ first pitch to chains, then a right-trending second pitch onto a slab whose grade and free-climbing status were unconfirmed as of the last documented ascent (2015).
routes.beta: P1 climbs past bolts to chains at roughly 5.10+. P2 moves right and up a slab reported as '5.12? — not freed as of 2015' on Mountain Project, i.e. the pitch's grade and completion status are unresolved in available beta.
routes.approach: From Mazama, park before the Spires Gully pullout on SR-20 (Washington Pass area). Descend from the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it downstream briefly, then climb the slope toward the crag, contouring left; about 20-25 minutes one-way.
routes.descentText: Lower/rappel from the P1 chains is documented; descent method from the P2 slab is not detailed in available sources — verify current beta before attempting the full route.
routes.bail: Lower from the P1 chain anchor if the unconfirmed second pitch is not desired.
routes.turnaround: Given the second pitch's uncertain grade and unclear free-climbing history, treat it as a point to reassess and be ready to bail via the P1 anchor.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly late June through September, matching the general Washington Pass season.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through the Methow Valley/Washington Pass corridor.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted P1 to chains — standard quickdraws suffice. P2 protection is not clearly documented given its unconfirmed/unfreed status as of 2015.
routes.proNeeds: P1 is bolted; P2's protection and grade are unresolved in available beta — confirm current status before committing.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the creek crossing
Willingness to bail from P1 if P2 conditions/beta are unclear
proTips: Check for more recent trip reports/ticks before attempting P2 — as of the last documented beta (2015) it had not been confirmed free.
watchOut: P2 grade and free-climbing status were unconfirmed as of 2015 — verify current beta before committing.
Off-trail approach is only informally marked.
knownHazards: Unmaintained, off-trail approach with a creek crossing.
Uncertain protection/grade on the upper pitch.

routes.approach: From Mazama, park before the Spires Gully pullout on SR-20 (Washington Pass area). Descend from the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it downstream briefly, then climb the slope toward the crag, contouring left; about 20-25 minutes one-way.

knownHazards: Unmaintained, off-trail approach with a creek crossing.
Lightly-trafficked crag — confirm current bolt/anchor condition before climbing.

pitchDetail.notes: Shares the start with Chitlins Con Carne; goes straight up after the 3rd bolt into a hard slab crux, then continues past bolts (5.11-) to chains.

road.name: State Route 20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: Seasonal — paved but gated in winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically closes near Ross Dam (MP134) / Silver Star (MP171) gates in early December due to avalanche danger; reopens in spring once conditions allow.
road.driveNote: From Mazama, drive SR-20 to the pullout/parking area before the Spires Gully parking area in the Washington Pass climbing zone.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Slopes North Cascades / Washington Pass area
climate.typical: Dry, generally stable summer weather from late June through September; afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.spring: Snow/ice can linger into June; SR-20 may still be closed.
climate.summer: Warm and often hot at lower elevations.
climate.fall: Cools and can turn wet/snowy after September.
climate.winter: Highway closed for the season.

blurb: Mamie Peak (6,108 ft) is a complex mass of granite in the North Cascades of Whatcom County, Washington, rising above the Hannegan Pass Trail in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. A limited but excellent cluster of trad and sport crag routes was developed on its center buttress starting around 2009, chiefly by Darin Berdinka and various partners, offering multipitch and single-pitch granite climbing as a lower-traffic alternative to Washington's busier crags.

routes.fa: Darin Berdinka & Owen Lunz
routes.face: Broad arete forming the right-hand side of the Mamie Peak crag
routes.overview: A short, punchy single-pitch sport line up balancy friction and face holds on the broad arete forming the right side of the Mamie Peak crag.
routes.beta: Fully bolted (8 bolts) single-pitch sport route starting from a ledge about 25 feet off the ground; features small intrusions used as footholds/handholds on otherwise blank-looking granite. Low traffic and lightly documented — expect an adventurous, quiet outing.
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 255 (Bellingham), take WA-542 (Mt Baker Hwy) east about 46 miles toward Glacier; continue roughly 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center and turn left (north) onto Forest Road #32 (Hannegan Pass Road); follow FR-32 about 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead (el. ~3,100 ft). Hike the Hannegan Pass Trail a few minutes to a clearing/cross-ditch, leave the trail, and traverse through brush into a dry creekbed heading toward a large white boulder; continue up through forest and mossy slabs to the base of the crag. About 1 hour and 1,200 ft of gain total.
routes.descent: Rappel from the anchor with a single rope.
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the single pitch back to the base of the crag; a standard single rope reaches the ground from the anchor. Retrace the brushy climbers' path back down to the Hannegan Pass Trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.turnaround: Since this is a short single-pitch route, turnaround pressure is more about the approach/descent through brush and unmaintained terrain than the climbing itself — start early enough to hike out in daylight, especially if the use-path is unfamiliar.
routes.bestSeason: Short season, dependent on snowpack in the approach gully; the crag sits high enough above the Ruth Creek valley floor to avoid the valley's notorious mid-summer black flies.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted; no natural gear required.
routes.proNeeds: 8 bolts to a fixed anchor — bring 8-10 quickdraws.
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to brushy/off-trail hiking
proTips: Aim for the large white boulder on the approach to help locate the correct bushwhack line.
Low-traffic, lightly documented crag — check for recent trip reports on current trail/approach conditions before going.
watchOut: The approach beyond the initial trail junction is unmaintained and brushy; it's easy to lose the use-path.
Route sees little traffic, so holds and anchors have seen limited testing/wear compared to popular crags.
knownHazards: Black flies are a significant mid-summer nuisance in the Ruth Creek valley floor below the crag, though the crag itself sits above the worst of it.
Access trail is lightly maintained, increasing route-finding difficulty on the approach.

road.status: Generally drivable to the road-end trailhead (5.3 mi from WA-542); flood/storm damage has at times limited access for larger vehicles beyond about mile 5.2 — verify current status before driving.
road.driveNote: Turn onto FR-32 from WA-542 about 12.5 miles east of the Glacier Public Service Center and follow it 5.3 miles to the road-end Hannegan Trailhead (el. ~3,100 ft).

routes.fa: Justin Thibault & Darin Berdinka
routes.rappels: Single-rope rappel from the chain anchor at the top (~90 ft).
routes.face: Center of the Mamie Peak crag, up a large thin-flake handcrack
routes.overview: A single-pitch handcrack splitting a large, thin rock flake up the center of the crag — sustained, well-protected trad climbing to a chain anchor.
routes.beta: Nice, continuous handcrack formed by a very large, very thin flake. Starts from a good ledge about 25 feet off the ground, reached either by rappelling in after climbing Woodland Critter Christmas, or via a short 5.10 pitch linking into Woodland Critter Christmas before starting Blood Orgy proper.
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 255 (Bellingham), take WA-542 (Mt Baker Hwy) east about 46 miles toward Glacier; continue roughly 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center and turn left (north) onto Forest Road #32 (Hannegan Pass Road); follow FR-32 about 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead (el. ~3,100 ft). Hike the Hannegan Pass Trail a few minutes to a clearing/cross-ditch, leave the trail, and traverse through brush into a dry creekbed heading toward a large white boulder; continue up through forest and mossy slabs to the base of the crag, then either rappel in from above (via Woodland Critter Christmas) or climb a short 5.10 connector pitch to the starting ledge.
routes.descent: Rappel from the chain anchor with a single rope.
routes.descentText: From the chain anchor atop the flake, rappel ~90 ft with a single rope back to the starting ledge/base, then descend the brushy climbers' path back to the trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.turnaround: As a short single-pitch climb, the main time pressure is the brushy approach/descent — allow enough daylight margin to hike out safely if the use-path is unfamiliar.
routes.bestSeason: Short season, dependent on snowpack in the approach gully; the crag sits high enough above the Ruth Creek valley floor to avoid the valley's notorious mid-summer black flies.
routes.detailedRack: Double set of cams from finger-size to hand-size to protect the sustained crack.
routes.proNeeds: Steady handcrack protection; a double rack fingers-to-hands is recommended given the sustained, consistent crack width.
gear: double cam rack, fingers to hands
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to brushy/off-trail hiking
proTips: Combine with Woodland Critter Christmas (rappel in or climb the short 5.10 connector) rather than approaching Blood Orgy in isolation.
Low-traffic crag — check for recent conditions/trail reports before going.
watchOut: The approach beyond the initial trail junction is unmaintained and brushy; it's easy to lose the use-path.
Starting ledge is ~25 ft off the ground, so the start itself requires either a rappel-in or a linking pitch.
knownHazards: Black flies are a significant mid-summer nuisance in the Ruth Creek valley floor below the crag, though the crag itself sits above the worst of it.
Access trail is lightly maintained, increasing route-finding difficulty on the approach.

routes.fa: Matt Alford & Darin Berdinka October 2009
routes.aidGrade: 5.10 A0 (optional aid alternative to the 5.11a free crux on pitch 8)
routes.rappels: 8 single-rope (70m) rappels down the route from fixed/bolted anchors; the anchor above pitch 3 sits about 30 ft climber's-left of the belay (rap off a tree there); manage ropes carefully below pitch 6 to avoid snagging in cracks/flakes.
objHaz: loose rock/hollow flakes on some pitches
routes.face: Center buttress of Mamie Peak, the main 8-pitch line up the best rock on the peak
routes.overview: An 8-pitch, roughly 800 ft outing up the best rock on Mamie Peak's center buttress: three pitches of techy slab lead into five sustained, exposed pitches of face, friction, and crack climbing to a 5.11a crux (or 5.10 A0 alternative).
routes.beta: Named for FA climber Matt Alford's daughter Ella. Pitch 1 is a bolted groove; pitches 2-3 mix flakes, thin cracks, seams, and steep friction (the mental crux); pitches 4-7 climb knobby, well-bolted face and corner terrain up to sustained 5.10; pitch 8 finishes on a steep juggy face into a flaring corner at 5.11a (or 5.10 with a point of aid). Protection mixes bolts with natural gear throughout — most pitches are partially bolted.
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 255 (Bellingham), take WA-542 (Mt Baker Hwy) east about 46 miles toward Glacier; continue roughly 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center and turn left (north) onto Forest Road #32 (Hannegan Pass Road); follow FR-32 about 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead (el. ~3,100 ft). Start up the Hannegan Pass Trail; about 5 minutes in, leave the trail and hike up toward a large white boulder, traversing through brush into a dry creekbed. Continue up through forest and mossy slabs, following a climbers' path through steep timber to the base of the route. About 1 hour and roughly 1,200 ft of approach gain to the base.
routes.descent: Rappel the route: 8 single-rope (70m) raps from bolted/fixed anchors back to the base.
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the line of ascent using a single 70m rope for all 8 rappels. The anchor above pitch 3 is offset about 30 ft to climber's-left (rap off a tree there rather than straight down). Take care feeding rope below pitch 6, where it can snag in cracks and flakes. Once at the base, retrace the brushy climbers' path down through timber and mossy slabs back to the Hannegan Pass Trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: A single long day: hike in (~1 hr), climb the 8 pitches, rappel the route back to the base (8 raps), then hike out. Not typically climbed as a multi-day or bivy objective.
routes.bail: Any bolted belay station can serve as a bail/rappel point on the way up; the route is set up for a full single-rope rappel descent from any pitch, so retreat is straightforward from most stances. Note the pitch-3 anchor is offset to climber's-left off a tree.
routes.turnaround: With 8 pitches plus a full rappel descent and a brushy approach/exit, build in a healthy margin — start early enough that a full ascent-and-rappel sequence, plus the hike out, comfortably finishes with daylight to spare, especially if you're unfamiliar with the crag or rope management on the raps.
routes.bestSeason: Short season, dependent on snowpack; conditions and access can vary significantly year to year. The crag sits high enough above the Ruth Creek valley floor to avoid the valley's notorious mid-summer black flies, and the approach trail has been reported overgrown/brushy in recent years — expect some route-finding on the way in.
routes.detailedRack: Double rack from small cams (red X4/C3-size) up to #1, plus single #2 and #3; offset cams reportedly work well on the seam pitches (especially pitch 3). Bring at least 10 quickdraws/slings for the well-bolted face pitches.
routes.proNeeds: A medium rack to 3" with doubles of fingers-to-tight-hand sizes covers the trad placements; most pitches are partially bolted, so 10+ draws are also needed.
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to brushy/off-trail hiking
proTips: Bring offset cams for the seam/crack terrain on pitch 3 — reported to work well by parties who've climbed it.
Identify the bushwhack approach line (toward the large white boulder) carefully; several parties note it's easier to find on the way down than up, given the overgrown trail.
Low-traffic, lightly documented route — check recent trip reports for current approach and route conditions before going.
watchOut: Hollow-sounding flakes and loose blocks have been encountered and cleaned on the route — test holds carefully.
The approach trail beyond the initial junction is unmaintained and has been reported heavily overgrown; expect bushwhacking.
Careful rope management is needed on the rappel below pitch 6 to prevent snagging.
knownHazards: Loose rock/hollow flakes reported on multiple pitches, requiring caution and occasional cleaning by parties.
Black flies are a significant mid-summer nuisance in the Ruth Creek valley floor below the crag, though the crag itself sits above the worst of it.
Access trail is lightly maintained and has been reported overgrown, increasing route-finding difficulty and time on the approach.

pitchDetail.notes: Bolted groove to a spacious ledge.
Flake, thin crack, and face moves to a bolted belay.
Right-leaning seam traverse into steep face on desperate friction moves — reported mental crux; offset cams useful.
Knobs and face holds; well-bolted.
Corner climbing finishing with a short bolted layback.
Ramp/corner with sparse gear leading into steep bolted face; manage rope carefully here on the rappel descent.
Sustained friction climbing protected by bolts and small cams.
Steep, juggy face into a flaring corner; crux pitch, can be aided at A0 to avoid the 5.11a free moves.

routes.corrections: Route length is reported as 700 ft by one trip-report source (stephabegg.com) vs. 800 ft on Mountain Project's official route page; both agree on 8 pitches. Used Mountain Project's 800 ft as the primary figure.

routes.fa: Darin Berdinka & Justin Thibault June 2009
routes.rappels: Single-rope rappel from the chain anchor at the top (~90 ft).
routes.face: Buttress near Ellation, up the obvious long fingercrack layback corner
routes.overview: A single-pitch fingercrack/layback corner on the buttress near Ellation, offering sustained 5.8+ crack climbing to a chain anchor; can be used as an approach/link pitch into Blood Orgy.
routes.beta: Follows the obvious long fingercrack layback corner on a buttress near Ellation. Chain anchor at the top. Often combined with Blood Orgy (rappel or downclimb between the two).
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 255 (Bellingham), take WA-542 (Mt Baker Hwy) east about 46 miles toward Glacier; continue roughly 12.5 miles past the Glacier Public Service Center and turn left (north) onto Forest Road #32 (Hannegan Pass Road); follow FR-32 about 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Trailhead (el. ~3,100 ft). Hike the Hannegan Pass Trail a few minutes to a clearing/cross-ditch, leave the trail, and traverse through brush into a dry creekbed heading toward a large white boulder; continue up through forest and mossy slabs to the base of the crag — the crag and route are obvious from the approach.
routes.descent: Rappel from the chain anchor with a single rope.
routes.descentText: From the chain anchor atop the route, rappel ~90 ft with a single rope back to the base. Some parties instead continue directly into Blood Orgy from the top before rapping both routes. Retrace the brushy climbers' path back down to the trail and out to the trailhead.
routes.turnaround: As a short single-pitch climb, the main time pressure is the brushy approach/descent — allow enough daylight margin to hike out safely if the use-path is unfamiliar.

routes.detailedRack: Rack to 3 inches, with doubles of 0.5" and 0.75" cams recommended for the sustained fingerlock/layback crack.
routes.proNeeds: Gear to 3 inches; double up on 0.5" and 0.75" sized cams for the sustained crack section.
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to brushy/off-trail hiking
proTips: Can be linked directly into Blood Orgy from the top rather than descending and re-approaching separately.
Double up on 0.5"-0.75" cams — the crack stays a consistent size for much of the pitch.
watchOut: The approach beyond the initial trail junction is unmaintained and brushy; it's easy to lose the use-path.
Sustained crack climbing at the grade — pumpy for a 5.8+ if pace/technique aren't dialed.
knownHazards: Black flies are a significant mid-summer nuisance in the Ruth Creek valley floor below the crag, though the crag itself sits above the worst of it.
