hierarchyNote: GEOGRAPHY MISMATCH: Steeple Rock is NOT in the North Cascades / Cascade Range at all. It is a ~200-ft basalt pillar in the OLYMPIC MOUNTAINS, inside Olympic National Park, Clallam County — beside Obstruction Point Road a few miles from the Hurricane Ridge Visitor Center, on the Olympic Peninsula (opposite side of Puget Sound from the Cascades). This is confirmed independently by Mountain Project (area is filed under "Olympics & Pacific Coast," area id 118351599), Wikipedia ("Steeple Rock (Washington)," Clallam County, Olympic Mountains), and SummitPost (approach directions from Port Angeles via Hurricane Ridge Road), all giving consistent coordinates around 47.962°N, -123.454°W. If this peak's parent chain in the database currently threads up through a North Cascades/Cascade Range node, that placement is wrong and should be corrected to an Olympic Mountains / Olympic National Park branch — I only researched and returned route content here, I did not have access to move the area in the hierarchy tree myself.
blurb: Steeple Rock is a roughly 200-foot basalt spire in Washington's Olympic Mountains, rising directly beside Obstruction Point Road a few miles from the Hurricane Ridge Visitor Center in Olympic National Park (Clallam County). Its rock is Eocene basaltic oceanic crust thrust up as part of the Olympic dome — geologically distinct from the granite and volcanic rock typical of the Cascades. The pillar offers several short, roadside trad lines ranging from easy 5th-class scrambles (East Ridge, North Ridge) up to the formation's hardest and most sustained pitch, Wings (5.8).

routes.overview: An easy 5th-class scramble up the east end of Steeple Rock, one of the two 'walk-up' lines on this small basalt pillar and a common descent option for parties who climbed a harder route.
routes.beta: Climbs the east end of Steeple Rock, scrambling along the south side of the ridge to a tree just below the summit. Short, low-angle, and often combined with a harder line as an up-and-over.
routes.face: East end of the formation, south side of the ridge
routes.season: Jul-Sep (limited by seasonal snow closure of Obstruction Point Road, which typically doesn't open until late June and closes again by October)

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble along the south side of the ridge on the east end of the formation to a tree just below the summit.

routes.approach: From Port Angeles, drive south to the Olympic National Park entrance and continue onto Hurricane Ridge Road for roughly 17 miles to its junction with Obstruction Point Road on the left. Follow the narrow, unpaved Obstruction Point Road about 2 miles, passing the Morse Creek/Cox Valley trailhead; the road climbs directly beneath Steeple Rock, a 200-ft basalt pillar visible on the left (north) side. Park along the road shoulder near the base.

road.name: Hurricane Ridge Road to Obstruction Point Road
road.status: Seasonal — both roads confirmed open in mid-2026 (Hurricane Ridge Rd as of 5/4/26, Obstruction Point Rd as of 6/17/26 per NPS)
road.seasonalGate: Gated closed each winter due to snow; Obstruction Point Road is unpaved, narrow, and winding, and is not suitable for RVs or trailers
road.driveNote: 17 mi of paved road (Hurricane Ridge Rd) followed by ~2 mi of gravel (Obstruction Point Rd); steep drop-offs and no guardrails in places

access.landManager: National Park Service — Olympic National Park
access.fees: NPS entrance fee applies: $30/vehicle (7-day) or $55 Olympic NP annual pass (2026 rates)
access.permit: No specific climbing permit found for day climbing at this roadside formation; overnight backcountry camping elsewhere in the park requires a wilderness permit
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful interagency pass or Olympic NP pass accepted at the entrance station
access.closures: Hurricane Ridge Road may close temporarily for weather; Obstruction Point Road is closed entirely outside the summer/fall snow-free window

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with strong orographic lift off Pacific storms — heavy precipitation, especially in winter; summers are comparatively dry

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Clallam County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: 360-417-2459 (24 hr); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, Port Angeles — (360) 565-3100
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, 939 Caroline St, Port Angeles, WA 98362

watchOut: Obstruction Point Road is narrow, unpaved, and winding with steep drop-offs and no guardrails in places.
The road is only open seasonally (roughly summer through early fall) — check current NPS road status before driving up.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light hiking boots (roadside approach)
Water — no water source at the rock
Rain shell — Olympic weather changes quickly
routes.corrections: Given elevation was null; sources disagree on Steeple Rock's summit elevation — Mountain Project lists 5,314 ft, Topozone (USGS quad) suggests ~5,440 ft, and Wikipedia (citing USGS GNIS/Mount Angeles quadrangle) lists 5,567 ft with 567 ft of prominence. Used the Wikipedia/GNIS-sourced figure as the most authoritative, but treat as approximate given the spread across sources.

routes.overview: A short, easy scramble gaining Steeple Rock's north ridgeline via scree and heather on the peak's west slope — the other 'walk-up' line on the formation.
routes.beta: From Obstruction Point Road, pick a line up scree and heather on the west slope to reach the north ridgeline, then follow easy class 2-3 / easy 5th-class terrain to the summit area.
routes.face: North ridge, gained via the west slope
routes.season: Jul-Sep (limited by seasonal snow closure of Obstruction Point Road, which typically doesn't open until late June and closes again by October)

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend scree and heather on the west slope to gain the north ridgeline, then follow easy terrain to the top.

routes.approach: Same access as East Ridge/Direct Finish/Wings: from Port Angeles via Hurricane Ridge Road (~17 mi) to Obstruction Point Road, then ~2 mi of gravel road past the Morse Creek/Cox Valley trailhead to the base of Steeple Rock. From the road, head to the west slope below the north ridgeline.

routes.overview: A short, direct finishing pitch on Steeple Rock that joins the standard line partway up at a ledge feature climbers call 'the terrace' and climbs straight to the summit rather than skirting around it.
routes.beta: From the terrace (reached via one of the other lines), climb a steep short wall to an alcove, continue up to a right-slanting ledge, then go right around a corner and follow easier rock to the summit.
routes.face: Upper pillar, above 'the terrace'
routes.season: Jul-Sep (limited by seasonal snow closure of Obstruction Point Road, which typically doesn't open until late June and closes again by October)

pitchDetail.notes: From the terrace, climb a steep short wall to an alcove, continue to a right-slanting ledge, then go right around a corner and follow easy rock to the summit.

routes.approach: Same access as the other Steeple Rock routes: from Port Angeles via Hurricane Ridge Road (~17 mi) to Obstruction Point Road, then ~2 mi of gravel road past the Morse Creek/Cox Valley trailhead to the base of the formation.

routes.overview: The hardest and most sustained line on Steeple Rock, following the northernmost crack system up the pillar's west face — regarded as the area's best natural climb.
routes.beta: Ascends the northernmost crack on the west face: climb a left-facing corner along a pillar to a small belay stance atop the pillar, then continue right across some loose rock and follow a shallow chimney up to 'the terrace,' from which the summit is reached via easier ground shared with Direct Finish.
routes.face: West face, northernmost crack line
routes.season: Jul-Sep (limited by seasonal snow closure of Obstruction Point Road, which typically doesn't open until late June and closes again by October)

pitchDetail.notes: Left-facing corner along the pillar's northernmost crack to a small belay stance atop the pillar.
Traverse right on loose rock, then follow a shallow chimney up to 'the terrace'; easier ground beyond leads to the summit (shared with the Direct Finish line).

routes.approach: Same access as the other Steeple Rock routes: from Port Angeles via Hurricane Ridge Road (~17 mi) to Obstruction Point Road, then ~2 mi of gravel road past the Morse Creek/Cox Valley trailhead to the base of the west face.

Sparsely protected ('R'-rated) sections reported.

blurb: Crystal Lake Tower is a ~7,923-foot rock tower forming the westernmost point of the summit ridge of McClellan Peak, on the east side of Crystal Creek between Chessmen Ridge and the Nightmare Needles, in the Stuart-Enchantments group of Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Wenatchee/Leavenworth area). It presents a steep, roughly 1,400-1,500-foot southwest-facing rib of Cascade granite/granodiorite that offers a long, moderate, and rarely-visited alpine rock climb outside the busier core Enchantments zone. Its main route, the Southwest Rib, was first climbed by Pete and Judy Doorish in September 1982, as documented in Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide.
hierarchyNote: Confirmed as a real, distinct feature: Mountain Project, SummitPost, and Cascade Climbers trip reports agree Crystal Lake Tower is the westernmost point of McClellan Peak's summit ridge (Stuart-Enchantments group, Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Chelan/Kittitas county line area), with Mountain Project giving coordinates 47.46872, -120.79532 and elevation 7,923 ft. Two notes: (1) some older Cascade Climbers trip reports (2012) call the same feature/route "Crystal Lake Spire" rather than "Crystal Lake Tower" — same place, alternate name, not a different peak. (2) This is technically in the Central Cascades / Icicle-Enchantments group near Leavenworth, not the North Cascades proper (which lie further north, e.g. Mt. Baker/Pickets/Glacier Peak areas) — geographically plausible as a "WA Cascades" peak but worth flagging if this batch is meant to be strictly North Cascades. No conflict found between the given name/parent-hierarchy framing and the real mountain otherwise.

routes.fa: Pete and Judy Doorish, September 1982
objHaz: lingering snow/ice on approach before mid-July
routes.face: Southwest Rib (the tower's southwest-facing rib)
routes.overview: A long, moderate alpine rock line that follows the crest of Crystal Lake Tower's prominent southwest rib for roughly 1,400-1,500 feet from its toe in Crystal Creek Basin to the summit, mixing an indistinct lower crest, a knife-edged ridge section, corner systems, and a gendarme-studded upper headwall at sustained mid-5th-class-to-5.8 difficulty.
routes.beta: A full-value, remote adventure alpine rock climb that is infrequently traveled and requires real route-finding rather than following an obvious line. Roughly 19 ropelengths (60m) wind up the rib and around towers: intermittent treed/sandy ledges, slabs, and short steep steps lead up an indistinct crest for about 500 vertical feet until it becomes a defined, knife-edged ridge (marked by a large open slab visible on climber's left from a distance); the rib then relaxes into low-angled, sometimes loose/sandy corners for another ~400 feet; the upper climb is steeper and more continuously exposed, featuring prominent gendarmes, a sharp ridge traverse, a left-trending ramp below a steep white headwall, and a final summit block to reach the top Beckey identifies as Crystal Lake Tower's summit.
routes.approach: From Wenatchee, take US-2 West/US-97 South about 14 miles, continue on US-97 South roughly 7 more miles, then turn onto Ingalls Creek Road and follow it about a mile to Ingalls Creek Trailhead (elev. ~1,900-1,950 ft). Hike the Ingalls Creek Trail roughly 7.7 miles (reported as mostly forested and gently graded, with intermittent blowdown past mile 4) to the unmarked, unmaintained boot path up Crystal Creek (leaving the Ingalls Creek Trail roughly 100 ft before it crosses Crystal Creek, about 0.9 miles from Cascade Creek campground). This boot path climbs steeply through Nightmare Gorge toward Crystal Lake (~7,000 ft) and requires real off-trail route-finding; the base of the Southwest Rib can be reached either from lower Crystal Creek Basin (via Ingalls Creek) or by descending into the basin from the Enchantment Plateau via Crystal Lake.
routes.descent: Traverse east over the Westernmost Chessman to reach the Enchantment Plateau, or descend south from Crystal Lake following its outlet stream back down into Crystal Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: The most practical descent from the summit continues over the Westernmost Chessman tower, from which the Enchantment Plateau is readily reached and can be used to exit toward Snow Lakes or Aasgard Pass, or to return to a Crystal Lake camp. Alternatively, from Crystal Lake an easy descent follows the outlet stream south back into Crystal Creek Basin to rejoin the approach route toward Ingalls Creek. Both options involve loose, off-trail terrain and benefit from prior familiarity or careful navigation, especially in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Ingalls Creek Trailhead and backpack in via the Ingalls Creek Trail and the Crystal Creek/Nightmare Gorge boot path to a camp in Crystal Creek Basin or at Crystal Lake. Day 2: Climb the Southwest Rib (roughly 19 pitches) to the summit, then descend via the Westernmost Chessman to the Enchantment Plateau or back to Crystal Lake; return to camp. Day 3: Hike out via Crystal Creek and Ingalls Creek Trail to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The rib's crest and gendarme-studded upper section make lateral escape difficult once well established on route; the most likely retreat is downclimbing and/or rappelling established gear anchors back down the rib toward the toe in Crystal Creek Basin. Carry a rack sufficient to build multiple rappel anchors given the route's remote, infrequently-traveled character.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, ~19-pitch length, route-finding demands, and a descent that itself requires further travel over connecting terrain (Westernmost Chessman or Crystal Lake outlet), set a turnaround time before starting that guarantees reaching the top and completing the descent in daylight, with a safety margin for route-finding delays or afternoon weather typical of Cascades summers.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed roughly June through September; documented ascents span from late June to mid-August. Carry an ice axe for lingering snow on the approach and lower route before mid-July; later in the season, running water can become scarce, so parties often carry a stove to melt snow or treat limited water sources.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack to about 3 inches, with multiple ascent parties specifically noting they placed a lot of #3 Camalot-sized pieces — doubling up in the #2-#3 range is worth considering. A 50m rope is the stated minimum, though many parties climb it in the 60m-ropelength count Mountain Project lists.
routes.proNeeds: Alpine rack to 3 inches (nuts plus cams, doubled through #2-#3) for the corner systems and cracks along the rib; gear anchors are also needed for any rappel-based retreat.
gear: Sturdy approach shoes/boots for off-trail scrambling
whatToBring: Backpacking/camping gear for a Crystal Creek Basin or Crystal Lake camp
Stove and water treatment (streams may be low/dry late season)
Free self-issue wilderness permit (obtained at Ingalls Creek Trailhead)
Map/GPS for the unmaintained Crystal Creek/Nightmare Gorge boot path
Extra food, water, and layers for a long, remote day
proTips: The Ingalls Creek/Crystal Creek approach sees far less traffic than the Enchantments' Snow Lakes or Stuart Lake entries — expect genuine route-finding through Nightmare Gorge, not a boot-beaten path.
Many parties descend by traversing over the Westernmost Chessman to the Enchantment Plateau rather than reversing the climbing route.
Several trip reports specifically mention placing a lot of #3-size cams — don't skimp on that size.
watchOut: Route-finding issues are common, especially on the indistinct lower ~500 feet of rib and around the towers near the top.
Loose rock/choss is present in sections; this is a full-on adventure route, not a manicured classic.
The route lies outside the quota-controlled core Enchantment Permit Zone but is still within Alpine Lakes Wilderness — a free self-issue wilderness permit is still required at the trailhead.
This is a remote, infrequently traveled line; self-rescue capability and conservative decision-making matter more than on popular routes.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose blocks occur along sections of the rib and the upper gendarmes.
The lower rib and the towers near the summit present genuine route-finding challenges rather than an obvious line.
The long, partly off-trail approach via Ingalls Creek and Crystal Creek/Nightmare Gorge increases commitment and the difficulty of self-rescue if something goes wrong.

pitchDetail.notes: Indistinct crest; intermittent treed, sandy ledges, slabs, and short steep steps.
Rib becomes well-defined and knife-edged; landmark is a large open slab visible on climber's left from a distance.
Rib becomes less distinct again; low-angled corners, some filled with loose blocks and sand.
Prominent gendarmes; continuously steeper and more exposed climbing; sharp ridge traverse, left-trending ramp below a steep white headwall, and a final summit block to top out.

road.name: Ingalls Creek Road
road.status: Open; Forest Service trailhead page reports no seasonal gate
road.driveNote: From Wenatchee, take US-2 West/US-97 South about 14 miles, continue on US-97 South about 7 more miles, then turn onto Ingalls Creek Road and follow it about a mile to the Ingalls Creek Trailhead.

climate.typical: Dry, warm east-slope Cascades rain-shadow summer climate; the route is climbed roughly June-September.
climate.spring: Lingering snow on the approach and lower rib into June/early July; carry an ice axe before mid-July.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season; warm and mostly dry, but approach water sources can dry up by mid-to-late summer.
climate.fall: Possible early-season snow by late September and shortening daylight windows.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District — Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle day-use fee or $30 annual pass at Ingalls Creek Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass and Interagency passes also honored)
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead; the Crystal Creek approach to this route is outside the quota-controlled core Enchantment Permit Zone, so no advance overnight-lottery permit is needed
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Pass, or day-use fee for trailhead parking

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start recommended given the ~19-pitch length, route-finding demands, and a descent that itself requires additional travel (over the Westernmost Chessman or via Crystal Lake).

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 dispatch: (509) 663-9911 (or 911); Chelan County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue, supported by the volunteer Chelan County Mountain Rescue for technical mountain rescues

emergency.notes: Remote, off-trail approach through Nightmare Gorge and a rarely-traveled route mean rescue response will be slow; cell coverage in Crystal Creek Basin is unconfirmed and likely unreliable — do not count on it.

routes.corrections: Given seed data listed pitches as 0 and left routeFt/highPointFt/grade null; Mountain Project documents 19 pitches, a route length of 1,500 ft (SummitPost independently describes a similar ~1,400 ft southwest face), and a peak elevation of 7,923 ft — all added here. FA party/date given (Pete and Judy Doorish, Sep 1982) matches Mountain Project/SummitPost exactly and is retained unchanged.

blurb: Cutthroat Wall is a large, west/northwest-facing granite buttress on an informally-named summit near 6,978 ft ("Billygoat Buttress" per first-ascensionist Blake Herrington), south of Cutthroat Lake near Washington Pass in Washington's North Cascades. The clean but still-weathering granite is riddled with roofs, corners, and "choose-your-own-adventure" crack systems that yield several 5-8 pitch trad lines, first developed by Blake Herrington and partners beginning in 2007. A brushy, unmarked approach keeps it far quieter than the roadside classics at Washington Pass a few miles away.
hierarchyNote: Cutthroat Wall is a real, climbable granite buttress south of Cutthroat Lake near Washington Pass, but it is NOT the same feature as the named "Cutthroat Peak" (8,066 ft) that sits ~2 miles west along SR-20 and has its own well-documented Mountain Project/SummitPost/Peakbagger pages (South Buttress, SE Buttress, etc.) — those are a distinct, higher peak and should not be conflated with this wall. Cutthroat Wall itself sits on an unnamed/informally-named summit that FA climber Blake Herrington's blog identifies as "Pk. 6,978'"; Mountain Project's area GPS pin (48.5458, -120.64786) is tagged with a 6,120 ft elevation, but that appears to mark the base of the routes / lower buttress rather than the true 6,978 ft summit above — treat 6,120 ft as a base-area reference point, not the peak's high point. Because this is an obscure, informally-named sub-feature rather than a named, listed peak, SummitPost and Peakbagger do not have dedicated pages for it (only for the separate Cutthroat Peak/Pass); Mountain Project, a Blake Herrington first-ascent trip report/blog, and USFS trailhead data were the usable primary sources instead.

routes.fa: Max Hasson, Blake Herrington (2007). Variation pitches likely climbed previously also.
routes.face: Right side of the main buttress
routes.overview: A 5-pitch, 800-ft moderate trad line up the right side of Cutthroat Wall, weaving through featured granite - corners, cracks and an easy alpine slab finish past a distinctive spiral tree - with multiple variations at nearly every pitch.
routes.beta: A 'choose-your-own-adventure' wall route: the topo describes a default line but notes several parallel options at P1, P3 and P4. Rock is generally solid but still lichen-covered and loose in spots since the routes are relatively young (established 2007+). No bolts and very little fixed gear - tree slings mark some anchors, including a memorable 'spiral tree' landmark on P3.
routes.approach: From SR-20 about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (roughly 28 miles west of Winthrop), turn onto Forest Road 400 (signed for Cutthroat Lake) and drive 1 mile to the Cutthroat Lake Trailhead (4,500 ft). From the trailhead kiosk, branch off onto an old roadbed that parallels the Cutthroat Lake Trail for about 1/4 mile, watching for cairns marking a left turn uphill. From there it is roughly 1.5-2 miles of moderate bushwhacking, staying climber's-right toward the buttress, to reach the base's giant granite step features. Allow about 1.5-2 hours.
routes.descent: Single-rope rappel from a slung horn/tree at a notch near the top, then ascend a gully/ledge system on skier's left back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the topout, walk roughly 100-200 ft along the ridge/east to a minor notch separating the westernmost buttress from the higher central buttress, marked by a slung horn/tree. A single-rope rappel (over a chockstone tunnel that can snag the rope on the pull) drops to a dirty ramp with a small cave. From there, find and follow an upward-trending gully or ledge system on skier's left (do NOT descend directly down the obvious gully below the notch, which is loose and hazardous), curving around back to grassy terrain near the base of the routes. Use a single 70m rope.
routes.bail: Any of the tree/horn anchors noted in the pitch topo (the gnarled trees atop P2 and the spiral tree partway up P3) can serve as multi-pitch rappel stations back toward the base.
routes.turnaround: Given the loose, routefinding-heavy descent gully, parties should plan to be off the upper slabs with enough daylight remaining to navigate the descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once seasonal snow has cleared the upper pitches and approach slabs; access is additionally gated by SR-20 itself, which is normally open only from around April/May through early December.
routes.comms: No specific cell-coverage data found for this wall; the Washington Pass corridor generally has spotty-to-nonexistent cell service given the surrounding high terrain.
routes.detailedRack: Standard Washington Pass rack: stoppers, small cams, a double set of cams up to big hand size, and a single fist-sized piece. Minimal fixed gear beyond occasional tree slings; a single 70m rope is recommended over doubles.
routes.proNeeds: Stoppers plus a doubled cam rack from small through hand size and one fist-sized piece; natural anchors (trees, horns) are used at several belays and the rap station.
whatToBring: Guidebook topo (Blake Herrington's Cascades Rock) or downloaded route beta
Extra water - approach and descent add significant time
proTips: Reference the spiral tree landmark on P3 constantly to confirm you're on the intended line, not a variation.
Carry Blake Herrington's Cascades Rock topo, since the wall's many variations aren't fully documented on Mountain Project.
A single 70m rope is preferred over double ropes for efficiency on this route.
watchOut: Multiple parallel lines exist on this buttress - it's easy to drift onto a variation without realizing it.
The descent gully system is loose and easy to get wrong if you pick the wrong drainage.
Expect lichen and some looseness typical of a lightly-traveled wall established only since 2007.
knownHazards: Loose rock and lichen typical of a newer, lightly-climbed wall.
Rockfall potential in the descent gully.
Sparse fixed anchors - some belays/raps rely on trees and horns rather than bolts.

pitchDetail.notes: 36m. Steep rock with an undercut start into an open book; multiple line choices (featured face, crack/arete, or corner) lead to a quality crack with a medium-cam anchor.
25m. Awkward flared groove leads left to a yellowish flake, then a wide crack to a ledge with two big gnarled trees that make solid anchors.
~25m. Climbs right of the gnarled trees, rambling to a minor bowl with an excellent tree anchor below the notable spiral tree landmark.
60m+. Hand-crack dihedral through a blocky section past the spiral tree, or alternatively an obtuse shaft with spaced protection to a small ledge belay.
60m. Multiple options off a huge ledge; described line follows an orange coffin flake and a quality crack system into a steep, fun chimney.
60m+. Easy slab climbing trending left toward the summit with short boulder problems; may require simul-climbing with limited anchor material near the east summit edge.

road.name: Forest Road 400 (off SR-20)
road.status: Gravel, generally passable to standard vehicles
road.seasonalGate: Accessible only while SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) is open, roughly mid/late April through early December depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: Turn off SR-20 about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (or ~28 miles west of Winthrop) onto FR-400, signed for Cutthroat Lake; 1 mile of gravel road to the trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades East / Washington Pass corridor
climate.typical: Dry, warm summer days with cool nights typical of the east-slope Cascades rain shadow; afternoon thunderstorms are possible
climate.spring: Snow lingers into June on the approach and upper pitches
climate.summer: June-September is prime season; hot, dry, with occasional afternoon storms
climate.fall: Cools quickly by late September/October; early snow can force the seasonal road closure by November-December
climate.winter: SR-20 is closed for the season and the area is inaccessible

access.permit: Digital Day Pass, Northwest Forest Pass, or America the Beautiful interagency pass accepted

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911; Okanogan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 422-7200
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 W Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA 98862, (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA; Mid-Valley Hospital, Omak, WA
emergency.notes: Remote alpine terrain with limited-to-no cell coverage. Aero Methow Rescue Service and Life Flight (based in Brewster) provide air medical evacuation for the Methow Valley/Washington Pass corridor; Okanogan County Search and Rescue (volunteer, under the Sheriff's Office) supports ground rescue.

routes.fa: 2007 - Dan Hilden, Blake Herrington
routes.face: Left side of the main buttress
routes.overview: An 8-pitch, 800-ft moderate-to-sustained trad line up excellent crack-and-corner granite on the left side of Cutthroat Wall, capped by a standout 100-ft right-facing corner near the top.
routes.beta: Stellar rock quality with excellent crack and corner climbing plus slab sections; because of the demanding approach, parties typically have the route to themselves. Use 'Zebra Corner' as the key landmark throughout the climb to confirm you're on route - it can seep water early season. Blake Herrington's Cascades Rock topo is the recommended reference since the wall has more variations than are documented online.
routes.approach: From SR-20 about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (roughly 28 miles west of Winthrop), turn onto Forest Road 400 (signed for Cutthroat Lake) and drive 1 mile to the Cutthroat Lake Trailhead (4,500 ft). Branch off the Cutthroat Lake Trail onto an old roadbed for about 1/4 mile, then follow cairns left and uphill through roughly 1.5-2 miles of moderate bushwhacking, staying climber's-right toward the buttress. Allow about 1.5-2 hours.
routes.descent: Shared Cutthroat Wall descent: single-rope rappel from the notch to a ramp, then an ascending gully/ledge system on skier's left back to the base - study the beta carefully, as it's easy to get wrong.
routes.descentText: The descent has real potential to ruin your day if you don't study it beforehand. From the topout, reach the notch marked by a slung horn/tree between the westernmost and higher central buttress, rappel one rope-length over a chockstone tunnel to a dirty ramp with a small cave, then find the upward-trending gully/ledge system on skier's left (not the loose gully directly below) and follow it around back to the base.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible by rappelling the route on gear anchors; the wall has enough tree/horn belays to allow bailing from most points if weather or time forces a retreat.
routes.turnaround: Given the demanding, routefinding-heavy descent, plan to top out with enough daylight left to navigate the descent gully before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; Zebra Corner and other seep-prone features are wettest earliest in the season. Access is further limited by SR-20's normal open season of roughly April/May through early December.
routes.comms: No specific cell-coverage data found for this wall; the Washington Pass corridor generally has spotty-to-nonexistent cell service.
routes.detailedRack: Cams: doubles from tips to #2, plus a single #3 and single #4. Stoppers: single set.
routes.proNeeds: A doubled small-to-medium cam rack (tips through #2) with single #3 and #4 for occasional wider sections, plus a single stopper set; minimal fixed protection.
whatToBring: Extra water for the long approach/descent day
proTips: Zebra Corner is the key waypoint on this route - reference it constantly to confirm you're on-line.
Bring Blake Herrington's Cascades Rock topo since online beta doesn't capture every variation.
Rack light on big gear (single #3/#4) but double up small-to-medium cams for the sustained crack pitches.
watchOut: Zebra Corner can seep water in early season, affecting climbing on that pitch.
The descent is notoriously easy to get wrong - 'has the potential of ruining your day.'
Expect some lichen and looseness typical of the wall's younger routes.
knownHazards: Early-season seepage in Zebra Corner.
Complex, loose descent gully system.
Remote wall with minimal fixed protection and no quick rescue access.

pitchDetail.notes: 100 ft right-facing corner (RFC) that varies hands to fingers - very secure but sustained; the highlight pitch of the route.

routes.fa: Ben Johnson, Tim Foster; Sept. 2020
routes.face: About 150 ft climber's-left and up from Easy Getaway, starting in an open book left of a tree patch
routes.overview: A 6-pitch, 700-ft sustained trad line up a left-facing broken corner system on Cutthroat Wall, just left of Easy Getaway, with a well-regarded 'money pitch' tips corner near the top.
routes.beta: Follows a left-facing, broken corner system with sustained, quality climbing and comfortable belay stations. Watch for loose rock on P2 near the treed ledge, including stacked blocks at the top of the corner that should be treated with care.
routes.approach: From SR-20 about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (roughly 28 miles west of Winthrop), turn onto Forest Road 400 (signed for Cutthroat Lake) and drive 1 mile to the Cutthroat Lake Trailhead (4,500 ft). Follow the Cutthroat Wall approach - old roadbed then cairned bushwhack (roughly 1.5-2 miles, staying climber's-right toward the buttress) - to the base, then locate the start about 150 ft climber's-left and slightly above Easy Getaway's start, in an open book left of a stand of trees. Allow about 1.5-2 hours.
routes.descent: Three 60m rappels from trees, starting slightly uphill of the P5 belay.
routes.descentText: Climbers have found it faster to rappel the route itself rather than use the shared walk-off descent: three full 60m rappels reach the ground, beginning from a tree slightly up and left of the belay atop pitch 5.
routes.bail: Rappel from the tree anchor up and left of the P5 belay, or from any lower gear/tree anchor along the corner system, using 60m ropes.
routes.turnaround: Given the sustained upper pitches and rappel-heavy descent, plan to top out with enough daylight to complete three 60m rappels before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, consistent with the rest of Cutthroat Wall; access is further limited by SR-20's normal open season of roughly April/May through early December.
routes.comms: No specific cell-coverage data found for this wall; the Washington Pass corridor generally has spotty-to-nonexistent cell service.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from BD 0.1 to 3; a #4 is handy but not required. A rack of nuts, and RP-style micro-nuts, are nice to have for thin placements.
routes.proNeeds: A doubled small-to-medium cam rack (0.1-#3) with a #4 optional, plus a full nut set including RPs for thin/tips-corner protection on the crux pitches.
whatToBring: Guidebook topo or downloaded route beta
proTips: The route starts about 150 ft climber's-left and slightly up from Easy Getaway - locate the open book left of the tree patch before racking up.
Bring RPs and a full nut set for thin protection on the P4 'money pitch' tips corner.
Rappelling the route (3x60m from the tree left of the P5 belay) is faster than the shared walk-off descent used on the wall's other routes.
watchOut: Watch for loose rock on P2 approaching the treed ledge, including stacked blocks near the top of the corner - move carefully.
P2 and P4 carry the technical crux (5.10d and 5.10) with sustained, thin protection.
As one of the newest lines on the wall (FA 2020), expect some lichen/looseness in spots.
knownHazards: Loose rock and stacked blocks reported on pitch 2.
Sustained, thin-protection crux pitches (P2, P4).
Remote wall with minimal fixed gear and no quick rescue access.

pitchDetail.notes: 55m. Open book with a rightward slab traverse into a dual-cracked corner.
25m. Treed-ledge approach, then a left-slanting crack into a corner; a variation exists around 5.10. Watch for loose rock, including stacked blocks near the top.
40m. Hand crack leading to face climbing and a left-facing dihedral.
25m. The 'money pitch' - a tips corner with laybacking.
45m. Traverse into a steep right-facing hand crack/layback corner.
Easy, wandering terrain to the summit.

blurb: West McMillan Spire (8,004 ft) is the higher, western summit of the twin McMillan Spires in the Southern Picket Range, deep within North Cascades National Park's Whatcom County backcountry. The peak is built of the steep granite and gneiss typical of the Pickets, rising above Terror Glacier and Terror Basin, and is reached only by a long, unmaintained approach up Goodell Creek. Fred and Helmy Beckey made the first ascent via the West Ridge on August 29, 1940, during their pioneering 1940 exploration of the Picket Range.

routes.overview: A longer, more technical trad line up the southwest side of West McMillan Spire, rated 5.8- over 6 pitches (Grade III), in contrast to the scrambling West Ridge.
routes.beta: Documented on Mountain Project with a grade and rack but almost no route-finding beta; the page explicitly directs climbers to Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide ('the red guidebook') for the actual pitch-by-pitch description. Shares the same remote Southern Pickets approach zone (Goodell Creek to Terror Basin/Terror Glacier) as the West Ridge, though the exact line taken to reach the base of the ridge from the glacier is not detailed in available online sources.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, glacier-guarded approach and limited published beta, parties should set a conservative turnaround based on remaining daylight, weather trends, and rock conditions rather than pushing into terrain they cannot down-climb or retreat from safely.
routes.bestSeason: Summer climbing window, roughly June through September, consistent with the broader Southern Pickets climbing season documented in trip reports for this peak; specific season data for this exact line was not found beyond that general window.
routes.proNeeds: Medium alpine rock rack with 8-10 runners, a helmet, and one ice axe (per Mountain Project route page).
proTips: Online beta for this route is sparse; consult Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide for the actual route description before attempting it, as Mountain Project itself defers to that guidebook.
knownHazards: Remote Southern Pickets setting means any rescue is slow and difficult.
Loose rock is characteristic of Picket Range ridgelines generally.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Goodell Creek
road.status: Open year-round at Goodell Creek/Newhalem (west of the seasonal high-country closure gates)
road.seasonalGate: SR 20's seasonal closure gates sit well east of here, between roughly Ross Dam (MP 134, or MP 120 at Newhalem in shoulder season) and Silver Star/Early Winters near Mazama; Goodell Creek (~MP 119) is normally on the accessible side.
road.driveNote: Paved highway to the Goodell Creek Campground turnoff near Newhalem; the unmarked, unmaintained climbers' trail begins on the east side of Goodell Creek.

climate.forecastZone: NWS West Slopes North Cascades and Vicinity zone (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate with heavy orographic precipitation, especially in winter; summers are drier but can still bring fast-moving weather.

routes.corrections: Original data had pitches=0 and routeFt=null with fa listed as 'unknown'. Mountain Project's dedicated route page (id 119579098) gives Grade III, 6 pitches, 1600 ft, 5.8-/VS 4c, with FA explicitly listed as unknown/undocumented (so fa is left null rather than the placeholder string 'unknown'). Note the West McMillan Spire AREA page's route-list text shows a conflicting '16 pitches' figure that appears to be a mis-parsed grade-conversion/length string; the individual route page's '6 pitches, 1600 ft' is treated as authoritative.

routes.fa: Fred and Helmy Beckey, August 29, 1940
routes.overview: The classic and most-climbed route on West McMillan Spire: a long glacier and scramble approach from Terror Basin over Terror Glacier, up a steep snow/ice gully to gain the West Ridge, then a 3rd-4th class scramble along scree, talus, and an exposed summit ridge to the summit block.
routes.beta: From a Terror Basin camp, cross rocky benches and the outlet of a glacial lake, climb slabs and snow onto Terror Glacier below the McMillan Spires, then ascend a steep snow/ice gully to reach the crest of the West Ridge. From there it is a mellow but exposed 3rd-4th class scramble up scree and talus to the summit block. The glacier crossing is normally done unroped but crampons and an ice axe are standard; some parties encounter thin, hollow snow over rock and short mixed moves on the upper ridge, especially in a low-snow year.
routes.approach: Park at the Goodell Creek Campground/Cross-Country Zone trailhead off SR 20 just west of Newhalem (start elevation ~600 ft) - use the unmarked, unmaintained climbers' trail on the east side of Goodell Creek across from the Visitor Center sign, not the Goodell Creek Campground sign on the west side of the creek. The trail gains roughly 4,000-5,000 ft in about 2 miles of steep, brushy hiking through numerous blowdowns, reaching heather benches and a rocky viewpoint before a short rocky section leads to a ridge notch (~6,250 ft) with a view down into Terror Basin, a good bivy site with flat spots and running water (there is no reliable water between the top of the hillclimb and Terror Basin). From Terror Basin, traverse rocky benches, cross the outlet of a glacial lake, and climb slabs/snow onto Terror Glacier below the McMillan Spires.
routes.descent: Reverse the West Ridge route back down the snow/ice gully to Terror Glacier and Terror Basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the exposed 3rd-4th class summit ridge and scree/talus back to the top of the steep snow/ice gully, then descend the gully (facing in if icy) back onto Terror Glacier. Recross the glacier and benches to Terror Basin camp, then reverse the steep, loose, brushy climbers' trail back to Goodell Creek. Some parties note the north-side notch descent without snow cover is intimidating steep loose choss and recommend staying skier's right.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Goodell Creek trailhead and hike the steep, brushy climbers' trail (~5,000 ft gain) to a bivy at Terror Basin (~5,800 ft). Day 2: Pre-dawn alpine start; cross benches and the glacial lake outlet, ascend slabs/snow onto Terror Glacier, climb the steep snow/ice gully to the West Ridge crest, and scramble 3rd-4th class terrain to the summit; reverse the route back to Terror Basin camp. Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: The technical crux is the steep snow/ice gully between the glacier and the ridge crest; below that, retreat is a straightforward reversal of scrambling terrain. If conditions in the gully deteriorate (ice, rockfall), parties can retreat back across Terror Glacier and out via Terror Basin rather than continuing to the ridge.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent retraces steep snow/ice and loose, exposed scrambling terrain, parties should set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and building afternoon weather, and should not linger on the summit ridge if clouds or thunderstorms are developing.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June through September based on documented ascents (trip reports span early June to late August/September). Earlier-season trips have more continuous snow for the glacier crossing and gully (requiring axe and crampons throughout), while by late summer the glacier surface softens, the gully can be melted out to loose scree, and rockfall/looseness increases with warming.
routes.detailedRack: Not a rock climb in the traditional sense - the technical crux is a steep snow/ice gully and short mixed moves near the false summit. Ice axe and crampons are standard; some parties carry a short rope and a few slings for the gully and the exposed summit block scramble, though most ascents are done unroped.
routes.proNeeds: Crampons and an ice axe are required for the Terror Glacier crossing and the snow/ice gully to the ridge crest (per Mountain Project).
whatToBring: bivy/camping gear for a 2-3 day trip
water treatment (no reliable water source between the top of the hillclimb and Terror Basin)
sturdy mountaineering boots suited to scrambling and snow travel
navigation (map/GPS/compass) for the unmarked, unmaintained approach trail
bear canister if required for the camping zone
proTips: Find the correct trailhead: use the unmarked trail on the east side of Goodell Creek across from the Visitor Center sign, not the Goodell Creek Campground sign on the west side.
Terror Basin is the standard bivy - flat spots and running water, but no water between the top of the initial hillclimb and the basin itself.
Carry ice axe and crampons even late in the season; multiple trip reports describe using them for the glacier crossing and gully despite it being a relatively 'easy' 8,000-footer.
A helmet is recommended near the notch/section between Inspiration Peak and McMillan Spire due to looseness.
watchOut: Hundreds of blowdowns litter the unmaintained lower climbers' trail.
The gully and upper ridge can hold thin, hollow snow over rock, especially late season - probe before committing.
Reddish rock near the summit approach is described as poor quality/deteriorating.
The north-side notch descent without snow cover is steep, loose choss - stay skier's right.
knownHazards: Terror Glacier crossing carries crevasse and glacier-travel hazard even though it is typically done unroped.
The summit ridge is narrow and heavily exposed, with large cliffs on both sides in places.
Rockfall and loose rock are recurring themes in trip reports of this route.
Weather can deteriorate quickly this deep in the Picket Range, complicating the long retreat.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep snow/ice gully gains the West Ridge crest from the glacier; crampons and axe required, occasionally roped depending on conditions.
Scree and talus scrambling along the ridge.
Exposed summit block scramble to the true summit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20) to Goodell Creek

road.seasonalGate: SR 20's seasonal closure gates sit well east of here, between roughly Ross Dam (MP 134, or MP 120 at Newhalem in shoulder season) and Silver Star/Early Winters near Mazama; Goodell Creek (~MP 119) is normally on the accessible side.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, take SR 20 east about 14 miles to Newhalem; turn onto Newhalem Creek Road, left at the T-junction, then right to Goodell Creek Campground. The unmarked climbers' trail starts across the creek from the Visitor Center sign.

climate.forecastZone: NWS West Slopes North Cascades and Vicinity zone (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate with heavy orographic precipitation, especially wet, heavy snowfall in winter, raising avalanche danger in shoulder seasons.
climate.spring: Continuous snow cover on the glacier and gully; ski/splitboard descents of the upper West Ridge have been reported in late April with corn-snow conditions.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; glacier crossing softens and the gully can melt out to loose rock/scree by late summer.

access.permit: Overnight wilderness permit required, reserved via Recreation.gov and finalized by email (noca_wilderness@nps.gov) or in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.

access.closures: Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount operates mid-May to mid-October; outside that window permits are handled by email/self-issue.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from the Terror Basin camp (trip reports describe leaving camp around 3:30-4:00 AM) to cross the glacier while snow is firm and clear the summit ridge before afternoon weather.

routes.corrections: Original fa listed only 'Fred Beckey 1940'; Mountain Project's route page (id 112189946) specifies the FA was made by Fred AND Helmy Beckey on 8/29/1940, so fa was expanded accordingly. Original pitches=0 and routeFt=null corrected to 1 pitch / 2000 ft per Mountain Project. Reported round-trip approach distance is inconsistent across sources (WTA: 16.0 mi RT with 9,800 ft gain; other trip reports describe 20-24 mi RT depending on route/days taken) - gainFt (9,800 ft) is taken from WTA's dedicated page for this hike/climb, but a single distKm value was not set given the spread between sources.

blurb: Minuteman Spire (also called Minuteman Tower) is a compact granite tower in the Liberty Bell Group above Washington Pass in Washington's North Cascades, standing just south of and dwarfed by neighboring Concord Tower and Liberty Bell Mountain. It shares the same high-quality Washington Pass granite as its larger neighbors and offers a single well-regarded multi-pitch line, the East Face, first climbed by Scott Davis and Bill Lingley on July 30, 1967. At roughly half the height of Liberty Bell, it sees far less traffic than the group's more famous towers but rewards parties with excellent crack climbing on its upper pitches.
hierarchyNote: No mismatch found — Minuteman Spire genuinely exists as named, in the Liberty Bell Group at Washington Pass, North Cascades, Okanogan County, WA (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest). Worth noting for hierarchy purposes: Minuteman is a small subsidiary tower attached to/immediately in front of Concord Tower (itself part of the Liberty Bell massif) rather than an independent, separately-prominent summit — sources describe it as "really a part of Concord Tower." This is normal for how it's cataloged as a minor sub-formation, not an error, but flagging so it isn't confused with a standalone peak of similar name elsewhere.

routes.overview: A 6-pitch, Grade III 5.10- moderate alpine rock route up the East Face of the smallest tower in the Liberty Bell Group, climbing progressively better rock to a stellar hand-crack finish near the summit.
routes.beta: The route ascends Minuteman's East Face, working up and slightly right through three to four pitches of looser, route-finding-dependent rock before the climbing dramatically improves in the final third: a 5.10 fingers-to-hand crux pitch with excellent exposure, followed by a burly 5.8 roof/flake move into one of the best 5.8 hand cracks at Washington Pass. Sources describe it variously as 5-6 or 6-7 pitches depending on how sections are linked; 6 pitches and 600 feet is the most commonly cited length.
routes.approach: Park at the small pond pullout on the north side of SR-20 (North Cascades Highway), a few hundred yards east of Washington Pass, or start from the Blue Lake Trailhead about half a mile west of the pass (the standard bivy/approach point for the whole Liberty Bell Group's west-side routes; a Northwest Forest Pass is required there). From the pond pullout, follow a well-trodden climbers' path southwest through forest, breaking out into open talus below Liberty Bell's East Face after roughly 45 minutes to an hour at a mellow pace. Traverse under Liberty Bell toward the gully separating Lexington Tower and Minuteman to reach the base of the East Face — the same approach used for Lexington Tower and for Liberty Bell's own East Face routes. Snow can linger at the base into early summer.
routes.descent: About 3 rappels with a single 60m rope back to the approach talus.
routes.descentText: From the top, trip-report beta describes traversing a short catwalk and building rappel anchors roughly 100 feet west of the true summit; Beckey's guidebook instead directs parties to rappel north until they can cross the gully separating Minuteman from Liberty Bell, then downclimb to a bush anchor for a final rappel. Either way, about three rappels on a single 60m rope typically regain the approach talus. Existing fixed slings around horns/blocks should be inspected (and backed up if in doubt) before weighting, since they're exposed to UV and rockfall.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a single long day from a car camp or bivy near Washington Pass: early start, a 45-minute-to-1-hour approach, roughly 6 pitches of technical climbing, then a 3-rappel descent back to the approach talus and the same trail out. No bivy on the route itself is standard.
routes.bail: Most any belay can be rappelled back down the line just climbed using natural gear/slings on horns and blocks; parties can also traverse off into the gully separating Minuteman from Lexington Tower/Liberty Bell if needed.
routes.turnaround: This is a moderate-length technical route with a rappel descent rather than a walk-off — build in a turnaround buffer for afternoon thunderstorms (common in North Cascades summers), for the party's pace on the crux 5.10 pitch, and for descent time; retreating from any point is straightforward via rappel, so indecision is more costly than turning around early.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September is the reliable window; snow and ice can linger at the base into early summer in high-snow years. The East Face gets morning sun, roughly 6am to 2pm during peak season, before going into shade.
routes.fa: Scott Davis and Bill Lingley (July 30, 1967)
routes.rappels: ~3 rappels, single 60m rope
objHaz: loose rock on the lower two-thirds of the face
lingering snow at the base early season
afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers
routes.face: East Face
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams from finger size to about 3-4 inches plus a set of stoppers covers the route; bring extra slings, including several long/double-length runners, to manage rope drag on the wandering, looser lower pitches. A single 60m rope is standard for both the climbing and the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally good on the upper crack pitches (finger to hand size) but the lower two-thirds is looser rock requiring careful route-finding and more deliberate gear placement; doubling up common finger/hand-size cams is worth it for the 5.10 crux pitch and the hand-crack finish.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the talus approach
extra water — no water source on route
proTips: The upper third of the route is markedly better rock than the lower two-thirds — expect the best climbing, including one of the area's best 5.8 hand cracks, near the top.
Approach and descend as for Lexington Tower/Liberty Bell's East Face; parties already familiar with those approaches can find Minuteman quickly.
The route sees much less traffic than Liberty Bell or Concord Tower, so expect more route-finding and looser rock, especially low on the face.
watchOut: Loose rock on the lower 3-4 pitches — a helmet and careful rope management are warranted.
Snow can linger at the base of the route into early summer.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the North Cascades in summer — start early and watch the weather.
knownHazards: Loose, choss-prone rock on the lower two-thirds of the face.
Route-finding is nontrivial low on the face; parties have reported wandering off-route.
Always inspect (and be ready to back up) any fixed rappel slings before weighting them — the Washington Pass area has seen serious rappel-anchor-failure incidents on other nearby towers.

pitchDetail.notes: Start up the lower face; loose rock, moderate climbing with route-finding required.
Continues up the lower two-thirds of the face on looser but manageable rock.
Lower-face climbing continues; route-finding is key through this section.
Last of the lower, looser pitches before rock quality improves higher up.
Crux pitch — fingers-to-hand crack with excellent exposure below; the technical highlight of the route.
Opens with a burly roof/flake pull rated 5.8, then one of the best 5.8 hand cracks in the Washington Pass area.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: paved, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter roughly early December through April/May at the Early Winters and Newhalem/Silver Star area gates (exact closure points shift with conditions); no winter avalanche control is performed, so the road is simply shut down.
road.driveNote: Roadside pullout near a small pond on SR-20 a few hundred yards east of Washington Pass serves as the standard parking for Minuteman, Lexington Tower, and Liberty Bell's east-side approaches; the Blue Lake Trailhead about half a mile west of the pass serves west-side approaches to the same group.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Western Okanogan County mountain zone / NWAC Washington Pass mountain-weather zone
climate.typical: Dry, sunny alpine summer days with a real chance of afternoon thunderstorms; the East Face gets morning-to-early-afternoon sun (roughly 6am-2pm) then goes into shade.
climate.spring: Snow and cool temperatures linger into June at this elevation; SR-20 itself often doesn't reopen for the season until April or May.
climate.summer: Prime season, late June through September; hot, dry days with afternoon convective storm risk.
climate.fall: Stable weather can persist into September/early October before the highway closes for winter; nights turn cold.
climate.winter: SR-20 is closed and unplowed; the peak is essentially inaccessible by normal means.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: No climbing fee; a Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful pass) is required to park at developed trailheads such as Blue Lake TH. The informal pond pullout used for the east-side approach is not a developed fee trailhead.
access.permit: No climbing permit required.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at Blue Lake Trailhead
access.closures: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) closes for winter, typically early December through April, eliminating all road access to Washington Pass.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff/911 dispatch: 509-422-7232 (emergency: 911)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), 24 W Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, 507 Hospital Way, Brewster, WA (~60 mi from Washington Pass); Family Health Centers clinic in Winthrop, WA (~35 mi) for non-emergency care
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage should be assumed in the Liberty Bell Group cirque itself. SR-20 closes for winter (roughly Dec-Apr), cutting off vehicle access and rescue response routes from the east side during that period.

blurb: Wilmans Spires are three eroded volcanic towers (roughly 300-500 ft tall) strung along the west ridge of Wilmans Peak, directly above Glacier Basin and the historic Monte Cristo mining townsite in the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), Snohomish County, in the North Cascades. The rock is metamorphosed volcanic breccia - chossy overall but among the better examples of its type on the west-facing aspects, with looser rock on the east sides. East Wilmans Spire, the most frequently climbed of the three and topped by a famously tiny, table-top summit, was first climbed by Fred Beckey and Herb Staley in August 1945.

routes.overview: A short, moderate alpine rock route up the easternmost of the three Wilmans Spires, a needle of volcanic breccia dividing Glacier Basin from Seventy-Six Basin, ending on a famously tiny, table-top summit with commanding views over the Monte Cristo group.
routes.beta: Climbed via the historic 1945 Beckey-Staley line on the spire's southeast side: from the east notch, a short pitch gains a ramp system that is followed up and west, then a final slab/crack pitch (crux) leads to a summit ledge. Two short raps and a downclimb return to the notch. The rock is loose in places (especially early in a pitch or on the east aspect), so care with hand/footholds and rope management is important on this wandering line.
routes.approach: From Granite Falls, drive the paved Mountain Loop Highway east roughly 31 miles (passing the Verlot Public Service Center) to Barlow Pass, where pavement ends and the Monte Cristo Road is gated to motor vehicles. Hike or mountain-bike the flat, gated road about 4 miles along the South Fork Sauk River to the historic Monte Cristo townsite (a washout near the Twin Bridges area may require a ford or log crossing of the river). From the townsite, pick up the Glacier Basin Trail and follow it a short distance before leaving the trail to climb a steep talus/scree gully - snow-covered and firmer in early season, loose and increasingly rockfall-prone once melted out by mid/late summer - up to the east notch/col below East Wilmans Spire, where the roped climbing begins.
routes.descent: Two single-rope rappels from fixed anchors back to the east notch, then downclimb the approach gully.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble back to the top of the route and make two single-rope rappels (bolted/fixed anchors) down the line of ascent to the east notch. From the notch, downclimb the approach gully - glissading remaining snow where present, or picking carefully through loose talus/scree later in the season - back to the Glacier Basin Trail, then retrace the approach through Monte Cristo townsite to Barlow Pass.
routes.itinerary: Car-to-car is feasible in a long day for a fit, fast party (biking the Monte Cristo road saves significant time and energy), but many parties break the roughly 4,000 ft of total elevation gain into an overnight at Glacier Basin, climbing East Wilmans Spire the following morning and hiking/biking back out to Barlow Pass the same day.
routes.bail: The route is short enough that retreat from any belay is straightforward: rappel or downclimb back to the east notch and descend the approach gully rather than continuing higher.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on gully conditions and party speed rather than the clock - the approach gully becomes progressively more rockfall-prone as the day warms and as snow melts out, so prioritize being off any snow-covered gully section before afternoon softening. Retreat is straightforward from anywhere on this short route.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in the shoulder season, roughly May-June, while the approach gully is still snow-covered - this gives firmer, more predictable travel and meaningfully less rockfall exposure than the loose scree/talus that emerges by midsummer. By late July-August the gully is largely melted out, loose, and considerably less pleasant (though still climbable); the route remains an option into September in good weather.
routes.season: May-Sep (best May-Jun while the gully holds snow)
routes.face: Southeast Face (Beckey-Staley Route)
routes.fa: Fred Beckey & Herb Staley, August 1945
routes.rappels: 2 single-rope rappels from fixed anchors to the east notch
objHaz: rockfall in the approach gully once snow-free
loose scree/talus underfoot on the approach in late season
knownHazards: The route's volcanic breccia rock is loose in places, particularly on east-facing sections and low on pitches.
The approach gully sheds rockfall once it melts out of snow, typically by mid/late July.
Late-season talus/scree in the gully is loose and unpleasant, increasing slip and rockfall risk.
The summit is very small and exposed on all sides.
watchOut: Helmet on early - the approach gully is an active rockfall funnel once snow-free.
The wandering, wide-drag line means extra slings help more than a stiff, straight rack.
Two old fixed pins protect the P3 crux crack - inspect and back up with gear where possible.
Route-finding on the ramp and slab sections is not always obvious; the correct line trends west then back east near the top.
proTips: Climb early season (May-June) while the approach gully is still snow-covered for firmer, safer travel and far less rockfall exposure than the bare, loose gully later in summer.
A mountain bike on the flat, gated Monte Cristo Road saves significant time and effort on the roughly 4-mile approach to the townsite.
A 50m rope is sufficient for the route as described in 3 pitches; a 60-70m lets you combine pitches but adds rope drag on this meandering line.
A #2 Camalot-size piece is specifically useful gear for the crux crack section.

pitchDetail.notes: Belay from the east notch and climb up the east side of the spire a short ways, then downclimb a 5.5 crack about 20 ft to reach the ramp.
Ascend the ramp up and west to the corner where it ends; set up a belay.

Climb a short slab to a platform leading to a 1-inch crack protected by two fixed pins; joining the crack is 5.6, the crack itself eases to 5.4. The crack leads to a ledge; from there head a little east to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack is sufficient: a set of nuts and cams (small to about 2 inches, with a #2 Camalot noted as particularly useful), optionally a few hexes, plus extra slings/draws to manage rope drag on the wandering line. Two old fixed pins protect the crux crack on P3 but should be backed up with gear where possible.
routes.proNeeds: Standard light alpine trad rack - nuts and cams up to roughly 2 inches - protects all three pitches adequately; the crux P3 crack has two old fixed pins as well. Plenty of slings help on the meandering ramp/corner terrain to reduce rope drag.
whatToBring: ice axe and crampons (early season, for the snow-covered approach gully)
approach shoes or light boots for talus/scree
mountain bike (optional, for the Monte Cristo road)

road.name: Monte Cristo Road
road.status: Gated at Barlow Pass; closed to motor vehicles, open for hiking and mountain biking
road.seasonalGate: Not a seasonal gate - the road has been closed to motor vehicle traffic long-term; the paved Mountain Loop Highway itself can have separate winter closures near Barlow Pass
road.driveNote: Paved Mountain Loop Highway ends at Barlow Pass (about 31 miles east of Granite Falls via the Verlot Public Service Center). Park at the Barlow Pass Trailhead and continue on foot or bike on the gated Monte Cristo Road.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime West Cascades weather typical of the Glacier Peak Wilderness/Monte Cristo area - the range receives heavy winter snowpack and frequent precipitation even in the shoulder seasons.
climate.spring: Approach gully typically still snow-covered into May-June, giving firmer travel but requiring snow travel gear.
climate.summer: Gully melts out by mid/late July, exposing loose talus/scree and increasing rockfall; warm, drier weather is more reliable July-August.
climate.fall: Climbable into September in good weather; increasing chance of early snow and shortening days.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective - deep snowpack, avalanche terrain in the approach gully, and a closed access road make it impractical.

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service - Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required at Barlow Pass Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency pass
access.closures: Monte Cristo Road beyond Barlow Pass is permanently gated to motor vehicles (foot/bike only). A washout near the Twin Bridges beyond Monte Cristo may require fording the South Fork Sauk River.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 emergency; Snohomish County Sheriff's Office coordinates search and rescue via Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue (non-emergency office line 425-388-3328)
emergency.rangerStation: Verlot Public Service Center, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District), ~11 miles east of Granite Falls on the Mountain Loop Highway
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (Level IV Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: This is a remote drainage reached by a multi-mile foot/bike approach on a gated road - expect limited to no cell coverage beyond the highway, so plan accordingly (satellite communicator recommended). Per USFS advisory, surface water in the Monte Cristo area may contain arsenic and other heavy metals exceeding drinking-water standards; standard backcountry filtration does not remove heavy metals.

routes.corrections: Sources disagree on the route's overall grade and pitch count: Mountain Project describes the current line as Alpine Grade II, 3 pitches, 140 ft, with a 5.6 crux. SummitPost and a CascadeClimbers.com trip report instead describe the historic Beckey-Staley line as Grade III; 5.4, in 2 pitches. Both likely describe the same route with different eras/conventions of description; this entry follows Mountain Project's more detailed current pitch breakdown for rockGrade/pitches/routeFt while noting the alternate III/5.4/2-pitch description. Summit elevation also varies slightly by source: ListsOfJohn gives 6,248 ft (used here); PeakVisor gives 1,911 m / 6,270 ft. The given peak elevationFt was null in the source data and could not be assigned a single figure in this schema since Wilmans Spires comprises three separate towers of differing height (Mountain Project's area page cites 6,458 ft, which may refer to a different one of the three spires, not East Wilmans Spire specifically).

hierarchyNote: Osprey Wall is a crag (not a summit peak) at GPS 47.43549, -121.42322, elev. ~3,543 ft, in the Alpental valley off I-90 Exit 52 near Snoqualmie Pass. Mountain Project's own hierarchy places it under Washington > "Central-West Cascades & Seattle" > "Snoqualmie Pass Area" — i.e., Central Cascades / I-90 corridor terrain, NOT the North Cascades (which refers to the Mt. Baker / North Cascades National Park country well to the north, along/beyond Highway 20). If this area is currently filed in the DB under a "North Cascades" parent bucket, that placement should be corrected to a Central Cascades / Snoqualmie Pass Area bucket — the peak identity and route data themselves are otherwise well-confirmed and internally consistent across Mountain Project's area and all three route pages, with no conflicting information found on SummitPost, Peakbagger, or theCrag (none of which have a page for this small, recently-established crag, first documented ~2019-2020).
blurb: Osprey Wall is a small, secluded alpine rock crag in the Alpental valley near Snoqualmie Pass (King County, WA), reached via a short woods-and-scree approach off Alpental Road. Its variable-quality, moss-prone slab rock is best climbed once fully dried out in summer; the wall's three documented routes (Salish, Traverse, and Prey) were all put up between 2019 and 2020 by local climbers Tom Beirne, Christian Junkar, and Mik Metzler. Prey, the wall's flagship line, follows corners, roofs, and an arete on quality white marbled rock to a celebrated 5.10c "money pitch."

routes.fa: Tom Beirne and Christian Junkar, September 2019
routes.face: Upper ledge system linking Salish's rap tree to Prey's P1 belay ledge
routes.overview: Traverse is a short connector pitch that links the top of Salish with the P1 belay ledge of Prey via an obvious exposed ledge system, rather than a standalone destination climb.
routes.beta: Connects top of Salish and the Prey P1 belay ledge through an obvious ledge system. Poor rock quality and a few step-downs make it an exciting and exposed outing.
routes.approach: From the west-side pullout across from the Alpental neighborhood entrance off I-90 Exit 52 (Alpental Road), a maintained trail through the woods (~5 min) reaches a large boulder and scree field; ascend up and left past the end of the rockfall to the mossy ramp (~15 min total from the car). Traverse itself is accessed from the top of Salish.
routes.turnaround: As a short, low-elevation crag with a fast (roughly 20-minute) approach, turnaround decisions here should be driven by remaining daylight, party pace across the loose rock, and incoming weather rather than a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Best in summer (roughly June-September) once the mossy rock has fully dried; the approach can hold steep snow into early season and the ledge system is markedly worse when wet.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack, nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Single rack with nuts is sufficient for protecting the ledge traverse.
whatToBring: Sturdy approach shoes suited to loose, step-down terrain
proTips: Treat it as a connector pitch between Salish and Prey's P1 ledge rather than a standalone objective
Move carefully through the step-downs — rock quality is poor in sections
watchOut: Poor rock quality with loose sections
A few insecure step-down moves
PG13 rated — some runout
knownHazards: Loose rock along the ledge system
Exposed traversing above the wall

pitchDetail.notes: Ledge-system traverse from Salish's rap tree to Prey's P1 belay ledge; poor rock and a few step-downs.

road.name: Alpental Road (Forest Service Road No. 9040), from I-90 Exit 52
road.status: Paved and maintained; the Alpental valley is accessible year-round as it serves the Alpental ski area, though winter snow/ice affects driving conditions
road.driveNote: Take I-90 Exit 52, follow Alpental Road into the valley, and park at the marked west-side pullout across from the Alpental neighborhood entrance — do not park in unauthorized spots in this residential area.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Maritime Cascades climate: wet, snowy winters and drier summers; at ~3,500 ft the approach and wall can hold snow well into early summer.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on approach slopes into May/June and the slab is often wet.
climate.summer: July-September is driest and most reliable; rock quality on the mossy slab is best once fully dry.
climate.fall: Conditions remain summer-like through September before wetter, colder weather sets in.
climate.winter: Steep snow coats the approach and wall; not a winter rock objective.

routes.corrections: Given data listed pitches=0 and grade=null; Mountain Project confirms YDS 5.4 (PG13), ~210 ft (64 m), a single connector pitch, FA Tom Beirne & Christian Junkar, Sept. 2019.

routes.fa: unknown
routes.rappels: Single 60 m rope reaches the starting ledge via a fixed rappel; a rap tree tops the route.
objHaz: Undocumented/aged fixed gear found in cracks
routes.face: Far right side of Osprey Wall's main face
routes.overview: Salish climbs the far right side of Osprey Wall's main face: fourth-class moves lead to a starting ledge below an obvious rock "shield," and a quick boulder move gains the shield's top at a rap tree that also serves as the start of Traverse.
routes.beta: Begins on the far right side of the main face; a few fourth-class moves lead to a starting ledge below the shield's roof. A quick boulder move leads to the obvious shield, which ends at a rap tree (~5 m). The route connects into the Traverse pitch. An old hex and Eiger USA biner were found stuck in the shield, suggesting an earlier unrecorded ascent; the route is credited to the Salish people who occupied this land long before rock climbing existed here.
routes.approach: From the west-side pullout across from the Alpental neighborhood entrance off I-90 Exit 52 (Alpental Road), a maintained trail through the woods (~5 min) reaches a large boulder and scree field; ascend up and left past the end of the rockfall to the mossy ramp (~15 min total from the car).
routes.descent: Rappel from the rap tree at the top of the shield, or continue via the Traverse pitch.
routes.descentText: A rap tree marks the top of the shield; a single 60 m rope reaches the starting ledge on rappel. From the top, climbers can either rappel back down or continue across the ledge system via the Traverse pitch toward Prey's P1 belay.
routes.turnaround: As a short, low-elevation crag with a fast (roughly 20-minute) approach, turnaround decisions here should be driven by remaining daylight and party pace rather than a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Best in summer (roughly June-September) once the mossy slab has fully dried; the approach can hold steep snow into early season and the shield is worse when wet.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack; the crack system "eats stoppers," so bring extra small-to-mid nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Single rack of nuts/stoppers; extra small-to-mid sizes recommended since placements consume gear.
proTips: Bring extra small-to-mid nuts — the cracks "eat stoppers"
A single 60 m rope reaches the start ledge via rappel
Old hardware (a hex and biner) found in the shield hints at an earlier, unrecorded ascent
watchOut: Loose fourth-class moves before the starting ledge
Awkward gear placements that can consume your rack
knownHazards: Unrecorded FA and aged hardware found in place suggest caution around any old fixed gear
Exposure on the finishing shield above the rap tree

pitchDetail.notes: Fourth-class approach moves to a ledge, then a boulder move up the shield to the rap tree; ~90 ft total.
